Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment          PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ
1       INTRODUCTION
1.1.1   This Environmental and Social Baseline Conditions Report is an Annex to the Environmental and
        Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) for the Rogun Hydropower Project (HPP) (hereafter referred to as
        ‘the Project’).
1.1.2   The Laws of Tajikistan, including international agreements to which Tajikistan is a party, will apply to
        the Project. There are potentially a number of financial institutions (hereafter referred to as
        ‘Lenders’) involved in the Project, including the World Bank (WB), Asian Infrastructure Bank (AIIB),
        Islamic Development Bank (IsDB), Asian Development Bank (ADB), and several others. A full list
        including an overview of their environmental and social (E&S) frameworks is provided in Volume 1,
        Chapter 2 (Legislation and Standards).

1.2     PROJECT OVERVIEW
1.2.1   The Project when complete will include a total of six 630 megawatt (MW) turbines. The installation
        and commissioning of the turbines is being completed incrementally. As of 2023, two 400 MW
        turbines have been installed and are currently operational.
1.2.2   Once complete, the dam will have an overall height of 335 meters (m), with a generation capacity of
        3,780 MW and an average annual generation of over 17,000 gigawatt (GW) hours. Construction of
        the Project (hereafter referred to as Stage 2) is ongoing and is expected to be completed in 2032,
        with full inundation of the reservoir area expected to be reached by 2038.

1.3     PURPOSE OF THE REPORT
1.3.1   This document describes the full environmental and social baseline conditions for the Project and is
        an Annex to Volume 1: ESIA. A summary of environmental and social baseline conditions is
        presented in Volume 1, Chapter 7 of this ESIA.
1.3.2   This document presents information from a wide range of publicly available information, as well as
        independent studies carried out for the ESIA and associated documentation. The existing
        information has been reviewed for consistency and these studies are referenced where appropriate.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 1 of 370
2       AIR QUALITY

2.1     INTRODUCTION
2.1.1   This section presents the baseline data for Air Quality for the Project. The information presented
        here is summarized in Volume 1, Chapter 7 of this ESIA.

2.2     CLIMATIC CONDITIONS
2.2.1   Temperature, precipitation and wind have the potential to impact baseline air pollutant
        concentrations through the limitation of the availability of pollution sources and their dispersion.
        Section 3.1: Climate Baseline of this Annex provides a summary of the climatic conditions at the
        Project Site.

2.3     EMISSION SOURCES
2.3.1   The following areas of the site contain potential sources of emissions to air:

        Figure 2-1 - Upstream Area - Stockpiles, Plant, Conveyors and Transport Routes for Lot 2
        Dam Construction (WeBuild)




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |

2.4     POLLUTANT SPECIES
2.4.1   The main pollutants of interest during construction are:
           Dust in the form of deposited dust and particulate matter (PM) – PM10 and PM2.5 (note that
            baseline information for PM2.5 is not available); and
           Nitrogen oxides (NOx) and carbon monoxide (CO) from fuel combustion, particularly vehicles
            and plant within enclosed spaces.
2.4.2   Air pollutant concentration data has been collected to characterize the air quality baseline. The data
        evidence a suite of readings which have recorded outdoor and indoor dust (Total Suspended
        Particulate), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), nitrogen oxide (NOx), sulfur dioxide
        (SO2), hydrogen sulfide (H2S), methane (CH4), propane (C3H8) and silicon dioxide (SiO2).

2.5     BASELINE DATA COLLECTION METHODOLOGY
        LEGISLATIVE REQUIREMENTS
2.5.1   As a requirement of the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the Protection of Atmospheric Air
        (2012), air quality monitoring for the Project is required under Article 28.
2.5.2   Air quality monitoring is undertaken by the Republican Military Mine Rescue Service (RMMRS),
        under the Head of the State Safety Control Service in Industry and Mining under the Government of
        the Republic of Tajikistan. A branch of the RMMRS was established for the Rogun HPP in 2021
        (OSCE, 2022).




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 3 of 370
2.5.3   As a contractor requirement, WeBuild commenced a routine monitoring program at the site in 2023.
        Samples were taken in final week of (across one day or two days maximum) each month between
        November 2023 and October 2024.
        TRAINING
2.5.4   The RMMRS has historically held responsibility for the training of specialists to complete monitoring
        and management of air quality. Their responsibilities include ensuring that the air quality in mines
        meets Tajik safety standards, derived from the Russian Federation Occupational safety standards
        system (RussianGost, 1988), to protect workers from hazardous conditions1. A branch of the
        RMMRS have been commissioned to undertake various elements of safety and maintenance,
        including emergency drills and cleaning the surface of the reservoir as well as ensuring safety of the
        air environment within the tunnels. Monitoring hazardous gases, particularly dust and explosive
        gases, is one of the core responsibilities of the RMMRS.
        EQUIPMENT
2.5.5   All monitoring is undertaken using hand-held devices which are provided by central government.
        Scheduled and unscheduled monitoring takes place. The RMMRS use the following equipment for
        air quality monitoring and have been provided training on their use.
           Psychrometer – used to check humidity inside the tunnels;
           ShI-10 mining interferometer (ШИ-10) – used to measure methane (CH4)
           PKA-01 (ПКА-012) – measurement of airborne dust;
           M-02 – combustible gases, CH4 and explosive CH4 compounds, carbon monoxide (CO),
            hydrogen sulfide (H2S); and
           Cup anemometer – wind speed.
        CALIBRATION
2.5.6   The 2023 training schedule shows that eight training sessions on air quality monitoring and analysis
        were undertaken in that year. The current policy prescribes that the equipment is sent every six
        months to the Agency on Hydrometeorology under the Committee for Environmental Protection for
        calibration.
        POLLUTANT SPECIES
        SAMPLING LOCATIONS
2.5.7   Samples are taken outdoors and indoors at the following outdoor and indoor locations:
        Outdoor
            ▪   Upstream cofferdam (dust, CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
            ▪   Downstream Coffer Dam (dust, CO, NO2, NO, SO2, H2S, CH4, C3H8 and SiO2);
            ▪   GIS yard (portal of T4) (dust);


        1 Russian standard GN 2.1.5.1338-03 on maximum allowable (permissible) concentrations for pollutants in the outdoor air

        of populated areas.

         MITIGATION CRITERIA
2.5.10   The mitigation criteria currently applied to the site are summarized in Table 2-1.

         Table 2-1 – Mitigation Criteria (Air Quality)

          Pollutant                  Criterion                         Units

          Dust                       6 (6,000)                         mg/m3 (µg/m3)

          Oxygen                     19.5                              %

          CO2                        0.5                               %

          CO                         17.5 (20.1)                       ppm (µg/m3)

          NO2                        2.5 (4.7)                         ppm (µg/m3)

          NO                         5 (6.3)                           ppm (µg/m3)

          SO2                        3.8 (10.0)                        ppm (µg/m3)

          H2S                        7.2 (10.2)                        ppm (µg/m3)




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 7 of 370
          Pollutant                        Criterion                                 Units

          CH4                              1                                         %

          SiO2                             0.5                                       %

          Wind speed                       0.1                                       m/s

          Radiation                        1.37                                      µSv/h

          Conversions between ppm and µg/m3 assume standard air pressure of 1013 mb and ambient temperature of 25°C.

2.5.11   The criteria are applied as indicators of elevated concentrations of pollutants to determine the need
         for mitigation beyond international best practice. They are not intended to be used as indicators of
         compliance or otherwise with the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the Protection of Atmospheric
         Air (2012) for which the Project Standards are defied in Volume 1: Chapter 08: Air quality, of this
         ESIA.

2.6.3   The monitoring data collected for the period November 2023 and October 2024 indicate the
        presence of dust at all sampled locations inside and outside the tunnels. Outside the tunnels, the
        highest spot samples were taken upstream and downstream of the cofferdam and the main dam
        core outside winter when temperatures are higher. Inside the tunnels, the highest samples were
        taken in the tunnel 2 entrance and tunnel GIS yard. There were no exceedances of the dust criterion
        in the sampling period.
2.6.4   Pollutant species produced by fuel combustion (CO, NO2, NO and SO2) were detected at the
        cofferdam, main dam core, quarry no. 26, GGT and the asphalt plant. However, no exceedances of
        the mitigation criteria were detected.
2.6.5   The pollutants H2S and SiO2 have been detected outside and crucially indoors. Exceedances of the
        carbon monoxide silica dioxide mitigation criterion indicate elevated levels of this pollutant were
        present in the sampling period.
        SITE AUDIT (2023)
2.6.6   WSP completed an independent site audit in May 2023 to assess the routine monitoring program
        compliance with international best practice, and the veracity of the results.
2.6.7   During the audit, a series of air quality spot samples of particulate matter (PM10) were collected.
        These were taken using a hand-held light-scattering laser photometer that provided real-time
        aerosol mass concentrations.

        Results
        Visual Inspection
2.6.9   A visual assessment of the site described both in previous audits and various site visits provides the
        following additional context (see Figure 2-76 to Figure 2-80)
           The construction area on the left bank generates significant dust, which is visible as a cloud
            above the site when viewed from a distance.
           A substantial amount of fugitive dust is generated due to the movement of construction traffic on
            dry and dusty haul roads within the site, primarily on the left bank—this is the case only in dry
            conditions, which would prevail in summer and early autumn. Most above-ground roads are
            unpaved, and watering of road appears to be inconsistent across the site, with left bank roads
            better (but not adequately) watered, but the Lot 3 right bank access road to the site being
            extremely dusty and poorly watered, resulting in large fugitive dust emissions when used by
            vehicles.
           Fugitive dust is generated by handling of dusty materials, and evidently from trucks dumping
            materials down the side of embankments on a regular basis.
           Borrow pits, spoil storage areas and stockpiles are uncovered and observed as major sources of
            fugitive dust under windy conditions, although there are relatively few workers present to be
            exposed.
           Trucks carrying materials are not covered, but exposed materials nevertheless appear to be a
            relatively minor contributor to fugitive dust across the site.
           The existing conveyor belt transporting materials from the upstream stockpile and primary
            crusher to the dam site is covered and generates little dust It has replaced a fleet of dump
            trucks, thus reducing traffic and dust by a significant amount in recent years.
           An 8-9km covered conveyor (called the ‘flying belt’) has been constructed to takes materials
            from the downstream quarry and stockpiles to the dam site. This led to a reduction in fugitive
            dust and exhaust emissions by reducing the number of vehicles required to transport materials.
           Fugitive dust and particulates are generated by underground vehicles, contributing to very poor
            air quality in the tunnels. In many locations, there is poor or no mechanical ventilation. In
            underground roads where daylight is not visible, and where no natural ventilation is possible,
            visible dust is present despite water ingress into tunnels from pipe work and other sources,
            which would be expected to have a mitigating effect (see Error! Reference source not found.).
           A high intensity of diesel-powered vehicle movement in the tunnels continue to emit PM2.5, NO2
            and CO resulting in poor underground air quality. A deep haze in the air of many tunnels and in
            the powerhouse and other underground workplaces (especially where penstock and intake
            tunnels and shafts are being excavated) is likely to be a combination of humidity, diesel
            emissions, and fine particulates, which is considered almost certainly to present a significant
            health hazards to those who experience chronic exposure—this includes powerhouse staff and
            also construction staff since construction has been ongoing for nearly a decade and will continue
            until 2029.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 48 of 370
            Ventilation of underground spaces and works is not considered sufficient, and the equipment
             observed (large fans with no connecting ducts or tubes) simply moves dirty air around the
             tunnels as opposed to introducing clean air from outside.
2.6.10   Visible dust generation above ground and the deep haze underground demonstrate that more
         comprehensive management is required to improve current conditions at the site, and in particular to
         reduce risks for those working on site (particularly underground), those now residing in nearby
         Construction Camp 1, the settlement located in Construction Site 2, and future worker
         accommodation in Construction Site 2 and Area 5 downstream of the dam.

         Audit Summary
2.6.11   The results show that the measurements taken were generally within the adopted criteria for safe air
         quality within the working environment, however, given the short-term nature of sampling, the
         findings should be considered indictive only. The results cannot be used to assess regulatory
         compliance.

2.7      SUMMARY
2.7.1    To meet the requirements of the Law of the Republic of Tajikistan on the Protection of Atmospheric
         Air (Article 28), a comprehensive monitoring program has been established at the Project site. An
         independent audit of the program in 2023 determined that to indicate the presence of air pollutants
         and localized elevated concentrations, the program is for purpose. However, given the short-term
         nature of sampling, the findings should be considered indictive only and cannot be used to assess
         regulatory compliance.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment             PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                            SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page 57 of 370
2.7.2   The program has been designed to detect a suite of air pollutants including dust, combustion
        products, H2S, CH4 and SiO2. All pollutants were detected at the site with plumes of visible dust
        dispersed at rates determined by prevailing meteorological conditions. Only the pollutant SiO2 and
        CO triggered the mitigation criteria during the short sampling program.
2.7.3   For the purpose of assessing compliance with the air quality Project Standards described in Volume
        1, Chapter 8: Air quality, the monitoring program in its current form does not provide adequate
        data for the following reasons:
           Spot samples do not represent air pollutant concentrations for the same exposure periods as the
            Project Standards for both ambient and occupational exposure;
           The sampling period is not a full year and therefore may not represent pollutant concentration
            fluctuations caused by all meteorological conditions; and
           The instrumentation used is not able to sample for the exposure periods required to assess
            against the Project Standards.
2.7.4   Further work will now be completed to refine the monitoring program to provide data to inform air
        pollutant management actions, embedded design mitigation and regulatory compliance.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |

        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 58 of 370
3       CLIMATE AND GREENHOUSE GASES

3.1     CLIMATE BASELINE
        CURRENT CLIMATE BASELINE
3.1.1   Tajikistan is the smallest country in Central Asia; approximately 93% of its territory is mountainous
        regions and 6% is occupied by glaciers. Tajikistan is in the temperate continental climate zone with
        moderate climate variables typical of Central Asia. Climate variability is linked to its geological sub-
        state, which includes plain areas and mountains. Tajikistan is prone to natural disasters; typically
        avalanches in winter, flash floods in the spring and high temperatures and dust storms in the
        summer (World Bank, 2020).
3.1.2   The glaciers form an important role in regulating the climate whilst also retaining water and
        controlling flows (UNDP). Climate change impacts are already observed in Tajikistan, with 20 billion
        cubic meters of ice volume within glaciers already lost during the 20th century (about 2.5%), which is
        likely to be accelerated by increasing temperatures. Observations since 1930 suggest that the total
        area of glaciers has decreased by approximately 30%. It is estimated that the total glacial area will
        decline by up to 20% within the next 30-40 years, depleting water resources and increasing the risk
        of mudflows and floods (Global Support Programme).
3.1.3   The climate change data describes trends in precipitation, temperature, wind and the impacts
        throughout the operational design life. Detailed hydrological data and trends affecting the operation
        of the Project can be found in Volume 1: Chapter 13: Impacts on Water of this ESIA.
3.1.4   Information on the current climate of Tajikistan has been obtained from the World Bank Climate
        Change Knowledge Portal (World Bank , 2020) and this serves as the baseline for the assessment.
        The Project is located in the Districts of Republican Subordination (DRS) located across central and
        west Tajikistan, which the World Bank Climate Change Knowledge Portal refers to as Tadzhikistan
        Territories and baseline climate information for the sub-region of the DRS is presented as
        representative of the regional climate.
        Temperature
3.1.5   The temperatures experienced across Tajikistan are moderated by the mountainous terrain.
        Average monthly temperatures in the DRS are slightly warmer in comparison to the rest of Tajikistan
        as presented in Figure 3-1.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 59 of 370
         Figure 3-1 - Average Monthly Temperature for the DRS (red) and Tajikistan (orange) (1991-
         2021)




3.1.7    The coldest month in the DRS is January with an average temperature of -8.5°C and an average
         minimum temperature or -12.9°C. July is the warmest month with average temperatures in the
         region of 17.1°C, and maximum summer temperature reaching 24.1°C.
3.1.8    Seasonal temperatures range from an average of -6.9°C in winter (December, January, February)
         and 15.9°C in the summer (June, July, August). The average minimum temperature in winter is -
         11.0°C and average maximum summer temperature is 23.1°C.
3.1.9    Table 3-1 shows historic temperature records from the 30 year time periods covering 1961 to 1990
         and 1991 to 2021. The data shows that minimum average winter temperatures have increased by
         1.07°C and maximum average summer temperatures have increased by 0.37°C.

         Table 3-1 – DRS Historic Average Temperature Comparison

          Year                            Minimum        Maximum       Average Annual Temperature (°C)
                                          average        average
                                          winter         summer
                                          temperature    temperature
                                          (°C)           (°C)

          1961-1990                       -12.10         22.71         4.17

          1991-2021                       -11.03         23.08         4.86

         Precipitation
3.1.10   Average monthly precipitation in the DRS is generally higher in comparison to the rest of Tajikistan,
         as presented in Figure 3-2.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 60 of 370
         Figure 3-2 - Average Monthly Precipitation for the DRS (blue) and Tajikistan (pink) (1991-
         2021)




3.1.11   Within the DRS, the wettest month is March recording 126.9mm of precipitation. September is the
         driest month with 8.5mm of precipitation recorded.
3.1.12   Seasonally, average winter (December, January, February) precipitation is 289.5mm with the
         summer season (June, July, August) recording on average 76.4mm.
3.1.13   Precipitation records from the 30 year time periods covering 1961 to 1990 and 1991 to 2021 indicate
         increases in summer, winter and annual average precipitation rates of the DRS as detailed in Table
         3-2 which shows that from 1991 to 2021, average annual precipitation in the DRS was
         approximately 67mm higher than the average for 1961-1990. Increased precipitation, and changes
         to seasonal precipitation can result in flash flooding.

         Table 3-2 – DRS Historic Average Precipitation

          Year                     Minimum average           Maximum average           Average Annual
                                   winter precipitation      summer precipitation      Precipitation (mm)
                                   (mm)                      (mm)

          1961-1990                60.96                     253.35                    801.65

          1991-2021                76.42                     289.49                    868.95

3.1.14   Water stress indicators for the region show that the Project is in an area of low (<10%) annual water
         stress. The Project is in an area at medium risk (0.4 – 0.6) of drought in the current baseline, where
         drought risk is categorized as low between 0.0 and 0.2 and high between 0.8 and 1.0) (World
         Resources Institute, 2021).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 61 of 370
         Wind
3.1.15   Wind speed data is not available from the World Bank Climate Change Knowledge Portal. However,
         Meteoblue presents observed climate data for the DRS (Meteoblue, 2024) with data obtained from a
         weather station approximately 72km away from Project site shown in Error! Reference source not f
         ound.Figure 3-3. This data is provided as an indication of the wind speeds at the Project site. Wind
         speeds are typically lowest in July and August and highest in March.

         Figure 3-3 - Average Monthly Wind Speed (mph) for the DRS 2022




3.1.16   Dust storms are common in Tajikistan due to the aridity of the region situated in the ‘global dust belt’
         which extends from the Sahara to the Gobi deserts. Dust storms are typical between April and
         November, however they are primarily associated with lowland areas. Between 2000 and 2014, the
         average annual number of days with a dust haze in Dushanbe (located approximately 100km west
         of the Project) was 12, and seven of those were noted as a strong haze were visibility is less than
         2km (Abdullaev, S. F., and Sokolik, I. N., 2019).
         Solid Mass
         Mudflow and landslides within mountainous and foothill regions are unpredictable and result from
         saturated ground, generated through precipitation and/or melting glaciers. Avalanches are where
         large amounts of uncompacted fresh snow are set into motion. The DRS is classified as having a
         high risk of landslides as rainfall patterns, terrain slope, geology, soil, land cover and (potentially)
         earthquakes that make localized landslides a frequent hazard phenomenon (GFDRR, 2020). Most
         mudflows occur in spring in lowland areas and in summer in highland regions when snowmelt
         occurs. Avalanches are typically observed in February and March (OSCE, n.d.)

3.2      GREENHOUSE GASES (GHG)
         Tajikistan, as a signatory to the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change, submits reports on
         the country’s GHG emissions. The most recent Nationally-Determined Contribution (NDC) report
         was submitted in 2021 and includes a GHG inventory covering the period 1990-2016. This data
         shows that Tajikistan’s emissions fell precipitously after 1990, from a high point of over 35,000
         MtCO2eq to a low of around 8,000 MtCO2eq in 1998. Total emissions remained around or below
         10,000 MtCO2eq from the mid-1990s until the mid-2010s. Increases were noted after 2015,
         although it is noted that in 2016, total emissions remained just 39% of the 1990 total. These large
         falls in emissions came largely from the energy and agriculture sectors (Updated NDC of the
         Republic of Tajikistan, 2021).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 62 of 370
Figure 3-4 - GHG Inventory of Tajikistan, 1990-2016 (MtCO2eq)




Data from the International Energy Agency is less complete than the NDC Report, with very limited
data regarding agricultural and industrial emissions as only CO2 from fuel combustion is covered.
However, the data covering the period 2000-2022 shows a similar direction of travel, with static, low
emissions recorded from 2000 until the mid-2010s, with a sharp rise from 2014 until 2022. This rise
was driven largely by increased burning of coal and oil (IEA, 2023). It should be noted that this has
occurred in the context of significant population growth, with the Tajik population increasing from
approximately 6.3 million in 2000 to 10.1 million in 2023 (World Bank Group Data, 2024), as well as
poverty reduction efforts (see Section 12, Social baseline).

Figure 3-5 - Estimated fuel combustion emissions of the Republic of Tajikistan (IEA) (MtCO2)
and population 2000-2022




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 63 of 370
3.2.1   Tajikistan’s energy sector causes approximately 75% of the total GHG emissions of the country.
        While hydropower provides over 40% of the energy mix of Tajikistan and over 90% of domestic
        electricity generation (IEA, 2023), industrial and residential coal consumption has increased sharply
        in recent years. The Tajikistan Development strategy by 2030 states the policy goal of scaling up
        energy production to meet both domestic and export demand primarily through increased
        hydropower capacity, as well as the development of renewables such as solar (UNECE, 2024).
3.2.2   Hydropower is considered to be a clean and renewable source of energy, although emissions are
        incurred at various points throughout the lifecycle of a plant. During construction and maintenance,
        emissions and incurred by the production and transportation of materials, and the operation of on-
        site plant and facilities. Reservoirs can also be net emitters of GHGs, especially early in their
        lifecycle, due to the decomposition of organic matter which produced methane which then dissolves
        in the water, and is released either as bubbles or as water passes through the turbines, although the
        strength of this effect is determined by the specificities of the reservoir, and in some cases
        reservoirs may be carbon sinks (International Hydropower Association, 2022) (BBC, 2024) (Office of
        Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, n.d.). Indirect emissions due to land use change may
        also occur, if carbon sinks are lost (Alsaleh, Mansur Abdulwakil, & Samad Abdul-Rahim, 2021).

4.1     INTRODUCTION
4.1.1   The baseline noise conditions presented in this section correspond to current conditions. The
        original submission of the ESIA did not include baseline noise data.

4.2.1   A baseline noise survey was undertaken in May 2023 to establish the noise climate within the
        Project site. The baseline noise conditions are representative of typical construction activities at a
        given time on site and they do not intend to represent the noise climate of the entire duration of the
        construction program. Baseline measurements consisted of short-term attended measurements and
        are therefore only representative of conditions at the time the measurements were undertaken.
        However, the baseline data acquired serves as useful indicator of the existing noise climate.

5       SOILS AND GEOLOGY

5.1     INTRODUCTION
5.1.1   While the western part of the Central Asia is generally low-lying and gently undulating, the high
        mountains of the Pamir and Tian Shan ranges in the south and east are the westernmost extension
        of the Himalayan Mountain belt. Glaciers, together with permanent snow and ice cover, are
        widespread. Several peaks rise to over 5000m.
5.1.2   Figure 5-1 shows the proportion of soil types present nationally across Central Asia (European
        Comission & FAO, 2023).

        Figure 5-1 - Soil types by proportion in Central Asia




5.1.3   The high elevations of Tajikistan mean that the upper slopes are permanently covered with ice and
        snow that support permafrost-affected soils. Lower slopes are covered by grass and shrubs that
        provide inputs of organic matter to the soil. However, limited rainfall and low temperatures for most
        of the year mean that soil-forming processes are slow. The mountain slopes eventually grade into
        broad plains, which are crossed by ephemeral rivers that often drain into land-locked saline basins.
        Lowland soils are poorly developed, generally gravelly or sandy, with sparse vegetation. More than
        90% of Tajikistan is mountainous territory. Half of the country sits 3,000 meters or more above sea
        level (asl). The valleys are important for Tajikistan’s human geography but make up around 7% of
        the country’s area. The Vakhsh river valley lies within the Project’s AoI and is the largest river in
        Tajikistan.

5.2     SOILS
5.2.1   As most of Tajikistan is covered by mountainous terrain, with only 7% of the land area in valleys, the
        soils of the country are varied and unevenly distributed. The country’s varied climatic conditions also


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 79 of 370
        result in a range of different types of soils. The parts located at lower altitudes have soils that are
        generally dark brown, and in the highlands are typically light brown (ADB, 2019).
5.2.2   Tajikistan’s soil is typically poor in humus but rich in mineral nutrients. Sand, shingle, scree, bare
        rock and permanent snow and ice cover about two-thirds of the surface.
        Cambisols
5.2.3   Based on the soil forms of the Harmonized World Soil Database (HWSD), the site is underlain by
        Cambisols (FAO, 2015). Most Cambisols occur in regions with a precipitation surplus but in terrain
        positions that permit surficial discharge of excess water which is consistent with conditions around
        the site (FAO).
5.2.4   A Cambisol in the World Reference Base for Soil Resources (WRB, 2022) is a soil in the beginning
        of soil formation, so soil horizon differentiation is weak. This is evident from weak, often brown
        discoloration and structure formation in the soil profile and the presence of appreciable quantities of
        weatherable minerals. Cambisols are derived from a wide range of geologies, mostly in alluvial,
        colluvial and aeolian deposits. Most Cambisols are medium-textured and have a good structural
        stability, a high porosity, a good water holding capacity and good internal drainage, making them
        good agricultural soils, and are intensively used. Cambisols in temperate climates are among the
        most productive soils on earth, subject to climatic and slope conditions.
5.2.5   Cambisols are common in areas with active erosion where they may occur in association with
        mature tropical soils. Many of the photographs taken at the project site do show soils that qualify as
        Cambisols (refer to Figure 5-2. The other soil forms identified at the site included Ferralsols,
        Arenosols and Leptosols. The distribution of these soils is shown in Figure 6-8.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page 80 of 370
        Figure 5-2 - Cambisols




        Leptosols
5.2.6   Leptosols are also present on Site and are largely found in mountainous regions and in areas where
        soil has been eroded to the extent that hard rock comes near to the surface. These are very shallow
        soils over hard rock (refer to Figure 5-3). Leptosols are very typical of areas with slopes as steep as
        those of the study area. This means that they often have limited depth (soil surface within 25cm of
        continuous hard rock from the soil surface). This is because soils are constantly eroded off the


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 81 of 370
        slopes, leaving only a thin layer of soil and bedrock. Their depth limitations make these soils are
        unattractive rainfed agriculture because of their inability to hold water. As such, and as is the case
        around the AoI, areas where leptosols are present are mainly used for grazing livestock. However,
        given it is relative stability, this can lead to sustained soil erosion. This is discussed further later in
        this section.

        Figure 5-3 - Leptosols




        Arenosols
5.2.7   Arenosols are also present within the Project’s AoI. These are sandy soils with no soil profile
        development and are devoid of subsurface clay accumulation. They are dry, desert-like soils that
        have low organic content and are sparsely vegetated by drought- or salt-tolerant plants.



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 82 of 370
5.2.8   Their excessive permeability, low humus and nutrient content limits their agricultural capacity without
        irrigation. This also characterizes them with a surface horizon which is light in color, dry for most of
        the year and often by a significant accumulation of migrated layer silicate clay, soluble salts, or
        sodium ions (Britannica, Earth sciences, n.d.). These can be fluvial soils, as is the case across much
        of the Site (refer to Figure 5-8).




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 83 of 370
Figure 5-4 - Fluvial Arenosols




Ferralsols



Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page 84 of 370
5.2.9   Ferralsols are present at the Site and are red and yellow weathered soils which are technically
        defined by a fine-textured subsurface layer of low silt-to-clay ratio, being rich in kaolinite and metal
        oxides, particularly iron and aluminum. Because of the residual metal oxides and the leaching of
        mineral nutrients, they have low fertility (Britannica, Britannica, n.d.). They are typically deep soils
        that are very homogeneous in color, texture, structure and mineralogy (see Figure 5-5).
        Figure 5-5 - Ferralsols




        As mentioned, much of the study site was highly disturbed. A soil that has been heavily modified
        due to long-term human activity is classified as an Anthrosol. For the purposes of this study, where
        soils could fit into more than one soil form category, they have been given more descriptive names,
        such as Ferralsol Anthrosols, Cambisol Anthrosols and Arenosol Luvisols. Where red Ferralsols and
        brown Cambisols have been identified stockpiled in construction areas, these have been called
        Ferralsol Anthrosols (see Figure 5-6) and Cambisol Anthrosols (see Figure 5-2). Where sandy
        Arenosols have been identified on the riverbanks where they have clearly been deposited by fluvial
        activity, these have been called Arenosol Luvisols (see Figure 5-4).




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |

        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 85 of 370
Figure 5-6 - Ferralsol Anthrosols

Figure 5-9 - Focussed Site Soils




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page 88 of 370
         SOIL DEGRADATION
5.2.10   Land degradation is a major obstacle for food security and broader sustainable development in
         Central Asia. This region, consisting of Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and
         Uzbekistan, is a major hotspot of land degradation across the world3. Among the countries of the
         region, Tajikistan is considered the most vulnerable to desertification. Soil erosion is caused by
         natural processes of wind and rain and anthropogenic from activities such as overgrazing livestock
         and vehicle movements. Climate change is a significant driver of wind erosion in particular, as long
         hot, dry periods cause the soil to become looser and more friable.
5.2.11   In Tajikistan common causes have been referenced as rising temperatures, droughts, the irrational
         use of land and inaccessibility to irrigation. Moreover, unmanaged water flows led to surface run off
         gradually eroding the topsoil of irrigated land. The worst affected area in Tajikistan is the Khatlon
         province where 885 ha have been taken out of agricultural circulation (Central Asian Bureau for
         Analytical Reporting (CABAR), 2024).
5.2.12   Since 2015 the country began to implement the state program to return degraded lands to
         agricultural use. This has brought activities such as strip cropping, mulching, gabion construction,
         gully rehabilitation, plantation of shrubs and trees, wind break poplar plantation and pasture rotation.
         This has led to a decrease in the rate of soil erosion from 150 t/ha to 8-15 t/ha/ year in Tajikistan. By
         employing appropriate soil management practices, a considerable amount of nutrients such as
         nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur and potassium was retained in farmland for crop production, instead of
         being lost in the wind or water runoff to streams and rivers.

5.3      GEOLOGY
5.3.1    The Republic of Tajikistan has a complex relief and geological structure. The rocks covering the
         country were formed from the Archean-Proterozoic to the Quaternary period and are mainly
         represented by rocks formed through volcanic activity and rocks of sedimentary origin. The
         geological structure of the country is divided into Karamazar (Northern Tajikistan), Central Tajikistan
         (Hissar – Alai), Pamir (with Darvaz), Tajik depression and Fergana depression. The project is
         located in the Hissar-Alai region (ADB, 2019) at the junction between two of the major structural-
         facies zones in Central Asia, specifically the Tajik depression and the South-Western Tien Shan,
         this being expressed in the vicinity of the dam by the Vakhsh Fault, which is a steep thrust fault
         (Torgoey et al, 2017). According to Torgoey et al, 2017 most of the Project facilities are situated
         within a single tectonic block, bounded by two subparallel branches of the Vakhsh Fault: The
         Ionakhsh and Gulizindan faults respectively.
5.3.2    The Rogun TEAS (Coyne et Bellier, 2014) found that the main lithological groups identified at the
         dam site include:
          The Upper Jurassic salt formation, primarily composed of salt, subordinate gypsum and a thin red
           mudstone layer. In the Project area it is shaped as a wedge truncated by the Ionakhsh Fault, the
           thickness of which is assumed to increase by 15m for every 100m in depth.




         3Mirzabaev, A., (2023) ‘The impact of land degradation on agricultural profits and implications for poverty reduction in

         Central Asia', Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0264837722005579 (Accessed:
         10.10.2024).


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                            Page 89 of 370
         The Lower Cretaceous continental sequence, mainly composed of sandstone, siltstone and
          mudstone layers. The thickness of this sequence at the dam site is in the range of 1,100m.
         The Marine sequence - principally Upper Cretaceous - composed of sandstone, siltstone and
          shales, typified by the presence of limestone, marls and gypsum layers.
5.3.3   The primary tectonic feature at the site is the Ionakhsh fault which extends along the reservoir,
        broadly following the left of the Vakhsh valley, crossing the river within the upstream section of the
        dam. The fault then continues in the rock mass on the right side of the valley, forming a steeply
        dipping fault at an angle of between 65o (Torgoey et al, 2017) and 80o ((Kolichko and Fil’, 1981) to
        the southwest under the dam (Kolichko and Fil’, 1981).
5.3.4   Along the fault, Upper Jurassic and Cretaceous rocks come into contact (Torgoev et al, 2017), with
        the Jurassic sediments comprising predominantly halite overlain by mudstone. The Lower
        Cretaceous strata are reported by Torgoev et al (2017) to comprise irregularly interbedded
        sandstones and siltstones with some subordinate mudstones. In the reservoir area Kolichko and Fil’
        (1981) report the presence of Upper Cretaceous marine mudstones, limestones, sandstones, and
        gypsum.

        Salt Wedge Treatment
5.3.5   The Rogun dam site and reservoir lie in close proximity to the Vakhsh Fault, Ionakhsh Fault and
        Gulizindan Fault. In particular, the Ionakhsh fault crosses the site on the upstream part of the dam
        axis (Figure 5-10).




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 90 of 370
Figure 5-10 - Location of the main geological faults in relation to Rogun HPP




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page 91 of 370
5.3.6    These faults are comprised of Jurassic-Cretaceous sediments which, through successive
         earthquakes have pulled the Jurassic salt formations to the surface. A key consideration of the dam
         design is the management of the risk of freshwater intrusion through the dam to these sediments
         preventing the salt formations from dissolving. The potential impact on the Dam from the Salt
         Wedge has been studied since the initial design during Soviet times in the 1970s.
5.3.7    The Salt Wedge that fills the Ionakhsh Fault, is approximately10 m thick at the top (at around 950 m
         asl) and increases in thickness with depth.
5.3.8    The area is tectonically very active, and geodetic measurements carried out before 1978
         demonstrate that both the Lonakhsh Fault and Vakhsh Fault were creeping at a rate of about 1.5 to
         2 mm per year. When it comes to evaporites, and especially salt, leaching is a phenomenon that
         may be very rapid and could have significant consequences to the Project ( Figure 5-11).
5.3.9    The 2014 Engineering and Dam Safety Panel of Experts for Rogun HPP reviewed the Geological
         and Geotechnical Investigation of the Salt Wedge in the Dam Foundation and Reservoir as set out
         in the TEAS 2014. The following mitigation was proposed:
            ▪   Hydraulic and grouting barriers.
            ▪   Installation of a monitoring system to follow-up performance of the treatment works during
                reservoir filling and operation.
            ▪   The monitoring system triggers pre-defined indicators.
            ▪   Contingency plans which identify remedial measures to be implemented in the event of salt
                leaching.
5.3.10   With the implementation of hydraulic and grouting barriers, related monitoring system, and the ability
         to undertake remedial works when required, the DSPoE 2014 concluded that the Project is feasible.
5.3.11   Additional investigations by LOT2 Contractor WeBuild were undertaken in 2017 for the Salt Wedge
         to estimate rock permeability at the riverbed, with the implementation of ongoing Salt Wedge
         Monitoring in 2020.
5.3.12   The Ionakhsh fault is the most important of the local faults that could provide a preferred path out of
         the reservoir. It is associated with a salt wedge at the river crossing and at the intake to the diversion
         tunnels and the right abutment and extends to Nurek Dam. It is reported by OSHC "Barki Tojik",
         2013 that within the Ionakhsh fault zone there is a salt unit comprising halite and anhydrite caused
         by the tectonic stresses in the region migrating into the valley floor where it is eroded and dissolved
         by the overlying river. OSHC "Barki Tojik", 2013 report that the salt unit has a wedge shape with a
         thickness of approximately 1.5 m to 2 m in the left bank and up to 12 m in the right bank. OSHC
         "Barki Tojik", 2013 report that thickness of the salt unit increases with depth, increasing in thickness
         by approximately 15 m with every 100 m depth. In the Rogun dam and reservoir area the
         Quaternary sediments are reported by Torgoev et al (2017) as being fragmented in distribution and
         varying in thickness and origin. Talus deposits including a loam matrix are reported as being present
         on the upper sections of the right-bank slope, reaching a thickness of up to 60 meters. Within the
         dam area itself Kolichko and Fil’ (1981) report talus cones of up to 20 meters in thickness with
         boulder-gravel deposits not exceeding 8 m to 12 m in thickness. In the upper part of the reservoir
         Kolichko and Fil’ (1981) report alluvial terrace deposits up to 100 m thick.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 92 of 370
Figure 5-11 - Geological and tectonic structure of Rogun area, with the upper figure being a
plan of faults in the dam area and the lower diagram comprising a schematic cross section of
the site showing the geological structure and geodynamic setting (from Torgoev et al, 2017)




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page 93 of 370
5.3.13   The salt wedge has been considered a project risk from the early stages of project development and
         mitigation thereof has been an important part of the design phase. An early attempt to mitigate
         potential dissolution of the salt wedge included grouting the top of the salt wedge and maintaining a
         high hydraulic head downstream thereof to keep the groundwater above the wedge saturated by the
         injection of brine.
5.3.14   Downstream pressure balancing injection wells were supposed to be connected to the pressure
         tunnel, while the brine was injected upstream of this line of pressure balancing wells. Unexpectedly,
         the absorption of brine reduced to very low values, indicating clogging of the injection wells, and
         creating doubts about the viability of the brine injection system. Subsequent attempts at salt wedge
         mitigation focused on reduction of the rock mass conductivity to reduce salt leaching to very low
         values.
5.3.15   The operation of the salt wedge mitigation system is particularly important during main dam phase 1,
         but once the dam reaches elevation level 1110m the core of the main dam becomes the impervious
         element, and the importance of the salt wedge mitigation system decreases as the pressures will be
         balanced by the reservoir waters.
5.3.16   A staged approach to the technical studies is being undertaken, Seismic studies have been
         completed for the Stage 1 of the construction (i.e. up to 1,110 m asl). Seismic studies for the Project
         to full height are being commissioned and will commence in 2025. These studies are not expected
         to require substantive design changes, however, where concerns are identified, updates to the
         design will be undertaken in line with the Management of Change Process as required by the
         ESMP.
5.3.17   The following features have potential influence on the Rogun Dam design.
          The salt wedge as elaborated upon above.
          Evaporite masses and karst - Some foothills of the Vakhsh Ridge on the left bank of the Vakhsh
           river, are formed by evaporite rock masses aligned on the Vakhsh Fault. The visible part of these
           masses is principally made up of gypsum, but there may also be salt present. They were also
           identified in the valley of Passimurakho, which is the extension along strike of the Vakhsh Fault
           lineament. It is thus possible that a salt diapir would underlie the interfluve between
           Passimurakho and Obi-Djushon valleys. These exhibit intensive karst dissolution features. These
           and the formation of sinkholes can be accompanied by superficial landslides. Such processes
           have previously led to sinking in the city of Rogun, causing damages to buildings. Seismic Scarps
           - Evidence of Upper Quaternary and modern age earthquakes was found in the reservoir area.
           Seismic scarps are evident over a segment of at least 15km at the foot of the Vakhsh Ridge. A
           seismic scarp between the Tagikamar and Khodzhaalisho streams could be linked to an ancient
           rockslide, which could be reactivated as water levels rise above the 1250m elevation. Slope
           stability - Significant potential slides have been identified, corresponding to an evaluation of slope
           stability. The most important issue is related to the landslide mechanism triggered by the
           dissolution of salt at the toe of the slope. Such landslides would mostly occur in the left bank
           (Coyne et Bellier, 2014). According to the existing Project ESIA (Poyry Energy Ltd., 2014), the
           main geological formations of the general Rogun area are the Paleozoic granite on the right bank
           of the Vakhsh River valley and the Mezo-Cenozoic terrigen-carbonate formation on the left bank.
           A South-Hissar group of structural-formational zones are located on the right bank of the valley.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 94 of 370
          The left bank consists of deposits of the ‘Afghan-Tajik depression’. The regional tectonic stresses
           involve sudden co-seismic rupture and permanent aseismic creeping (Poyry Energy Ltd., 2014).
5.3.18   The site geology largely alternates between sandstone and siltstone. Sandstone weathers to
         produce soils with a sandy texture and siltstone weathers to produce soils with a silty texture. Sand-
         and silt-dominated soils tend to be largely devoid of macrostructure, as it is typically the clay fraction
         of a soil that provides structure.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 95 of 370
6       CONTAMINATED LAND

6.1     INTRODUCTION
6.1.1   Heavy metals, persistent organic pollutants (POPs) from industry and pesticides, and novel
        pollutants originating from pharmaceuticals and personal care products are most commonly found in
        contaminated land across the world. Hydrocarbons and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are also
        common sources of pollution and most commonly affects land close to transport infrastructure and
        industrial facilities.
6.1.2   Industrial waste is a major driver of land contamination. The best available data for the volume of
        mining and industrial waste produced annually in Tajikistan is 1,267,646 tons in 2014. There is no
        available data on the volume of hazardous waste produced annually, but it is widely acknowledged
        that it is a prominent issue as there is no defined method for treatment and management of
        hazardous waste in Tajik law. Approximately there are 65 sites affected by land contamination from
        the industry practice and the disposal of industrial and municipal waste in Tajikistan (Ulugov, U., et
        al., 2018).
6.1.3   Soviet-era agrochemicals are a significant source of land contamination in Central Asia, as policy
        mandated the use of pesticides in agriculture which were used extensively in Tajikistan, many of
        which are now known to be hazardous. Contaminated sites are particularly present in the west of the
        country as that is where arable land is primarily located.
6.1.4   Historically, Tajikistan had several regions of large-scale agriculture and extensive pesticide use,
        with an estimated 63,000 tons of agricultural pesticides delivered for agricultural use between 1965
        and 1990. Tajik use of dichloro-diphenyl-trichloroethane (DDT) between 1960 and 1970 for growing
        cotton was estimated to be 8,700 tons per year, primarily in the western regions of the country
        (Barron, Mace G. et al., 2007).
6.1.5   Tajikistan is known to contain multiple large uncontrolled pesticide burial sites. These include the
        Soviet-era pesticide disposal site in the southwest of the country near the city of Vakhsh around
        100km from the site, which has been estimated to contain 3,000–9,500 tons of uncontrolled and
        obsolete organochlorine pesticides.
6.1.6   During the 1990s, many former pesticides stored in Tajikistan were privatized, resulting in the
        discovery of obsolete pesticides by the new owners. These pesticides have since been disturbed
        and spread by construction works and erosion, leading to soil contamination in villages (FAO, 2022).
6.1.7   The average waste generation per year in Tajikistan is approximately 2.6 million tons. There are
        approximately 70 official municipal solid waste landfills in Tajikistan.
6.1.8   Landfill sites are known to be a significant potential source of contamination risks as they produce a
        substance known as leachate, which is formed when water soaks through layers of waste and
        accumulates a high concentrations of water-soluble chemicals and suspended particles, some of
        which are highly toxic. Leachate can then seep into the surrounding environment and spread
        contamination to soil and groundwater. Older sites may also contain hazardous chemicals which
        have since been banned.
6.1.9   Plastic waste can also be a source of land contamination wherever human activities are present.
        Plastics are generally non-hazardous in the short term. However, improperly discarded plastic waste



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 96 of 370
         can have a number of negative environmental impacts, including on the soil. Plastics do not
         biodegrade but break into smaller and smaller microplastics over time which are absorbed into the
         soil, water environment, and food chain. Many plastics also contain hazardous additives which leach
         into the soil as the plastic begins to degrade. Some plastics may degrade into novel pollutants over
         time or under UV radiation, which has a demonstrable negative impact on soil organisms as well as
         polluting the surrounding environment (Ford, F., et al., 2022). This is a risk for the Project where
         plastic waste is disposed of at landfill sites created for the Project or resettlement sites, or in cases
         of improper disposal (e.g., littering by Project workers).
6.1.10   WSP attended site between Monday 4th and 8th Friday September 2023, during which time WSP
         were escorted around several key areas, including both areas identified as part of the 2014 ESIA
         works (ESIA Report (Final) Vol III Environmental and Social Management Plan, 2014), areas of
         potential concern identified during a review of existing information and areas identified at the time of
         the site walkover. These included, but were not limited to:
            WeBuild on-Site diesel fueling station;
            WeBuild workshop;
            WeBuild waste oil and waste filters storage location;
            Batching Plant A;
            WeBuild rock crusher; and
            Upstream reservoir banks (left side).
6.1.11   It is noted that site conditions are not considered to be materially different than described in the
         ESIA. Some improvements have been observed, such as recent improvements in occupational
         health and safety and traffic management, and there has definitely been an improvement in
         supervision of environmental and social performance by Contractors. These improvements are in
         response to actions taken in advance of the effectiveness of the Environmental and Social
         Commitment Plan.WSP staff’s judgement of the general situation around the site were restricted
         because they were mainly shown WeBuild locations, which was not a representative sample of the
         whole site. There were marked differences in the management and operation of the sites and
         facilities between the different contractors and organizations, this report does not look to identify
         individual companies or organizations for good or bad practice but instead seeks to identify general
         trends and or areas requiring improvement and action. The key findings and observations from the
         visit are summarised in this section. No stakeholder engagement was undertaken by WSP staff
         during their site visit.

6.2      FUEL STORAGE
6.2.1    WSP visited 6no. tank farms (4no. were active, 2no. were abandoned or inactive) and multiple single
         tank installations (both active, inactive and abandoned) during the site walkover. As a general
         comment, there was not consistency to the fuel storage standards being applied across the site, with
         difference organizations citing different standards that they had implemented, or that installation had
         complied with standards when installed but had not been updated since the time of installations
         (sometimes decades old).
6.2.2    Most commonly the operators referred to either new or current ‘Russian Standards’, the compliance
         of the installations against the ‘Russian Standards’ is beyond the scope of this report. However, it


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 97 of 370
        should be noted that a number of operators stating their installations were compliant with the current
        ‘Russian Standard’ had significantly variable installations, including but not limited to; the
        construction of the tanks (single skinned / doubled skinned), tanks sited within a bund, tanks being
        buried / covered, pipework running above or underground, installations installed on concrete hard
        standing and spill response equipment.
6.2.3   The contractor management plans related to fuel storage and contaminated land will provide details
        on fire safety and spill prevention measures in the fuel storage areas; plans for presence of
        appropriate signage; the level of staff awareness and experience regarding spill or fire incident
        procedures; and the availability of trained staff on site.
6.2.4   In general, conditions remain approximately the same as noted in the ESIA: fuel storage is variable,
        with some leaking tanks and some GIIP. Resolution will be ensured and actions will be taken
        pending World Bank Project effectiveness, and overall increased attention to environmental and
        social matters.
        MAIN ROGUN TANK FARM (CONSTRUCTION AREA 1).
6.2.5   The ‘Main’ Rogun Tank Farm, located in Construction Area 1, has been identified in previous reports
        as a potential area of concern, with anecdotal evidence of surficial soils being saturated with diesel
        and ‘dripping’ out of the southern retaining wall. During the site visit conducted in September 2023,
        WSP did not find the soils to be saturated with diesel, however, there was evidence of localised
        spills around refilling points and seepage from below the level of tanks (Note: It had not rained for
        several days before and did not rain during the September 2023 visit). It should be noted that ‘lower’
        buried tanks on the southwestern corner of the installation were being filled at the time of the visit.
        Figure 6-1 - Visible Seepage from ‘Main’ Rogun Tank Farm




6.2.6   The buried tanks at the highest point of the tank farm (southeastern corner) are situate on the top of
        a slope above the main site access road. It was noted that the previously buried tanks have started
        to become exposed with recent rains washing the surficial soils down the slope towards to the main
        access road.
        ABANDONED & DISUSED TANK FARMS
6.2.7   WSP visited 1no. abandoned and 1no. disused tank farms.
6.2.8   WSP could not establish the operator of the ‘abandoned’ tank farm, which anecdotally was installed
        during the soviet era, and has not been active for a least 4 years. Access to inspect the tanks was
        limited to high level gantry situated above the level of the tanks. From the limited vantage point, no
        visual or olfactory evidence of contamination was noted during the September 2023 visit.

        settlement pond was in the process of being excavated to the east of the ‘sewage treatment works
        laboratory building. WSP did not enter the building, but the building appeared derelict from the
        outside.
6.4.3   Unknown effluent appeared to be bypassing the settlement tanks, the origin of the effluent could not
        be established during the site visit however, appeared to be entering the area from multiple
        upgradient directions. There were no solid wastes observed either within the settlement tanks or in

        WASHOUT AREAS
6.5.3   The majority of the Contractors spoken to during the September 2023 site visit seemed to be
        unaware of the polluting nature of concrete wash waters i.e., high pH (11-12) due to calcium
        hydroxide (derived from the cement); high suspended solids; and other trace materials (originating
        from both cement and from additives).
6.5.4   Across the site WSP witnessed a number of different setups and practices with respect to the
        ‘washing out’ of cement mixers, ranging from well considered wash down areas that drained into
        settlement pits with subsequent to water treatment to washout areas with no control, capture or
        treatment of the washout water. With the latter being the more common practice witnessed across
        the site.
6.5.5   A number of areas appeared to drain directly into the reservoir or river, with visible buildups of
        cement down gradient of the washdown areas. When discussed with the contractors / operators,
        they referred to surface water monitoring undertaken by another party and that if there was a
        significant environmental impacts Rogun HPP would have halted their practices.

        bunded segregation to general waste areas with no discernible segregation. Anecdotal information
        suggested some areas belonged to contractors who have not operated onsite for at least 5 years.
6.6.3   Additional areas of waste storage included a large stockpile of tires with no clear management or
        ownership. Waste oil storage in some cases was not noted to be bunded and was draining to
        ground. Multiple drums of bitumen stored on open ground; however, it was noted that this waste was
        awaiting collection from an appropriate waste carrier. WSP were told a number of the sites would
        only be cleared, on completion of the contractors works, with a couple of contractors stating it was
        the responsibility of Rogun HPP to remove the waste, this contradicted discussions with the Rogun
        HPP team.

6.7     SUB-STATIONS & TRANSFORMERS
6.7.1   A number of transformers and sub-stations were noted around the construction site. WSP did not
        visit or inspect any of the transformers or equipment, however, based on the age of the project it is
        likely PCB (Polychlorinated Biphenyls) transformer oil was utilized historically onsite and may still be
        present within the some of the legacy equipment.
6.7.2   Speaking with a representative from the utility company onsite, the oil is tested before
        decommissioning to assess whether the oil is suitable for re-use or disposal offsite.

6.8.1   Possible Asbestos Containing Materials (ACMs) in the form of corrugated asbestos sheeting were
        noted on a handful of buildings within Construction Area 1.

6.9     SUMMARY
6.9.1   In summary, there are some small areas on the construction site where fuel and other petroleum-
        based compounds have contaminated the surface and shallow subsurface soil, and some locations
        where asbestos fibers are present and exposed. As indicated above, detailed surveys have not
        been completed. Nonetheless, going forward, waste management plans will be prepared by the
        contractors (based on the waste management frameworks included in the ESMP) to ensure proper
        management of each type of waste.
