8.1.12   The area between the confluence of the Surkhob and Khingob rivers and the village of Nurobad
         (where the river Muksu meets the Surkhob river), is characterized by high sediment deposits which
         form distinctive braided channels and sand bars (refer to Figure 8-7). Large areas of flatter land are
         present here which is mostly occupied by village settlements and agricultural land use. The
         floodplain is quite wide in this area and submergence due to FSL 1290 will be contained by the
         existing riverbanks. Maximum resettlement is anticipated in this area during the second stage of the
         resettlement program due to the extent of lower-lying land. The existing elevation of the riverbed
         varies between 1230 and 1260 m amsl.

The landscape of the project site is quite similar, where the river has low-lying flood plains with
agricultural land and settlements on one side and high hills on the other side. There are also limited
stretches where the river is confined within high hills on both sides (refer to Figure 8-8). These
enclosed reaches of the valley have narrower floodplains and are prone to landslides every year,
contributing to the sparse vegetation on these unstable slopes.

9       BIODIVERSITY

9.1     BASELINE DATA COLLECTION
9.1.1   The baseline presented in this report represents the current condition after existing and approved
        disturbances, such as the partially-completed dam wall with its current inundation to 1070m asl, and
        therefore Project impacts to be assessed include the filling of the reservoir up until the maximum
        inundation level (i.e. to 1290m – hereafter referred to as the ‘flooding zone’), along with other Project
        components such as new roads and stockpiling areas. The baseline therefore does not consider any
        impacts that have already occurred to date, although for context, a review of historic aerial imagery
        from over the last two decades reveals that the habitat assemblages lost to the now partially-
        completed project are a continuation of what has been recorded further upstream, with significant
        modification prevalent throughout. Both terrestrial and aquatic biodiversity baseline conditions are
        described, within a varying Area of Influence (AoI – described for each receptor/receptor group
        within Volume 1: Chapter 15: Biodiversity of this ESIA). The objective of the baseline data
        collection was to collect sufficient information to describe the ecological conditions across the
        Project’s AoI (which encompasses the flooding zone and other Project components, together with a
        conservative estimate of a wider area where Project impacts may reasonably be assumed to occur
        and across the wider study area as appropriate (e.g. such as the requirement to understand bird
        movements across the wider area)).
9.1.2   Baseline data collection has relied upon the existing information contained within the 2014 ESIA,
        supported by additional desktop study and targeted fieldwork. A summary of the baseline and
        assessment presented within the 2014 ESIA is provided.

9.2     TERRESTRIAL ASSESSMENT (2014)
9.2.1   The 2014 ESIA was informed by an extensive desktop study and a sampling-based suite of
        vegetation surveys at 25 representative sample sites within close proximity to the Project footprint
        (these field surveys undertaken in 2011). Exact details regarding survey methods are unclear,
        although it appears sampling comprised a detailed botanical inventory at each location. No habitat
        mapping was undertaken, instead species occurrence across the sample sites was described in
        terms of broad habitat types recorded within altitudinal bands, as follows:
         Floodplain of the Vakhsh River and the riverbanks.
         Elevation from 700 to 1500m asl.: wheatgrass-dominated meadows, transitioning to mesophilic
            broad-leaved forest at higher altitudes within this band.
         Elevation from 1500 to 2500m asl.: juniper-dominated mesophilic woodland.
         Elevation from 2800 to 3400m asl.: subalpine meadows.
         Elevation from 3400 to 3800m asl.: alpine meadows.
9.2.2   Much of the information presented within the 2014 ESIA relates to areas that are likely to be wholly
        outside the Project AoI. With regards to habitat composition across the Project itself, the 2011
        surveys recorded a habitat assemblage comprising the following broad habitat types:
           River and floodplain habitats which is a natural habitat, with little impact from humans.
           Cultivated land including crops for humans and livestock and therefore lacks natural vegetation.


        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 163 of 371
            Urban areas where the settlements are located, including buildings and roads as well as garden
             including a high number of fruit trees.
            Pasture habitat which is heavily degraded by grazing by livestock.
9.2.3    With regards to flora of interest, a number of Tajikistan Red Book species were recorded, although
         none of these were within the Project or its AoI.
9.2.4    The 2014 ESIA concluded that impacts from the Project to habitats and flora would be negligible and
         not significant due to the prevailing modified habitat conditions and lack of species of conservation
         interest/value. The exception to this was identification of two floodplain habitats, which will be lost
         within the flooding zone. It was suggested within the 2014 ESIA that these areas potentially qualify
         as Natural Habitat (as per ESS6) and will therefore require a No Net Loss (NNL) plan to be
         developed.
9.2.5    The faunal assemblage reported in the 2014 ESIA comprised two amphibians, 12 reptiles, 37
         mammals and 154 bird species, with these records seemingly derived from an extensive desktop
         study. These species were described with reference to the five altitudinal bands listed above and
         included a number of species listed on either/both of the Tajikistan Red Book and IUCN Red List (as
         threatened), such as snow leopard, Himalayan brown bear and Egyptian vulture.
9.2.6    The study concluded that impacts to fauna will be small, by virtue of the Project AoI being heavily
         modified and therefore not supporting important populations of any rare/notable species (most of
         which were recorded/described from higher altitudes). It was acknowledged that increased human
         influence at higher altitudes may have an impact although this wasn’t discussed further.
         AQUATIC ASSESSMENT (2014)
9.2.7    In 2011, three field surveys were completed to inform the 2014 ESIA. These included surveys of the
         Obi Garm and Chorsada areas (May 5-9), Rogun Reservoir area (July 18-23) and Nurek Reservoir
         (September 12-27). The fieldwork considered fish and benthic organisms and concluded that 12 fish
         species were expected to occur within the Project AoI.
9.2.8    During these studies, three fish species were confirmed to be present within the existing Rogun
         Reservoir, with a further two native species believed to occur within the area. These include the
         Common Marinka (Schizothorax intermedius), Small Turkistan Sheatfish (Glyptosternon oschanini),
         Amu-Darya Trout (Salmo trutta oxianus), Tibetan Stone Loach (Triplophysa stolickai) and Eastern
         Crested Loach (Paracobitis longicauda) (Table 9-1).
9.2.9    Further to this, three introduced species were reported to occur within the Project area, these
         include Rainbow Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss), Brown Trout (Salmo trutta afario) and Common
         Carp (Cyprinus carpio).
9.2.10   Benthic organisms were also sampled as they are an important food source for many fish. Data
         included 2 species of Acaridae (mites), 2 species of Oligochaeta (worms), 2 species of Trichoptera
         (caddisflies), 6 species of Ephemeroptera (mayflies), 12 species of Diptera (flies) and 1 species of
         Mollusca (snails) (ESIA, 2014).

9.2.11   It should be noted that since the completion of the 2014 ESIA, there have been nomenclature
         updates to three of the species:
            Turkestan Catfish (Glyptosternon reticulatum). Records for this species from Afghanistan,
             Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan have now been assigned to G. oschanini (B. Karimov, 2020).
            The Tibetan Stone Loach (Nemacheilus stoliczkai) has been assigned Triplophysa stolickai
             (Daniels, 2022).
            The Eastern Crested Loach (Nemacheilus malapterurus longicauda) has been assigned
             Paracobitis longicauda (Frethof, et. al., 2014).
9.2.12   The 2014 ESIA concluded that fish diversity and abundance within the Project AoI was low, and that
         fish were not economically important for the local population. These conclusions were based on the
         fact that longitudinal connectivity has already been lost as a result of downstream dam construction
         (such as Nurek), which prevents fish migration. Additionally, the Vakhsh River has a very high
         sediment load, which may be limiting to fish.

9.3      DESKTOP STUDY
9.3.1    The desktop study included review/interrogation of the 2014 ESIA as discussed, with an update to
         this information. Additionally, several online databases and publicly available resources were
         reviewed. These are summarised within the following sections.
         RED BOOK OF TAJIKISTAN
9.3.2    The second edition of the Red Book of the Republic of Tajikistan (TRB) was reviewed to inform the
         assessment on the value/sensitivity of flora and fauna recorded through the desk study/field survey
         exercise. The TRB was updated in 2017 and so any updates would not have been included within
         the earlier 2014 ESIA.
         IUCN RED LIST OF THREATENED SPECIES
9.3.3    The 2024-1 edition of the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s Red List of Threatened
         Species was reviewed to inform the assessment of the global conservation status of species
         recorded or expected to occur within the Project AoI as identified through the desktop studies or field
         surveys.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 165 of 371
        REVIEW OF PUBLISHED LITERATURE / AVAILABLE ONLINE RESOURCES
9.3.4   A substantial array of published literature has been reviewed to inform this assessment. These
        papers / articles are referenced throughout where applicable. In addition to the review of those
        resources that are readily available (i.e. through online search engines), a detailed exercise of
        engagement with in-country ecologists has been undertaken to inform the assessment. This has
        involved seeking direct advice from national experts, as well as the identification and review of
        national papers / reports to provide a more informed interpretation of these resources (some of
        which are in Tajik/Russian).
9.3.5   A component of the desk study has been the review of eBird records for the birds relevant to this
        study. This resource captures amateur ornithologist records from across the world and is a useful
        supplementary resource when undertaking baseline data collection. Similarly, data obtained from a
        review of the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) (GBIF, n.d.) has also been used where
        considered appropriate.
        INTEGRATED BIODIVERSITY ASSESSMENT TOOL (IBAT) REPORT
9.3.6   A biodiversity screening reporting from the IBAT website5 provided a high-level appraisal of likely,
        key biodiversity sensitivities present across the Project site, and a 50km study area. This report is a
        standard tool for flagging potential presence of Critical Habitat/Natural Habitat triggers and is an
        important component in the scoping process for additional study (e.g. field survey).
        REVIEW OF GOOGLE EARTH
9.3.7   Google Earth was also reviewed to inform the general appreciation of the habitat baseline and gain
        an understanding of the wider landscape context of the AoI, as well as the changes over time.
        Historical imagery over multiple years dating back to 2003 was used to qualitatively assess changes
        within the landscape (i.e. changes to vegetation cover within the floodplain, changes to the river
        channel, etc.).
        REMOTE SENSING
9.3.8   A remote sensing exercise was undertaken using readily available mapping data, to accurately
        map/classify habitat polygons across the Project and for a buffer distance of 2km. The process for
        habitat classification utilized remotely sensed aerial imagery and a suite of tools within the industry
        leading ESRI GIS software. Pre-processing of aerial imagery was first conducted to ensure a
        continuous dataset both spectrally and temporally giving a consistent base dataset to perform the
        automated classification technique. Two methods were considered here, untrained and trained
        classification. In this instance trained classification was utilized which enables the user to define
        spectral ranges across habitat types such as established vegetation, grassland, open water and
        bare earth while taking into account nuances such as topography, shading and profile. The tool
        builds this knowledge into the classification process to cover a large area quickly and produce
        raster-based results on the user’s input. The model and process was then re-run and refined based
        on conversations and additional data with specialists and field teams and this included manual
        amendments if needed. A final output was generated by translating the raster data to polygons to
        allow a seamless coverage that enables area calculations and querying.



        5 https://www.ibat-alliance.org/

9.3.9    This process underwent ongoing iteration following site visits and review of satellite imagery to
         assist with defining the boundaries of the various habitat types; in particular those of greatest
         sensitivity.

         HPP, as well as ongoing RfI submissions with these organizations. Information obtained has been
         included throughout this assessment where considered relevant.

         FIELD SURVEYS
9.3.13   A field survey scope was developed that reflected the nature/extent of existing information for the
         Project as presented within the 2014 ESIA. In line with the requirements of ESS6 an appropriate
         level of field survey was scoped and undertaken to address any gaps or components that require
         further or seasonal consideration. Detail regarding the fieldwork undertaken is provided within the
         following paragraphs.
9.3.14   Surveys in the vicinity of the Project were undertaken at 15 representative sampling sites, based
         upon surveyor expertise and to provide information from each habitat type across the AoI, as
         illustrated on Figure 9-1. Additional ‘ground-truthing’ of other pre-identified areas was also
         undertaken and is described where relevant (such as to obtain more detailed information regarding
         the most sensitive habitat areas).
9.3.15   Surveys were undertaken in late-spring and autumn 2023, to identify any seasonal variation in the
         biodiversity baseline that will require consideration within the Project mitigation plans. The survey
         team comprised various specialists and was led by Dr. Alikhon Latifi.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 168 of 371
Figure 9-1 - Biodiversity sampling sites




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                    Page 169 of 371
         HABITAT SURVEYS
9.3.16   Habitats were mapped through a combination of the remote sensing exercise described above,
         supplemented with field-based ground-truthing and cross-reference to existing information on the
         habitat assemblage across the Project and surrounding area. Mapping was undertaken across the
         flooding zone and a buffer distance of 2km (hereafter the ‘habitat study area’), which was later
         consolidated within the AoI (which is the spatial basis for habitat descriptions from this point
         forward). Habitats were mapped into broad habitat types in line with descriptions provided within the
         2014 ESIA but updated where considered appropriate, using resources such as the IUCN habitat
         classification system (IUCN, 2012) and the Illustrated Flora of Tajikistan and adjacent areas (Nowak,
         2020).
         FLORA SURVEYS
9.3.17   Targeted botanical surveys were undertaken in April and May 2023 at the 15 representative sample
         sites focusing on rare and vulnerable plant species across the AoI. The aim of these surveys was to
         corroborate previous surveys and to update these findings given the time that has passed since data
         were last collected to inform the 2014 ESIA. At each location, noteworthy species were recorded
         (such as those included on the TRB and/or IUCN Red List); in addition, habitat suitability was
         assessed for species with the potential to be present based upon desktop study results and
         surveyor expertise. If the habitat was found to be suitable for these species, but they were not
         observed at the time of survey, they were added to the list of species as a precautionary approach.
9.3.18   Surveys were completed by experienced botanists, including those who have previously been
         engaged on the Project (i.e., as part of the 2014 ESIA studies).
9.3.19   In addition, updates to the botanical data were made during additional targeted field survey visits, in
         relation to habitats of greatest sensitivity to increase the ecological understanding of these areas.
         These took place in April, September and October 2023.
         FAUNA SURVEYS
9.3.20   Prior to the surveys, the desk study undertaken informed a list of target species, which included
         amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammal species. These target species included rare and vulnerable
         species (including those listed on the TRB and IUCN Red List) as well as species specific to
         ecosystems present within the AoI. Surveys were then undertaken in April and May 2023, focusing
         on the 15 representative sample sites.
9.3.21   Herpetofauna (amphibians and reptiles) surveys were completed along transects of varying width
         dependent upon habitat type during both day and night-time, with surveyors recording individual
         animals as encountered as well of signs of presence and habitat suitability. Surveys were
         undertaken during spring, when herptiles are most active, and during dry/calm weather conditions
         for all surveys.
9.3.22   Bird surveys comprised walked transects of up to 1km in length and 50m wide. Birds were recorded
         as encountered, including at distances beyond the transect width, with the aid of binoculars and a
         spotting scope; bird calls were also surveyed. Supplementing this was net-trapping of birds in
         suitable locations. During October and November 2023, migratory bird surveys were undertaken
         from three vantage points (VP) overlooking the main expanse of braided channel habitat. These
         surveys comprised scanning the airspace for flight activity by migrating birds, together with recording
         use of the braided channel habitats as stopover features for migrating birds.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 170 of 371
9.3.23   Mammal surveys comprised various survey techniques. Visual observations, of both live animals
         and animal signs (such as tracks, droppings and burrows) were made from walked transects. For
         some species/species groups, this also comprised night-time surveys with spotlights to observe
         those animals with more nocturnal behavior such as porcupines and bats. Some trapping was
         undertaken for small mammals/rodents.
9.3.24   A search for bats, including roost sites, was undertaken alongside the above surveys; however,
         more detailed bat survey effort was not completed due to the abundance of desk study information
         available (including from Rogun construction site) and the difficulty in accessing areas of greater
         potential interest to bats, such as cliffs/crevices on higher slopes.
9.3.25   Surveys were completed by experienced zoologists, including those who have previously been
         engaged on the Project (i.e., as part of the 2014 ESIA studies).
         Aquatic Surveys
9.3.26   Access to the Vakhsh River within the Project area is limited due to the steep terrain. The river
         features deep, turbid, and fast-flowing waters, and as such, the river is not accessible by boat or
         conducive for wading across and therefore is not suitable for traditional survey techniques such as
         electrofishing or netting.
9.3.27   Comprehensive sampling would require people in the water, and this would not be safe under the
         existing river conditions. As the result, the Vakhsh River has not been well studied, and any
         information gained is considered valuable.
9.3.28   To this end, limited sampling using a range of equipment was conducted at four locations upstream
         of the Rogun HPP construction site. The areas sampled included Subordination, Yust, Sangdevo
         and Ali-Galabo (Figure 9-2). Sampling was completed using equipment that could safely be
         operated from the shoreline, and included, cast nets and rod and line (hooks).
9.3.29   To enhance our understanding of the aquatic biodiversity within the Vakhsh River system, water
         samples were collected for Environmental DNA (eDNA) analysis. This method is particularly
         effective for sampling aquatic biodiversity in environments where traditional techniques are
         impractical. eDNA analysis stands out as a highly appropriate and comprehensive approach for
         establishing a robust baseline of aquatic biodiversity.
9.3.30   At each sampling point, a sub-set of samples were collected along the bank, to maximize the
         chance of recording genetic material from different species that may utilize different habitats within
         the river. The sub-samples were combined to form a single composite sample (± 3 liters), from
         which water could be filtered at each site. Samples were filtered according to the collection and
         processing protocol prescribed by NatureMetrics. The process involves pushing approximately 2.5
         litres of water through a filter with a syringe. Once complete, the provided preservative was added
         and the filter sealed, and labelled for transport back to the UK, where the sequencing was
         undertaken. All permits for export and import were obtained for transportation of the filters.
9.3.31   The study design considered the following areas (Figure 9-3):
            The Vakhsh River below the Rogun HPP, upstream of the Nurek Reservoir.
            Upstream of the flooding zone in the 3 main rivers coming into the proposed Rogun
             impoundment.
            Within Nurek Reservoir.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 171 of 371
            Within the Rogun flooding zone for comparative purposes and to track changes over time.
9.3.32   Downstream of the Nurek Reservoir was not sampled, as from a fisheries perspective it is
         considered a connectivity barrier within the landscape. As such, it was not assessed as there are no
         predicted material impacts there and was scoped out as a result. This is set out in Table 15-3 -
         Receptor/Impact Summary Table of Volume 2: Chapter 15: Biodiversity, of this ESIA.
9.3.33   Whilst on site, local fishermen were also consulted in an informal manner to determine what species
         they were targeting, catching and historically knew to occur within the Project area.
         WSP SITE VISITS
9.3.34   WSP specialists visited pockets of the AoI on three separate occasions, during March, May and
         September/October 2023. These site visits provided landscape context and further insight into the
         Project to support the writing of the baseline report and assessment of impacts. The time in country
         was also used to collect additional data on a more anecdotal basis and provided the opportunity to
         meet with stakeholders and visit key habitat areas in order to verify understanding of species
         assemblage and habitat condition in these areas.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page 172 of 371

Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                    Page 174 of 371
         BASELINE STUDY LIMITATIONS AND ASSUMPTIONS
9.3.35   The baseline data collection exercise has focused upon an update to the findings presented within
         the 2014 ESIA in order to reflect a current baseline that exists within the context of a partially-
         developed project, within a heavily modified landscape. As such, the field survey components
         comprise a more targeted approach to surveys than would be expected for a ‘new’ project.
         Additionally, there are areas of the AoI that are difficult to survey from an access/H&S perspective.
         As such, it is acknowledged that for some terrestrial species groups (most notably bats), the level of
         survey effort has been lower than would be expected to fully align with GIIP. To appropriately
         address this limitation, a precautionary approach has been taken to assessment of impacts in places
         (and this is referenced accordingly). This has also informed requirements for ongoing survey, which
         would be necessary regardless given how the main impacts to biodiversity (i.e. from flooding) will be
         realized incrementally over a number of years.
9.3.36   With regards the aquatic biodiversity baseline, there were significant constraints to completing
         ‘traditional’ survey effort within the Project AoI as described above. As such, the inclusion of eDNA
         analysis was incorporated into the study design.
9.3.37   After collection and sequencing, the eDNA sequences are compared to a library of species’ DNA
         which exists from previously collected (known) samples, otherwise known as the ‘reference
         database’. If the target species are not available in the reference database, the sequencing will not
         yield any results as there is nothing to compare the results to. Due to the lack of previous studies
         and information within the Study Area, many species are likely to be missing from the reference
         database. Although this is a limitation of the technology, it was understood in the survey design and
         provides further insight into a relatively unstudied and inaccessible system.

9.4      CURRENT BASELINE
9.4.1    The baseline presented in this report represents the current condition after existing and approved
         disturbances, such as the partially-completed dam wall with its current inundation to 1070m asl, and
         therefore Project impacts to be assessed include the filling of the reservoir up until the maximum
         inundation level (i.e. to 1290m – hereafter referred to as the ‘flooding zone’), along with other Project
         components such as new roads and stockpiling areas. The assessment therefore does not consider
         any impacts that have already occurred to date, although for context, a review of historic aerial
         imagery from between 2011-2023 reveals that the habitat assemblages lost to the now partially-
         completed project are a continuation of what has been recorded further upstream, with significant
         modification prevalent throughout. Both terrestrial and aquatic biodiversity impacts are considered,
         within a varying Area of Influence (AoI – see Volume 1, Chapter 15 (Biodiversity) of this ESIA.
9.4.2    Detail within this section is provided for aspects of the current baseline that have materially changed
         since the 2014 ESIA; otherwise, a high-level summary is provided, with the previous studies cross-
         referenced as appropriate.
         SITE CONTEXT
9.4.3    Tajikistan is situated within the temperate zone, although it has a more pronounced continental
         climate, with marked intra-annual temperature fluctuations. The notable landscape diversity
         comprising three major mountain chains separated by valleys and plains results in hugely varied
         microclimate, soil and vegetative conditions. This results in an unusually high level of species
         diversity for a country in the temperate zone. There are over 23,000 species of flora fauna found


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 175 of 371
        within Tajikistan of which approximately 1,900 are endemic. Much of this diversity is associated with
        areas where human influence is absent/minimal and one of the most significant ongoing threats to
        biodiversity within the country is the ongoing/increasing modification of habitats and ecosystems as
        a result of anthropogenic pressures, most notably overgrazing by domestic livestock.
9.4.4   Tajikistan is a mountainous country (approximately 93% of the country is above 1000m in altitude,
        with over 50% above 3000m), with most ecosystems falling into one of seven broad mountain
        categories (Figure 9-4). At lower altitudes, most ecosystems comprise modified habitats in which
        agriculture and urban areas are dominant.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                             SEPTEMBER 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page 176 of 371
Figure 9-4 - Tajikistan ecosystems schematic (as taken from Safarov, 2003)




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                    Page 177 of 371
9.4.5   The Project is located within a heavily modified landscape, broadly classified as an agroecosystem
        of gardens, forest-plantations, personal plots (number 23 on the schematic above), on the Vakhsh
        River in the southern region of Tajikistan.
9.4.6   The Project is located in the Upper Amu Darya Freshwater Ecoregion, which encompasses the
        basins of the upper Amu Darya tributaries: Pyandzh (Piandj), Surkhandarya, Kafirnigan, Vakhsh,
        and Kunduz (FEOW, 2019). It also includes the upper Zeravshan, upper Tedzhen, Murghab rivers,
        and river drainages in northern Afghanistan west from Kunduz. The ecoregion encompasses mostly
        mountainous areas ranging from 240 m to over 7100 m asl in the Pamir Mountains (FEOW, 2019).
9.4.7   Habitats across the areas now lost to/affected by the partially-completed dam structure and
        associated infrastructure/works are considered to be a continuation of those still present upstream of
        the existing impoundment. The longitudinal connectivity of the Vakhsh River has already been
        interrupted by the construction of numerous downstream dams, including the Nurek Reservoir (see
        Figure 9-5). As such, the focus of this assessment is to describe the existing baseline conditions,
        which have been heavily influenced Project’s history (construction first began in 1976) and location
        (Vakhsh HPP cascade), supported by relevant information collected as part of the 2014 ESIA.
9.4.8   The Vakhsh HPP cascade comprises Rogun (under construction), Nurek (approximately 17 km
        downstream), Baipaza, Sangtuda 1, Sangtuda 2 and Golovnaya, which extends to approximately
        150 km downstream of Rogun.

         HABITATS
9.4.11   There has been no significant change to the habitat composition recorded during the 2014 surveys,
         although studies during 2023 have highlighted the presence of an additional habitat category within
         the maximum flooding zone. This area is a relatively small area of woodland that was likely missed
         because of mapping discrepancies and miscalculations during the previous habitat mapping
         undertaken for the 2014 ESIA.
9.4.12   Corroboration on habitat composition was obtained during field surveys in 2023, and this is provided
         within the following paragraphs, expanding upon a summary of previous studies where appropriate.
         The habitats are illustrated on Figure 9-7.
9.4.13   Most of the habitats present across the AoI (and including the new settlement areas) have been
         modified by humans as part of settlements and agriculture land use, with some areas also
         comprising built-up areas. Along with remnant natural/semi-natural habitats, this has resulted in a
         landscape that comprises the following habitat types (in order of relative extent across the AoI):
            Pasture/degraded grassland habitat which is heavily degraded by grazing by livestock; includes
             eroded hillsides.
            River and floodplain habitats which is natural/semi-natural habitat, with little impact from
             humans.
            Urban areas where the settlements are located, including buildings and roads as well as gardens
             including a high number of fruit trees and smallholding agriculture.
            Cultivated land including crops for humans and livestock and therefore lacks natural vegetation.
            Mesophilic juniper woodlands on the valley sides, with signs of modification but retaining most of
             their natural species’ composition.
9.4.14   A high-level summary of overall habitat composition within the AoI is provided in Table 9-3 (as
         adapted/updated from the 2014 ESIA).

         Table 9-3 - Habitat composition

          Habitat type                         Area (ha)                       Relative coverage within AoI (%)

          Pasture/degraded grassland           8690                            44.6

          River/floodplain                     4850                            29.9

          Urban areas (inc. associated land)   1441                            8.8

          Agriculture                          978                             6

          Woodland                             283                             1.7

          TOTAL                                16242                           100

         Pasture/Degraded Grassland
9.4.15   Pasture/degraded grassland is the predominant habitat across the AoI and comprises a short sward
         habitat of grasses and occasional common herbs (such as Crocus sp.) Species diversity is very low
         due to ongoing human influence (see Figure 9-8 and Figure 9-9).

         Figure 9-8 - Pasture/degraded grassland habitat near Mehrobod




         Figure 9-9 - Degraded grassland habitat near Mehrobod (eroding cliffs also visible)




9.4.16   Pasture/degraded grassland habitat is maintained through continual grazing pressure as part of
         livestock farming within the region. Much of the land being used for new settlements comprises




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                           SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page 184 of 371
         pasture (as described within the 2014 ESIA), including new settlements at Saidoni Nav, Yoli
         Garmoba and some parts of Moinkaj, Rudaki and Darband.
9.4.17   This habitat also includes areas of bare ground, which reflects the heavily degraded nature of the
         habitat, which results from the seasonal movements of large livestock herds within large parts of the
         Vakhsh valley.
         Cultivated land
9.4.18   Similar to the above, cultivated land persist through ongoing human influence, and the habitat
         supports no notable flora. Some areas of historically cultivated land are included within the new
         settlement areas including Chorsada 2, Toichi, Namuna 2, Sebiston and parts of Moinkaj, Rudaki
         and Darband.
         Urban areas
9.4.19   Given the favorable topography of the Vakhsh River valley within the AoI (relative to the wider
         mountainous terrain) there are numerous established settlements. These do not support any natural
         habitat or notable flora.
         River and floodplain habitat
9.4.20   As described within the 2014 ESIA, floodplain and braided channel habitats are present across the
         Vakhsh River valley within the AoI, with the most notable areas being located at the following
         locations:
            Adjacent to the settlement of Mehrobod
            Around the confluence of the Vakhsh and Obikhingou rivers and extending north
            Adjacent to the settlement of Navobod
9.4.21   Most floodplain habitats have been modified through ongoing human influence, including building of
         settlements (see Figure 9-11), together with extensive pasture use and grazing. As a result, these
         areas are not thought to support species of increased conservation interest (both fauna and flora).
9.4.22   There are two exceptions to the above, totaling c. 68ha: one being an area of floodplain habitat
         close to the confluence of the Vakhsh and Obikhingou rivers (within the Obikhingou River), which
         potentially aligns with floodplain pre-identified within the 2014 and is considered to be Natural
         Habitat (as per ESS6) (see Figure 9-10); and another ‘new’ floodplain area identified approximately
         6km upstream of this confluence (see Figure 9-12). Here, there are far fewer signs of habitat
         modification, with an established scrub community present, comprising dominant tamarisk Tamarix
         ramosissima, with hawthorn Crataegus azarolus, buckthorns Hippophae rhamnoides and Rhamnus
         dolichophylla all abundant. Other large scrub species include Russian olive Elaeagnus angustifolium
         and barberry Berberis vulgaris. The floodplain is also interspersed with grasses (including common
         reed Phragmites australis) and herbs (including liquorice Glycyrrhiza glabra), together with pockets
         of seasonally inundated channels/hollows. The newly identified area appears to be present as a
         result of recent geomorphological changes, which could also explain why it has persisted in a
         relatively unmodified state when compared to other floodplains in the AoI. Furthermore, the relative
         inaccessibility of both areas may mean that it they are unsuitable for livestock grazing. When
         comparing satellite imagery from recent years, it is clear that the new area was previously part of the
         braided channel; however at some point over the last 20 years, a significant amount of sediment has
         been deposited here, which appears to have elevated it to a position where it is not routinely


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 185 of 371
         inundated (Figure 9-13 and Figure 9-14 show changes to channel conditions over 20 years). This
         habitat had not been previously identified (i.e., within the 2014 ESIA) and was the subject of a
         dedicated field survey during October 2023 to provide a more robust understanding of its
         composition and structure.
9.4.23   It is unlikely that this floodplain has been formed as a result of purely natural processes, instead it
         seems that a change to land use on the left bank has resulted in bank de-stabilization that has
         caused a significant landslip, with the dislodged sediment/material being deposited at the above
         floodplain location (see Figure 9-15 and Figure 9-16). However, the resulting habitat is considered to
         be relatively unmodified. These figures illustrate the bank collapse (red circles), and the downstream
         disposition (green circles), as a result.
9.4.24   There is also an area downstream of this confluence, which is heavily modified and devoid of any
         natural vegetation communities.

9.4.25   Within a river system, habitat availability and diversity are major attributes for the biota found within,
         and thus knowledge of the quality of habitats is important in an overall assessment of ecosystem
         health. Habitat assessment can be defined as the evaluation of the structure of the surrounding
         physical habitat that influences the quality of the water resource and the condition of the resident
         aquatic community (Barbour et al., 1999). Both the quality and quantity of available habitat affect the
         structure and composition of resident biological communities (USEPA, 1998). Habitat quality and
         availability plays a critical role in the occurrence of aquatic biota.
9.4.26   Please refer to Section Error! Reference source not found. for information on turbidity in the Vaksh r
         iver.
9.4.27   More specifically, high turbidity has been shown to have physiological effects on fish, which include
         gill trauma (abrasion), blood chemistry and reduced reproduction and growth (Bash et. al., 2001).
9.4.28   The substrate of a river is defined by the organic and inorganic materials making up the riverbed.
         The inorganics include a range of sizes, from fine silts/sands, through gravels and pebbles to
         boulders and bedrocks. The organic materials are dominated by leaf litter, aquatic plants and
         wooded debris. The velocity of the water, determined by gradient erodes and deposits the different
         materials to form a heterogenic substrate or habitat.
9.4.29   Substrate heterogeneity is an important factor in determining both abundance and diversity of biota,
         with more stable substrate showing higher diversity and abundances (CBD, 2012). As particle size
         increase, so does physical complexity, so clay or sandy substrates would be considered poor sue to
         their stability, whereas cobbles and rocks would be more stable. A mixed substrate would obviously
         be the best with a variety of habitats and microflow patterns available for different biota.
9.4.30   The Vakhsh River is primarily made up of alluvial cobbles and boulders, with areas of deposited
         sediments as described above. In high velocity area such as gorges, the scouring affect is high,
         while in open channel areas, deposition occurs, to the extent that in the Novabad area, a 1.4 km
         wide braided channel has formed (Figure 9-19). This is further exacerbated by slope stability as
         shown in Figure 9-16 Within the Vakhsh River, the cobbles and boulders will provide the primary
         habitat for aquatic organisms.
9.4.31   Within the river channel, and along the banks, little to no vegetation was observed, with no riparian
         buffer zone bordering the river, and with eroding cliffs present within many sections (see Figure 9-9).
         The organic component within the channel was also limited with no leaf litter, root wads or woody
         debris observed. As a result, no canopy and little cover was present at site.
9.4.32   The confined portions (gorges) of the river channel were not accessible due to the steep banks and
         loose soils. Water velocities were generally higher as a result of the narrow channel, and it is
         expected that these sections offer the deepest water. Substrate is expected to largely be comprised
         of rock as smaller particles would be washed downstream (Figure 9-17).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 193 of 371
         Figure 9-17 - Confined channel of the Vakhsh River with steep banks.




9.4.33   Open gorges are present when the river valley opens, allowing the channel to somewhat meander
         or widen. This has resulted in the deposition of cobbles and boulders, as well as the accumulation of
         silt within the interstitial spaces where flow is reduced (Figure 9-18).

         Figure 9-18 - Open gorges of the Vakhsh River with alluvial cobbles and boulders




9.4.34   A third habitat type, braided channels are linked to incoming tributaries and bank collapse where the
         river channel widens and deposition of silt within the channel occurs. The largest of these is a 1.4
         km wide braided channel at Novabad (Figure 9-19). The water depth in the braided channels is
         relatively shallow when compared to the other habitat types, and the substrate material is made up
         of smaller particles such as silt, sand and gravels. Although this does offer a different habitat type for
         aquatic organisms, the conditions (e.g. Mobile sediments, little cover and turbidity) are not ideal for
         aquatic macroinvertebrates or fish. An investigation of aquatic macroinvertebrates showed that the



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 194 of 371
         diversity and abundance of taxa was greater in the clearer tributaries than in the turbid waters of the
         main channel, where more tolerant species (e.g. Simuliidae) were dominant.

         Figure 9-19 - Braided channels within the Vakhsh River




9.4.35   Within the Project area tributaries that do not have large catchments or that have catchments that
         are still relatively intact, had lower turbidity that the main Vakhsh River (Figure 9-20). Within the
         landscape, these tributaries are generally short in length, have a high gradient and substrate is
         comprised primarily of cobbles and boulders. Although fish migration up the tributaries will be limited
         as a result of the gradient, the lower portions are expected to be important for fish as refuge areas
         off the main Vakhsh River channel, as they offer clear, cooler waters.

         Figure 9-20 - Tributaries of the Vakhsh River




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 195 of 371
9.4.36   Overall, based on the low habitat diversity and unfavorable conditions (e.g. High turbidity) within the
         Vakhsh River, a diverse aquatic community is not expected within the Project AoI. These
         observations align with the 2014 ESIA.
         Woodland
9.4.37   Remnant mesophilic juniper woodland habitat is present within the AoI at five locations on the left
         bank of the Vakhsh River. This habitat partially aligns with the mesophilic broad-leaved forest
         described within the 2014 ESIA as occurring from 1500m to 2500m asl, although following surveys
         in 2023, a distinct woodland habitat at these five locations from approximately 1000m asl is
         considered more appropriate. This is a characteristic native woodland type for the region, occurring
         within this subalpine belt (known as ‘archevniki’) in which tree cover is relatively sparse. The
         woodland recorded from the AoI here can be further classified within this category as thermophilous
         woods, dominated mainly by J. polycarpos var. seravschina, which is an endemic tree to Middle
         Asia (Nowak & Nobis 2020).
9.4.38   The juniper woodland represents a habitat that would have formerly extended across much of the
         Vakhsh River valley but has been reduced in extent by ongoing human influence; mainly grazing
         pressure and collection of firewood/timber. Alongside J. seravschina, at slightly higher altitudes
         there is frequent Turkestan maple Acer turkestanicum, walnut Juglans regia, sycamore Acer
         pseudoplatanus, and smaller tree and scrub species such as juniper J. commune, wormwood
         Artemisia sp., Clematis sp. Afghan redbud Cercis grifilhii, apple species (including Malus sierversi),
         cherry Prunus sp. almond and hawthorn Crataegus sp.
         The woodland areas are illustrated on

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 196 of 371

         CRITICAL HABITAT / NATURAL HABITAT
9.4.39   No Critical Habitat has been identified within the AoI (as per ESS6). Two, non-critical, natural
         habitats were identified, by virtue of the absence/partial absence of modification and the presence of
         natural plant assemblages. Namely, floodplains habitat (upstream of the confluence of the Vakhsh
         and Obikhingou rivers, within both channels) and juniper woodland. Ful details regarding this
         assessment is provided within the Critical Habitat Assessment (Volume 2, Annex 15-1 of this
         ESIA). For clarity, these areas are illustrated on Figure 9-23.

         FLORA
9.4.40   Tajikistan supports a hugely diverse floral assemblage, with over 9,700 species of plant recorded, of
         which over 1,100 are endemic. Given the legacy of human influence across the AoI, the floral
         assemblage has been greatly reduced.
9.4.41   The 2014 ESIA described 63 species as being present across the Project site and wider area, with
         most of these species being common and widespread, and many being indicative of ongoing human
         influence. There were 11 species included within the TRB, and this was increased to 18 following
         surveys undertaken in 2023; however, the majority of these were recorded from outside the Project
         AoI. No species on the IUCN Red List (or CITES) have been identified across the Project site and
         wider area. Table 9-4 provides details on these species, including locations of their likely/confirmed
         presence.

9.4.42   No invasive plant species were noted during studies to inform the 2014 ESIA (with no mention of
         whether they were considered as part of the baseline studies), or from field surveys in spring 2023,
         although it is known that invasive plants are increasing in presence/extent across in the country (as
         per a CBD progress assessment undertaken 20186). During WSP’s visit in September/October 2023
         extensive presence of Bathurst burr Xanthium spinosum was recorded (likely missed from the spring
         surveys due to having a late-growing season here), in particular within modified floodplain and
         braided channel habitats throughout the flooding zone. This species is a highly invasive weed that is
         capable of growing under a range of environmental conditions. It readily establishes in cultivated
         land, pastures, meadows and riverbanks and can threaten native or endemic wildlife (Pitcher, 1989).



         6 As accessed at: https://chm.cbd.int/database/record?documentID=247303




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                        PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                       SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 202 of 371
         FAUNA
9.4.43   The following sections describe the faunal baseline as described from the field surveys, and
         includes data captured in the field and those obtained through eDNA analysis (the eDNA sampling
         exercise revealed non-aquatic fauna from three separate animal groups: mammals, amphibians and
         birds. No other animal groups were represented with the bioassays used).
         Mammals
9.4.44   Tajikistan supports over 80 species of mammals, one of which is endemic (the Bukhara or Pamir
         shrew Sorex buchariensis); and half of which have been designated a conservation status in the
         TRB.
9.4.45   A total of 38 species were recorded from literature review as being present within the Vakhsh River
         valley, which is one more than was presented within the 2014 ESIA. Of the mammal species
         recorded during the desktop studies, 12 are considered to be of increased interest/conservation
         status; these are presented in Table 9-5.
9.4.46   Nine common/widespread species were recorded during field surveys, with two (least weasel
         Mustela nivalis and Eurasian otter Lutra lutra) being included within the TRB. The remaining seven
         field survey records are as follows:
          Red fox Vulpes vulpes flavescens
          Wild boar Sus scrofa
          Tolai hare Lepus tolai
          Golden marmot Marmota caudata
          Pygmy field mouse Sylvaemus uralensis
          Northern mole vole Ellobius talpinus
          Common vole Microtus arvalis
          Eurasian otter Lutra lutra
9.4.47   Bats were recorded in low numbers from the AoI during both walkover surveys in spring 2023 as
         well as from the eDNA survey, although most of the habitat is generally considered sub-optimal as a
         result of ongoing habitat loss/degradation across most areas. No roosts/potential roost site/features
         were identified, and these are likely to be relatively limited in number/extent when considering the
         large survey area, partly due to the lack of many significant features such as caves and mature
         trees, but also the history of habitat modification that has occurred within the Vakhsh River valley, in
         particular the loss of woodland but also the associated slope stability that has followed, with many
         areas displaying evidence of mobile substrate that would not offer roost potential. However, roost
         sites may be present, potentially including hibernation and maternity roosts within discrete features
         such as rock/cliff crevices, localised caves and in disused buildings; and as such a slightly more
         precautionary approach is adopted here with regards species presence.

9.4.48   *Habitats Directive Annex IV – protection against deliberate killing and disturbance and maintaining/restoring conservation status of these
         species.

9.4.49   The analysis of eDNA from water samples collected within the project area, identified 36 species of
         mammals, from 11 Families. Of the 36 species detected, 17 were identified down to Species level,
         12 to Genus level, 6 to Family level and 1 unidentified at an Order level.

         Figure 9-24- Species richness at each sample location - mammals




9.4.50   The species/animal groups with the greatest amount of genetic material (both in terms of frequency
         of occurrence, and sample volume) are generally those that will likely be fairly numerous and
         present in close to the proximity to the watercourses sampled. Animals such as mice, voles,
         mustelids (such as weasel and marten) and fox have been detected with the highest levels of
         genetic material, which although not a reliable indicator of abundance, does provide some
         confidence as to the level of activity from these species. Conversely, those species/animal groups
         with less frequent/smaller sample volumes likely reflect greater scarcity or low abundance. The latter




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                             SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                       Page 206 of 371
is likely true of otter, which have quite extensive home ranges (up to around 30km of river length7)
and so will have fewer animals shedding genetic material. In the case of otter, the relatively low (and
unconfirmed) nature of the eDNA results from only two sample locations, likely also reflects the
general scarcity of this species in the region.

9.4.51   Of the 36 species detected, only one (that was described to species level) was unexpected based
         upon desk study and field data recorded to date – Siberian ibex Capra sibirica. While present within
         the 2014 and 2023 ESIA desk study exercises, this species’ presence within the flooding zone and
         wider AoI is not considered likely. The species is known to inhabit mountainous areas across
         Central Asia and is generally found above the tree line at elevations of greater than 500m, most
         commonly above 2000m, and up to 5500m. Three potential eDNA occurrences for this species were
         recorded, with two of these from the upper areas of the study area: Rogun Garm 1 and Rogun
         Kamarob 2; and one from near to the dam: Rogun Res 2. Whilst it is feasible that genetic material
         could have been transferred from higher altitudes to the sample sites in the upper reaches of the
         study area, the location of a potential Siberian ibex DNA close to the Rogun dam (together with the
         lower support level for each of these samples) would suggest that domesticated goat is the most
         likely source of this DNA (ibex are genetically close to domesticated goats, with some species able
         to hybridize).
9.4.52   Of the other mammal species detected, there was confirmation of the presence of increased interest
         to the Project such as a brown bear (recorded from seven separate locations), with the greatest
         amount of genetic material recorded at the location where prints were identified during the sample
         collection. This incidental record of brown bear was made during the eDNA sampling exercise, along
         the Siyohdara River in the upper reaches of the study area, with fresh prints recorded alongside the
         watercourse at this location (Figure 9-25).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment             PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                            SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                      Page 209 of 371
         Figure 9-25 - Brown bear prints




9.4.53   Similarly, otter prints were identified close to the confluence of the Vakhsh and Obikhingou rivers,
         and while no confirmation of otter was obtained from the eDNA analysis, there were records of an
         unknown mustelid species, which could feasibly be otter (from three separate locations).

         Figure 9-26 - Otter prints




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 210 of 371
         Amphibians
9.4.54   Tajikistan has 14 native amphibian species, two of which are endemic to the region. The desktop
         study recorded identified only two amphibian species within the Vakhsh River valley: green toad
         Bufo viridis; and lake frog Rana ridibunda. Both species were also recorded during the 2023 field
         surveys and are common and widespread within the region (and not included on any conservation
         lists, e.g., IUCN Red List), in areas where standing water is present.
9.4.55   One amphibian from the Bufonidae Family – Xinjiang toad Bufotes pewzowi was recorded for the
         eDNA sampling, and this represents a new species to the known/predicted amphibian assemblage
         present across the Project AoI. This species is listed as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List and is
         commonly found across Central Asia, east from Uzbekistan into Mongolia and China.
         Reptiles
9.4.56   A diverse reptile fauna is present across Tajikistan, with 46 species confirmed as being present, 22
         of which are endemic to the region. A total of 13 species were recorded from the literature review as
         being present in the Vakhsh River Valley, which is one more than was presented within the 2014
         ESIA. Most of the habitat present across the flooding zone is sub-optimal for reptiles given the very
         short sward length of the degraded grassland habitat and the general lack of shelter elsewhere.
         However, pockets of suitable habitat do exist, especially around the settlements and in the less
         accessible valley/gorge areas. Nine species were recorded during field surveys in 2023, and
         additional habitat suitability identified for four species. The vast majority of these are considered
         common and widespread, and not of any elevated conservation interest/status (e.g., included on
         IUCN Red List or TRB). Four exceptions to this are detailed in Table 9-7:

         Table 9-7 - Notable reptile records

          Species name           Habitat and distribution                              Conservation status

                                 ephemeral vegetation, gardens and along field

                                 the AoI.

          Orange-tailed skink    Two records were made during the 2023 field           TRB EN

         Birds
9.4.57   Tajikistan supports a diverse bird assemblage, with over 380 species having been recorded, four of
         which are regional endemic species. The country is within the broad Central Asian Flyway and
         supports several migratory species. The greatest species diversity is from the west of the country,
         where habitat diversity is relatively higher (including the Vakhsh valley) and reflecting the generally
         lower altitudes. Despite this diversity, only 14 species are known to occur that are listed as
         Threatened on the IUCN Red List, and there are no country endemic species (Birdlife, 2023).
9.4.58   A total of 158 species were recorded from the literature review as being present in the Vakhsh River
         Valley, which is seven more than were presented within the 2014 ESIA. Of these, 47 species were
         recorded during field surveys.
9.4.59   Most species recorded are considered to be common and widespread, which reflects the prevalence
         of habitat modification that has occurred; although 18 species of the 158 recorded are listed on the


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 212 of 371
TRB and/or the IUCN Red List. No regional endemics were recorded. Additionally, a number of
species recorded will be present as migrating birds; both those migrating to the region to breed or
overwinter (such as Egyptian vulture), and those that migrate through the area during spring/autumn
within the broad movement through the Central Asian Flyway. Table 9-8 provides further information
for these species.

                                        optimal (scrub vegetation is only sparsely present) it
                                        is expected that the species will be present within
                                        the AoI, albeit at low numbers

9.4.60   It is worth noting that none of species listed above have any significant affinity with the AoI (that
         would suggest the AoI offered any notable importance to the species beyond surrounding areas);
         their presence/likely presence will be as part of a large home range, or because of migratory
         behavior which is dictated more by prevailing topography. The legacy of habitat modification across
         the AoI will have played an important role in reducing the relative importance of the AoI for the
         notable bird species listed above.
9.4.61   The analysis of eDNA from water samples collected within the project area, identified 31 species of
         birds, from 16 Families. Of the 31 species detected, 13 were identified down to Species level, 10 to
         Genus level, 4 to Family level and 4 unidentified at an Order level.
9.4.62   Of the 31 species detected (to species level), none were unexpected based upon desk study and
         field survey data collected for the Project to date. Indeed, many of the species identified were
         recorded visually (either anecdotally, or through dedicated bird surveys) for the Project, which likely
         reflects their relatively common nature.
9.4.63   Table 9-9 provides details on the species identified.
9.4.64   As with the eDNA results for mammals, the nature/extent of bird species/groups detected is also
         likely a useful indicator of the relative frequency/abundance of the species, along with clear increase
         in records/volume of genetic material for birds associated with the aquatic environment.
9.4.65   The most commonly occurring bird within the samples was a duck, which was expected and was
         recorded from eight separate sampling locations. The next most common was a species of
         dove/pigeon, which again is logical due to the species’ common and abundant occurrence across
         the study area. Other commonly recorded species such as flycatchers is likely representative of the
         birds’ attraction to the river as a result of their prey also being abundant here.
