Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                             SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page 218 of 371
         Fish
9.4.66   Although approximately 36 fish species are known to be present within the Vakhsh River, many of
         them are now only found downstream of Nurek or the Vakhsh HPP Cascade, with the upper reaches
         of the Vakhsh River known to have much lower species composition than the lower parts of the river
         (Mirzoev, 2019). Sampling in the river upstream of Rogun in 2011 and 2023, determined that 21 fish
         species are likely to occur within the Rogun Project AoI. Of these 21 species, 10 have been
         sampled, with 3 being recorded in 2011 (ESIA, 2014), and 9 being recorded during the two 2023
         field surveys, and 17 detected through the application of eDNA (Table 9-10).
         Of the 21 fish species expected to occur in the Project AoI:
          Twelve (12) have been introduced into the Project AoI.
          One (1) is listed as Endangered in the Red Book of the Republic of Tajikistan.
          Two (2) are listed as Vulnerable in the Red Book of the Republic of Tajikistan, and both Salmo
           trutta oxianus and Glyptosternon oschanini (previously referred to as G. reticulatum) have been
           confirmed in the study area.
          One (1) is Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List, although from the Aral Sea basin, Cyprinus carpio
           inhabits slow-flowing and still waters, such as lowland rivers and large, well vegetated lakes. This
           species has been introduced in all types of waterbodies.
          One (1) is Near Threatened on the IUCN Red List, although Hypophthalmichthys molitrix has
           been introduced into Tajikistan. This status is based on the decline of natural populations from its
           native range in China.
          Twelve (12) are currently listed as Least Concern (LC) on the IUCN Red List. Species in this
           category are considered to be widespread and abundant.
          Two (2) are currently listed as Data Deficient (DD) on the IUCN Red List, while five (5) have not
           yet been assessed.
          None (0) are listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red list.
           • The Small Amu-Darya shovelnose sturgeon Pseudoscaphirhynchus hermanni and Amu Darya
             shovelnose Sturgeon Pseudoscaphirhynchus kaufmanni, listed as CR are not known to occur
             within the Vakhsh River upstream of the cascade of impoundments and were not detected
             during eDNA analysis (Volume 1: Chapter 15.1 of this ESIA).
          No (0) migratory species as listed under CMS are expected to occur within the Project AoI.
           • As above, both the Amu-Darya shovelnose sturgeon and Amu Darya shovelnose Sturgeon are
             listed in Appendix II and are potamodromous.
          None (0) are listed under CITES.
           • As above, both the Amu-Darya shovelnose sturgeon and Amu Darya shovelnose Sturgeon are
             listed under Appendix II: International trade monitored.
9.4.67   For the purpose of this ESIA, the vulnerable status of the Turkestan Catfish Glyptosternon oschanini
         as presented in the TRB under G. reticulatum will be carried through as a precautionary approach.
9.4.68   The analysis of eDNA from water samples collected within the project area, identified 22 species of
         fish, from 6 Families. Of these 22 species, five (5) species were excluded by the laboratory based


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 219 of 371
         on distribution and their unlikely occurrence in Tajikistan. These were interrogated based on in-
         country literature and are discussed below.
9.4.69   Of the 17 remaining species, 8 were identified down to Species level, 5 to Genus’s level, 3 to Family
         level and 1 unidentified at an Order level.
9.4.70   Considered analysis of the results, species identified in the field and distributions, provided enough
         information to assume the following:
          Species
            • Salmo trutta (Brown Trout), detected at 8 sites within the Rogun Reservoir. Known to occur in
              the Nurek Reservoir. Possibility of Amudarya trout.
            • Alburnus alburnus (Common Bleak), detected at one site at the Nurek Reservoir. Assumed to
              be introduced for aquaculture.
            • Paracobitis longicauda (Eastern Crested Loach), detected at 2 sites within the Rogun
              Resivour. Found in Nurek Reservoir.
            • Carassius auratus (Goldfish), detected at both site at the Nurek Reservoir. Assumed to be
              introduced for aquaculture.
            • Ctenopharyngodon Idella (Grass Carp), detected at both site at the Nurek Reservoir. Assumed
              to be introduced for aquaculture.
            • Channa argus (Northern Snakehead), detected at 1 site within the Rogun Reservoir. There is
              anecdotal evidence of the species being introduced within the Vakhsh drainage.
            • Hemiculter leucisculus (Sharpbelly), detected at both site at the Nurek Reservoir. Assumed to
              be introduced for aquaculture.
            • Pseudorasbora parva (Stone Moroko), detected at one site at the Nurek Reservoir. Known to
              occur in the Nurek Reservoir.
          Genus
            • Cyprinus could be Cyprinus carpio. Detected in both Nurek samples and known to have been
              stocked in the reservoir.
            • Triplophysa could be Triplophysa stolickai (Tibetan loach). Detected throughout the Rogun
              Reservoir Study Area at 10 sites and was previously believed to occur within the study area.
            • Glyptosternon could be Glyptosternon oschanini (Turkestan Catfish). Detected thought out the
              Rogun Reservoir study area at 12 sites and captured during the 2011 fieldwork as part of the
              2014 ESIA, and in 2023.
            • Hypophthalmichthys could be Hypophthalmichthys molitrix or Hypophthalmichthys nobilis, both
              reported on site in 2023. Detected at one site (Rogun Obin). Both species are known to have
              been stocked in Nurek.
            • Schistura from the Nemacheilidae Family could be Iskandaria pardalis (Turkestan loach).
              Detected at 4 sites and captured on site during the 2023 fieldwork. The Nemacheilidae (stone
              loaches), are a family of cypriniform fishes that inhabit stream environments of Eurasia.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 220 of 371
          Family
             • Cyprinidae recorded in high abundance10 at all sites is likely Schizothorax intermedius
               (Common Marinka), as this is widespread and common in the study area, detected at 13 sites
               in the Rogun Reservoir and within the Nurek Reservoir.
             • Salmonidae could be Salmo trutta oxianus (Amudarya trout). Detected at 2 sites and captured
               on site during the 2011 fieldwork as part of the 2014 ESIA.As mentioned above, the
               occurrence of Salmo trutta could represent the Amudarya trout as it is a subspecies, meaning
               that this Salmonidae detection could be Rainbow trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss), which also
               have the potential to occur within the study area.
9.4.71   The sequences for the following four (4) species were a 100% match to known refence material,
         however, were removed from the analysis based on distribution. Due to the high level of species
         introduction, these were checked, and the following was concluded:
          Esox Lucius (Northern Pike) - Rogun Kamarob 2
           • Esox lucius are native to North America and Eurasia. In Eurasia, it is known from the Caspian,
              Black, Baltic, White, Barents, Arctic, North and Aral Seas and Atlantic basins, southwest to
              Adour drainage. Widely distributed in central Asia and Siberia eastward to Anadyr drainage
              (Bering Sea basin). Although its distribution does not extend into Tajikistan, the National
              Strategy and Action Plan on Conservation and Sustainable Use of Biodiversity in Tajikistan
              (2003) does make mention of the species. Pike spawn in flooded marsh areas and requires
              submerged vegetation, it is unlikely to occur in the mountain headwaters of the Kamarob
              where it was detected.
          Couesius plumbeus (Lake Chub) - Rogun Res 3
           • Couesius plumbeus are native to North American. They can be found widely throughout
              Canada and in the north-temperate latitudes of the United States. There are no records in
              Tajikistan or Eurasia.
          Lepomis macrochirus (Bluegill) - Rogun Siyohdara 1
           • Lepomis macrochirus are native to North America and have been introduced widely in places
              such as Hawaii, Africa, Asia, South America, and Europe. There are no records in Tajikistan or
              surrounding countries. As this species prefers to live in lakes and slow-moving streams, it is
              unlikely to occur in the mountain headwaters of the Siyohdara where it was detected.
          Gasterosteus aculeatus (Three-spined stickleback) - Rogun Kamarob 1
           • Gasterosteus aculeatus are found across the northern hemisphere. In Europe it is found
              between 35 and 70°N. In Asia, the distribution stretches from Japan and the Korean peninsula
              to the Bering Straits. Freshwater populations are restricted to coastal areas in both Asia and
              North America. With no records in Tajikistan, the Three-spined stickleback is unlikely to occur
              within the study area.
          Clupea harengus (Atlantic Herring) – Rogun DS 1
9.4.72   Clupea harengus are native to the Atlantic Ocean and are found in the pelagic zone of marine
         waters, as well as coastal zones of throughout their geographic reach. With a single record in Asia
         (Uzbekistan in 1949), it is unlikely that Atlantic Herring will occur within the study area.


         10 Inferred by a stronger eDNA signal. This signal may be linked to abundance of species in the environment but should be interpreted

         only as a coarse measure because the signal is also impacted by biological (e.g., biomass, life stage, activity, body condition),
         environmental (e.g., temperature, pH, salinity, conductivity), and technical factors (e.g., primer bias, PCR stochasticity).

Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                                                             PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                                                            SEPTEMBER 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                      Page 228 of 371
         Figure 9-29: Amu Darya Trout from 2011 survey (ESIA, 2014)




           Salmo trutta oxianus, a sub-species of the Brown Trout that was captured in 2011 and has a
          designation of Vulnerable in the TRB. The specimens were captured in the Mudzhiharv Creek
          where the water was relatively clear (ESIA, 2014) and was detected during eDNA sampling.
          The Amu Darya Trout is endemic to the Amu Darya River. It is found in Qaratogh, Kofarnihon,
          Vakhsh, Surkhob, Kamarov, Obikhingov rivers and their tributaries, and in Nurek Reservoir
          (Red Book, 2017).

9.4.73   The most commonly reported species within the Project area was the Common Marinka (S.
         intermedius), which is targeted by local fisherman (Figure 9-30). Common Marinka is a widespread
         species that inhabits a wide range of rivers and also occasionally lakes, but it is usually found in
         flowing waters (Karimov and Mamilov, 2020). The species is variable and some of its subpopulation
         possibly should be recognized as separate species. For example, S. intermedius and S.
         fedtschenkoi both are considered as synonyms of S. curvifrons by Froese and Pauly (2013), but all
         three are recognized as valid species in the Catalog of Fishes (2023).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                             SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page 229 of 371
         Figure 9-30 - Common Marinka caught by local fishermen




9.4.74   Downstream, within the Nurek Reservoir, a total of 16 fish species were recorded between 1979 –
         1982 (ESIA, 2014). See paragraph Error! Reference source not found. for further information.
9.4.75   It should be noted that there have been nomenclature updates to two further species of fish, these
         include:
            The Turkestan loach Nemacheilus pardalis which has a valid status of Iskandaria pardalis
             (Turdakov 1941).
            The Samarkand khramulya Capoeta сapoeta heratensis which has a valid status of Capoeta
             heratensis (Keyserling 1861).
         Freshwater Diversity (Excluding Fish)
9.4.76   The 2011 aquatic studies sampled benthic organisms as they are an important food source for many
         fish. Data included 2 species of Acaridae (mites), 2 species of Oligochaeta (worms), 2 species of
         Trichoptera (caddisflies), 6 species of Ephemeroptera (mayflies), 12 species of Diptera (flies) and 1
         species of Mollusca (snails) (ESIA, 2014).
9.4.77   During the October 2023 site visit, an exploratory investigation for aquatic macroinvertebrates was
         conducted. The clear water of the tributaries showed to have the highest diversity of taxa, and
         included sensitive species such as Blephariceridae, Heptageniidae, Tricorythidae and
         Hydropsychidae. Within the turbid waters, the rocks were often covered in silt, with more tolerant
         taxa such as Simuliidae present.
9.4.78   The analysis of eDNA from water samples collected within the project area, identified 483 species of
         freshwater Animalia, comprised Arachnida, Clitellata, Gastropoda, Hexanauplia, Insecta and




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                             SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page 230 of 371
         Ostracoda. Of these 483 species, 16 were identified down to Species level, 81 to Genus level, 295
         to Family level and 8 unidentified at an Order or Phylum level.
9.4.79   No species designated Vulnerable, Endangered or Critically Endangered were detected in the
         samples. No invasive species were detected in the samples.
9.4.80   Diptera (Flies) were dominant across all sites.
9.4.81   Hexanauplia (Copepoda and Thecostraca) and Lepidoptera (Butterflies) were only detected in the
         two Nurek samples. Overall, Nurek showed significantly lower species richness than any of the other
         sites.

         Figure 9-31 - Species Richness (non-fish) as detected through eDNA analysis




9.5      FUTURE BASELINE
9.5.1    The future baseline under a ‘do nothing’ scenario would most likely reflect conditions currently
         presented across habitats upstream of the impoundment. There is no reason to believe that the
         dominance of heavily modified habitats would not persist, with land use likely to remain at current
         levels into the foreseeable future.
9.5.2    The only slight exception to the above would be for woodland habitat, which is in decline across all
         of Tajikistan (USAID, 2012) and is significantly reduced within the Vakhsh valley. It is therefore
         reasonable to assume that the remnant woodland identified during surveys would decrease in extent
         further, as human pressures continue in these areas (e.g., low levels of firewood collection, grazing
         pressures, etc.). The same logic for this consideration applies to notable flora, especially those
         currently collected for firewood, foodplants, pharmaceutical use, etc.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 231 of 371
10       ECOSYSTEM SERVICES

10.1     SOURCES OF BASELINE DATA
10.1.1   The following sections describe the information relevant to the baseline for ecosystem services in
         respect of the Project. The baseline data collected to inform this assessment has comprised a
         review of:
            Previous ESIA documentation;
            Information from other technical workstreams, specifically the Biodiversity and Social Technical
             Annexes (including the primary data they have collected such as field surveys and stakeholder
             engagement – no additional primary data collection was held for the completion of this technical
             annex). Further details regarding the baseline data collection exercise are provided within the
             Biodiversity and Social Technical Annexes.
            Academic literature and other relevant documentation available online.
10.1.2   A summary of baseline conditions for ecosystem services are presented in Volume I, Chapter 7.
         Please note the following limitations of the Ecosystem Services assessment:
            This is a high-level assessment that complements the Biodiversity and Social assessment. Its
             aim is to provide a general overview of ecosystem services that are likely to be impacted by the
             project and demonstrate the link between biodiversity and social impacts.
            This assessment was applied retrospectively. The analysis is based on pre-existing data;
             primary data was not collected for the sole purposes of the ecosystem services assessment.
            In light of these characteristics:
             • The assessment comprehensively investigates a large breadth of provisioning, regulating,
               cultural and supporting ecosystem services, and extracts those that are key to consider for the
               Project.
             • However, the assessment is qualitative. Furthermore, to manage uncertainty – which is a
               known limitation of a high-level and retrospective assessment – the precautionary approach
               has been applied to the extent that it was deemed appropriated based on professional opinion
               and experience. The precautionary approach is recommended by ESS6 and follows the
               principle of, where there is a lack of information, a cautious scenario is applied.

10.3     GENDER CONSIDERATIONS
10.3.1   A key aspect of the assessment is to identify those services which are used particularly by females
         and vulnerable groups. Services identified for the Rogun HPP Project as being of particular
         importance to females are:
               Access to water: this is a significant consideration for women. In consultation, many
                stakeholders cite the limited access to water at their current locations as a big complaint. As
                the majority of home-based responsibilities are placed on women and they are often the
                main users of utilities at home, issues with access to water can disproportionately impact
                women.
               Agriculture: Many women are engaged in agriculture and tending to orchards on their
                home-based plots. Additionally, a significant proportion of (mainly) men are working
                overseas due to labor migration and sending remittances home, which leaves women all the
                more responsible for agriculture and tending to their home plots. Furthermore, women can
                rely on informal income generation activities such as the sale of agricultural products, a
                household survey that was undertaken shows over 4.5 percent of women highlighting arable
                agriculture as their primary source of income and a total of nearly 6.5 percent when including
                cattle breeding and subsistence farming.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 237 of 371
11       SOCIAL BASELINE
11.1.1   This section sets out the baseline for the social aspects of the Project, including socio-economics,
         labor and working conditions, and community health, safety and security (CHSS).

11.2     DATA COLLECTION
11.2.1   Desktop research, secondary data resources and primary data collection tools were used for the
         social baseline and impact assessments. Secondary sources covered during desktop review
         included:
            Published literature on socioeconomic research conducted in Tajikistan;
            Government data resources (PMG, DFZ, local authorities, Tajikistan National Statistics-TajStat);
            International data resources such as the United Nations (UN) and World Bank;
            Satellite imagery, maps;
            Previous environmental and social studies for Rogun HPP;
            Reports published by other donor and international organizations; and
            Online media, newspaper, articles.
11.2.2   Primary data was collected through quantitative and qualitative research methods including:
            Labor audit interviews, conducted by WSP in March 2023;
            Key informant interviews with key stakeholders and service providers for the gender
             assessment, conducted by a WSP local gender specialist, July 2023;
            Focus Group Discussions (FGDs), conducted by M-Vector Tajikistan in July and August 2023 –
             36 in total (18 women FGDs in resettled, to-be resettled, host communities and 18 men FGDs in
             resettled, to-be resettled and host communities) covering around 285 participants;
            Key informant interviews with Jamoat leaders, conducted by M-Vector in July 2023 – 12
             Jamoats;
            Household surveys, conducted by M-Vector Tajikistan in August 2023 – 900 households; and
            Site visit observations.
11.2.3   Primary data has been used for the resettlement-related studies and labor, gender and stakeholder
         engagement plans that have been developed by WSP, as well as feeding into the social baseline
         presented here. All data was collected from respondents aged 18 years and above. Additionally,
         face-to-face interviews and meetings were conducted with various project stakeholders on
         resettlement, livelihoods, gender, labor and stakeholder engagement between December 2022 and
         September 2024:
            PMG;
            Rogun JSC;
            Contractors;
            DFZ;



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 238 of 371
            Ministry of Labor, Migration and Employment of Population (MLMEP);
            Committee for Women and Family Affairs (CoWFA);
            Department of Women and Family Affairs at Districts;
            State Investment Committee (SIC);
            State Unitary Enterprise for the Registry of Real Estate (SUERRE);
            Bureau on Technical Inventory (BTI);
            State Committee on Land Management and Geodesy;
            UN organizations (UN Women, UN Population Fund (UNFPA), International Labor Organization
             (ILO));
            Non-governmental organizations (NGOs) supporting women and livelihood generation in
             Tajikistan;
            Local jamoats, district governors and other local government representatives; and
            Community members.
11.2.4   The main limitations faced during primary data collection were related to lack of centralized data
         systems and reliance on manual data collection, which led to data inconsistency and errors.
11.2.5   Baseline information is provided at a national level for context, with more localized information
         provided where available. Project impacts are considered up until 2032 when all resettlement activity
         is anticipated to be complete. Full reservoir inundation level (i.e.1290m) is anticipated to be
         achieved in 2038. It is noted that many households continue to use their previous lands until
         inundation occurs, even once resettlement in terms of their lands, assets and house construction are
         complete.

11.3.7   The population of young (15-24) people in the household survey sample reported to be not in
         employment, education or training (NEET) is high, at 41.3% (see Table 11-6). A higher proportion of
         women (57.8%) than men (27.5%) are NEET. This implies a potential social exclusion risk if the
         Project does not address the particular challenges for young people and especially young women in
         the job market, particularly in terms of livelihood restoration for resettled communities.

         NATIONALITY/ETHNICITY
11.3.8   The household survey questionnaire included nationality/ethnicity in the household roster. Due to
         cultural and political sensitivities, it was not possible to ask about languages spoken at home. Out of
         the 5,148 family members, 5,129 (99.6%) were recorded as Tajik, nine members as Uzbek, seven
         as Russian, two as Turkmen, and one as Ukrainian (see Figure 11-1).

         RELIGION
11.3.9   Islam is the primary religion in Tajikistan, with approximately 98% of the population identifying as
         Muslim (95% Sunni and 3% Shia).

11.4     SOCIO-ECONOMICS: LOCAL ECONOMY AND EMPLOYMENT
11.4.1   A selection of economic indicators for Tajikistan is presented in Table 11-7. The most current data is
         presented and compared where possible to the statistics presented in the 2014 ESIA (dated 2010).

11.4.2   In 2023 it was estimated that 21.2% of the Tajikistan population was living below the national
         poverty line. This demonstrates a large improvement from the 2010 national poverty rate of 47.2%.
         Remittances, provided to households from members who are working as a migrant abroad, have
         played an important role in reducing poverty and providing an income to an estimated 30-50%
         Tajikistan households, comprising almost 40% of GDP in 2023 (World Bank, 2023). Incomes,
         particularly in rural areas such as the Rasht Valley region, tend to depend on unstable factors such
         as agriculture, livestock and remittances. Emigration out of Tajikistan is high, particularly amongst
         younger Tajiks, due to the limited availability of jobs and the higher incomes available abroad.
11.4.3   Agriculture makes up 27.8% of the country’s GDP (2021 estimate (Statista, 2024)) and provides
         44% of employment (2022 estimate (World Bank Group, 2024)), with the most important agricultural
         products from an economic perspective being cotton and wheat (Food and Agriculture Organization
         (FAO), n.d.). Extensive cultivation of fruit, nuts and vegetables, both for sale and personal
         consumption, also occurs (FAO, 2022).

         Figure 11-2 - GDP share of different sectors in the economy of Tajikistan (Q4 2021) (Statista,
         2024)

11.4.4   Tajikistan is included in the UNDP’s global Multi-dimensional Poverty Index (MPI) measurement,
         which is done by measuring overlapping deprivation across ten indicators over three dimensions:
         health, education and standard of living. The most recent MPI for Tajikistan estimates that 7.4% of
         the population is multidimensionally poor, while an additional 20.1% is vulnerable to multi-
         dimensional poverty (UNDP, 2023). Full details of the ten MPI indicators for Tajikistan are presented
         in Table 11-8 (Oxford Poverty and Human Development Initiative (OPHI), 2023).

11.4.5   According to the household survey data shown in Table 11-19, the main source of income is based
         on the remittances from labor migration (33.1%), followed by pensions (19.6%), public sector work
         (11.9%), agriculture (6.9%), self-employment (6.1%) and seasonal work (5.3%). Employment on the
         Project (either in Rogun HPP or construction sites) is only stated as being the main source of
         income by 5% of households. While the majority of the women headed-households are dependent
         on pensions, the main income source for male headed-households are remittances from migrant
         labor work.

11.4.6   Only 14 % of the women in the sample population reported having a source of income. Among those
         who have an income, 50% receive a pension, most likely from their husbands. Around 17% (339 out
         of 2478 women) of the women in the sample are engaged in public sector work (mainly as teachers,
         healthcare workers, and civil servants) earning regular income. Household farming is often
         undertaken in the affected communities for subsistence, however only 7% of women are recorded
         as farmers. This could imply that women are undertaking unpaid agricultural work within their
         households.
11.4.7   Interviews with the leaders of the affected Jamoats provided further detail of the existing income
         sources in the affected populations, as well as the planned economic activities following
         resettlement, detailed in Table 11-10.

11.4.8   Tajikistan is in the process of developing its nascent tourism industry. The Committee for Tourism
         Development was set up in 2017 and, in 2018, the Development Strategy for Tourism in Tajikistan
         for the Period to 2030 was published. Data provided by CEP states that Tajikistan welcomed more
         than 400,000 visitors in 2017, compared to just over 86,000 in 2016, and that there were ambitions
         to increase this figure to 1 million people per year by 2020. This goal was achieved ahead of
         schedule in 2018. However, out of approximately 1.035 million visitor arrivals, just 74,000 stated
         their purpose as ‘holidays, leisure, and recreation,’ while the vast majority (956,000) stated ‘other
         personal purposes,’ such as visiting friends and family (UN Tourism, 2022), which suggests that the
         majority of these visitors are not engaged in tourism per se.
11.4.9   A World Bank Group study of tour operators in 2019 found that the package-tour market in
         Tajikistan, which is the most significant tourist activity in Tajikistan in terms of revenue generation
         (Statista, 2024), was largely driven by adventure tourists from high-income countries in Europe,
         North America and Australia, and to a lesser extent China, who were mostly interested in nature and
         cultural activities. 80% of the surveyed tour operators reported increased interest in Tajikistan in
         recent years, and 89% stated that they believed Tajikistan had not reached its full potential
         regarding tourism (World Bank Group).

11.5     LAND ACQUISITION AND RESETTLEMENT
         OVERVIEW OF RESETTLEMENT TO DATE
11.5.1   The Project will result in the involuntary resettlement of over 50,000 people from 69 settlements
         situated within the future footprint of the Project in Rogun City and Nurobod and Rasht Districts.
         Details about the resettlement are included in the Resettlement and Livelihood Restoration
         Framework (RLRF), which was disclosed in June 2024 (https://energyprojects.tj/index.php/en/rogun-
         hpp/eko-sots-instrument/1251-rlrf). RAP 2 also includes details on the second phase of
         resettlement. It is available at energyprojects.tj.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 249 of 371
11.5.2   The reservoir will be filled over a period of several years, up until approximately 2038, with
         resettlement of the affected villages phased over this period. Physical resettlement is due to be
         completed by 2032.
11.5.3   Resettlement of the affected villages began in the early 1980s as construction of the Project
         commenced. In the early 1990s construction was paused after the breakup of the Soviet Union led
         to the independence of Tajikistan. Many of those who had been resettled returned to their previous
         residences during this period. In 2008, construction and resettlement activities resumed. DFZ was
         set up to manage resettlement and other activities in 2011.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 250 of 371
11.5.4   A summary of documents prepared to guide the implementation of resettlement activities is provided
         in Table 11-11.

         Table 11-11 - Resettlement Documents

          Resettlement and         2024       Updates the 2014 Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) and
          Livelihood Restoration              Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) and provides guidance for the
          Framework (RLRF)                    preparation of RAP 2 and Livelihood Restoration Plan 2 (LRP 2) for
                                              the years 2017-2026 of the second phase of the resettlement and
                                              livelihood restoration, as well as describing when future RAPs and
                                              LRPs will be prepared for the remaining stages of the reservoir filling.
                                              The aims of the RLRF are to:
                                                    Assess the ongoing resettlement process and provide
                                                       recommendations for bringing RAP 2/LRP 2 in line with the
                                                       lenders’ requirements;
                                                    Set out a strategic plan to effectively manage involuntary
                                                       resettlement, ensuring living conditions for Project-affected
                                                       communities (PACs) are equivalent to, if not better than,
                                                       those which existed before the Project;
                                                    Co-ordinate resettlement activities, including decision-making
                                                       and providing sufficient resources; and
                                                    Establish livelihood restoration principles.
                                              The objectives of the RLRF are therefore to:
                                                    Detail resettlement objectives and principles, institutional
                                                       arrangements, indicative number of affected people,
                                                       summary of entitlements, indicative budget and timetables,
                                                       and livelihood restoration modalities for upcoming
                                                       resettlement activities for the period 2026 until the end of
                                                       2032, when resettlement will be completed before the
                                                       inundation. The RLRF will guide the preparation of future
                                                       RAPs and LRPs that will need to be prepared in
                                                       synchronization with the stages of reservoir inundation after
                                                       2025.
                                                    Summarize the Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) for
                                                       resolving potential conflicts involving displaced persons; and
                                                    Describe the arrangements for implementing and monitoring
                                                       the resettlement process, including internal and external
                                                       monitoring arrangements.

11.5.5   Resettlement has been managed by DFZ since 2011. RAP 1 was completed in 2018 to manage
         resettlement of the first 8 villages, and a completion audit in 2018 confirmed that living standards for
         resettled communities had improved significantly, though livelihood restoration took some time. The
         processes set up during the RAP 1 period guided the initial RAP 2 program, which is now being
         updated as part of this TA package.
         PHASE 1 RESETTLEMENT
11.5.6   Resettlement comprising eight villages in Rogun City and Nurobod District, 326 households, and
         2,697 PAPs, was guided by RAP 1 developed in 2014, with an associated Livelihood Restoration
         Plan 1 (LRP 1) created in 2015. These villages were either situated within the construction footprint
         or were due to be flooded by early reservoir filling. According to audits on Phase 1 resettlement
         (Household-level Resettlement Audit (Intermediate) (2014), Dr. Eva Rossi and Lead Technical
         Consultant ; and Resettlement Audit of the Flooding Zone of Rogun Hydro Power Plant (2016),
         Baker Tilly), the resettlement program met the principles established in 2014 RPF, 2014 RAP 1, and
         LRP 1 and hence, was in line with the WB’s previous requirements (WB OP 4.12).15 However, the
         audits noted the time it took to restore livelihoods.
         PHASE 2 RESETTLEMENT
11.5.7   In the 2014 E&S documentation for the Project, this phase referred to all resettlement activities after
         2018 until 2032, covering the reservoir and the remaining area affected by dam construction.
         Phase 2 is currently divided into 2017-2026 (the RAP 2 period), and from 2026 until the end of 2032
         (future RAPs).

11.6     LABOR AND WORKING CONDITIONS
         WORKFORCE DATA
11.6.1   Indicators related to the labor force in Tajikistan are presented in Table 11-12 and include the most
         current data available, compared to the indicators presented in the 2014 ESIA where possible
         (dated 2010).

         Table 11-12 - Labor Indicators

          Labor Indicator                                                              2010           2023

          Unemployment (% of total labor force)                                        10.9           7




         15 These have since been updated with new requirements under WB’s ESF, specifically ESS 5 (refer to Volume 1,

         Chapter 2: Administrative Framework).


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                           SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                     Page 252 of 371
          Labor force participation rate (%, female) (age 15 and older)       29.2         32

          Labor force participation rate (%, male) (age 15 and older)         54.8         51

11.6.2   According to the local authority data, in Nurobod, the district with largest displaced population
         (23,284 affected persons), the unemployment rate was recorded as 7.5% in 2020, similar to the
         national average.
11.6.3   The 2016 Labor Code of Tajikistan is the key legislation which governs employment relationships in
         Tajikistan and is the main guiding legislation for all contractors working on the Project. There were
         no Project-wide labor management plans or policies in place prior to the development of the 2024
         Labor Management Plan (LMP) that formed part of this TA package, although some individual
         contractors had and continue to have their own Human Resources (HR) systems and policies.
11.6.4   In August 2023, a total of 14,746 people were identified as being engaged as workers on-site at the
         Project, which increased to 17,198 people by October 2024, and peak employment is likely to reach
         20,000 people by the late 2020s. In 2023, women constituted around 5.1% of the total workforce.
         18% of the total workforce is skilled workers (i.e., technical engineers), and the proportion of women
         in the skilled workforce is 5.8%. Current Project employees are primarily Tajik nationals (93.8%), of
         which 5.3% are women (as of August 2023).
11.6.5   In March 2023 it was understood that approximately 23% of the workforce was sourced from within
         50 km of the Project. Employment fairs are regularly held in the local area to attract local workers,
         and a Job Centre in Rogun has been opened as part of an ADB-financed Skills and Employability
         Enhancement Project to improve the vocational, technical and soft skills and employability of local
         youth (15-29 years old), women and migrant labor.

         Table 11-13 - Project workforce statistics (August 2023)

          Total workforce (no. workers)       14,746                          5.1%

          % skilled workers                   18%                             5.8%

          % Tajik nationals                   93.8%                           5.3%

          % locals (within 50km)              23%                             [no data]



         WORKING CONDITIONS IN TAJIKISTAN
         Occupational health and safety (OHS)
11.6.6   Occupational health and safety (OHS) in Tajikistan is governed by the Labor Code, which includes
         requirements for employers to:
            Be responsible for ensuring safe working conditions and safety of work at every workplace
             (Article 331) and for informing workers of workplace conditions and the results of labor
             inspections (Article 332);



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 253 of 371
            Apply the means to protect workers individually and collectively (including protective clothing and
             equipment) (Article 335);
            Provide appropriate work and rest regimes;
            Training workers in their jobs and safe methods of work (Article 350);
            Provide instructions on labor protection (Article 336);
            Test and verify the knowledge of workers in working safely;
            Provide certifications of workplaces at least every five years;
            Investigate accidents (Article 342);
            Provide sanitation and medical services;
            Provide access to premises by state officials; and
            Providing social insurance for accidents and diseases.
11.6.7   Legislation also sets limits for exposure to harmful substances and noise, as well as requirements
         for adequate lighting, ventilation, hygiene, and fire and equipment safety. Personal protective
         equipment (PPE) must be provided as needed at no cost to workers. Emergency response plans,
         including regular drills, are required to be prepared and implemented. First aid arrangements must
         be in place. Recording and reporting of accidents by employers is required (Rivermate, 2024).
11.6.8   Workplace incident statistics from the National Information Agency of Tajikistan ‘Khovar’ and media
         organizations are presented in Table 11-14. The significant increase in deaths in 2023 relative to
         2022 are attributed to a single accident at the Panjkent water treatment facility which killed 7 due to
         a gas leak (Ховар (Khovar), 2024). ILO-reported statistics from 2006 are also presented for
         comparative purposes. The number of injuries decreased dramatically between 2006 and 2023,
         although fatalities increased.

         Table 11-14 - Workplace incident statistics

                             2006 (ILO, 2008)                            2022 (Aslanova,      2023 (Ховар
                                                                         2024)                (Khovar), 2024)

          No. accidents       -                                          40                   52

          No. fatalities     23                                          25                   36

          No. injuries       105                                         46                   34

         Workers’ rights
11.6.9   Tajik legislation (Article 208 and 174 of the Labor Code) prohibits employment of children under 15
         years of age and bans workers under the age of 18 from participating in heavy, underground,
         harmful and hazardous works. The GoT conducted a survey of child labor in 2012 and found that
         23.5% of children under the age of 17 were employed. This resulted in the adoption of the decree
         “On the National Program to Eliminate the Worst Forms of Child Labor in the Republic of Tajikistan
         for 2015-2020.” However, no official updated statistics have been provided (Central Asian Bureau
         for Analytical Reporting (CABAR), 2021).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 254 of 371
11.6.10   Forced labor remains a significant issue in Tajikistan, despite official efforts to eliminate it. The most
          significant risks are for vulnerable Tajik citizens being trafficked internationally, particularly to or
          within Russian, or for the cotton harvest, as well as sex trafficking of women and children. Within
          Tajikistan, vulnerabilities to trafficking exist for Afghan refugees and internally displaced people
          (U.S. Department of State, 2024).
11.6.11   Tajikistan scored 82.5 out of 100 in the 2024 Labor Rights Index. The right to form unions and
          participate in collective bargaining is recognized in Tajikistan, although there are some restrictions
          on the right to strike (Wage Indicator & Centre for Labour Research, 2024). Unions are active
          among the Project workforce and the majority of workers are covered by collective bargaining
          agreements.

11.7      COMMUNITY ASSETS AND INFRASTRUCTURE
          HEALTHCARE FACILITIES
11.7.1    Tajikistan has a healthcare system which provides services to individuals who fulfil particular criteria.
          However, a significant number of people do not fulfil such criteria and are therefore not eligible for
          services. Health spending in Tajikistan is the lowest in the WHO European Region, despite
          increased spending over the past few years. The country also has the lowest ratio of healthcare
          workers to population in the WHO European Region, with significant inequalities in the distribution of
          such workers across the country (World Bank, 2021). However, a shift in focus in recent years to
          focus on primary healthcare has increased coverage and make steps towards universal healthcare
          (WHO, 2024).

          Table 11-15 - Healthcare indicators (WHO, 2024) (World Bank, 2021)

                                              2018                                2021

           Health expenditure (% GDP)         7.2                                 8.01



          UTILITIES
11.7.2    While the majority of the population in Tajikistan has access to electricity, many experience
          blackouts during winter due to the current power system being unable to meet demand. From 1990,
          production and consumption of electricity declined, due to an aging power infrastructure which was
          poorly maintained and not replaced.
11.7.3    Access to clean drinking water continues to be a problem in Tajikistan, particularly in rural areas.
          Amongst rural populations, 18% do not have access to even basic drinking water services. Access
          to clean drinking water across Tajikistan has improved in the last decade. The proportion of the
          population with access to improved water sources increased from 75% to 84% between 2012 and
          2020, although only 55% were using water supplies considered to be safely managed (World Bank
          Group, 2024). In the Project area, drinking water access is high but not universal. Data provided by
          I. Halimov, Qal’ai Surkh, Navobod town and Boqi Rahimzoda Jamoats have shown that at least
          some people within the jamoat do not have clean drinking water. Interviews with Jamoats and focus
          group discussions also indicated issues with water availability at resettlement sites, however it was
          also stated that many of these issues have been resolved and water availability has improved.

11.7.4   In Rogun City, water and power outages are relatively common. The Project and worker camps
         generate their own power from direct connection to the HPP and generators. Water is supplied to
         the Project site by tankering, and, in the case of one camp, a private borehole.
         INFRASTRUCTURE IN THE PROJECT AREA

         associated with education and healthcare, are presented in Figure 11-3, based on Jamoat
         interviews in July 2023. The availability of community facilities, such as schools, religious meeting
         places, and healthcare services vary greatly across the affected Jamoats. This data covers both pre-
         resettlement communities and resettlement sites.

11.7.6   Some specialized and regionally and nationally important infrastructure is present in the flooding
         zone. In Navobod, which will be totally resettled, there is an orphanage housing 110 children, which
         serves the whole of Rasht District, a college which is well respected for its teacher training courses.
         The Navobod psychiatric hospital, which has around 40 in-patients, accepts patients from
         throughout Tajikistan.

11.8     COMMUNITY HEALTH, SAFETY AND SECURITY
         POPULATION STRUCTURE
11.8.1   Tajikistan is a relatively young country, with nearly 50% of the population under 25 years old. The
         population currently totals 10,389,799, and is projected to increase to 15,574,642 by 2050, due to a
         combination of high birth rate and increased life expectancy (WHO, 2024). A breakdown of the
         population is shown in Figure 11-4.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 259 of 371
         Figure 11-4 - Tajikstan population pyramid 2023, and projected change 2050 (WHO, 2024)
11.8.2   The age pyramid for the sample population in the household survey implemented for this TA
         package (Figure 11-5), also has 1-17 years as the highest proportion.

11.8.3   Tajikistan has a relatively high fertility rate above 3 live births per woman, and annual population
         growth of 2% per year (WHO, 2024). This is significantly lower than during the Soviet era, when
         Tajikistan had the highest birth rate of all the Republics, peaking at 7 live births per woman in the
         1960s (World Bank Group, 2024) (Henry & Juraqulova, 2020). Family planning services are
         available but limited. In 2017, the proportion of women of reproductive age (15-49 years) who were



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 260 of 371
         married or in a union and were using, or whose partner was using, a method of contraception was
         29.3%. A 2020 study found that the total fertility rate exceeded the wanted fertility rate in all
         provinces of Tajikistan (Henry & Juraqulova, 2020).
         PUBLIC HEALTH
         General health of the population
11.8.4   Life expectancy in Tajikistan has increased significantly in recent years, from a low of just 53 years
         for the whole population and 46 for men during the civil war years of the 1990s, to over 70 years in
         the 2020s. This has been accompanied by an increase in healthy life expectancy, and a narrowing
         of the gap between males and females (Table 11-17). Public health has steadily improved, with
         decreasing infant and maternal mortality and under-nutrition, and increasing immunization. A
         selection of population health indicators is presented in Table 10-18. Where possible the most up to
         date indicator has been presented alongside the data provided in the 2014 ESIA (dated 2010).

11.8.5   The leading cause of death in Tajikistan is non-communicable disease, which led to approximately
         67% of deaths in 2019, the most recent year for which data is available (WHO, 2024). The most
         common of these are ischemic heart disease and stroke. Birth-related deaths and road accidents
         also led to large number of deaths. Violent deaths were rare, with injuries (including accidental
         injuries) leading to 0.1% of deaths.

         Figure 11-6 - Top causes of death in Tajikistan (2019)




         Communicable disease
11.8.6   According to WHO data, infectious disease was responsible for 578 deaths in 2017, the most recent
         year for which data is available. Of these, 278 deaths were from tuberculosis, and a further 149
         were from diarrheal diseases. HIV/AIDS was responsible for 7 deaths (WHO, 2023).



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment            PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                           SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page 262 of 371
          Figure 11-7 - Deaths from infectious and parasitic disease in Tajikistan in 2017 (WHO, 2023)




          Mental health
11.8.7    The Ministry of Health and Social Protection of Tajikistan stated that, in 2022, 44,725 people were
          known to be suffering from a mental illness, of whom 10,782 were children. Mental healthcare in
          Tajikistan is mostly focused on specialized institutions, both for in-patients and out-patients, however
          training in mental health by primary care providers is generally limited, inhibiting their ability to
          provide diagnoses and care. The National Healthcare Strategy 2021–2030 does not provide
          specialized measures or programs for mental health. Factors exacerbating poor mental health
          amongst the Tajik population included stressful conditions encountered during labor migration for
          men, and restrictive gender norms and high levels of family violence for women.
11.8.8    Mental healthcare workers and facilities are rather scarce. In 2020, there were just 454 registered
          mental health professionals in 2020, equivalent to 4.87 per 100,000 population. This comprises 187
          psychiatrists (including 6 pediatric psychiatrists), 261 mental health nurses, and 6 psychologists.
          There were a total of 26 mental health facilities: 13 out-patient units attached to hospitals, and 13
          inpatient facilities (11 psychiatric hospitals and 2 in-patient psychiatric units at general hospitals,
          including 1 pediatric facility). Mental health accounts of 0.7% of total government spending.
11.8.9    The suicide rate per 100,000 population was 5.32 in 2019, representing a slight increase since 2013
          (5.04). This is significantly below the global average of 9.2 per 100,000. There were 375 recorded
          cases of psychosis in 2020.
11.8.10   Alcoholism is rare in Tajikistan, which has a low rate of alcohol consumption. The average Tajik
          adult consumed just 0.7 liters of alcohol in 2020, significantly lower than its CIS neighbors
          Uzbekistan (2.1 liters) and Kyrgyzstan (3.6 liters), and far lower than other CIS countries such as
          Russia (10.5 liters) and Ukraine (9.2 liters). Tajik women, on average, consume even less alcohol
          than do men (0.2 liters compared to 1.3 liters) (World Bank Group, 2024). In 2016, the WHO
          recorded that 53.8% of Tajiks aged 15 or older were lifetime abstainers from alcohol – 39.1% of men



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 263 of 371
          and 68.6% of women – and that alcohol use disorders affected less than 1% of the adult population,
          compared to 8.8% across the WHO European region. Smoking rates are also relatively low and
          concentrated almost entirely within the male population – 17.0% of Tajik men were smokers in 2017,
          compared to 0.3% of women (WHO, Tajikistan).
11.8.11   Due to its border with Afghanistan, Tajikistan is a transit country for illicit drugs, particularly heroin
          and to a lesser extent cannabis. Heroin is one of the most widely used drugs in Tajikistan, although
          its use remains socially unacceptable and stigmatized (Global Organized Crime Index, 2023). It was
          estimated in 25,000 intravenous heroin users in Tajikistan in 2017 (Latypov, 2017). Drug use and
          addiction has become a steadily increasing public health concern, with treatment centers often
          poorly equipped and abstinence-focused (Peyrouse, 2018).

          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page 264 of 371
          SAFETY AND SECURITY
          Crime and terrorism
11.8.12   The rate of violent crime in Tajikistan is low. In 2020, the most recent year for which data was
          available, there was just 1 intentional homicide per 100,000 population, similar to its CIS Central
          Asian neighbors Uzbekistan (1) and Kyrgyzstan (2). In 2023, Tajikistan was rated one of the safest
          countries in the world by the Gallup Safety Index, which surveyed self-reported feelings of safety –
          92% of Tajik respondents stated that they feel safe walking alone at night where they live, which
          was equaled or exceeded by only Kuwait (99%), Singapore (94%), Saudi Arabia (92%) and Norway
          (92%).
11.8.13   The risk of terrorism in Tajikistan is largely associated with the Afghan border. Armed incursions by
          terrorist groups across the Afghan border are a regular but infrequent occurrence (Gov.uk, 2024).
          The main organizations of concern for the Tajik state are ISIS-K and Jamaat Ansarullah, which aim
          to overthrow the GoT and establish an Islamist regime, and stage attacks across the Tajikistan-
          Afghanistan border. The GoT actively participates in domestic and international counter-terrorism
          efforts and stated that 31 terrorist actions were prevented in 2022 (U.S. Department of State, 2023).
          Road safety
11.8.14   Traffic accidents have been decreasing in Tajikistan in recent years, from 1303 recorded incidents in
          2017 to 1096 incidents in 2022. Deaths due to traffic incidents also decreased nationally from 2017-
          2021, from 431 to 375 deaths, although an increase in mortality was recorded in 2022 (416 deaths).
11.8.15   Locally, in the Rasht zone, both accident rates and fatality rates have remained relatively steady
          over the same period. The fewest accidents occurred in 2021 (3) and the fewest fatalities in 2019
          (2). The most accidents occurred in 2022 (11) and the most deaths in 2020 (7). Although evidence
          lacks on the traffic accidents for the Project area specifically, both women and men FGDs highlight
          road safety concerns of communities and the poor road conditions due to ongoing construction and
          use of heavy trucks.

11.9     INCLUSION, VULNERABILITIES AND GENDER
11.9.1   In the last decade, Tajikistan has made significant progress in poverty reduction, and social
         protection and assistance systems are going through modernization to capture more vulnerable
         people in need. However, still the country faces challenges in ensuring the inclusion and protecting
         the rights of all members of society, whether along gender lines or issues faced by vulnerable
         populations.
11.9.2   ESS 1 of the WB ESF defines vulnerable people as ‘disadvantaged or vulnerable [individuals or
         groups], who may be more likely to be adversely affected by the project impacts and/or more limited
         than others in their ability to take advantage of a project’s benefits. Such an individual/group is also
         more likely to be excluded from/unable to participate fully in the mainstream consultation process
         and as such may require specific measures and/or assistance to do so. This will take into account
         considerations relating to age, including the elderly and minors, and including in circumstances
         where they may be separated from their family, the community or other individuals upon whom they
         depend.’




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                                                                                          PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                                                                                         SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                                                   Page 267 of 371
         VULNERABLE GROUPS IDENTIFIED FOR THE PROJECT
11.9.3   Certain individuals and groups, such as those based on age, gender, ethnicity, religion, disability,
         social exclusion, or economic disadvantage, may be more vulnerable to negative impacts from the
         Project, find it more difficult to enjoy Project benefits, or risk social exclusion. Vulnerable groups
         identified in the 2014 ESIA (Chapter 13.5.6) included:
            Disabled people, e.g. those with physical or mental disabilities or chronic illnesses, who will
             require physical assistance if the village is to be relocated.
            Female-headed households (as well as women acting as de facto HoH while the HoH is absent,
             e.g. for labor migration), who may require additional assistance in building their new houses.
            Elderly people with no younger family members within their household, who may also require
             additional assistance in building their new houses.
            Young people (aged 24 or under) who may require additional assistance in finding a job in their
             new location.
11.9.4   Based on the household level data, FGDs and secondary data sources, a vulnerability framework
         has been developed using different proxy indicators to identify project-specific vulnerable groups.
         Besides gathering average income data, household expenditure patterns and income sufficiency for
         food were also asked as a proxy indicator for assessing socio-economic strata.

Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                             PUBLIC |
