Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                      Page 272 of 371
          Orphans
11.9.6    The UN estimates that approximately 160 out of every 100,000 children in Tajikistan lose parental
          care each year, including those who are left with relatives while their parents undertake labor
          migration, those placed in institutions, and those whose parents are deceased. There are estimated
          to be 1500-3000 orphans due to AIDS aged 0 to 17 in Tajikistan. UNAID estimates that there are
          15,000 – 16,000 people living with HIV in Tajikistan, giving an incidence rate of 0.01 per 100 people
          (1 in 10,000) across all age groups (UNICEF, 2021).
11.9.7    Data on orphans were provided by two Jamoats, including N. Makhsum in Rasht, which will be
          affected by reservoir impoundment and is home to 110 orphans residing at an orphanage in
          Navobod. It was reported by DFZ that orphanages are provided in all resettlement sites.
          Gender
11.9.8    In Tajikistan, discrimination based on gender is illegal, and equal work for equal renumeration is
          mandated. However, family-related factors and social institutions mean that gender inequality
          remains high, particularly in the areas of tertiary education enrolment, labor pay, household
          decision-making and control over assets. In 2023, women held 27% of the seats available in
          parliament, compared to 19% in 2010 (World Bank Group, 2024).
11.9.9    Domestic violence is prevalent in Tajikistan, and while the 2013 Law on The Prevention of Violence
          in The Family offers victims some protection, it does not criminalize domestic violence or marital
          rape. It is estimated that around one-third of Tajik women are abused by their husbands, although
          reporting is low (United Nations, 2023).
11.9.10   The participation of women in the labor force in Tajikistan is low, with approximately 69% of working
          age females not in paid employment in 2016, and the proportion of females in the total number of
          employed declining. Tajikistan’s Labor Force Participation Rate gender gap of 21.5% is much higher
          than in comparable low-income countries (average 15.3%). Gender equality is affected by
          Tajikistan’s Labor Code, in which Article 216 prohibits women from working in certain occupations
          deemed to be dangerous, difficult or involving heavy and manual work (Sevimli & Rajabov, 2022).
11.9.11   Unemployment, particularly in rural areas, has led to a high proportion of Tajik migrant workers who
          seek employment and better opportunities elsewhere (particularly Russia). It is estimated that about
          30% of the wives left behind by their migrant husbands have been abandoned, adding the family’s
          financial burden on their shoulders along with traditional responsibilities of taking care of children
          and the elderly and dealing with domestic work (ADB, 2020).
11.9.12   Particular vulnerabilities exist for women with limited education. In some rural areas there are only
          primary schools and children must attend boarding schools for secondary education, returning to
          their parents at weekends. Many families do not want their daughters to leave the family for such a
          long period of time, so they do not continue their education and are left with only basic literacy skills.
          Therefore, women may be exposed to additional impacts, as without education their only livelihood
          options are to be housewives dependent financially on their husbands, to seek low-skilled work such
          as agricultural labor, or to be involved in informal businesses.
11.9.13   Only 14% of the women in the household survey sample stated that they had an income source.
          Household surveys showed that amongst women with an income, the most significant income
          source was from pensions (approximately 25%), and a significant proportion worked within the




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 273 of 371
          public sector (approximately 14%). Women in the area often supplement their income with informal
          sale of agricultural surplus.
11.9.14   According to Tajikistan law, each household must have one registered HoH. Women and men have
          equal legal rights to be HoH, although in practice this role is typically occupied by men. However,
          labor migration by Tajik men has led to an increase in the numbers of female-headed households.
          Nationally, approximately 23% of households in Tajikistan have a female HoH. These households
          tend to be smaller households and have a higher risk of poverty than those headed by males. Within
          the Project AoI, 18% of HoH of surveyed households were female. Female HoH are often divorced
          or widowed women, and sometimes face additional difficulties in the resettlement processes, for
          example lacking suitable labor for house construction within their households or struggling with
          administrative processes that may previously been managed by their husbands.
11.9.15   Primary data on women in Project-affected communities was gathered via the household surveys
          and FGDs with women and men in 2023. The key findings from the women-only FGDs were as
          follows:
           The current socio-economic conditions of women participating in the FGDs varied greatly
            depending on the village they come from and available infrastructure; in the most underserved
            villages, women had limited education, and the majority did not work. Women in larger and more
            developed settlements had greater access to facilities and employment opportunities.
           Women with access to kindergartens stated that these were essential for their ability to work.
           Few women are employed on the Project, with participants citing the distance to the project and
            the 15-day shift pattern as barriers, as well as husbands not wanting their wives to work in a
            male-dominated environment.
           Where women are heads of household (HoH), they are free to make decisions regarding
            resettlement. Household members who are not HoH have a limited formal role in the resettlement
            process, and their decision-making power depends on their level of influence with the HoH. Some
            women reported a high level of influence, others had little to no involvement in the process.
           Participants were generally positive about the opportunities that they perceive come with
            resettlement, particularly for employment, education, and improved community infrastructure.
            Some women who had not previously worked expressed a desire to do so after resettlement,
            provided there were culturally suitable jobs available (e.g., pharmacy, food processing shop).
11.9.16   Concerns were raised around the following:
           The compensation process, with some worried about timing and others worried about the
            valuation.
           Availability of water in their new residences.
           Assurance that their conditions (jobs, property and availability facilities) would be provided for.
           Both male and female FGD participants were unsure whether adult women currently living with
            their parents could receive a land plot. All participants who raised this point were supportive of
            this idea.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 274 of 371
           Participants also raised concerns on behalf of the vulnerable women in their communities, such
            as those with disabilities, divorced women and widows who would need additional support during
            the resettlement (e.g., moving and construction of new residences).
11.9.17   Women also highlighted how they would prefer to be resettled alongside their existing neighbors and
          community.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment            PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                           SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                     Page 275 of 371
12       CULTURAL HERITAGE

12.1     INTRODUCTION
12.1.1   This section presents the cultural heritage study compiled in relation to the Project. The general
         objective of the cultural heritage baseline study, as part of the overall ESIA process, is to collect
         robust baseline data to describe the cultural heritage conditions of the proposed Project area.

12.2     SOURCES OF BASELINE DATA
12.2.1   Baseline data has been collated from a combination of available secondary data sources and
         primarily data collection. A summary of the main sources informing the baseline is presented below:
            A. Donish Institute, 2013: Report on archaeological research in the flood zone of the Rogun
             hydroelectric power station: 1st Stage;
            Pӧyry Energy Ltd., 2014: Vol I Rogun HPP ESIA: Environmental and Social Assessment for the
             Rogun Hydro Power Plant;
            A. Donish Institute, 2015: Nurobod and Rogun: Ethnographical Studies;
            A. Donish Institute, 2017: Anthropological study of the population of the village of Sicharogh
             (Cemetery Relocation Report);
            Information available at the National Museum of Tajikistan (March 2023);
            Household survey data for villages to be resettled gathered by M-Vector in 2023;
            Mineral and fossil data: recorded on Mindat.org, an online database operated by the Hudson
             Institute of Mineralogy and obtained during recent geological reconnaissance undertaken in
             support of the Project (2022);
            Geographical Information Systems (GIS) data provided by University College London (UCL)
             Institute of Archaeology Central Asian Archaeological Landscapes Project (CAAL); and
            Preliminary archaeology and ethnographic surveys undertaken by the team at the A. Donish
             Institute of History, Archaeology and Ethnography (hereafter, the A. Donish Institute) in June,
             August and September 2023.
12.2.2   These sources are supplemented with information collated during WSP’s site visits in March and
         June 2023, during meetings with the team at the A. Donish Institute in Dushanbe (7th and 8th March
         and 9th June) and to the Rogun reservoir area undergoing active resettlement (10th March).
         Additional archaeological and cultural context has been provided through reviews of existing
         literature and academic studies (as listed in Section 12.5). Detailed information gathered from the
         field surveys undertaken on the site visit are available in Volume 3: Annex 20.6: Cultural Heritage
         Field Reports, of this ESIA.

12.3     BASELINE METHODOLOGY
         BASELINE DATA COLLECTION METHOD
12.3.1   The preparation of a comprehensive cultural heritage baseline was completed in three stages: a
         rapid desktop review of existing data, two phases of targeted archaeological survey and two phases
         of community consultation specific to ethnographic cultural heritage. The detailed methodology of


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 276 of 371
         these three stages is presented below. WSP’s program of ESIA update has been supported by
         experts at the A. Donish Institute in Dushanbe, who are also legally mandated (Law of the Republic
         of Tajikistan on the Protection and Use of Objects of Historical and Cultural Heritage, Chapter 3,
         Article 23, 2017) to carry out all cultural heritage survey within the proposed Project zone.
12.3.2   The baseline study area was defined as the maximum inundation extent (1,290 masl), and all
         villages in the second phase of resettlement phase (RAP 2), plus a 20 km buffer. The buffer has
         been incorporated to adequately consider known cultural heritage within the wider area, to better
         understand the context of resources potentially impacted by the Project in particular, their rarity,
         historical associations and significance.
12.3.3   The baseline study area for cultural heritage is shown in Drawing 20-1 (Annex 20.5).
         Rapid Desktop Review
12.3.4   An initial rapid desktop review was undertaken to inform the specific requirements (and locations) of
         the rapid, targeted field surveys to follow.
12.3.5   The desktop study included:
            A legislative review (law of the Republic of Tajikistan);
            Local data acquisition and a literature review (within the archives of the A. Donish Institute)
             including an examination of existing studies undertaken to date (e.g., archaeological field survey
             and ethnography surveys completed in support of the 2014 ESIA);
            Reviews of recent aerial imagery to inform the locations of the targeted field surveys to follow,
             supported by a parallel phase of GIS data acquisition through the Institute of Archaeology
             Central Asian Archaeological Landscapes (CAAL) Project database; and
            Reviews of the current RAP 2 schedule to identify settlements within the inundation that have not
             been targeted for ethnographic study and related community consultations prior to 2023.
         Targeted Field Surveys – Archaeology
12.3.6   The A. Donish Institute archaeology field team undertook a program of rapid, targeted
         reconnaissance in June 2023, and again in August and September 2023, to characterize the
         baseline environment in support of the 2023 ESIA update. The objective of these survey phases
         was to identify and record evidence of past human activity (e.g., archaeological remains) in selected
         “sample areas”, to inform any requirement for further archaeological work and additional studies in
         support of the Project. The walkover surveys, which involved visual (non-intrusive) inspection, were
         targeted to upper terrace levels not previously surveyed (for the 2014 ESIA). Limited archaeological
         evaluation (one small scale test pit) was undertaken where it was considered necessary to help
         characterize a site.
12.3.7   A ‘site record sheet’ was completed for each archaeological or historical feature identified during the
         surveys, which included GPS coordinates, a brief description and detail of the feature’s condition
         and level of disturbance. Each feature was photographed and associated features were recorded
         (e.g., the wider landscape setting and access paths).
12.3.8   For ease of mapping, each identified archaeological, historical and paleontological resource has
         been given an identification number prefixed “RHPP-AR”, e.g., RHPP-AR-01.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 277 of 371
          Targeted Field Surveys – Ethnography
12.3.9    The A. Donish Institute ethnography field team undertook a rapid, targeted consultation program
          informed by the preceding desktop studies in June 2023, and again in August and September 2023.
          The objective of these survey phases was to identify and record local cultural resources and
          intangible heritage practice, in participation with a select number of settlements/communities to be
          resettled under the current (RAP 2) resettlement schedule. Formal ethnographic consultations were
          conducted in the following villages:
             June survey: Khakimi bolo, Khakimi poyon, Obiboriki bolo, Obiboriki poyon, Khumrogh (formerly
              Kumok or Qumoq) and Lughuri poyon.
             August-September survey: Mehrobod (formerly Komsomolobod), Roghuni poyon, Aligalaboni
              bolo, Khumrogh, Chanor and Bediho.
12.3.10   These community consultation phases were guided by the requirements of ESS8 (2016) to
          sensitively record intangible practices (ceremonies, traditional crafts, music etc.,) and the specific
          geographical locations of cultural receptors (cemeteries, memorials and natural sites of spiritual
          significance, e.g., medicinal plants, sacred watercourses) identified by Project Affected Peoples
          (PAPs).
12.3.11   Focus group discussions were held to identify key informants in each targeted settlement. Key
          Informant Interviews (KII) were subsequently undertaken with community / cultural leaders. The
          communities were asked to provide details of their knowledge of sites of cultural importance (e.g.,
          religious places, cemeteries, burials, traditional practice areas, sacred watercourse or shrines) within
          their settlement (and near vicinity).
12.3.12   The survey team then sensitively recorded these resources with the assistance of the villagers/site
          guardians by completing a ‘site record sheet’ (including GPS coordinates, photographs and a
          description of the site) for each identified.
12.3.13   Where sites of cultural activity were identified, the field team sensitively recorded (with permission)
          any associated intangible cultural heritage practices, e.g., associated festivities, ceremonies, local
          taboos (e.g., sites which are restricted for the initiated only or exclusively men/women), which help
          to ascertain the relative significance of the site and any appropriate mitigation and management to
          follow. Where sensitive cultural sites and ‘secret’ areas were identified, one-to-one (private and
          confidential) interviews with a village elder or cultural leader were undertaken.
12.3.14   During their field surveys, the archaeology team also identified a small number of active cultural
          resources (e.g., cemeteries and shrines) in additional villages they passed through. These are
          incorporated where appropriate.
12.3.15   For ease of mapping, each identified cultural and sacred resource has been given an identification
          number prefixed “RHPP-CR”, e.g., RHPP-CR-01.

12.4      ASSUMPTIONS AND LIMITATIONS
12.4.1    The extent of the cultural heritage study area was defined in consideration of the Project footprint at
          the time the field surveys were completed (June, and August to September 2023). Any subsequent
          design changes and/or alterations may require new surveys to be conducted. Furthermore, these
          field surveys were rapidly targeted and subject to constraints imposed by restrictive ground visibility,




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 278 of 371
         health and safety and inaccessibility. These constraints are typical of baseline field data collection
         and as a whole did not compromise the objectives of this survey.
12.4.2   There was no intention to undertake any significant below-ground investigations during this phase of
         work but limited archaeological evaluation (one small-scale test pit) and manual clearance of
         vegetation was necessary to clarify the nature and extent of particular sites. Equally, there was no
         scope to remove surface objects en masse from identified sites, as this will be undertaken, if
         necessary, during further stages of work.
12.4.3   The collection of archaeological baseline information provided a valuable baseline study of the
         visible cultural heritage resources within the study area. However, it does not discount the possibility
         that other (potentially more important) remains may survive undiscovered in below-ground deposits,
         or in areas that are inaccessible to survey (e.g. where a feature cannot be located and positioned
         accurately).
12.4.4   Typical of cultural heritage consultations, the ethnographic information presented is limited to that
         which the communities consulted were willing to share with the survey team. There remains a
         potential for unidentified features of local cultural sensitivity to exist throughout the study area. Not
         all of the cultural heritage resources identified in consultation with local communities could be visited
         by the survey team due to the aforementioned constraints, and the exact locations of these
         resources are yet to be determined. Recommendations for ongoing and future consultation
         programmes are presented in Chapter 20 of the ESIA and the Cultural Heritage Management Plan
         (CHMP, Volume 3).
12.4.5   Due to seasonal constraints, the active nature of Project construction and health and safety
         considerations, the entirety of the cultural heritage AoI (defined in Chapter 20 of the ESIA) has not
         yet been fully inspected for cultural heritage sensitivities. Visual (non-intrusive) cultural heritage
         survey and sensitivity mapping of the following parts of the AoI is ongoing and described in further
         detail in the CHMP (Volume 3):
            •   along the left bank of the Vakhsh River from the village of Sangdevor to the dam itself;
            •   on the right bank of the Vakhsh River, along its tributary, the Lugur Stream;
            •   on the right bank of the Obikhingou River; and
            •   along the left bank of the Obikhingou River, in the upper part of the flood zone.
12.4.6   Only villages and settlements that are currently undergoing resettlement (i.e., under RAP 2) were
         visited by ethnography team during the June and August-September 2023 surveys. Those villages
         to be resettled under future RAPs were not visited.
12.4.7   Community consultation in all villages, in relation to the documentation of cultural and sacred
         resources and any related intangible heritage, is also ongoing, as described in the CHMP (Volume
         3).
12.4.8   Preconstruction survey will be required for any elements of the evolving Project design situated
         outside the current AoI, such as the proposed new resettlement sites, the left bank roads and
         bridges and overhead transmission lines. The cultural heritage Pre-construction Survey Process is
         described in Volume 3, Chapter 1 (and Volume 3: Annex A1: Environmental Social
         Management Plan of this ESIA).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 279 of 371
12.4.9   Potential impacts on cultural heritage receptors downstream of the study area in the event of dam
         failure, including those in the riparian countries of Afghanistan, Uzbekistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyz
         Republic and Turkmenistan, are considered to be beyond the scope of this assessment.

12.5     EXISTING CONDITIONS
12.5.1   This section summarizes the relevant desktop studies and fieldwork phases previously undertaken
         within the baseline study area.
         SUMMARY OF EXISTING STUDIES UNDERTAKEN FOR THE PROJECT:
         ARCHAEOLOGICAL
12.5.2   A summary of previous archaeological investigations completed in support of the Rogun HPP
         project is outlined to follow. This includes phases of field survey undertaken by the A. Donish
         Institute between 2013 and 2017.

         Table 12-1 - Summary of Previous Archaeological Surveys (prior to 2023)

          Study / survey    Timing   Purpose

          Archaeological    2013     Archaeological desk-based assessment, visual reconnaissance and targeted
          Survey                     intrusive evaluation in support of the baseline of the 2014 ESIA.

          Archaeological    2014     Reconnaissance mission informed by desktop study to identify archaeological
          Survey                     resources for the 2014 ESIA.

          Sicharogh         2016 –   Archaeological excavation of a cemetery dated to the 18th – 19th Centuries CE
          Cemetery          2017     near to the (resettled) village of Sicharogh. The cemetery was excavated as
          Excavation                 part of the planned relocation of burials in advance of village resettlement.

         2013 Archaeological Survey
12.5.3   In 2013, archaeological desk-based assessment, visual reconnaissance and targeted intrusive
         evaluation was undertaken by experts at the A. Donish Institute, in support of the baseline of the
         2014 ESIA. These were targeted to the “first stage of flooding” (below 1,110 masl). At this lower
         level, within the floodplain, the archaeological potential was concluded to be limited, with no
         evidence of ancient settlement or land use observed.
12.5.4   Just above this first inundation level, prehistoric burial mounds (approximately six, in a radial
         pattern) were identified in proximity to Sicharogh village, at a height of 1,100 masl, tentatively dated
         to the Kushan Period (1st Century BCE – 4th Century CE). Two mounds were excavated by the
         2013 field team and concluded to be ‘false burials’ (possibly to deter grave robbers) and the
         remainder were unevaluated due to time constraints. This area has since been cleared and levelled
         during Project preparation works. Burials (identified as Late Bronze Age) and ceramics were also
         recorded near the village of Yurst, with human remains visible on the exposed (and unstable) river
         terraces, at a height of approximately 1,400 masl.
12.5.5   The remains of two fortresses (over 1,100 masl) were also identified, noted to be in poor condition
         and damaged by construction (‘Jamkhur’ fortress at Kalai Dahoni Obigarm) and by the
         encroachment of modern settlement (unnamed fortress at Komsomolobod). Pottery, including
         painted sherds, and iron arrowheads were identified at these locations. Though limited further




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 280 of 371
          details were reported, a tentative date between the 12th and 18th centuries for the fortress at
          Komsomolobod was suggested.
12.5.6    The 2013 baseline study concluded that the upper terraces of the Vakhsh River (above 1,100 masl)
          have greater archaeological potential, observed during the field survey at locations near the villages
          of Pandovchii and Yurst. These observations were supported by studies undertaken by research
          teams (Mandlestam et al) in the late 1950s, recording approximately 60 archaeological sites in the
          vicinity of Rogun and the Vakhsh River. This research verified the proximity of the Karategin sub-
          section of the ancient Silk Road Route and associated small fortresses or outposts to deter raiders,
          and the locations of trading settlements (Tatyana Filimonova, Pers. Comm. 9th March 2023). The
          2013 study made a recommendation for ongoing archaeological reconnaissance, targeted to the
          river terraces between 1,100 – 1,300 masl which would be potentially impacted by the Project as the
          valley is inundated and the upper terraces as subject to under-cutting and landslip.
          2014 Environmental and Social Impact Assessment
12.5.7    The 2014 ESIA describes 14 archaeological resources identified during a reconnaissance mission
          informed by desktop study. These sites fall into three broad categories: Neolithic settlement (3rd –
          1st Millennium BCE); towers/fortresses, visible as earthworks (5th – 17th Century CE) and
          cemeteries (2nd Century BCE – 1st Century CE). They are dispersed throughout the valley, with the
          fortresses generally located in proximity to a confluence. All fourteen resources, with the exception
          of the ancient fortresses at Navdonak village, were found to be in poor condition / damaged by road
          construction or other development. An archaeological investigation was recommended at Navdonak
          prior to development. This site is located at the eastern extent of the reservoir, on the southern bank
          of the Vakhsh River. The recommended phase of evaluation, prior to inundation was confirmed as
          incomplete (Tatyana Filimonova, Pers. Comm. 9th March 2023).
12.5.8    The ESIA does not discuss the heightened archaeological potential of the upper terrace levels of the
          valley though the archaeological potential of the Project area in relation to its proximity to the Silk
          Road is also noted. The potential for (surviving) subsurface remains associated with partially
          damaged sites is not discussed and direct impacts are described in the ESIA to include submersion
          and damage during construction. Indirect impacts, to environmental setting / tourist access, or
          through (secondary) landslip, outside the direct inundation area are not evaluated, however, the
          Navdonak fortress is noted as vulnerable to shoreline erosion.
          2017 Sicharogh Cemetery Relocation Report
12.5.9    Between 2016 and 2017, the A. Donish Institute carried out an archaeological excavation of a
          cemetery near to the (resettled) village of Sicharogh. The cemetery was excavated as part of the
          planned relocation of burials in advance of proposed flooding. Some of the earliest burials
          excavated show a burial position characteristic of a transition period between the ancient Persian
          faith of Zoroastrianism to Islam, within single-chambered, stone-lined graves, orientated around the
          central shrine (mazar). 18th to 19th Century CE burials were also recorded.
12.5.10   Anthropological research was completed in parallel with the archaeological excavation process by a
          team from a Moscow-based research institute, with aims to characterize the local population
          according to their ethnicity, from study of the human remains. These studies have employed an
          outdated and controversial, technique known as craniology, which involves the measuring of skulls
          to identify genetic traits to indicate ethnicity or race. In the provided documentation, craniological
          systematics are used to define parameters aligned with race classification (or crainotypes) through


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 281 of 371
          measurement of the skull, forehead nasal index etc. These parameters are then linked to ethnic
          groups (e.g., Tajik, Kyrgz and Turkmen). The purpose and dissemination of data from this work is
          unclear.
          SUMMARY OF EXISTING STUDIES UNDERTAKEN FOR THE PROJECT:
          ETHNOGRAPHIC
12.5.11   A number of ethnographic studies have been undertaken for the Project. General summaries of
          each study are provided below. Detailed summaries of the findings in each of the targeted villages
          are included in Annex 20.2 and Annex 20.3.

          Table 12-2 - Summary of Previous Ethnographic Surveys (prior to 2023)

           Study / survey      Timing   Purpose

           Focus group         2011     Focus group discussions were held within villages to be resettled in support

           Ethnographic        2014 –   An Ethnographic Study of Nurobod and Rogun report (2015) was compiled

          2014 Environmental and Social Impact Assessment
12.5.12   During fieldwork for the 2014 ESIA (completed in 2011) focus group discussions were held within
          the villages to be resettled (including Aligalaboni bolo, Khakimi poyon, and Mehrobod). ‘Holy places’
          were identified within the proposed flooding zone, including monumental assets associated with
          spirituality as well as sacred (and ancient) trees linked to the ‘luck’ of the village (associated with
          harvest and health) and frequented by local pilgrims to ensure good fortune (ESIA Annex, 13.4.3,
          p.116 - 117). The ESIA Annex also notes that the villages “have not heard anything about the
          relocation of these holy places” (ibid).
          2015 Ethnographic Study
12.5.13   The subsequent Ethnographic Study of Nurobod and Rogun report (2015) was compiled by the A.
          Donish Institute following a period of survey in 2014. Two survey phases were completed with the
          aim of collecting information on the local traditions of the regions’ inhabitants. The study targeted six
          villages within Nurobod district (Mehrobod (partly), Chorsada, Aligalaboni bolo, Furudgoh, Sari pul
          and Mahallai poyon) and six villages within Rogun city (Mirogh, Tagi Agba, Tagi Kamar,
          Talhakchasma, Sicharogh and Sich). The following high-level observations are highlighted:
             Data was collated through a visual inspection of objects, the collection and purchase of local
              handicrafts and through interviews with residents.
             The following traditional craft activities were observed within the villages surveyed: wool carpet
              weaving; carpentry painted with regional motifs; needlework and embroidery; iron-mongering;
              musicians making their own instruments and playing traditional music.
             Tangible sacred and / or cultural sites were observed (though not located and recorded)
              including natural monuments and memorials, including ancient trees and shrines, attracting
              pilgrims e.g., for healing properties of the adjacent land, containing ritual / fetish objects. The
              cultural importance of springs was highlighted, as their presence is a key factor in determining


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 282 of 371
              the suitability of a location for settlement. Traditional architectural styles were also identified,
              including clay hearths dug into the ground (made by local craftswomen) and central ‘magical
              greeting’ pillars within traditional house plans.
             Oral histories of early 20th Century CE pottery production were noted and legends around the
              naming of a village and its origin were commonplace. Legends and taboos associated with
              specific villages or notable residents and related to specific locations and / or tree species
              (mulberry, pear) were also recorded.
             The practice of traditional medicine was observed, with plants and herbs gathered from the
              mountains (e.g., wild rose, sea buckthorn) and traditional ceremonial practices (associated with
              seasonal festivities, birth, circumcision, marriage and death) were noted and widespread.
             The influence of both the ongoing Project and outside influence / globalization as concurrent
              drivers for societal and economic change (and loss of traditional lifestyles and skills) was
              observed in the Project area.
             The relocation of cemetery sites was inferred, however, the detailed recording and/or
              management of other identified resources (prior to clearance and inundation) was not discussed
              and their permanent loss through resettlement is suggested, potentially as an inevitable outcome
              e.g., “none of the inhabitants identified are sure they will install a magical greeting pillar in their
              new house…”
             Two regional museums were noted (in Rogun City and Mehrobod) and a selection of
              ethnographic artefacts, mainly ceramics, were purchased from residents, by the researchers, for
              the National Museum of Ethnography in Dushanbe.
          ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL PANEL OF EXPERTS SITE VISIT
12.5.14   The Environmental and Social Panel of Experts (ESPoE) visited resettlement sites in 2023. The
          Panel visited a small museum in a community school which contains artefacts found in the Project
          area, some of which date back to the 17th Century CE. Some of the resettled population also
          donated items of past use from the abandoned areas.
12.5.15   The Panel considered this to be an ‘excellent example of how cultural heritage should be conserved
          and shared with communities’ and encouraged this approach to be undertaken in other resettlement
          areas, such as Nurobod (Republic of Tajikistan ESPoE, 2023). This museum (in Tursunzoda) was
          visited by WSP in March 2023, and is discussed in Chapter 20 of the ESIA.

12.6      PALEONTOLOGICAL BACKGROUND
12.6.1    Fossils of prehistoric animals, including dinosaurs, have been found across Tajikistan. Bones of a
          Kansajsuchus extensus (a paralligator) have been found in the Sughd Province in the north of the
          country. This neosuchian crocodile inhabited much of Central Asia in the Late Cretaceous Period
          (60–100 million years ago). In Shirkent National Park in the west of Tajikistan, over 400 dinosaur
          footprints from the Jurassic (164.7–155.7 million years ago) and Late Cretaceous periods survive.
          Fossils of the Cleoniceras species of ammonite, a group of extinct marine mollusc, survive in the
          Pamir Mountains in eastern Tajikistan. They lived in the ancient seas which covered this part of the
          country approximately 120 million years ago.
12.6.2    Bones of a southern mammoth have been discovered on the shore of the Kayarakkum Reservoir in
          northern Tajikistan, estimated to be approximately 1.5 million years old. Late Pleistocene to middle


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page 283 of 371
         Holocene waterbird bones were identified in a cave over 4,000 masl in the Pamir Mountains,
         representing the first and only known high altitude avian fauna in Asia (Zelenkov, Sayfulloev and
         Shnaider, 2021).
12.6.3   Fossilized dinosaur footprints were identified in close proximity to the Project inundation zone during
         recent geological investigations. The multiple sets of footprints (RHPP-AR-36) from different species
         were recorded on a steeply dipping sandstone slab approximately 500m to the west of the existing
         dam, immediately adjacent to an existing construction road (see Figure 12-1). It is noted that the
         slab in places shows evidence of ripples that are consistent with a shallow inter-tidal beach
         environment. Based on the available geological data, these date to the Cretaceous period (~145 –
         65 million years ago). During the September 2024 site visit by the WSP team it was noted that the
         condition of the footprints had deteriorated due to dust caused by nearby construction work. There
         was no demarcation or protection of the footprints in place. Another set of footprints was identified
         approximately 1.5 km to the south of the current crest of dam.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 284 of 371
         Figure 12-1 - Fossilised dinosaur footprints identified adjacent to an existing construction
         road (RHPP-AR-36) (Seller, 2023)




                              Approximately 2m




12.6.4   Several additional fossils are recorded within and around the proposed Project inundation zone on
         Mindat.org, an online database of minerals and fossils operated by the Hudson Institute of
         Mineralogy. It should be noted, however, that the co-ordinates of sites provided are estimates and
         have not been verified by an expert in the field. The fossil of a Jurassic dinosaur is recorded within
         the maximum inundation zone near the village of Sari pul (or Surkhob most) which will require field
         verification (see Volume 1, Chapter 20: Cultural Heritage of the ESIA). A Gissarites iljini fossil (a
         species of marine mollusc) dating to the Late/Upper Cretaceous (83.5–70.6 million years ago) is
         located a short distance outside the maximum inundation zone near the village of Sari Kosh.
12.6.5   A further four fossils are recorded within the 20 km study area. Additional Jurassic dinosaur fossils
         are recorded 2.5 km to the east and 5 km to the north of the maximum inundation zone. In the
         northern part of the study area, a Middle Jurassic Pternopteron mirabile fossil (a flying insect



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 285 of 371
         species) is recorded. A Praeneothyris darvasensis fossil (a brachiopod species) dating to the
         Late/Upper Cretaceous is recorded in the southern part of the study area.

12.7     CULTURAL HERITAGE BACKGROUND AND CONTEXT
12.7.1   The earliest evidence of human activity within the area which now comprises Tajikistan dates to the
         Lower Paleolithic period (1,000,000 BCE to 200,000 BCE). The oldest of the country’s major
         archaeological sites of this period, Kuldara, is situated approximately 46km to the south of the
         Rogun Dam. Archaeological material found at Kuldara has been dated to 0.85 million years ago,
         forming evidence of early human occupation of Central Asia. This dating is supported by
         stratigraphic, pedological and palynological data, shedding new light on the antiquity of stone tool
         making in this region and in Asia as a whole (Ranov, et al., 1995). Pebble and ﬂake tool industries
         have been found in well-stratiﬁed loess deposits at Kuldara (Davis & Ranov, 1999). Small stone
         tools used for scraping, chopping and grinding have been recorded here along with the bones of
         rhinoceroses, camels, panthers and hyenas.
12.7.2   Among the other known Lower Paleolithic sites within the region is Kuhi-Pioz (or Kukhi-Piyez) in the
         Vakhsh River basin, assigned to the early Middle Pleistocene in accordance with geological
         evidence (Ranov, et al., 1995).
12.7.3   Evidence of pebble-tool industrial sites, likely in the age range of 400,000 to 600,000 years, were
         identified during excavations in the 1970s in Karatau and Lakhuti in southern Tajikistan. These ﬁnds
         established the presence of Middle Pleistocene populations in the semi-arid interglacial environment
         of inner Asia (Dodonov and Baiguzina, 1995).
12.7.4   Nearly 8,000 stone tools were recorded during excavations at a Middle Paleolithic (200,000 BCE to
         35,000 BCE) site at Kara Bura (or Karaburo) on the banks of the Vakhsh River in the Dustri District
         of south-western Tajikistan. This stone tool production site is believed to be 40,000-50,000 years
         old. Excavations in the late 1950s at Kara Bura revealed a large number of pebble tools and
         Mousterian scrapers (Davis & Ranov, 1999). Evidence suggests that the people living in this region
         during the Middle Paleolithic were Neanderthals.
12.7.5   The Upper Paleolithic (35,000 BCE to 12,000 BCE) saw the introduction of Homo Sapiens in the
         region, although the onset of ice age in this period forced people elsewhere, resulting in a smaller
         number of archaeological sites.
12.7.6   Spears with stone tips, polished axes, knives, chisels and bone drills were produced and used from
         the Neolithic period (6,000 BCE to 4,000 BCE). Animal skins were also used to sew shoes and
         boots. Several Neolithic settlement sites have been recorded across the country, mainly comprising
         small stone-paved huts. One such site is located in Tutkavul in Nurek, 55km to the south-west of the
         Rogun Dam. Neolithic culture in Tajikistan is also referred to as the ‘Hissar culture’, with the first find
         dating to this period discovered in the Hissar Valley in 1948. Numerous Neolithic finds of Hissar
         tradition were discovered in Holocene soils on the surface of watersheds, comparable with
         Paleolithic sites in buried soils (Dodonov and Baiguzina, 1995).
12.7.7   In the mountain settlements of southern Tajikistan, many of which were investigated in the late
         1970s, implements of the local Neolithic Hissar culture were encountered. A Hissar Neolithic layer
         was discovered under the floor of one of the buildings of the Late Bronze Age settlement of Kangurt-
         Tut. It is believed that the Hissar culture in these mountain regions continued right up until the arrival
         of the Late Bronze Age Bactrian population. It is possible that the appearance of the steppe tribes



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 286 of 371
          brought about an intensification of livestock herding in the region. Long-distance livestock herding
          likely played a leading role in the development of the herding economy in the area (P'iankova,
          2014).
12.7.8    The Neolithic also saw the first pottery production in the region. Pottery vessels seem to have
          served a purely functional purpose initially, but the creation of decorated pottery became common
          from around the 6th millennium BCE. Ceramic pots remained important domestic items into the 19th
          and 20th centuries, and the local production of decorated pottery continues to this day across
          Tajikistan. A society centered around cattle breeding emerged in the 2nd millennium BCE in the
          lower reaches of the Vakhsh and Qizilsu (Kyzylsu) rivers and is known as ‘Vakhsh culture’. It is
          thought that Vakhsh culture was brought to this region by ancient tribes who arrived from elsewhere
          in Central Asia. Pastoralism was the basis of the economy of the Vakhsh culture. The people
          probably lived in temporary camps that might have been situated on the banks of the Vakhsh River,
          the traces of which have been washed away (P'Iankova, 2016).
12.7.9    Vakhsh culture graves lie in the upper foothill terraces of the river valleys adjacent to the mountain
          ridge watersheds (P'Iankova, 2016). There are various types of burial associated with Vakhsh
          culture, including round, triangular, T- and U-shaped and curved burials. Pair burials of men and
          women have been recorded, with the men typically facing north in a crouched position on the right-
          hand side and the women on the left. The majority of these mounds (or kurgans) have a ring of
          stones at their base. The burial grounds are characterized by uniformity of the burial structures and
          identical material culture (LT., 2016). Grave goods can include ceramic, metallic, stone and bone
          artefacts. Simple grave pits were also used, usually for children. Zoroastrianism is potentially
          reflected the beliefs of the ancient inhabitants of the lower reaches of the Vakhsh and Qizilsu. Its
          traces may be identifiable in the form of charcoal in graves, fires under the barrow fill and soot-
          covered vessels on small hearths in the burial chambers (P'Iankova, 2016).
12.7.10   In the settlements of Kangurt-Tut and Teguzak-ha near the Nurek Reservoir, there have been found
          both vessels made in the traditions of the settled farming milieu (of the Molali type), and pottery of
          the Bronze Age steppe tribes. In both settlements, two periods of construction have been identified,
          marked by extensive use of stone in their architecture (a distinguishing feature of Late Bronze Age
          settlements in southern Tajikistan). The influence of the pottery designs of the steppe tribes has
          been traced in vessels from the graves of the Vakhsh Culture (P'Iankova, 2016).
12.7.11   The ethnocultural composition of the population of southern Tajikistan during the Late Bronze Age
          was complex, with no unilinear development and traces of movement of various populations,
          representative of large tribal migrations characteristic of Central Asia in the second millennium B.C.
          All Bronze Age sites in southern Tajikistan have been dated to the last quarter of the second
          millennium B.C (P'Iankova, 2016). During the Hellenistic period (4th – 1st Century BCE), the
          southern extent of modern-day Tajikistan was within the Greco-Bactrian kingdom, a Greek state
          enduring throughout Central Asia. The region was effectively lost to Iranian nomadic tribes by c. 145
          BCE, with the onset of the Kushan Period (Guerra et al, 2009).
12.7.12   Evidence of prehistoric copper craftmanship includes arrowheads, spears, horse armor and axes.
          The ancient Greek historian Herodotus noted in the 6th century BCE that the inhabitants of Central
          Asia were skilled in the production of copper. Copper production increased significantly during the
          Samanid dynasty’s rule (819-999 CE), with high quality copper pots and goblets mostly made in
          Samarkand, the capital of the dynasty. Copper vessel manufacture reached its peak in the 19th
          Century CE.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 287 of 371
12.7.13   The territory of present-day Tajikistan was a crossroads for the many different ethnic groups that
          have controlled Central Asia over the past 3,000 years. Three main Silk Road routes pass through
          Tajikistan, with the Karategin route, linking China with Uzbekistan, primarily used throughout the 5th
          to 12th Centuries (UNESCO, 2013). The Silk Roads: Zarafshan-Karakum Corridor is one of two
          ‘cultural’ (rather than ‘natural’) UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Tajikistan. The site, which was
          inscribed in 2023, spans three countries (Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan and Tajikistan) and at its closest
          is approximately 65 km to the north of the Project area (UNESCO, 2023). The Tugay forests of the
          Tigrovaya Balka Nature Reserve ‘natural’ World Heritage Site is discussed in Section 12.8. Until the
          Arab invasions, beginning in the 7th Century CE, most of Central Asia was under Persian influence.
          This remained strong, and new Islamic Persian dynasties sprang up, the most important of which
          was that of the Samanids (875 to 999). The Samanid period made a major contribution to the
          development of the cultural identity of the peoples that were subsequently to call themselves Tajiks.
          The collapse of the Samanid Empire left a battleground for successive waves of invaders, primarily
          from the northeast and evident in the medieval-period fortress sites located in the study area, as
          discussed in Section 12.8.
12.7.14   The defeat of the Samanids by the Turkic Ghaznavid dynasty in 999 marked the beginning of the
          decline of Persian influence in Central Asia. Largely due to the protection provided by the
          mountainous terrain, the people of present-day Tajikistan were able to preserve their Persian
          culture. Tajikistan is the only former Soviet Republic with an Iranian language; and music, dance
          and poetry in the Persian tradition play a major role in Tajik society.
12.7.15   Until the Soviet period, the territory of what is now Tajikistan belonged to the Emirate of Bukhara. It
          was ruled by the Turkish governors of the Amir of Bukhara. In 1920, the Red Army ousted the Amir,
          who fled to Afghanistan. His people, the Tajiks, were assigned a new rulership chosen from among
          the leaders of the newly formed republic of Uzbekistan. In 1924, recognized as a people, the Tajiks
          were given their own republic with the capital at Stalinabad (the Soviet name for Dushanbe). In
          1929, the republic was awarded Union status. When the Soviet Union broke up in 1991, Tajikistan
          became an independent state.

12.8      CURRENT CONDITIONS
          ARCHAEOLOGICAL AND HISTORICAL RESOURCES
12.8.1    Targeted field surveys (visual reconnaissance) were completed by the archaeology team at the A.
          Donish Institute from 14 to 23 June and 15 August to 12 September 2023. The areas targeted in
          June, and August to September were informed by the preceding desktop study of available literature
          including the list of Registered Monuments (2017) and previous survey phases, particularly to
          understand the current condition of resources previously identified. The primary aim of the surveys
          was to rapidly record archaeological resources falling with the upper terraces (over 1,200 masl).
12.8.2    The data collected during the targeted field surveys is supplemented by the GIS data acquired
          through the CAAL Project database, which is based on analysis of remote sensing imagery.
          Features identified within 20 km of the maximum inundation zone are discussed in this section.
12.8.3    A gazetteer of all cultural heritage baseline resources identified within 20 km of the maximum
          inundation zone is provided in Annex 20.1. Drawing 20-2 (Annex 20.5) shows the distribution of
          these resources.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 288 of 371
         Geology and Topography
12.8.4   A series of river terraces line the course of the Vakhsh through the study area. Along much of the
         west bank of the river, the lower terraces have been washed away, with only high terraces (often
         over 100m higher than river level) remaining. Much of the west bank therefore comprises steep
         cliffs. On the eastern bank of the river, more of the expansive and flat lower terraces survive.
12.8.5   Between the dam and the confluence of the Vakhsh and the Khingob rivers (i.e., the south-western
         half of the study area), the Vakhsh River valley comprises six terraces: the current floodplain and
         five terraces of Mid-to-Upper-Pleistocene and Holocene age. Only small sections of the first and
         second lower terraces have been preserved (Figure 12-2). The third and fourth terraces (located at
         approximately 40m and 50-60m above river level respectively) were the focus of the 2023
         archaeological surveys.

         Figure 12-2 - Terraces 1 - 3 between the villages of Tagi Agba and Tagi Kamar, with the first
         and second terraces preserved only in narrow sections (Filimonova, 2023)




12.8.6   The bases of the terraces are composed of channel alluvium and pebbles. In the third terrace, the
         pebbles are covered with a layer of loess-like loam, mixed with mudflow sediments. Although the
         topography of the terraces is relatively level, the lack of fertile soil makes farming difficult. The soils
         are rocky and dry and there is a risk of dangerous mudflows. The fourth terrace is generally much
         narrower than the third. The pebbles here are covered by 1 – 3m of loess with less mudflow rubble,
         and dry soils. As a result of these challenging conditions, few rural settlements are situated on the
         third and fourth terraces, with most modern villages located on the fifth terrace. Here, the layer of
         loess is ~10m in thickness and the soil contains fewer stones. The presence of springs and streams
         also means that the soil is also less dry, with conditions more favorable for settlement historically.
12.8.7   The Tugay forests of the Tigrovaya Balka Nature Reserve, which is located approximately 180 km
         downstream of the Project area to its south-west, was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List
         in 2023. This is a ‘natural’ World Heritage Site, inscribed as an outstanding example “representing
         significant on-going ecological and biological processes in the evolution and development of
         terrestrial, fresh water, coastal and marine ecosystems and communities of plants and animals”
         (UNESCO, 2023). Potential impacts on this World Heritage Site as a result of the construction of the




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 289 of 371
         dam and suggested offset measures are set out in the No Net Loss Framework of the Biodiversity
         Management Plan.
         Nurobod District
         The June survey was targeted to two areas of heightened potential, between the villages on
         Pandovchi and Gharm on the western side of the reservoir area, and between Nurobod and Qal’ai
         Surkh on the eastern side. On the outskirts of Pandovchii Agba, on the edge of the upper river
         terrace, the team inspected the site of a 16th-Century fortress, Qal’ai Kofir (RHPP-AR-01), a
         nationally registered monument at 1,250 masl. No upstanding remains of the historical fortress were
         observed. The location has been in regular agricultural use (Figure 12-3).

         Figure 12-3 - Location of Qal’ai Kofir Fortress (RHPP-AR-01; Filimonova, 2023)




12.8.8   On the western outskirts of the Magzori Sir settlement, the fortress of Qal’ai Mir or Qal’ai Shokhon
         (RHPP-AR-02) was recorded. The site, dated to the 18th Century CE, was first identified by Soviet
         archaeologists in the 1950s and is listed on the National Register. During the June 2023 inspection
         of the site, the team observed a raised mound and stone-lined square pit to the west of the fortress,
         known locally as the Zindan Hill (Figure 12-4 and Figure 12-5), and a drainage culvert, which
         potentially supplied water to the fortress from the nearby stream. According to a local resident, a
         local Bek (chief or commander) lived in the fortress and people from the nearby village of Gulomon
         (meaning ‘slaves’) worked for him, cultivating the fields surrounding the fortress and on the lower
         floodplain terrace.
12.8.9   The fortress of Qal’ai Mir is close to the settlement of Darband and the fortress of Qal’ai Imlok
         (RHPP-AR-13), which dates to the 10th to 12th centuries. According to archaeologist A. M.
         Mandelstam, Qal’ai Imlok was the political and economic center of Karategin in the ancient and
         medieval periods, strategically located near the confluence of the Vakhsh and Khingob Rivers
         (Mandelstam and Rosenfeld, 1960).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 290 of 371
          Figure 12-4 - View to the west of Qal’ai Mir / Qal’ai Shokhon Fortress (RHPP-AR-02)
          (Filimonova, 2023)




          Figure 12-5 - View to the west of Zindan Hill at Magzori Sir village (Filimonova, 2023)




12.8.10   At Zarda village, the Kalai Chungi Zubaid fortress (RHPP-AR-46) was observed. It is situated above
          the maximum inundation zone, although its exact elevation is not known. The villages of Dehai
          Gulomon, Sari dasht, Sari kosh and Bulbuldara were also inspected during the reconnaissance
          survey, although no archaeological monuments were observed in the vicinity of these settlements.
12.8.11   During the August-September 2023 archaeological survey, poorly preserved structural ruins were
          recorded between two tributaries of the Vakhsh River, close to Khakimii poyon (RHPP-AR-37). The
          stone foundations of two buildings, which are situated within the maximum inundation zone,
          measure 7x3m and 4x3m. Small ceramic fragments found here date to the 19th Century CE.
12.8.12   The remains of a fortress were recorded within the maximum inundation zone near Mehrobod
          (formerly Komsomolobod; RHPP-AR-38). The fortress is situated on a narrow rectangular terrace
          below Mehrobod at the mouth of a tributary of the Vakhsh River, and two stepped platforms,
          separated by an earthen rampart, survive (Figure 12-6). The masonry of the outer wall of the
          fortress is visible from the southern edge of the upper platform. The team excavated a 1.5m wide
          test pit through the earthen rampart in an attempt to determine its structure. Several large stones



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |

from the destroyed masonry of the wall were encountered at a depth of 1m, and the remains of a
beam from the ceiling were found at 1.4m. Small fragments of 18th and 19th century CE molded
pottery were also found in the rubble (Figure 12-7). An oil depot was located at this site in the 1960s
and 70s.

12.8.13   The remains of a 9th – 12th century CE settlement was discovered during an earlier reburial of a
          Christian cemetery at Mehrobod, although this site has since been significantly disturbed by modern
          burials. A cave above the terraces to the east of Mehrobod was examined for possible rock art,
          although during the August-September 2023 survey nothing was identified.
12.8.14   Whilst examining the fourth terrace (50 – 60m above river level) to the west of the village of Yakhch,
          several stone objects were identified, mostly comprising flakes (RHPP-AR-45). They could not be
          dated due to their small number and lack of markings. It is possible that the objects have been
          redistributed from higher levels. No archaeological evidence was identified during the inspection of
          terraces between the villages of Yakhch and Sari Ju. Stone objects, comprising flakes and a pointed
          tool, were found in the upper ploughed layer of a terrace 1.5 km to the east of Sari Ju (RHPP-AR-
          44).
12.8.15   The poorly preserved remains of a small fortress, called Kalankok by locals (RHPP-AR-39), were
          recorded to the northeast of Sari Ju on the edge of the terrace, within the maximum inundation zone
          (Figure 12-8). The remains have been disturbed by ploughing. Fragments of ceramics were
          collected from the surface which date the fortress to the 18th – 19th centuries (Figure 12-9).

12.8.16   No archaeological evidence was identified during the examination of terraces between Sari Ju and

          (RHPP-AR-40) on a hill at the edge of a 50 – 60m high terrace, within the maximum inundation
          zone. The stone foundations of the walls are well preserved and the contours of partially buried
          buildings can be seen at the foot of the hill (Figure 12-10). Based on the ceramic material recovered
          from the site, it likely dates to the 18th – 19th centuries.

          surveyed, between the village of Yakhak and the bridge over the Khingob.
12.8.18   Several possible archaeological and historical sites have been identified with Nurobod District as
          part of the CAAL Project. The potential remains of structures have been identified via remote
          sensing, some of which may comprise parts of abandoned settlements. One such settlement site
          has been identified on a platform above the Vakhsh River to the west of the village of Kavlokho

          Rasht District
12.8.19   In the vicinity of Navdonak settlement, the fortresses previously identified and investigated by the A.
          Donish Institute in 2017 were noted by the field team. Qal’ai Bacha (RHPP-AR-05) and Qal’ai
          Dukhtarak (RHPP-AR-06) are located on the upper terraces, over 1,300 masl. The latter site houses
          a modern cemetery. Surface artefacts were noted in proximity, potentially associated with another
          possible fort location on the lower terrace (RHPP-AR-07). These sites have been tentatively dated,
          pending further analysis, to the 17th – 19th centuries.

12.8.20   Within the Obi Mehnat Jamoat, the settlement of Dakhana was noted to have higher archaeological
          potential. A fortress (RHPP-AR-03) is recorded from the 18th Century CE. Although no above
          ground remains survive, there is some potential for subsurface material. In proximity, a cone-shaped
          mound was observed, possibly the site of an associated watchtower.
12.8.21   Near the village of Dakhana, a raised earthen mound (RHPP-AR-04) was identified in proximity to
          the Dakhana Fortress (RHPP-AR-03). The mound (and others yet to be identified in the near

12.8.22   The location of a possible watchtower was also recorded within the maximum inundation zone
          between the villages of Dakhana and Obi Mehnat (RHPP-AR-11). A mazar, named the mausoleum
          of Khodja Abdullohi Vali, is currently situated atop this cone-shaped hill (Figure 12-16). Fragments

          opposite the mausoleum during the August-September 2023 survey. The outlines of structures were
          also identified here, suggesting it may be the site of an abandoned village.

12.8.23   According to archaeological data from the 1950s, a small fortress was located in the village of Sangi
          maliki. However, no trace of the fortress was found during the survey.
12.8.24   The terraces between the villages of Navobod and Dakhana on the western bank of the Vakhsh
          River were examined in greater detail during the August-September 2023 survey. The well
          preserved Gumushkala fortress (RHPP-AR-41), which is located above the maximum inundation
          zone close to the village of Dakhani gumush, was examined. The fortress likely dates to the 18th –
          19th centuries and was first examined by A. M. Mandelstam in 1954. It was located in a strategically
          advantageous position, overlooking the road on the western bank of the river.
12.8.25   A collection of stone tools was found during examination of the terraces around the village of Vahdat
          (RHPP-AR-43). The tools were found on the surface of the soil in the upper part of the fourth terrace
          to the east of the village. It is likely that the tools were originally deposited on the surface of the fifth
          terrace but have been redeposited as a result of erosion. The handaxe found in the collection is
          thought to be Late Stone Age (50,000 BP). The lithic scatters identified throughout the study area
          are considered to be resources of medium sensitivity due to their rarity and individual research
          value, although the sub-surface archaeological potential of these locations is likely to be very limited.
