12.8.26   The Yapoloki Fortress (RHPP-AR-42), located close to the village of the same name, was
          examined. The fortress is well preserved, and the remains of the outer rampart and small mounds
          from its rooms are still visible. Ceramic vessels previously recovered from the site, which are
          currently stored in the regional museum of Gharm, suggest that the fortress was originally erected in
          the early Middle Ages and was still in use in the 18th and 19th centuries. The fortress is located
          above the maximum inundation zone.
12.8.27   The potential remains of structures located on low ground have been recorded near Dehai Amirbek
          as part of the CAAL Project (RHPP-AR-28).
          Rogun District
12.8.28   An inspection of the terraces on the western bank of the Vakhsh River between the villages of
          Lughuri poyon and Chorsada was undertaken during the August-September 2023 archaeological
          survey. No traces of ancient buildings were identified during inspection at the village of Aligalaboni,
          and local residents claimed that the village was founded in the 1970s. Locals in Obi boriki poyon
          were not aware of any fortresses or other ancient buildings around their village.
12.8.29   In the village of Khumrog (formerly Qumoq), locals reported finding fragments of ceramics whilst
          digging in their gardens, and sherds from molded ceramic vessels dating between the late 18th and
          early 20th centuries were collected from vegetable gardens here during the survey. The chairman of
          the Jamoat told the team that, according to local legend, a watchtower was previously located on a
          hill near the village. However, most of the hill collapsed into the river when the base of the terrace
          was washed away. The existence of a watchtower here is disputed; the legend may simply be based
          on the fact that the shape of the hill would have made it an ideal location.
12.8.30   During the ethnographic studies undertaken in June 2023, a mill was identified in the village of
          Lughuri poyon (RHPP-AR-12). The owner of a mill believes that it is over 100 years old. It was
          inherited from the man’s father and has been used by at least three generations of the same family.
          The exact co-ordinates for the mill’s location within the village are unknown, although the informant
          suggested that it is likely to be situated within or adjacent to the maximum inundation zone. As



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 300 of 371
          stated in Volume 1: Chapter 20: Cultural Heritage of the ESIA, where the exact location of cultural
          heritage resource is currently not known, this will be determined through further community
          consultation, where possible.
12.8.31   The CAAL Project dataset includes three archaeological sites in Rogun City which are situated
          within the study area. These are the potential remains of abandoned settlements in the southern
          part of the study area, downstream of the proposed Project footprint (RHPP-AR-21, RHPP-AR-26,
          RHPP-AR-27).
12.8.32   A fortress was located at the confluence of the Obigarm and Vakhsh rivers in the Rogun District,
          although this was destroyed in the 1980s during the construction of structures associated with the
          Rogun hydroelectric power station.
12.8.33   Several archaeological and historical sites have been recorded in the southern part of the study area
          in the Baljuvon District as part of the CAAL Project. Archaeological finds recorded near the village of
          Mulokoni include a flint splinter, believed to date to the 6th to 4th millennia BCE (RHPP-AR-20). An
          abandoned settlement at Tagov Detai (RHPP-AR-23) is believed to date to the 1st to 4th centuries
          CE. A square stone with pitted sides recorded here may be associated with the abandoned
          settlement (RHPP-AR-22). The fortresses of Ruiob (RHPP-AR-16), Shohindara (RHPP-AR-17),
          Guldara (RHPP-AR-18) and Mulokoni (RHPP-AR-19) likely date to the 18th and 19th centuries. The
          Sarihisor Watchtower (RHPP-AR-15) dates to the 18th to 20th centuries.
          Summary of Archaeological and Historical Resources
12.8.34   In summary, 45 archaeological and historical resources have been recorded within the baseline
          study area, including 20 fortress sites, mostly dating to the 16th – 19th centuries and commonly
          situated on the outskirts of currently populated (ancient) villages. The conditions in the study area
          were not favorable for settlement historically, particularly on the lower terraces, although there was
          an upturn in human activity in the 18th and 19th centuries. It was during this period that the
          economic position of the Karategin territory, which was independent at the time, strengthened, as
          Tajiks returned to their ancestral lands and pushed the nomadic Kyrgyz to the upper reaches of the
          Qizilsu River, with most of the monuments in the area date to this period (Mandelstam, 1956). The
          majority comprise small fortresses and settlements along the road connecting Karategin with the
          plains. It is likely that many of the modern villages in the study area originated in this period. Larger
          fortified estates were also constructed in this period, belonging to local Beks who ruled the districts.
          The small fortresses and estates were mostly abandoned by the Beks in the 1920s and 1930s as
          Soviet power was established. Most of these sites have since been damaged as a result of
          ploughing and natural erosion.
12.8.35   The historical origins of settlements within the study area are further substantiated by the findings of
          the ethnographic survey, which recorded several ancient burial sites. The villages of Khakimii bolo,
          Khumrog and Lughuri poyon have burial sites dating from the 16th to 18th centuries.
12.8.36   Several earlier period resources were also identified, such as the prehistoric lithic scatter sites and
          the 4th – 6th century CE mounds in proximity to a fortress at Dakhana village. Remains of relic
          agricultural features were identified as part of the CAAL Project, concentrated on the north-eastern
          slopes of the Vakhsh Valley, including possible remains of field systems (RHPP-AR-33), such as
          terraces (RHPP-AR-35) and lynchets (RHPP-AR-34; terraces formed on slopes as a result of
          ploughing).



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 301 of 371
12.8.37   With the exception of the fortress near Mehrobod (RHPP-AR-38), none of the archaeological and
          historical resources within the study area have yet been subject to intrusive archaeological
          evaluation and their cultural heritage value cannot therefore be easily determined. However, they
          have high research potential, particularly in informing the archaeological record on the lower
          Karategin route of the Silk Road and the Surkhob-Vakhsh river valley. All fortresses located during
          the June survey have been previously listed on the official register of National Monuments of
          Tajikistan (2017).
          CULTURAL AND SACRED RESOURCES
12.8.38   Targeted ethnographic surveys were undertaken by the ethnography team at the A. Donish Institute
          from 14 to 21 June and 25 August to 7 September 2023. In the June survey, consultations were
          carried out in the villages of Khakimii bolo, Khakimii poyon, Khumrog, Obiboriki bolo, Obiboriki
          poyon and Lughuri poyon. In the August-September survey, consultations were carried out in
          Mehrobod (Komsomolobod), Roghuni poyon, Aligalaboni bolo, Kumrog, Chanor and Bedikho.
12.8.39   A series of targeted interviews at household level were also conducted by M-Vector in August 2023.
          Data concerning general demographic and socio-economic background, gender, education level,
          employment status, vulnerable populations, resettlement status, access to services, expectations
          and knowledge of cultural heritage was collected from across five regions: Rogun, Nurobod,
          Faizobod, Tursunzoda, and Danghara. A total of 900 participants were asked about their awareness
          of cultural heritage sites, whether they were consulted about them and their feelings regarding
          management and recording of cultural heritage in the Project area.
12.8.40   Of the 900 respondents, 35% (38% of male-headed households and 20% of female-headed
          households) stated that they were aware of cultural heritage sites in their village of origin. Of those
          who were aware of these sites, 81% of these 332 respondents (81% of male- and 74% of female-
          headed households) had been consulted regarding cultural heritage site relocation, primarily in
          relation to mosques and cemeteries. 79% of these 332 households stated that they were satisfied
          with the management of cultural heritage in their village, while 5% were generally satisfied but
          thought more could have been done, 8% had no or insufficient information and 8% stated that they
          did not know. Households undergoing resettlement in RAP 2 were more likely to have been aware of
          cultural heritage sites (46%), more likely to have been consulted (83%) and more likely to be
          satisfied (88%) than other households (20%, 72% and 52% respectively).
12.8.41   A gazetteer of all cultural heritage baseline resources identified within 20 km of the maximum
          inundation zone is provided in Volume 2, Annex 20.1 – Cultural Heritage Gazetteer - Drawing 20-
          2 (Annex 20.5) which shows the distribution of these resources. A gazetteer of villages studied in
          the current and previous ethnography surveys, which includes details of cultural and sacred
          resources within in each, is provided in Volume 2, Annex 20.2 - Drawing 20-3 (Annex 20.5) shows
          the location of all villages being resettled under RAP 2 of the resettlement program.
          Nurobod District
12.8.42   Within the Nurobod District, the heads of 568 households were consulted as part of the M-Vector
          survey regarding cultural and sacred heritage sites within their villages and their feelings regarding
          the recording and management of those sites. Just over one fifth of respondents (21%) answered
          that they were aware of cultural sites, including religious sites, local shrines, traditionally sacred
          places and historical and/or archaeological sites. This was the lowest level of awareness recorded
          across the five districts.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 302 of 371
12.8.43   Of those respondents who were aware of cultural heritage sites, it was found that 78% were directly
          consulted by the Project team regarding the cultural heritage prior to their relocation. Subsequently,
          58% of respondents who were aware of cultural heritage sites agreed that the cultural and sacred
          places were appropriately, and permissibly recorded and appropriate mitigation strategies were
          proposed, including maintenance of access. 12% of households responded that the cultural sites
          were recorded, but they were not consulted regarding the relocation or management of the sites.
12.8.44   Overall, 63% of respondents stated that they were satisfied with the management of cultural
          heritage sites in their villages of origin, with only 16% of respondents responding with uncertainty (“I
          don’t know”).
12.8.45   Two of the villages in which consultations were undertaken during the June 2023 ethnography
          survey are located within the Khakimi Jamoat in the Nurobod district:
                In Khakimii bolo, the chairman of the village and ten other locals were interviewed. When
                 asked about holy places in the village, one local referred to the village cemetery (RHPP-CR-
                 01) and the village mosque (RHPP-CR-05), both of which are modern.
                In Khakimii poyon, thirteen locals were interviewed, including the village chairman. The ‘holy
                 places’ identified in the village were a modern mosque (RHPP-CR-07), a modern cemetery
                 (RHPP-CR-06) and a small burial place of 18th and 19th century date (RHPP-CR-20).
12.8.46   The ethnography team consulted the register of monuments for the district and identified the
          following tombs and graves of religious scholars located in the villages visited in Nurobod, many of
          which are visited regularly by locals:
                The 17th to 18th century CE tomb of Oftobhuga (RHPP-CR-02), who, according to legend,
                 was a herbal healer and religious scholar who came from the southern regions and founded
                 the village of Khakimii bolo (khakim meant ‘healing’).
                Another 17th to 18th century CE tomb (RHPP-CR-03) in Khakimi bolo.
                The 19th Century CE tomb of theologian, Dumullo Olim, in Khakimii bolo which is visited by
                 pilgrims from all over Tajikistan (RHPP-CR-04).
12.8.47   Several cultural and sacred resources were also identified within the Nurobod district during the
          archaeological field survey undertaken in June 2023:
                In the village of Pandovchii agba, which is situated at 1,242 masl, an old cemetery was
                 identified (RHPP-CR-21). The cemetery is believed to be 200-300 years old and contains
                 over 100 graves. A modern stone mausoleum in the village, called ‘Xazrati Khujai Paron’ by
                 the locals, has been built on the site of an older one (RHPP-CR-22). The locals spoken to
                 said that they do not know who is buried in the mausoleum, but he is said to have been a holy
                 man. The archaeology team considered the mausoleum and the village’s late 20th Century
                 CE Mosque to have ‘no historical value’.
                A mosque (RHPP-CR-23) built in the 1980s was identified in the village of Bulbuldara (1,252
                 masl). The mosque, which is named after Shokh T.G. Filimonov, is no longer in use.
                An old mazar named after Vakhobiddin (RHPP-CR-24) was identified in the village of Zarda at
                 an altitude of 1,400 masl.
                In the village of Magzori Sir, a cemetery (RHPP-CR-25) was identified in which, according to
                 locals, a great priest is buried. The village is situated at 1,306 masl.
                An old cemetery (RHPP-CR-26), named after Oromgoh, was also identified in Sari Kosh
                 (1,286 masl), although no other cultural or sacred resources were identified in the village.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 303 of 371
12.8.48   During the August-September 2023 survey, the ethnography team visited the village of Mehrobod,
          which was formerly the center of the district and known as Komsomolobod. Villagers refer to the
          settlement as “Old District”. The grave of a Bibi Otun, an influential female religious figure, is located
          within the village (RHPP-CR-32). Bibi Otun are female informal spiritual leaders who perform rituals
          and prayers, teach religion to women and children, and help women with social and domestic
          matters. They have a high social status as religious authorities in Tajikistan (Cieślewska, 2015).
          According to locals, people visit this grave and rub dirt from it on wounded parts of their body to heal
          them.
12.8.49   A sycamore tree (or Chanor), believed by locals to be over 1,000 years old, is located in the village
          (RHPP-CR-33). According to a local legend, a large dragon once lived where the tree now stands
          and forbade anyone to pass through the area. The sycamore then slowly grew around the dragon,
          preventing it from harming anyone. It is said that, if you look closely, you can still see the shape of
          the dragon’s head in the tree.

          Figure 12-17 - Sycamore tree (Chanor), Mehrobod




          Rogun District
12.8.50   Within the Rogun District, awareness of cultural heritage sites was higher than Nurobod, at 39% of
          respondents. Of those who were aware, just over half (54%) were directly consulted regarding
          cultural heritage prior to relocation.
12.8.51   51% of respondents who were aware of cultural heritage sites answered that they were satisfied that
          the sites had been recorded with their permission and that appropriate compensation, relocation or
          management strategies had been proposed. Nearly a fifth (18%) responded that the places had
          been recorded, but that no consultation on further management or relocation had occurred to date.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 304 of 371
          Overall, 61% of respondents who were aware of cultural heritage sites stated that they were
          satisfied with the organization and management of the sites within their district.
12.8.52   The following sites were recorded in Rogun District during the June 2023 ethnographic survey:
               A modern mosque (RHPP-CR-08) and cemetery (RHPP-CR-09) in Khumrog.
               The 16th to 17th century CE tomb of the great theologian Mir Sayyid Aliya Hamadoni (RHPP-
                CR-11) and another 16th to 17th century CE tomb (RHPP-CR-10) in Khumrog.
               A modern mosque (RHPP-CR-14) and cemetery (RHPP-CR-12) in Obiboriki bolo. A spring
                (RHPP-CR-13) was also identified in the village.
               A modern mosque (RHPP-CR-15) and a spring (RHPP-CR-19) in Obiboriki boyon.
               A modern mosque (RHPP-CR-16) and cemetery (RHPP-CR-17) in Lughuri poyon along with
                an 18th Century CE tomb (RHPP-CR-18).
12.8.53   During the August-September 2023 survey, the ethnography team visited the village of Aligalaboni
          bolo, previously named Lake Ararakon. The shrine of Aligalabon, who was a scholar of Islam, is
          located here, and it is believed that his descendants still live in the village. There are three tombs
          located in the village: two of them are of unknown scholars of Islam who were supposedly martyred
          (RHPP-CR-35, RHPP-CR-36) and the third is the tomb of Eshan Asrari (RHPP-CR-34). Eshan
          Asrari’s tomb is visited more often than the other two.
12.8.54   There are several burial sites in the village of Bedikho. One is the mausoleum of a young scientist
          who, during the establishment of Soviet power, supposedly tried to escape the rulers and took
          refuge in the village (RHPP-CR-27). However, he was caught by the authorities and killed. A further
          four burial sites were recorded in the village (RHPP-CR-28, RHPP-CR-29, RHPP-CR-30, RHPP-
          CR-31).
          Rasht District
12.8.55   A mazar, named the mausoleum of Khodja Abdullohi Vali, is situated atop a cone-shaped hill
          located between the villages of Dakhana and Obi Mehnat (Figure 12-16). It was identified during the
          August-September 2023 archaeology survey. This may also have been the site of a watchtower
          historically (RHPP-AR-11).
12.8.56   A museum to the Tajik-Soviet writer, Boqi Rahimzoda (RHPP-CR-37), is located in Sangi Maliki
          village, within the inundation zone. It may be possible to move the museum up the valley to Boqi
          Rahimzoda’s birthplace, Sorbog, which will not be inundated.
          Faizobod District
12.8.57   The highest level of awareness of local cultural heritage was recorded in the Faizobod District
          (86%), although only 30 households were interviewed (compared with 568 in Nurobod and 84 in
          Rogun). Of those aware, all but one household was directly consulted about cultural heritage sites
          (97%).
12.8.58   Regarding permissible recording, further consultation and proposed mitigation strategies, 94% of
          respondents who were aware of cultural heritage sites answered that they felt cultural places were
          adequately recorded with their permission, and appropriate compensation, relocation and/or
          management strategies of those places was proposed. Consequently, 97% of respondents with
          awareness of cultural heritage sites agreed that the management of the cultural heritage in their
          villages of origin were well organized.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 305 of 371
          Tursunzoda District
12.8.59   In the Tursunzoda District, awareness of cultural heritage sites stood at 83% of respondents. Of
          those respondents who were aware, 97% responded that they were directly consulted regarding
          cultural heritage in their villages of origin, such as religious sites, local shrines and traditionally
          sacred places, prior to being relocated. The same percentage responded that these sites had been
          recorded with their permission, and that appropriate mitigation strategies had been proposed.
          Overall, 98% of respondents who were aware of cultural heritage sites were satisfied with their
          management. This was the highest rate of satisfaction recorded in the five districts.
          Danghara District
12.8.60   In Danghara District, 66% of respondents were aware of cultural heritage sites, and of these, 45%
          were directly consulted about them. Only 28% of respondents (the lowest across the five districts)
          who were aware of cultural heritage sites said that these sites were recorded with their permission,
          and that appropriate mitigation was proposed. The overall level satisfaction with how cultural
          heritage sites were being managed was, however, high (94%).
          Cultural Landscape
12.8.61   Community / cultural leaders in the targeted settlements expressed how the landscape is important
          to them during the Key Informant Interviews (KII). Specifically, they were asked to identify important
          landscapes and key viewpoints which may be impacted by the inundation. Generally, connections
          with the natural landscape were not noted as significant, though this viewpoint is very subjective and
          likely to vary between individuals, and potentially according to age and local family history.
12.8.62   In general terms, the cultural value of the landscape is heightened by the archaeological potential
          and the long-standing continued occupation of the land, particularly in the older settlements (e.g.,
          Mehrobod). The spiritual associations of specific topographic and natural features, and between
          wider elements of the landscape (i.e., views from individual receptors towards the mountains), are
          potentially multi-faceted and entrenched in local belief systems and oral history (stories around local
          landmarks including trees, spring sites and shrines, etc.).
12.8.63   It is considered that these cultural connections are important to the local sense of place, potentially
          helping to establishing the distinctiveness of a unique ethnographic landscape, with isolation
          fostering unique aspects of local culture and intangible heritage, which may vary between villages.
          These belief systems form the basis of local relationships with, and understanding of, the physical
          and spiritual world upon which a shared cultural identity has been built and how the local society is
          organized.
12.8.64   Viewsheds from the mountains towards the settlements / reservoir area are considered less
          significant than the connections to and between the villages, since general access to the tops of the
          mountains is limited.
          INTANGIBLE CULTURAL HERITAGE
12.8.65   Evidence of intangible cultural heritage was also recorded during the 2023 ethnographic surveys.
          The field reports (Volume 2, Annex 20.6) concluded that, although much of the Nurobod culture is
          similar to that found elsewhere in Tajikistan, there remain local peculiarities and differences which
          can be explained, in part, by the geography of the region and the isolation of many of the
          settlements.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 306 of 371
12.8.66   A gazetteer of villages studied in the current and previous ethnography surveys, which includes
          examples of intangible cultural heritage, is provided in Volume 2, Annex 20.2 - Drawing 20-3
          shows the location of all villages being resettled under RAP 2 of the resettlement program.
12.8.67   A number of common themes emerged over the course of the June 2023 ethnographic survey which
          are summarized in this section. Specific examples of intangible cultural heritage in different villages
          are outlined in Volume 2, Annex 20.3.
          Traditional Construction Styles and Techniques
          It was noted that many buildings in the villages surveyed retain traditional features characteristic of
          this mountainous region of Tajikistan. Dwellings generally have small terraces on their northern side
          and feature traditional wooden beam ceilings. Locally made traditional clay hearths, dug into the
          ground, were observed. Hearths are often made at home, although they can be bought ready-made.
          In new houses, a straight log, called "dstak", is placed on top of the house with a horn on it, for good
          luck.
          Traditional Crafts
          Needlework, carpentry, ceramics and instrument making were observed. Women continue to make
          clothes, carpets and decorations, such as ribbons for wedding bedspreads and cradles. Younger
          generations were noted as less interested in preserving craft traditions which are no longer seen as
          sources of income.
          Traditional Medicine
7.1.1.    Traditional medicine was observed including the use of dried herbs and plants helps to cleanse the
          blood of toxins and ‘refresh’ it. The most commonly eaten herbs and plants are mint, rhubarb,
          dandelion root, sorrel, mountain onions, cumin, blackcurrant and barberries. Plantain is used to treat
          stomach pain. Further detail on traditional medicine is provided in Volume 1: Chapter 06:
          Ecosystem Services of this ESIA.
          Traditional Food
7.1.2.    The process of making bread, such as chahlak, tanur and nonichavi, is widespread throughout the
          villages surveyed. Traditional dishes include umoch, which is a flour soup with small dumplings;
          atoll, which is flour porridge; atoli ordbieren, which is made from milk, flour, meat, onions and dried
          apricots and atolai bo shir, which is made from flour and milk with salt. Shurpo (soup) is served at
          funerals.
7.1.3.    During the September 2024 site visit, the WSP team spoke with women from Chorsada who had
          been resettled to Tursunzoda. The women told the team that when they lived in Chorsada, they
          would prepare food to leave in the mosque for travelers who got stranded in severe weather. They
          no longer do this in Tursunzoda because the climate is milder, and because the government have
          limited mosque opening hours for national security reasons.
          Traditional Clothing
7.1.4.    There is a mixture of styles from different regions and countries in the clothing of the inhabitants of
          the villages. Women mostly wear modern clothes rather than traditional dresses. They often wear
          small rumol (headscarves), and some wear larger headscarves that cover their shoulders. Some
          young girls wear hijabs. Men mostly wear European clothing, such as shirts, jeans and jackets.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 307 of 371
          Some wear a white calico skullcap, particularly in the month of Ramadan and during the reading of
          prayer.
          Traditional Practice – Family Life, Occupation, Beliefs, Rituals and Ceremonies
7.1.5.    Families in the villages are generally large. Women play an important role in ritual and ceremonial
          life, and they are expected to preserve and pass on their traditions to the next generation through
          the female line. Amulets are traditionally used to protect wearers. The bones of mountain goats, the
          teeth of bears and pieces of wood are commonly used to make amulets to protect a baby from evil
          spirits and prolong their life. Other traditional rites for children and babies include gavhorabandon,
          centered around a first-born’s cradle.
12.8.68   Marital relations in the villages were traditionally often intrafamilial, with cross-cousin marriages
          being preferred, in part to keep wedding expenses low and to prevent a family’s inheritance from
          falling into the hands of a stranger.
12.8.69   A law on the regulation of traditions, celebrations and ceremonies in Tajikistan now restricts wedding
          celebrations to one day. The aim of this law is to protect values of national culture and respect
          traditional customs whilst ensuring that families do not feel the need to spend more than they can
          afford on wedding celebrations. One common theme that emerged was the sharing of produce and
          resources with fellow villagers, with ‘collective attachment’ to the landscape a necessity of
          sustainable, remote rural lifestyles. Several informants said they thought of their neighbors as
          relatives who they would provide resources to and undertake work for free of charge.

12.9      PREDICTED FUTURE BASELINE CONDITIONS
          ARCHAEOLOGICAL, HISTORICAL AND PALEONTOLOGICAL RESOURCES
12.9.1    In the absence of the Project, it is likely that the archaeological, historical and paleontological
          resources within the AoI would continue in their existing condition, though potentially subject to
          pressures through localized settlement growth, agriculture, and environmental degradation (erosion,
          flooding etc.).
12.9.2    As a result of continued Project progress, the Rogun dam will be fully constructed, and the reservoir
          fully inundated by 2038. The entirety of the flooding zone will be cleared, inundated and therefore,
          effectively archaeologically sterilized as a result of Project.
12.9.3    Archaeological remains on the upper terraces (above the maximum inundation zone) may survive
          but are likely to be at a greater risk of damage and / or complete loss as a result of erosion and
          landslip caused by seasonal change in the reservoir’s water level.
          CULTURAL AND SACRED RESOURCES
12.9.4    In the absence of the Project, it is likely that (tangible) cultural and sacred resources within the AoI
          would continue in their existing condition, though potentially subject to pressures through localized
          settlement growth, agriculture and environmental degradation (erosion, flooding etc.).
12.9.5    All directly affected villages (within the inundation zone) are due to be resettled by 2032, meaning
          there is no future baseline for the majority of cultural and sacred sources, as these villages will
          cease to exist. However, cultural sites and areas in proximity to, but not within, the flooding zone
          (i.e., in the indirect area of impact) may experience loss of access, change in setting, changing
          demographics, and tourist influx.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 308 of 371
12.9.6   In the absence of the Project, it is likely that any cultural landscape features would continue in their
         existing condition. As a result of the Project, by 2038 the local landscape and sense of place will be
         fundamentally and permanently altered. 170 km² will be inundated and current views of the valley
         floor will be entirely lost and replaced with views of a significant body of water. This change will be
         gradual rather than sudden. The upper viewshed in key views (i.e., views of mountain peaks) will
         endure. Increased tourism and recreational activity in the area (e.g., people visiting the dam and
         partaking in water sports on the reservoir) may have an effect on the sense of place.
         INTANGIBLE CULTURAL HERITAGE
12.9.7   In the absence of the Project, it is likely that the intangible cultural heritage resources within the AoI
         would continue in their existing condition, although it is recognized that some adjustments in cultural
         behaviors and social organization are inevitable over time.
12.9.8   All villages within the inundation zone are due to be resettled by 2032. Given that intangible heritage
         inherently tied to the people and villages being resettled, there is limited future baseline to consider.
         However, sites and areas of traditional practice in proximity to, but not directly within the flooding
         zone may be affected by a loss of access, change in setting, demographics and tourist influx.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 309 of 371
13       MATERIALS AND WASTE

13.1     INTRODUCTION
13.1.1   This section on existing waste management infrastructure in Tajikistan has been drawn from a range
         of secondary baseline data sources.
13.1.2   Information on current Project waste management practices has been collated through review of
         existing Project reports, data provided by Project HSE personnel and WSP site visits undertaken
         during 2023-2024.

13.2     MATERIALS
13.2.1   The current land use in the AoI comprises predominantly mountainous, low-productivity grazing
         pasture. A small minority of land is floodplain, forest and scrub. On the Project site, existing assets
         comprise a reservoir, an underground substation, a network of dirt roads, various plant and
         monitoring and control equipment, borrow pits, tunnels and spillways. The construction, operation
         and maintenance of these assets will require the consumption of a number of specialist components
         and equipment, as well as some bulk products for repairs.
13.2.2   A preliminary overview indicates that there is high regional and national availability of construction
         materials from quarries on site and around in Tajikistan. The construction industry of the country has
         a significant mineral resource base for the production of bricks, inert materials, expanded clay,
         aggloporite, gypsum, cladding, sawing and quarry stone, lime, cement and other construction
         materials. It is noteworthy that in recent years, Tajikistan has become a net exporter of cement to
         markets such as Russia, China, Kazakhstan, Turkey and European countries (Embassy of the
         Republic of Tajikistan in the Federal Republic of Germany, 2021).
13.2.3   Table 13-1 sets out the gross volumes of the production of industrial mineral production between
         2015 and 2019.

         BORROW PITS AND QUARRIES
13.2.5   There are a number of borrow pits and quarries on Site which provide materials for the construction
         of the Project. These are set out in Table 3-10 of Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA.

13.3     WASTE
         EXISTING WASTE MANAGEMENT INFRASTRUCTURE IN TAJIKISTAN
13.3.1   According to the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP), waste management (including
         waste from mining, industry, medicine, households and other municipal sources) is one of
         Tajikistan’s biggest environmental challenges (UNEP, 2019). The current waste management
         system in Tajikistan relies heavily on landfilling and faces several challenges, such as insufficient
         landfill capacity and the lack of any centralized method for separating or composting for recycling in
         the country. According to the World Bank, access to improved drinking water sources and to
         sanitation connected to a functioning sewage system, are among the most severely limited and
         unequally distributed services in the country. Tajikistan has abundant freshwater resources,
         however outside Dushanbe, the availability and quality of water supply, sanitation and hygiene
         (WASH) services remain poor (World Bank Group, 2017).
13.3.2   The average waste generation per capita is 0.89 kg per day which equates to around 2.6 million
         tons annually across the country. There are approximately 70 official municipal solid waste landfills
         in Tajikistan, which hold about 12 million tons of waste and cover 296 ha (Boboeva, 2015). The
         average current deposition in these landfills is estimated to be 4 tons of solid waste per m2 (United
         Nations Economic Commission for Europe (UNECE), 2017). The per capita generation of municipal
         solid waste varies from city to city. Waste generation rates are higher in urban areas. Rural areas
         have lower waste generation rates, on average, as rural area residents are typically less affluent,
         have higher levels of recycling, reuse and composting, and purchase fewer items from stores. In
         Dushanbe approximately 1,200,000 m3 of municipal waste is generated every year, which is about
         218,000 tons and only about 50% of that is dumped at official, regulated landfills. The rest of the
         waste goes to illegal landfills (Boboeva, 2015).
13.3.3   Municipal solid waste in Tajikistan typically consists of food waste, leather, resins, plastics, metals,
         paper and cardboard, glass, ceramics, leaves and ash. Municipal waste is neither sorted nor treated
         in the country. Due to a shortage of trucks, the collection of municipal waste is poor even in urban
         areas. Waste is often disposed of illegally in the countryside and in parts of the cities (Boboeva,
         2015). National data on collected municipal solid waste are typically in m3, but individual operators
         tend to collect waste data in tons. Rural populations often dispose of waste by dumping or burying it
         in gardens (confirmed by site visit discussions).
13.3.4   According to research undertaken by UNECE in 2016, collection was only provided to 38.25 % of
         Tajikistan’s population. Modernization of waste services is required to increase the share of the
         serviced population and develop adequate disposal capacity. Municipal waste is collected from
         designated places, which may be equipped with containers. In some cases, waste is dumped on the
         ground or in large containers (skips) located at the entrances to houses, and a front loader is used
         to transfer the waste to a collection truck. Existing disposal sites at regional centers are overfilled
         and there is an urgent need to start developing a national network of landfills (UNECE, 2017).
13.3.5   According to Boboeva (2015), none of the landfills in Tajikistan meet current international standards.
         At the time of writing this information could be out of date as the Kurugsoy landfill at Tursonzoda


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 311 of 371
         (135km west of the Project Site) was observed by WSP colleagues to meet international standards.
         This 2015 study identified that waste separation had not yet commenced, although some progress
         had been made, as the collection of fluorescent lamps had started at this time. The 2015 research
         also revealed that Tajikistan generally lacks recycling infrastructure, except for recycling of scrap
         metals and paper. According to the UNECE’s Environmental Performance Review of Tajikistan
         (UNECE, 2017), Tajikistan does not have a national waste management strategy or action plan for
         waste management, however such a strategy is currently under preparation. The country also lacks
         waste management plans for regions, municipalities and individual waste generators.
13.3.6   The Vahdat landfill, which services Dushanbe and the surrounding area, was visited by WSP during
         a September 2024 site visit. The site has been in use since the Soviet era and was found to be in a
         very poor condition. Unsorted waste was dumped on bare ground (no lining was visible), where it
         was observed to be contaminating soils, and leachate was flowing into a nearby watercourse. The
         waste was affected by extensive fires generating large volumes of fumes, creating a respiratory
         hazard for workers, who were not observed to be wearing PPE and residents of local communities’
         houses were around 100m away from the landfill boundary.
13.3.7   The Rogun landfill was observed to be in a similarly poor condition. It is located on a steep hillside
         approximately 4km outside of Rogun city. Waste was observed to be dumped on the ground on the
         hill side and uncontained. There were no signs of containment in the form of bunds or lining. Waste
         was seen to be loosely deposited and partially cascading down the hillside into the valley below
         where a stream flowed through during wet periods.
13.3.8   Table 13-2 sets out design measures from the IFC guidance and whether they were observed as
         part of the design of the Rogun and Vahdat landfills by WSP staff during their site visits in
         September 2024. This table is inclusive of the list of design measures which WSP were able to
         ascertain during the site visits. It is not an exhaustive list of all in the IFC guidance.

13.3.9    In general, waste management remains an issue at the site. Although there have been some
          improvements by some Contractors waste management in some locations as a result of the
          increased attention to environmental matters, waste management has not changed materially. Site
          waste framework has been prepared, and contractors will subsequently prepare and implement strict
          waste management practices upon World Bank effectiveness
          HAZARDOUS AND MEDICAL WASTE
13.3.10   According to the UNECE (2017), there is little information on industrial/hazardous waste, because
          regular reporting is not carried out. Industrial enterprises and organizations, based on agreement
          with the road maintenance units, transport their waste to the municipal disposal sites, where it is
          disposed of together with municipal waste. CEP has begun to make an inventory of disposal sites
          used for industrial waste.
13.3.11   Inadequate regulation and management in the country mean that hazardous chemicals pose a
          considerable threat to the environment and public health. According to UNEP, there is little data
          available on chemical incidents or the number of chemical-induced poisonings. Obsolete and
          banned pesticide stockpiles also represent an increasing environmental hazard.
13.3.12   Understanding of hazardous waste is mostly limited to radioactive waste and pesticides. These
          types of waste are a priority at present and, once their situation has been improved, it is expected
          that progress will be achieved by defining an approach to waste with other hazardous properties.
          Medical waste management is improving under the influence of donor funded projects. However old
          management practices continue. Experience gained in pilot projects is not evaluated and good
          practice is not extended to all hospitals. No healthcare waste management strategy has been
          developed to manage this type of waste. According to the IHPA, there is a lack of adequate waste
          disposal facilities in the country. Rules and regulations for the construction and operation of landfills
          are not in place.
13.3.13   There is a lot of uncontrolled waste dumping, even along riverbanks, which directly affects water
          quality. There is no legislation regarding waste incineration and there are no special facilities for
          disposal of toxic and hazardous waste (IHPA, 2017). In 2016, Tajikistan acceded to the 1989 Basel
          Convention on Transboundary Movements of Hazardous Wastes and their Disposal. Participation in


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 318 of 371
          this treaty will increase the protection of the country from unregistered import of hazardous waste
          and will improve access to facilities for disposal of hazardous waste abroad. The country is not yet a
          party to the 1998 Rotterdam Convention on the Prior Informed Consent Procedure for Certain
          Hazardous Chemicals and Pesticides in International Trade and the 2013 Minamata Convention on
          Mercury (UNECE, 2017).
13.3.14   Over the coming years Tajikistan will establish an inter-ministerial chemicals management
          committee to strengthen the coordination of activities between governmental agencies responsible
          for chemicals and waste management at the national, regional and local levels, and improve the
          involvement of private and non-profit sectors working on chemicals and waste. Tajikistan will also
          establish a technical and legal working group to identify the gaps in national legislation that have
          prevented the country from accessing the Rotterdam and Minamata conventions. This group will
          then draft the necessary legal instruments and documents to become a party to these international
          treaties. To improve the implementation of the Stockholm and Basel conventions, Tajikistan will also
          create a national tool for information exchange on sound chemicals and waste management. This
          will further improve interagency cooperation among relevant government ministries, as well as
          increased cooperation with the private and non-profit sectors.
13.3.15   Tajikistan will also launch an awareness-raising program to strengthen knowledge and skills within
          government ministries and agencies, to help embed these issues into national development plans
          and policies. Training and roundtable discussions will also take place with private sector
          representatives and civil society groups (UN Environment Programme (UNEP), 2019).
          CURRENT PROJECT WASTE MANAGEMENT PRACTICES
          CONSTRUCTION WORKS
13.3.16   The construction works are being undertaken according to four main work packages. These are set
          out in Table 3-3 in Volume 1, Chapter 3: Project Description of this ESIA. The Project does not
          hold a site-wide waste inventory. This section provides summaries of the types and typical quantities
          of waste generated by the main Project contractors, as provided to WSP by the contractor HSE
          leads. Photographs and descriptions of on-site waste storage areas were provided to WSP, with
          further information and photographs obtained during WSP site visits during August – September
          2023.
13.3.17   At the time of compiling this report, WSP had been informed that disposal sites being used for
          general/domestic waste include Rogun municipal landfill as well as Vahdat municipal waste facility. lt
          is WSP’s understanding that waste removal contractors used for general waste include Rogun
          Municipal Services and PE Asoev.
13.3.18   With respect to hazardous waste, this is mostly being collected from site by waste contractor
          Yokubjon 2016 LLC. It has been confirmed that Lot 2 (WeBuild) hazardous waste is being disposed
          of at a waste facility in Dushanbe; it is not universally understood whether all other hazardous waste
          from site is also being disposed of at this same facility. Details of facilities being used for recycling of
          materials (such as metals) have not been confirmed – it has not been established if any wastes are
          being exported for recycling.
13.3.19   During site interviews with Project contractor HSE leads in August 2023, it was confirmed that waste
          contractors and disposal/recycling facilities currently being used have not been audited by Project




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                    PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                   SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page 319 of 371
          personnel to ensure that they are operating to acceptable standards and that the various waste
          streams are being disposed of appropriately.
13.3.20   It has also not been confirmed if all necessary waste-related permits and licenses are in place.
          Lot 1 Construction – Voith Hydro GmbH & Co. Contractor
13.3.21   Voith Hydro are scheduled to mobilize to site during 2024 to start work on construction of the new
          turbines and refurbish the existing ones.
13.3.22   A Voith Hydro HSE policy and draft HSE Plan (both dated April 2019) have been provided to WSP
          for review. The table of contents in the HSE plan indicates a Waste Management Plan should be
          attached as an Annex – this was not included in the version of the document reviewed by WSP.
          Lot 2 Construction – WeBuild Contractor
13.3.23   Facilities managed by the contractor WeBuild include a tank farm, maintenance yard and garage,
          warehouses, crushed-ore storage, and small-scale wastewater treatment plant (WWTP). The
          WWTP is further discussed in the Wastewater section of this Annex.
13.3.24   The Lot 2 contractor has a Waste Management Plan (WMP) (2022) (for hazardous and non-
          hazardous wastes) and a Water and Wastewater Monitoring and Management Plan (WWMMP)
          (2022) in place.
13.3.25   Waste streams generated by WeBuild include domestic/general waste (food, plastics, metal etc.),
          hazardous wastes (chemicals, used oil, batteries, etc.) and medical waste.
13.3.26   Information on types of waste generated, quantities, disposal methods and waste disposal
          companies used, has been provided to WSP (Table 13-3) by the Lot 2 Contractor.
13.3.27   According to the information provided, WeBuild has commissioned waste company PE Asoev to
          collect domestic waste to be disposed of at a landfill site in Vahdat.
13.3.28   Waste contractor Yokubjon 2016 LLC has been commissioned for the removal of hazardous waste,
          to be disposed of at a facility in Dushanbe. information about the Dushanbe waste site, including
          whether it is licensed or is suitable for receiving hazardous wastes is not universally understood.
          WSP has been informed that batteries collected from site are transported to Dushanbe for recycling
          of metals, however details of the location have not been confirmed. According to information initially
          provided to WSP, medical waste is transported to Obigarm Hospital for incineration. This information
          may, however, be outdated, as WSP has subsequently been informed that medical waste generated
          elsewhere on site (i.e. at the HPP central clinic) is currently being taken to a new incineration facility
          at Rogun Hospital, which has been redeveloped to international standards.
13.3.29   This is further discussed in the Medical Waste section. Some of the information provided and
          presented in Table 13-3 (in particular with respect to waste disposal locations and waste service
          providers being used) does not align with information in WeBuild’s Waste Management Plan (for
          example, the WMP states that domestic waste is uplifted by Rogun municipal service for disposal at
          Rogun landfill).

13.3.30   Photographs of WeBuild waste collection and storage facilities are presented in Figure 13-2 to
          Figure 13-7.
13.3.31   The domestic waste storage area shown in Figure 13-4 is constructed of concrete brick, fitted with
          concrete flooring, however there is no roofing, and hence no protection from rain, wind or snow. The
          waste requires containment so that it does not get dispersed across the site. Recyclable materials
          were observed in this area.
13.3.32   Hazardous waste is stored in the workshop service area prior to uplift by the waste contractor. The
          storage area is constructed from concrete and is covered with corrugated roofing. Waste is stored
          within open-top intermediate bulk containers (IBCs) or on top of wooden pallets within the concrete
          cubicles. Signs and labelling of waste are noted within the storage area, which is noted to have
          appropriate controlled access.

13.3.33   During the September 2023 WSP site visit, numerous drums of waste bitumen were observed in an
          area adjacent to the WeBuild asphalt plant (Figure 13-6). The drums are stored in an area covered
          in concrete hard standing. WSP were informed during the site visit that WeBuild are in the process
          of making arrangements for off-site disposal of this waste. The Project is to ensure that the material
          is disposed of at an appropriate and licensed waste facility which is operating to acceptable
          standards. Chain of custody documentation is to be completed and kept on file.

          Lot 3 Construction – TajikGidroElectroMontazh (TGEM) Contractor
13.3.34   Types of waste produced by the contractor TGEM include domestic/general waste, packaging,
          hazardous and construction waste, sewage and batching plant wastewater. Typical quantities of
          waste produced on a monthly basis are presented in Table 13-4. (At the time of compiling this
          report, it had not been confirmed when the data presented in Table 13-4 was generated, and if it
          represents current waste outputs).
13.3.35   According to information provided by TGEM, septic tanks are installed on site for the collection of
          sewage waste. Sewage waste is removed from site by Rogun municipal services. Wastewater from
          the Batching Plant at the TGEM facility is initially placed in settling tanks and after settling, ‘is
          removed and placed in internal dumps’. At the time of compiling this report, it had not been
          confirmed if there is yet another step in the wastewater disposal process. The majority of the
          domestic waste and packaging waste is collected by Rogun municipal services to be disposed of at
          Rogun landfill site. Metals are removed from site by LLC Yokubjon 2016, to be recycled, however
          the locations and means of removing wastes from storage areas are not universally understood.
13.3.36   Information provided indicates that hazardous wastes such as batteries, used oil and oil filters are
          collected by waste contractor LLC Yokubjon 2016 for reuse (recycling of metals, in the case of the
          batteries) or disposal, at a waste facility in Dushanbe. Information on the location of this facility or its
          capability in managing such waste to international standards are not universally known. The Oil-
          contaminated rags (which should be classified as hazardous) are being collected by Rogun
          municipal services for disposal at the Rogun municipal landfill site. All oil-contaminated waste must
          be disposed of at an approved hazardous waste facility. Construction wastes such as concrete,
          metal and wood are recycled or re-used; other components such as sand and excavated rock are
          stockpiled on the Project site.

13.3.37   Photographs of the domestic waste and un-contaminated construction waste storage areas used by
          TGEM are presented in Figure 13-7 and Figure 13-8, respectively.
13.3.38   Domestic waste is stored in metal skips, which do not appear to be covered or equipped with lids to
          protect the contents from rain, wind and snow.
13.3.39   According to information provided to WSP, the storage area shown in Figure 13-8 is for un-
          contaminated construction waste, however the photograph indicates that there may be hydrocarbon
          staining within the concrete cubicles. The storage area is not fitted with containment, as such


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                  SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 328 of 371
          contamination of the surrounding environment could occur, particularly during rain events.
          Hazardous wastes should be stored in suitable UN-rated drums (or equivalents) in bunded areas.
13.3.40   According to TGEM, hazardous waste is stored in metal barrels fitted with lids, which in turn are kept
          inside larger containers. Photographs of Rogun municipal landfill site are presented in Figure 13-7.
          Based on anecdotal descriptions provided as well as the photographic evidence, this facility does
          not appear to be an engineered landfill. WSP has been informed, anecdotally, that it was initially
          constructed simply as a deep excavation which is now full of waste, and as such waste material is
          not contained but is spreading around the site, and potentially beyond. A lack of appropriate design
          and construction means that there is potential for leachate generation and contamination of
          groundwater. There is also potential for fire risks due to possible production of methane. Anecdotal
          descriptions of some of the materials being disposed of at this site include plastic wastes and piles
          of 20L buckets, potentially disused paint containers. Any empty containers contaminated with
          chemicals, such as paint, would constitute hazardous waste, and must be disposed of as such at an
          appropriate hazardous waste facility.

          Summary Overview of Contractor Facilities and Workshops
13.3.47   During the September 2023 site visit, WSP observed significant differences between the waste
          management measures implemented by the various contractors on site, ranging from considered
          bunded segregation to general waste areas with no discernible segregation (Figure 13-9). WSP was
          informed during the site visit that some waste storage areas (and areas with abandoned equipment)
          belong to contractors who have not operated on site for at least five years.
13.3.48   WSP were also informed that a number of the waste areas would only be cleared, on completion of
          the contractors’ works, with a couple of contractors stating it was the responsibility of Rogun HPP to
          remove the waste. Hence, this contradicted discussions held with the Rogun HPP team.

          MEDICAL WASTE
13.3.49   Medical waste generated at the HPP central clinic (which had previously been transferred to
          Obigarm Hospital for incineration) is now being taken to a new incineration facility at Rogun Hospital.
          WSP has been informed anecdotally that the Obigarm facility is not adequately equipped to process
          medical waste appropriately.
13.3.50   According to information provided, a new medical waste incineration facility has recently been
          installed at Rogun Hospital. WSP has been informed it has 40 years’ capacity with 1m3 capacity,
          with burn temperature of 700-1000 °C. Waste from the Project site is delivered to the facility three to
          four times a week, and incineration is carried out every day or two. The incinerator is fitted with a 3-4
          m emissions stack. According to information provided, the Project contributes ‘only a small portion of
          the total waste received by the facility’.
13.3.51   WSP was informed anecdotally that medical waste stockpiled at the Rogun hospital facility, awaiting
          incineration, is not stored securely but is potentially accessible to unauthorized personnel. A ‘city
          truck’ with a load of medical waste for incineration has been observed adjacent to the facility, in an
          area which is not secure, with waste (including syringes) visible through clear waste bags.

          During the September 2023 site visit, WSP visited three ‘dump’ sites, two of which are located within

          blackened, stagnant leachate leaching emanating from some of the waste stockpiled in this southern

          SUBSTATIONS AND TRANSFORMERS
13.3.59   During the September 2023 site visit, a number of transformers and sub-stations were noted around
          the Project site. WSP did not visit or inspect any of the transformers or equipment, however, based
          on the age of the Project it is likely that transformer oil containing Polychlorinated Biphenyls (PCBs)
          was utilized historically onsite and may still be present within some of the legacy equipment.
13.3.60   During on-site discussion with a representative from the utility company onsite, the oil is tested
          before decommissioning to assess whether the oil is suitable for re-use or disposal off site.
13.3.61   A Hazardous Waste Management Framework was prepared as part of the ESMP. Each Contractor
          employed at Rogun HPP will prepare and implement a Hazardous Materials Management Plan
          (HMMP) that is consistent with this Framework, including PCB and other similar hazardous
          chemicals.
          ASBESTOS
13.3.62   During the September 2023 site visit, possible Asbestos Containing Materials (ACMs) in the form of
          corrugated asbestos sheeting were noted on several buildings within Construction Area 1.
13.3.63   Possible asbestos lagging was noted among the surficial debris and waste within the ‘Cecharo’
          Demolition, however, due to the potential hazardous, the area was not further inspected.

          WASTEWATER MANAGEMENT
13.3.64   Two types of wastewater, namely domestic wastewater (sewage) and industrial wastewater (i.e.
          from construction processes), are being generated on site. The bulk of the industrial wastewater is
          generated through concrete batching works.
          Information provided by Site
          According to information provided by site HSE representatives, the following wastewater facilities
          (refer to photographs Figure 13-16 to Figure 13-23 are being used on site:
             WeBuild Accommodation Camp - sewage waste from the camp (approximately 300 personnel) is
              held in a septic tank (capacity 6 m3 /day). The effluent passes through a screen at an inlet station
              and then undergoes chlorination treatment. The waste liquid is then pumped to the site WWTP.


          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                SEPTEMBER 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                          Page 340 of 371
    Sewage sludge from the septic tank is removed twice a year by a licensed waste contractor.
    Bacteriological testing of the liquid is carried out on an ad hoc basis. Individuals responsible for
    treating effluent from the WWTP are not known.
   WeBuild Main Office - sewage waste from the office (approximately 250 people) is held in a
    septic tank (capacity 5m3/day). According to information provided by site, the liquid component is
    discharged to an area of open ground just outside the perimeter fence (see Figure 13-18 and
    Figure 13-19). Sewage sludge from the tank is removed twice a year by the same waste
    contractor used to dispose of sludge from the WeBuild Accommodation Camp septic tank.
   Construction Area 5 - according to information provided, this Area (with approximately 250
    personnel) is equipped with a septic tank for collecting sewage waste. As with the set-up at the
    WeBuild Accommodation Camp, effluent passes through a screen at an inlet station and then
    undergoes chlorination treatment. The liquid is pumped to the site WWTP. Sewage sludge from
    the septic tank is removed twice a year by a waste contractor. Bacteriological testing of the liquid
    is reportedly carried out on an ad hoc basis.
   Rogun Permanent Camp – a portable facility (Fraccaroli & Balzan automatic double filtering
    treatment unit using quartzite and activated carbon) is in operation. Confirmation of the types of
    wastewater this facility is used to treat and where the water is ultimately discharged to, was not
    obtained.
   ‘Septic tanks’ are reportedly installed in other areas of the Project site including at the Lot 3 and
    Lot 4 early works construction areas. WSP was informed that sewage waste from Lot 3 is
    collected by Rogun municipal services and Lot 4 early works sewage waste is disposed of into
    the site WWTP. WSP has been informed that wastewater from the Batching Plant at the TGEM
    facility is initially placed in settling tanks and after settling, ‘is removed and placed in internal
    dumps’. It has not been confirmed what happens with the wastewater following transfer to the
    ‘internal dumps’.

13.3.65   During the WSP September 2023 site visit, a walkover of wastewater management facilities was

          Sewage
          Construction Site 1
13.3.66   The sewage effluent arrangements in place are not universally known, with contradictory information
          seemingly provided by Rogun HPP and the contractors on site.
          Construction Site 1
13.3.67   A sewage WWTP is located in Construction Site 1 Figure 13-20. The works currently consist of
          three settlement ponds with chlorine manual dosing into one of the ponds (It should be noted that
          WSP were not able to witness or confirm the dosing during the site visit). At the time of the visit, a
          new settlement pond was in the process of being excavated to the east of the ‘WWTP laboratory
          building’. WSP did not enter the building, however from the outside it appeared to be derelict.
13.3.68   Unknown effluent appeared to be bypassing the settlement ponds. The origin of the effluent could
          not be established during the site visit, however it appeared to be entering the area from multiple
          upgradient directions. No solid wastes were observed either within the settlement ponds or in the
          surrounding effluent. It was noted that effluent from the upper most settlement pond was overflowing
          and combining with the known effluent - this effluent was followed down gradient where it discharges
          into the dam. WSP were informed anecdotally that the sewage solid wastes are ‘collected’ at the
          contractors’ compounds / facilities and only the liquid effluent is piped to the sewage WWTP.

          Remote Contractors’ Compounds and Workshops
13.3.69   Across the wider site away from Construction Area 1, different contractors anecdotally utilized septic
          tanks to collect sewage. However, further discussions within the contractors indicated the liquid
          drained straight to ground and the solids were collected / disposed of (WSP were not able to verify

          witnessed for water runoff at a number of compounds (see section, Workshop Effluent & Runoff).
          Workshop Effluent & Runoff
13.3.70   The majority of the workshops visited by WSP during the September 2023 site visit, had considered
          water runoff from workshop areas, with collection channels noted on the majority of the sites.
          However, once collected only one contractor had sufficient infrastructure to collect and treat the
          runoff. A number of contractors had excavated ‘septic tanks’ adjacent to the workshop areas. It
          should be noted that these ‘septic tanks’ were not contained and drained straight to ground with no
          collection or treatment witnessed. One ‘septic tank’ had visible Light Non-Aqueous Phase Liquid
          (LNAPL) (most likely engine oil) on the surface of the ponded water.

          Washout Areas
13.3.71   During the September 2023 site visit, the majority of the contractors spoken to seemed to be
          unaware of the polluting nature of concrete wash waters i.e., high pH (11-12) due to calcium
          hydroxide (derived from the cement); high suspended solids; and other trace materials (originating
          from both cement and from additives).
13.3.72   During the September 2023 site visit, WSP witnessed across the site, a number of different setups
          and practices with respect to the ‘washing out’ of cement mixers, ranging from well-considered wash
          down areas that drained into settlement pits with subsequent water treatment, to washout areas with
          no control, capture or treatment of the washout water. The latter was the more common practice
          witnessed across the site.
13.3.73   A number of areas appeared to drain directly into the dam or river, with visible build-up of cement
          down gradient of the washdown areas (Figure 13-23). When discussed with the contractors /
          operators, they referred to surface water monitoring undertaken by another party and that if there
          was a significant environmental impact, Rogun HPP would have halted their practices.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |

          and or procedures.

          (including tank farms) had been relocated either within the Rogun HPP project or from other
          projects, with a number of tanks seemingly decades old.

          RESETTLED COMMUNITIES
13.3.80   As discussed in Chapter 3: Project Description, resettlement is managed and funded by the
          Directorate of the Rogun HPP Flooding Zone (DFZ), it is understood resettlement will be partly
          funded by the Lenders and as such is considered a core Project component.
13.3.81   Resettlement is planned in phases in line with the inundation levels of the reservoir. According to
          data from DFZ as of March 2023, resettlement planning for the Project is due for completion by
          2032. It covers 69 villages from the Rogun, Nurobod, and Rasht districts affecting more than 50,000
          Project Affected People (PAPs).
13.3.82   The Resolution of the Government of Tajikistan of January 20, 2009, No. 47, “On the Resettlement
          from the Flood Zone of Rogun HPP of the Population of Rogun City and Nurobod District” (amended
          in 2011 and 2015), governs the resettlement of the population of Rogun Town and Nurobod Rayon
          (District) within the Rogun HPP flooding zones. In 2024, Resolution No. 47 was updated to include
          the areas of the flooding zone within Rasht.
13.3.83   DFZ are responsible for managing resettlement and provision of community infrastructure, including
          connecting newly built houses to electricity, water, sewage systems, etc. A summary of waste
          services provided in Darband and a series of photographs (Figure 13-27 to Figure 13-33) have
          been provided by the DFZ. An overview of waste management facilities available in all the resettled
          villages is not yet available.
13.3.84   According to the DFZ, there are currently no general (domestic) waste removal contractors in the
          Nurobod District. General waste collection points have been established in five micro districts within
          the city of Darband (Nurobod District) by the DFZ. The collection points are equipped with metal
          skips for collecting household waste from local communities. Employees of the Housing and
          Communal Services of the Nurobod Region collect the waste from these points on a daily basis and
          transport it by truck to a designated location approximately 6km away, at an elevation of
          approximately 1400 a msl. According to the DFZ, this is in line with the “General Plan for the
          Placement of Household Land Plots in the City of Darband”.
13.3.85   The photographs of the waste collection points indicate that the waste skips are not equipped with
          lids/covers to protect the contents from wind, rain or snow. At some of the points, waste is noted on
          the surrounding ground, and at one of the collection points there is only one skip provided – plastic
          bags of rubbish on the ground beside the skip suggest that additional skips are needed at this
          collection point. Photographs of the trucks being used to remove waste from the collection points
          indicate that the vehicles are not fitted with tailgates or covers/sheeting, suggesting that they are
          inadequately equipped to contain the waste during transportation and offer no protection from wind
          and rain. The photographs provided also suggest that the general waste is not being disposed of at
          a licensed landfill site but is being deposited at an informal landfill / open ground. DFZ have
          confirmed that a sewage wastewater treatment plant (WWTP) has been installed in Darband. It is
