14       TRAFFIC AND TRANSPORT

14.1     INTRODUCTION
14.1.1   This section presents the baseline data for the Traffic and Transport Assessment for the Project.
         The information presented here is to be read in conjunction with Volume 1, Chapter 22: Traffic and
         Transport.
14.1.2   No primary transport baseline data collection was undertaken for the 2014 ESIA. The baseline
         conditions at the site reflect existing operations plus the construction work that is ongoing.
14.1.3   Secondary baseline data sources have been utilized through desk-based studies to identify key
         issues based on existing maps, previous studies, satellite imagery and other relevant material.

14.2     TAJIKISTAN TRANSPORT NETWORK
         BACKGROUND
14.2.1   Tajikistan is a landlocked and mountainous country, surrounded by Afghanistan, the People’s
         Republic of China (PRC), Kyrgyzstan, and Uzbekistan. Approximately 93% of Tajikistan’s terrain is
         mountainous, complicating transport and transit. Most of the transport infrastructure was built during
         the Soviet period and was designed to optimize the Soviet system of inter-republic linkages between
         other Soviet republics. After Tajikistan became independent in 1991, relationships between
         Tajikistan and the neighboring countries changed and the Tajik Civil War began, affecting the
         transport networks and reducing inter-country traffic volumes and trade.
14.2.2   As a result, passenger transport reduced by three quarters, freight transport by two thirds, and
         freight turnover by three quarters during 1990–1996. As such, government funding for the transport
         sector was significantly reduced which has since resulted in the deterioration of the nation’s
         transport infrastructure due to lack of maintenance (ADB, 2021).
         NATIONAL ROAD NETWORK
14.2.3   The dominant form of transport infrastructure in Tajikistan is roads, which play a key role in
         facilitating domestic and international connectivity. Road connectivity is oriented mainly toward
         Uzbekistan, accounting for more than half of the main road border crossing points (BCPs), as well
         as towards Kyrgyzstan through several BCPs. The nearest seaport to Tajikistan is Bandar Abbas,
         Iran (approximately 2,500km from Dushanbe, a 35-hour drive) which can be accessed through
         Afghanistan, but the available transport modes to reach the port are limited, and recent political
         stability and violence in Afghanistan makes transit through the country difficult.
14.2.4   The ADB 2021 transport sector assessment indicates that around 56% of Tajikistan’s roads are in
         poor condition, particularly smaller local roads (ADB, 2021).
         PUBLIC TRANSPORT
14.2.5   Public transport exists in cities in Tajikistan. In Dushanbe, buses and white vans (marshrutkas) with
         line numbers run to most destinations within Dushanbe. A refurbished Grand Bus Station opened in
         Dushanbe in 2013 which serves passengers who are travelling to suburban areas, other Tajik cities
         such as Bokhtar, Khujand and Khorog, and international destinations such as Termez (Uzbekistan),
         Osh (Kyrgyzstan) and Moscow (Russia).



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                              SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                        Page 353 of 371
14.2.6   However, aside from this, rural Tajikistan does not benefit from an extensive public transportation
         network. No buses or trains connect rural suburbs with urban centers. The main form of transport is
         by car. Rural residents travel either in their own private vehicles or participate in a taxi-sharing
         system with their neighbors in order to travel to local and regional centers. Some marshrutkas are
         also available.

14.3     ROADS IN PROXIMITY TO THE PROJECT
         PAMIR HIGHWAY – (M41 / E60 / AH65)
14.3.1   The M41, more commonly referred to as the Pamir Highway, is an international road traversing the
         Pamir Mountains through Afghanistan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and Kyrgyzstan. In Tajikistan, the
         Pamir Highway is divided into sections IB02, RB04, RB07. The RB07 section of the Pamir Highway
         (M41) runs from border of Kyrgyzstan – Jirgital – Obigarm – Vahdat adjoining with RB04 to
         Dushanbe, passing the Rogun HPP project site at Obigarm.
14.3.2   WSP visited this section of the M41 highway in September 2024 to assess its condition. The road is
         a single carriageway highway with a single lane in either direction with bridges and tunnels to
         traverse the mountainous terrain in which it sits. The asphalt used was of good quality, is wide and
         clear, and had no obvious defects such as potholes in the approximately 50km stretch assessed by
         WSP. Construction of the road was almost complete at the time of the visit and the program was due
         to finish at the end of 2024. Final works being completed included adding bridge expansion joints to
         the bridges crossing ravines along its length, adding in road markings, and fixing safety barriers.
         WSP met with the Deputy Director of the Project Management Group for M41 (Obigarm-Nurobod)
         who stated that the road will undergo a period of stress testing to ensure any defects are identified
         and rectified before it is opened to the public in early 2025, as is currently planned.
         M41 Obigarm-Nurobod Road Realignment Project
14.3.3   The Obigarm-Nurobod road section of the existing Pamir Highway (M41) will be inundated once the
         HPP reservoir has filled to operating levels. The Obigarm-Nurobod Road Realignment Project will
         provide a 75 km bypass road. The overall project was divided into 3 sections:
           • The Obigarm–Tagi kamar section is about 30km long financed by ADB and OFID;
           • The Tagi kamar–Nurobod section is about 44km long financed by the EBRD; and
           • The 920-meter-long bridge and its approaches will be financed by AIIB.
14.3.4   These are referred to under “Section 1” and “Section 2” in Paragraph 14.3.8.
14.3.5   In 2019, an Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) was prepared for the Obigarm-
         Nurobod Road Realignment Project by the Ministry of Transport of the Republic of Tajikistan. The
         Project includes 13 new bridge constructions, rehabilitation of three existing bridges, three new
         tunnel constructions and six village access roads.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                             SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                       Page 354 of 371
         Source: Central Asia Regional Economic Cooperation Corridors 2, 3 and 5 (Obigarm-Nurobod) Road Project ESIA 2019


         Figure 14-1 - Schematic of Pamir Highway (M41) Obigarm-Nurobod Road Realignment
         (Ministry of Transport of the Republic of Tajikistan, 2019)
14.3.6   Due to the international and national importance of the highway, in providing the only major
         continuous road connection across the Pamir Mountains, it is understood the realignment needs to
         be completed by November 2025 as the existing highway section will be inundated by the reservoir
         (Tajik Ministry of Transport, 2019).
14.3.7   Two major new roads are planned for the area surrounding the reservoir:
            One 55km road along the left bank of the lower part of the reservoir, from the dam to the main
             bridge which will cross the reservoir;
            One on the right bank, upper part of the reservoir, between the bridge and the town of Gharm,
             located approximately 50 km to the north-east.
14.3.8   As shown in Figure 14-2, the following roads will be affected by the Project:
            Section 1: Main road (M41) on the right bank in the lower part of the reservoir: a considerable
             part of the realignment of this international road started in the 1980s. Construction stopped
             shortly after the independence of Tajikistan (September 1991) and the constructed sections have
             deteriorated over the last 20 years. Recently, the Research and Design Institute in Dushanbe,
             the state agency responsible for planning and construction of roads, planned to finalize
             construction of the 76 km new road alignment, which also includes rehabilitation of the damaged
             sections.
            Section 2: Main bridge over the reservoir: the relocation of the international road also includes a
             major bridge with a span of 786 m.
            Section 3: Main road on the left bank in the upper part of the reservoir: only a small section of the
             international road needs to be replaced.
            Section 4: New access road on the right bank in the upper part of the reservoir: an access road
             needs to be built because of the rugged terrain.
            Section 5: New access road on the left bank in the lower part of the reservoir: an access road is
             required because of the reservoir.
14.3.9   Sections 1, 2 and 3 are part of the national and international road Dushanbe - Vahdat - Jirgital -
         (Kyrgyzstan) and are considered associated facilities within this ESIA. An EIA was undertaken as
         part of the project to rehabilitate the international highway Vahdat – Jirgital.


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                    SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                              Page 355 of 371
14.3.10   Sections 4 and 5 are at the pre-feasibility stage of planning and will only be built as a result of the
          Project. They are included within the Project’s assessment.
          THE KAMAROB VALLEY ROAD
14.3.11   The Kamarob Valley (also known as Kamarov) is a tributary valley on the right bank of the reservoir,
          located partly within the flooding zone in Rasht District. Currently there are 21 settlements within the
          valley and its tributary valleys, 9 of which are scheduled for resettlement and 12 of which will remain.
          The whole valley is covered by the Boqi Rahimzoda jamoat, so named for the Tajik-Soviet writer
          who was born in the valley. There is one road that goes into and out of the valley. The road
          continues beyond the uppermost village, but is impassable by vehicle, and the next settlement
          (Gorif) is a 48-hour walk away.17 This road passes through the mouth of the valley and will be
          flooded. It is currently unknown if, how and when the right bank road will be connected to the
          villages remaining within the valley. The total population of the valley (including Gorif) is
          approximately 12,000, including approximately 2,000 in the non-resettled villages.
14.3.12   On-site discussions with residents in the upper valley confirmed heavy reliance on travel to
          settlements outside of the valley to meet basic necessities, particularly Gharm and Rasht City. All
          interviewed residents in September 2024 reported travelling to these settlements at least monthly,
          usually utilizing taxi-sharing arrangements with their neighbors.




          17 Reported on site by the head of B. Rahimzoda jamoat, September 2024.

14.4     ACCIDENTS AND SAFETY
14.4.1   According to 2016 WHO statistics, the country had an estimated road accident fatality rate per
         100,000 population of 13.9 in 2021. For context, this figure is 6.7 and 15 per 100,000 for Europe and
         the world respectively (WHO, 2024). Of the fatalities, the majority relate to 4-wheeler vehicles (69%),
         19% relating to pedestrians, 9% 2 to 3-wheeler vehicles, cyclists 1% and others 2%. The country
         does not comply with many UN Vehicle Safety Regulations, with the only compliance being import
         inspections.
14.4.2   Accidents are often attributed to non-compliance with traffic rules by drivers and pedestrians, illegal
         purchasing of drivers’ licenses and inadequate road infrastructure in the country. The country has
         the highest road traffic accident rate in Central Asia with the WHO requesting advancing of efforts to
         reduce rates.
14.4.3   The Project prepares quarterly OHS reports on overall OHS performance for submission to
         authorities. The reports detail excess fatalities and serious incidents, particularly in relation to traffic
         accidents. The contents of these documents are not universally known so the material of which are
         unable to be reported in this baseline annex.
14.4.4   There are some settlements within or close to the construction site, surrounded by hauling roads,
         stockpiles and other construction site structures. Mostly, access to these villages takes place across
         the site, forcing pedestrians to interfere with the heavy traffic on the roads. As outlined in
         Table 14-1, data was provided by the Ministry of Transport of accidents on highways within
         geographic areas between 2017 and 2022.

         Table 14-1 - Summary of Ministry of Transport Accident Data 2017 - 2022

         since 2017.

         INTERNAL TRAFFIC FLOWS

14.5.2   The following data has been provided:
            The daily traffic flow inside the T3 tunnel is 852 units of vehicles, and the total amount of vehicle
             traffic per month is 25,560 units.
            The daily traffic flow at the entrance and exit of the T3 tunnel is 1,908 units, and the total amount
             of vehicle traffic in a month is 57,240 units.

14.5.3   Recordings provided from a network of CCTV cameras operating at the Project site have not been
         sufficient to provide detailed traffic flows for the Site, particularly given the expectation that traffic
         flows have a significant variation over time.

14.5.4   Trucks/heavy goods vehicles (HGVs) transport building materials to site daily routing between the
         Site and Dushanbe, equating to approximately 33 trips to and 33 trips from the site.
         The baseline situation for staff working on the site is approximately up to 12,000 workers at any one
         time. Estimates of existing worker travel patterns are as follows:
            Up to 4,400 in on-site accommodation;
            1,500 live in Rogun City adjacent to the site;


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                     SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 361 of 371
            Up to 300 live outside of Rogun City within 30km of the site and are bussed to site;
            Approximately 5,000 live elsewhere in Tajikistan with a shift change every 15 days (total of
             approximately 10,000 workers across two 15-day shifts; and
            There are a range of shift patterns for employees, including day and night 15-day shifts and
             office shifts for 5.5 days per week.
         SITE ENTRANCE GATE

14.5.5   Access to the site in Rogun City is via a single access point, which is security and military controlled,
         and is situated south of the main urban area of Rogun City and provides access to the construction
         site and dam wall area.

14.5.6   As of December 2024, approximately 5,000 workers live off-site, with approximately 12,500 workers
         living on the construction site. Off-site workers generally travel to the site on foot, car, taxi or bus.
         Some contractors provide bus transport to and from off-site accommodation at the start/end of shift
         times.
         SHIFT ROTATIONS
14.5.7   Most of the workforce (excluding office workers) are on a rotational shift pattern, working 15 days on
         and off, on a 12-hour day or night shift. The majority of the workforce, especially if accommodated
         on-site, return home for their rest period. Table 14-3 presents traffic flows on 15th September 2024
         on a shift rotation day where traffic flows are assumed at peak. All figures are approximate and
         calculated using CCTV footage.

14.6     EXISTING TRANSPORT MANAGEMENT PLAN (TMP)
14.6.1   WeBuild developed a TMP for the HPP Rogun Lot 2, dated 24 May 2023. The TMP was prepared to
         manage the anticipated increase of traffic flows associated with the resumption of the main dam
         core activities. This plan serves to ensure that adequate procedures are in place to minimize effects
         due to the presence of the Project’s site vehicles on the local traffic on affected road sections. This
         plan is being implemented by the Employer for the entire Project site, Rogun JSC. Its
         implementation is supervised by the HSE team of the Employer’s Representative. However, given
         the practical issues of limited control over the entire site, the compliance is variable between the
         contractors, with Lot 2 contractors demonstrating the highest adherence to the requirements.
14.6.2   The TMP states that from September to December 2022, the site vehicles and machineries
         deployed for the works included:
            Bulldozers;
            Roller compactors;
            Tractors;
            Excavators;
            Mobile cranes;
            Dumper trucks;


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                               SEPTEMBER 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                         Page 363 of 371
            Cement mixer trucks;
            Drilling machines;
            Fuel trucks;
            Water trucks; and
            Pick-ups.
14.6.3   Out of this list, bulldozers, roller compactors, tractors, excavators, mobile cranes and drilling
         machines typically are used locally at the main dam core without the need to be transported in and
         out of the Project site frequently. The remaining site vehicles enter and exit the main core site daily.
         It is understood from the TMP that the frequency of these traffic flows will increase significantly and
         rapidly once the concreting works gain momentum.
14.6.4   The TMP identifies that based on the current/projected site conditions, accesses to the main dam
         core will be via the following routes:
            a. External site road from the entrance of Tunnel T4 passing through the downstream
             embankment work area and heading towards the downstream side of the Main Dam Core.
            b. Tunnel road from the entrance of Tunnel T4 and passing through Tunnel T2, and finally exiting
             Tunnel T2 and making a left turn at the tunnel exit heading towards the upstream side of the
             Main Dam Core. Figure 14-4 shows the two routes.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |

14.6.5   The TMP includes the following key features:
            Two-way traffic allowed as normal for routes:
             •   Tunnel T22;
             •   Site access to cofferdam upstream work site;
             •   Site access to downstream of main dam core; and
             •   Tunnel T3 from upstream tunnel entrance to junction between T3 and T5’.
            One-way traffic restriction as indicated for routes:
             •   From Tunnel T4 to Tunnel T2;
             •   From Tunnel T2 to Tunnel T5’;
             •   From Tunnel T5’ to Tunnel T3; and
             •   From Junction of T3/T5’ to T3 exit (downstream), with special exceptions as stipulated in this
                 section.

         organizational, technical and behavioral rules that are intended to control and mitigate the risks
         related to road transport activities.

       Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment   PUBLIC |

       Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun”, DFZ
10      CLIMATE CHANGE

10.1    INTRODUCTION
10.1.1. To support the Climate Risk and Vulnerability Assessment (CRVA) (presented in Volume 1,
        Chapter 10: Climate Change of this ESIA) this Annex provides a summary of previous reports
        provided to inform the assessment, and a hydropower literature review to inform the sensitivity of
        Project receptors.

10.2    PREVIOUS REPORT SUMMARY
10.2.1. A summary of the previous reports reviewed as part of the assessment are summarised below.
        These reports have been used to identify measures which have been incorporate into the existing
        and proposed Project which will reduce the vulnerability of the Project to climate change.
        Environmental and Social Impact Assessment, 2014
10.2.2. Volume I Main Report of the 2014 Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) provided
        sectoral studies of the Project, of which climate data was reported as part of the study on the
        physical environment. Section 7 of the 2014 ESIA focussed on the effects of the Project on the local
        climate, in addition to how the changes in water availability with climate change may affect the
        operation of the Hydropower Plant (HPP). Section 20 developed the climate projections for the
        project area, indicating the importance of snow and glacier melting on future water resources and
        flooding and mudflows. The hydrological and glaciological projections as part of this section assess
        the impact of the disappearance of glaciers and the water balance of the basins. It also assesses
        the impact of the Project on the local climate, and environment due to climate change, such as
        downstream sedimentation. The assessment concluded that there are no significant adverse climate
        change risk affecting the operation of Rogun HPP in terms of water availability.
10.2.3. The ESIA did not include detailed studies on changes to hydrology from climate change, including
        Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOF), changes in flows due to local glacier retreat or expansion,
        changes in sedimentation, or other effects of climate change (e.g., increasing temperatures) that
        would increase the risk to the operation of Rogun HPP.
10.2.4. An assessment of climate change and its effects on Rogun HPP was excluded from the Volume II
        technical annexes.
        Techno-economic assessment study, 2014
10.2.5. Chapter 5 of the Techno-Economic Assessment Study (TEAS) is a comprehensive review of the
        existing climate documentation to assess the quality of the data, review the inflows assessment,
        estimate flood return periods and Maximum Probability Flood, and review the existing climate
        change projections.
10.2.6. The study analysed trends in precipitation and temperature but concluded that a critical review of
        climate change models is required to reduce the uncertainties inherent in downscaled climate
        change projections, to allow for the level of accuracy required when designing HPPs. It is currently
        not accurate to understand the local impact of climate change on Rogun HPP, and therefore derived
        climate adaptation measures would be inappropriate. However, the study reiterates that adaptation
        must be incorporated into the planning of Rogun HPP. The study suggests that even a robust and



        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment               PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun”, DFZ                                                   Page | 1
        conservative design should allow some flexibility to be resilient to drastic climatic changes. Adaptive
        capacity could be built in the form of monitoring systems e.g., early warning systems to improve
        preparedness to extreme phenomena.
        Environmental and Social Panel of Experts, 2014
10.2.7. Recommendations from the Environmental and Social Panel of Experts (ESPOE) include that
        coverage of climate change and adaptation needs to be improved, using the EIB Standard 5 as a
        guide.
        Review of Environmental and Social Aspects of Rogun Hydropower Plant, 2021
10.2.8. This review concluded that the Environmental and Social assessment should consider the impacts
        of climate change such as climate change adaptation and resilience issues. It is also recommended
        that the structural design should take into account climate change considerations.
        Dam Safety Panel of Experts, Second Report, 2023
10.2.9. Recommendations from the Dam Safety Panel of Experts (DSPOE) suggests that the TEAS Climate
        change impacts and risk assessments should be updated. This includes factoring in climate change
        into Reservoir Operation Simulation Studies and flood assessments.
        Environmental and Social Panel of Experts, Second Report 2023
10.2.10. The second report from the ESPOE reiterates that resilience, vulnerability, and adaptation need to
         be addressed in line with both World Bank and EIB guidelines.
        Procurement of Update of Hydrology and Climate Change Studies 2023
10.2.11. The proposal request (reference RHPP/CS/QCBS/05) sets out the term of reference for updating the
         previous hydrology and climate change studies. Specified task within the request include for:
         The review of past work and literature pertaining to hydrology and climate change for the Rogun
          Dam project;
         Preparation of a hydrological geodatabase containing relevant GIS-related data for the Rogun
          Dam basin.
         Analysis of historical hydrometric and meteorologic data for the Rogun HPP basin including river
          flows and sediment yield. Assess the statistical significance of the results and analyse trends.
         Re-evaluate and update the TEAS flood hydrographs for the Rogun Dam Project
         Evaluate the potential impacts of Glacial Lake Outburst Floods (GLOFs) and landslide-dam-break
          events on the Rogun inflow hydrographs.
         Set up a catchment model which can simulate similar hydrologic conditions, model glacial and
          snow melt and fooding.
         Climate Change Study to identify future trend scenarios for the key drivers of hydrologic
          processes in the Rogun catchment and to estimate the likely impact on the runoff-regime, floods
          and sediment yield.
10.2.12. Whilst it is recognised that the proposal request does not provide results or conclusion of the
         requested task, it does provide evidence of the detail and extent of future studies.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
        Project No.:  | Our Ref No.:                                                                   May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun”, DFZ                                                      Page | 2
10.3    LITERATURE REVIEW SUMMARY
10.3.1. A literature review has been undertaken to identify how hydropower plants may be susceptible to
        climate change. A summary of the key findings are outlined below.
10.3.2. HPPs are sensitive to water-related climate hazards due to water stress causing changes in levels
        and load factors affecting the energy generation capacity. Other water-related climate hazards
        resulting from changing precipitation patterns and hydrological variability make the Project sensitive
        to impacts from flooding (including Glacial Lake Outburst Floods) and associated damage to dam
        components, debris and instability of slopes. In particular, Mechanical and Electrical (M&E)
        equipment, offices and roads are sensitive to the impacts of flooding1.
10.3.3. HPPs are sensitive to hazards such as precipitation, drought and wind conditions that could lead to
        soil erosion, soil degradation and potential solifluction of reservoir banks as a result of climate
        change as this will increase the sediment load from the catchment, resulting in loss of storage more
        rapidly than would occur without the increased hazard due to climate change. HPP are also
        sensitive to more acute solid-mass related climate hazards such as avalanches and landslides, as
        this could cause direct damage to dam components, particularly spillways and tunnels, and could
        cause displacement waves in the reservoir.
10.3.4. HPP are sensitive to changing wind patterns which could cause additional loading on dam structures
        or damage from storms and wind events, particularly overhead transmission lines.
10.3.5. HPPs can be sensitive to increasing temperatures, particularly transmission lines which could
        overheat. M&E equipment is typically located underground and less sensitive to overheating. Other
        components that are sensitive to high temperatures such as increased evaporation from the
        reservoir itself, issues with material durability and damage from wildfire. Staff associated with
        construction, operation and maintenance of HPPs are also sensitive to high temperatures as this
        can lead to heat stress although the fact that most workplaces will be underground will reduce the
        risk during operation. HPPs are also impacted by low temperatures and cold snaps / frost due to
        damage from snow loading.

        2023)

Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment         PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ
1.       INTRODUCTION
1.1.1.   Environmental flows, (hereinafter referred to as E-Flows), are defined as “the quantity, frequency,
         timing, and quality of water and sediment flows necessary to sustain freshwater and estuarine
         ecosystems and the human livelihoods and well-being that depend on these ecosystems” (World
         Bank, 2018). Other terms used in relation to E-Flows are Environmental Water, Instream Flow
         Requirements, Minimum Flows, Minimum Environmental Flows, Downstream Flows, Ecological
         Water Demand and Ecological Reserve (World Bank, 2018).
1.1.2.   E-Flow assessment methods have been developing for more than 30 years, with numerous
         approaches being proposed. The underlying premise of such approaches is that a “natural flow
         regime’ of a river refers to the naturally occurring changes in water flow through the course of a
         year, and from one year to the next. These fluctuations are important to the ecological health of a
         river, and are essential in maintaining the biodiversity of rivers and their floodplain wetlands (Boivin,
         2012). Managing for E-Flows simply means that enough water is left in the river to ensure
         downstream environments, social and economic benefits are maintained (Boivin, 2012; IUCN,
         2003).
1.1.2.1. The Nurek Dam and Reservoir downstream of the proposed Rogun Dam has already impacted on
         the Vaksh River. Nurek Dam was constructed from 1961 to 1980 and the creation of the 10.5 km3
         reservoir and the commissioning of the hydroelectric station occurred from 1972 to 1979. This has
         resulted in the modification of the Vaksh River flow pattern to the following effect: According to the
         Nurek Phase 2 ESIA report, (OSHC "Barqi Tojik", 2020), the downstream river has adapted to the
         altered flow regime imposed by Nurek HPP but supports relatively limited biodiversity. Barqi Tojik,
         (2017), mentions that the project has no impact to the downstream disharge at a daily time scale
         during and after rehabilitation of the Nurek Dam given the existing operational rules and principles.
         Therefore no environmental flows are required given the fact that the water discharge is not
         modified.
1.1.3.   The World Bank Group has published the Good Practice Handbook for Environmental Flows for
         Hydropower Projects as a Guidance for the Private Sector in Emerging Markets (World Bank, 2018).
1.1.4.   The publication has been designed for practitioners, to ensure a rigorous and consistent approach is
         taken when assessing hydropower project impacts when determining E-Flow commitments. The
         handbook can be divided into nine sections covering Environmental Flows, Rivers and People,
         Hydropower, The Mitigation Hierarchy, Assessment Tools and methods, the Decision Tree and
         Adaptive Management and Management Plans (World Bank, 2018).
1.1.5.   The Area of Influence (AoI) associated with the E-Flow assessment confined to the affected stretch
         of river, where discharge could temporarily be reduced to zero. This stretch is a very short 15 km
         stretch between the Rogun Dam and the upper end of Nurek reservoir at FSL. Further downstream,
         river discharge is regulated by Nurek and Rogun therefore has no direct effect further downstream.
         There are no settlements, i.e., no water users, along this 15 km stretch. The importance of this river
         stretch for fish is very limited and even if there were fish migrations from Nurek reservoir upstream,
         these would be blocked by Rogun Dam.
1.1.6.   The E-Flow for this study has been calculated using the Desktop Method and compared with
         calculated results using the Tennant method and is based on the prescribed low-resolution method
         according to the WB and EIB requirements. The low-resolution method is justified based on the


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                          May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page 1
         potential impact of minimum flows due to the Rogun operation impacting only on the 15 km river
         reach between Rogun Dam and the Nurek Dam as well as further supported by minimal flow
         impacts as experienced downstream of Nurek due to the cascade.

1.2.     PURPOSE OF THE STUDY
1.2.1.   The purpose of this study is to determine the minimum flow requirements (E-Flows) for the Vaksh
         River stretch between Rogun and Nurek. This approach will aim to provide the necessary
         information for the design team to consider E-Flows upfront, so that mitigation measures and
         recommendations can be put in place to minimize impacts on the natural habitat.
1.2.2.   Potential risks and impacts, which in Rogun’s case are minimal, should be mitigated using the
         mitigation hierarchy. The hierarchy describes a stepwise approach that illustrates the preferred
         approach to mitigating adverse impacts as follows (Figure 1-1) (BBOP, 2009; World Bank, 2018):
          The preferred mitigation measure is avoidance: this can be achieved through the careful
           placement of infrastructure, as well as the design and operation thereof.
          Then minimization: this can be achieved through the provision of E-Flows.
          Then rehabilitation or restoration: this can be achieved through improved species, habitat or
           catchment management interventions, releases for cultural and religious rituals, or relocation of
           cultural infrastructure.
          Finally, offsetting residual, unavoidable impacts: this is the final step of the mitigation hierarchy,
           whereby off-site actions, such as protection of other rivers with similar biodiversity in the same
           adjacent catchments is undertaken.
1.2.3.   These risks and mitigation are further elaborated on in Section 0.

         Figure 1-1 - Mitigation Hierarchy Adapted from Business and Biodiversity Offsetting
         Programme 2009




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                          May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                    Page 2
2.       STANDARDS AND GUIDELINES
2.1.1.   This section of the report describes relevant aspects of national and international legal and
         regulatory frameworks relevant to environmental flows. Acts and regulations related to
         environmental management and protection reviewed in the context of the implementation of the
         project are described in the sections that follow.

2.2.     WORLD BANK
2.2.1.   The Good Practice Handbook for Environmental Flows for Hydropower Projects, published by the
         World Bank Group, provides guidance to practitioners on selecting an appropriate environmental
         flows assessment level for hydropower project developments, as well as guidance on taking
         rigorous and consistent approaches to assess and manage hydropower project impacts on
         downstream river ecosystems and people, through the assessment and provision of E-Flows (World
         Bank, 2018). The Handbook defines E-Flows as,” the quantity, frequency, timing and quality if water
         and sediment flow necessary to sustain freshwater and estuarine ecosystems and the human
         livelihoods and well-being that depends on these ecosystems.”

2.3.     EUROPEAN INVESTMENT BANK (EIB) ENVIRONMENTAL, CLIMATE AND
         SOCIAL GUIDELINE ON HYDROPOWER DEVELOPMENT
2.3.1.   Chapter 3 of the European Investment Bank (EIB), Environmental, Climate and Social Guidelines on
         Hydropower Development, October 2019, deals with environmental issues and impacts. Specific
         reference is made to the EIB requirements/recommendations for Downstream Hydrology and
         Limnology as stated below verbatim.
         22.   All hydropower projects financed by the EIB must assess and make provisions for an
               appropriate downstream environmental flow release (EFR) and any additional mitigation
               measures that may be required (as a minimum these would normally include measures for fish
               passage) to maintain the status of freshwater and estuarine ecosystems and support existing
               socio-economic uses of the water resource. These measures must meet national legislative
               requirements, but in many cases will require additional features such as varying flow releases
               at different times of year in response to seasonal habitat requirements, or periodic release of
               flood pulses (freshets) to promote downstream sediment or nutrient transport.
         23.   Any proposed mitigation measures to adapt the EFR must be properly costs within the project
               design, which may require flexibility to incorporate future adjustments as part of an EFR
               strategy.
         24.   The method used to determine the EFR should as a minimum be consistent with national
               standards or approaches but must be appropriate to the scale and complexity of the project
               and risks involved (e.g. presence of protected areas, critical habitats, high water-use intensity).
               In cases where the knowledge needed to determine the EFR is lacking, the precautionary
               principle should be applied and a high-resolution method for determining the EFR selected.
         25.   Outside the EU, the applied method must be comparable in approach to those used by EU
               Member States.
         28.   The EFR regime must include a continuous programme of monitoring (including both flow and
               biological indicators), evaluation and adjustment - commonly referred to as adaptive


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                         May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page 3
     management – so that it can be periodically reviewed and where necessary modified in
     response to increased understanding or changes in downstream ecosystem or socio-
     economic conditions.




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment          PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                 May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                           Page 4
3.       EFLOW DETERMINATION POSSIBLE METHODS AND
         APPROACHES
3.1.1.   Quantifying the E-Flows of a system involves integrating hydrological, hydraulic (channel cross-
         sections) and ecological habitat data to establish a modified flow regime that is expected to achieve
         some level of ecological functioning relative to the natural condition.
3.1.2.   Many methods and approaches have been developed for different countries and river types/
         characteristics (e.g. seasonal, perennial and intermittent) to determine E-Flows. These E-Flow
         methods can be grouped into four main categories as proposed by King et al. (1999), Tharme
         (2003) and Horne et al. (2017) depending on the information available for the river systems, effort
         and time to spend on the assessment and the level of confidence required in the results. These
         groupings are:
         A.     Hydrological
         B.     Habitat and hydraulic rating
         C.     Habitat simulation
         D.     Holistic or ecosystem approaches
         It should be noted that these groupings are not discrete, as a combination of approaches per
         grouping can be used or as a sub-component for an approach in another category, especially when
         the more holistic approaches are used. A short description of each grouping is provided in Table 3-
         1.

         Table 3-1 - Description of e-flow assessment groups (from E-flows for Morocco: Guideline
         and Recommendations, 2021)

          Method           Description and use

          Hydrological     Data used are mainly hydrological observed or modelled monthly or daily flow time
                           series.
                           The e-flow is usually a fixed percentage or minimum flow per month or season to
                           maintain or improve ecological conditions.
                           Ecological information is limited or excluded.
                           These are desktop methods used for reconnaissance level assessment of large river
                           basins for water resource planning purposes.

          Habitat and      Quantify relationships between discharge and biotic habitats by using changes in
          Hydraulic        hydraulic variables, such as wetted perimeter, velocities or depths estimated from a
          rating           hydraulic cross-section.
                           A threshold value of these hydraulic and habitat variables is selected that would
                           provide minimum or optimum flows to maintain habitats or ecosystems.
                           Data include both hydraulic (cross-section) and habitat ratings, and hydrological
                           observed or modelled monthly or daily flow time series.
                           These are desktop methods that require some field survey, mainly hydraulic cross-
                           sections.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                         PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                          Page 5
          Method           Description and use

                           These methods can be used independently or as input to the habitat simulation
                           methods.

          Habitat          These methods assess flow requirements based on biotic responses to instream
          simulation       habitat changes associated with changing flows by simulating the suitability of instream
                           habitat availability for biota under different discharges.
                           They integrate hydrological, hydraulic and indicator species data using habitat-
                           discharge curves and tables to specify optimum flows for the biota and so to determine
                           e-flows.
                           These methods are not normally employed on their own, but rather they are used to
                           provide relevant hydraulic and habitat information to assist holistic e-flow assessments
                           (Hirschowitz, et al 2007).
                           Data requirements are considerable, employing observed or modelled daily flow time
                           series together with detailed habitat and biological information.

          Holistic or      Holistic or ecosystem methods assume as stated in King, et al, (1999) “… that the
          ecosystem        natural hydrological regime of a river dynamically maintains all the instream biota,
                           channel geomorphology, and riparian, floodplain and wetland systems, as well as any
                           estuarine and offshore coastal systems affected by river flow (i.e. the entire riverine
                           ecosystem). It is further assumed that some baseflows and floods within the complete
                           flow regime are more essential than others for maintenance of the riverine ecosystem.
                           Adequate description of these flows in terms of magnitude, duration, timing, and
                           frequency, and their incorporation in the regulated flow regime should allow the extant
                           biotic characteristics and functional integrity of the river to persist.”
                           These methods are suited to detailed assessment of highly developed and regulated
                           river systems and can be implemented from site to basin scale with high accuracy.
                           They may include assessment of rivers, wetlands, groundwater and estuaries and the
                           integration between these and can consider socio-economic, livelihoods and
                           ecosystem goods and services.
                           Holistic methods allow evaluation of present and future operational/ management
                           scenarios to understand the socio-economic and ecological consequences of altered
                           flows.



3.1.3.   In addition, applying the European Investment Bank (EIB) approach for the selection of appropriate
         environmental flow assessment method will indicate the level of detail required for quantification of
         E-flows and therefore the specific method type can be validated. Figure 3-1 illustrates the selection
         process to determine the appropriate environmental flow assessment method. The current site and
         river system is already significantly modified therefore the selection moves to “No” for the
         assessment of effects on critical habitat/protected areas. The reach is a short reach as of 15 km
         between the Rogun and Nurek Dams. Therefore, the selection process results in a “Low resolution”
         method.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                       Page 6
         Figure 3-1 – Selection of Appropriate Environmental Flow Assessment Method (adapted from
         Brown, C. 2016 and taken from the EIB Environmental, Climate and Social Guidelines on
         Hydropower Development)




3.1.4.   The Desktop Method (Hughes and Munster, 2000) aligns with the outcome of the EIB selection
         process, in that this method is more rigorous than is required by the selection. This method was
         developed as a hydrological model for the estimation of E-Flows for planning/ reconnaissance
         purposes and is based on monthly hydrological flow time series. Where available, ecological
         information on the state of a river can be included to provide additional confidence in the results.
         The output of the method specifies the monthly E-Flows for different flow components (drought,
         maintenance low/ baseflows and floods) based on the reference hydrological flow time series.
3.1.5.   The Tennant method, (Tennant, 1976) or sometimes referred to as the Montana method that is
         grouped as a hydrological method, was also applied for the Vakhsh River. It calculates a percentage
         of the annual average flow to maintain width, depths and velocities in a river and is based on limited
         site observations. It only specifies the minimums for winter and summer baseflows based on overall
         biological state that can be from severely degraded to pristine. As the method does not account for
         daily, seasonal or yearly flow variations, it is difficult to transfer the results to other rivers that are
         morphologically different.

3.2.     SELECTED METHODOLOGY FOR THE ROGUN PROJECT
3.2.1.   The Desktop Method (Hughes and Munster, 2000) was used to determine the E-flows.
3.2.2.   A comparison of the simplified hydrological method, the Tennant method and the Desktop Method is
         provided in Table 3-2.

4.       PROJECT DESCRIPTION AND HYDROLOGY IN CONTEXT TO
         THE EFLOW ASSESSMENT
4.1.1.   There is no dewatered reach associated with the Project implying that no specific minimum flow
         would then be needed for that purpose.
4.1.2.   The instream flow regime would rather be specified to be applied to the downstream reach between
         the HPP outlet and the upper limit of the Nurek reservoir, 15 km downstream.
4.1.3.   By maintaining the Nurek Reservoir at maximum operating level all year round, the reservoir will be
         changed into a run-of-the-reservoir installation by the Project which will be operated in a way to
         maintain the flows downstream of the Nurek Reservoir.
4.1.4.   The duration of the dam filling to Full Supply Level (FSL) is planned to be 7 years.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |

5.       DESIGN MITIGATION AND GOOD INTERNATIONAL INDUSTRY
         PRACTICE
5.1.1.   Due to the low impact foreseen on biodiversity and human livelihoods, not many design mitigation
         measures can be implemented. The focus of the design mitigation measures will be to minimise E-
         Flow impacts on the already impacted ecosystem. Outlets should be designed in such a way to
         ensure the releases for the minimum or drought flows to provide current seasonality (high flows
         during summer, low flows during winter).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment            PUBLIC |

6.       BASELINE – CURRENT CONDITIONS
6.1.1.   For the baseline assessments associated with the Climate, Geology, Geohydrology, Hydrological
         setting and land use, Water use, Surface Water Quantity assessment and Transboundary aspects,
         refer to the Volume 1, Chapter 13:Water of this ESIA. The key information relevant to E-Flows is
         summarised in the sub-sections.

6.2.     CLIMATE
6.2.1.   Refer to Volume 2, Annex 7-1: Baseline Conditions Report of this ESIA for a full climate
         description. The section is relevant to the E-Flow assessment as taken from Volume 1, Chapter 13
         (Water) and Volume 1, Chapter 9 (Greenhouse Gases), and Volume 1, Chapter 10 (Climate
         Change) of this ESIA.
6.2.2.   Tajikistan is the smallest country in Central Asia; 93% of its territory is mountainous regions and 6%
         is occupied by glaciers. Tajikistan is in the temperate continental climate zone and with moderate
         climate variables typical of Central Asia. Climate variability is linked with its geological sub-state,
         which includes coastal, plain areas and mountains. Tajikistan is prone to natural disasters; typically
         avalanches in winter, flash floods in the spring, and high temperatures and dust storms in the
         summer (World Bank, 2020a).
6.2.3.   The glaciers are important in regulating the climate whilst retaining water and controlling flows
         (UNDP, n.d.). Climate change impacts are already observed in Tajikistan, with 20 billion cubic
         metres of ice volume within glaciers already lost during the 20th century (about 2.5%), likely to be
         accelerated by increasing temperatures. It is estimated that total glacial area will decline by up to
         20% within the next 30-40 years, depleting water resources and increasing the risk of mudflows and
         floods (Global Support Programme, n.d.).
         TEMPERATURE
6.2.4.   The temperatures experienced across Tajikistan are dictated by the mountainous terrains (World
         Bank, 2020a). The average monthly temperature registered at the Komsomolabad weather station,
         ranges from a minimum of approximately -3°C in January to a maximum of 25°C in July and August.
         The average monthly temperatures at Komsomolabad are presented in Figure 6-1 which has been
         approximated from Figure 7-2 of the 2014 Environmental and Social Impact Assessment for Rogun
         Hydro Power Plant (Pöyry, 2014).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 12
         Figure 6-1 - Average Monthly Temperature – Komsomolabad




         PRECIPITATION
6.2.5.   Based on the data gathered from the three available weather stations, Garm, Novobod and
         Obigarm, as presented in the 2014 Environmental and Social Impact Assessment for Rogun Hydro
         Power Plant (Pöyry, 2014), the annual weather pattern is consistent across the stations with
         maximum rainfall occurring in winter and spring (December to May) which primarily falls as snow,
         especially at higher altitudes with very little precipitation experienced in summer months with mean
         annual values of 8 – 16 mm recorded in July and 4 – 7 mm recorded in August. In the lower part of
         the catchment the precipitation ranges from 816 mm/a to 936 mm/a with this figure rising to 2,000
         mm/a in the upper parts of the catchment. Refer to Figure 6-2 for the average monthly precipitation
         where (1) represents data from the Garm station, (2) the Novobod station and (3) the Obigarm
         station.

         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                               Page 13

         l
         EVAPOTRANSPIRATION AND EVAPORATION
6.2.6.   Evapotranspiration and Evaporation are monitored to understand the part of the water cycle where
         water turns into vapour and is “lost” into the environment. Evapotranspiration and Evaporation are a
         fundamental part to estimate the annual water availability and its distribution across the year.
         Evapotranspiration for the project area is estimated to range between 750 and 1000 mm/year, while
         the evaporation from the reservoir is expected to be in the range of 800 to 1200 mm/year (Poyry
         2014).
         CLIMATE CHANGE CONSIDERATIONS
6.2.7.   The EIB (European Investment Bank, 2019), recommends that climate change should be evaluated
         for Hydropower projects. Hydropower projects have a very long design-life and therefore long-term
         climate changes will become relevant to the Hydropower Plant. Climate change projections need to
         be incorporated into the hydrological assessment to evaluate the long-term changes in average
         seasonal temperatures and/or rainfall conditions. Such changes can have significant impacts on the
         timing and magnitude of river flows and could possibly alter the flow seasonal trends. E-Flows and
         its effects due to the impact of climate change must feature in the Climate Risk and Vulnerability
         Assessment (CRVA) for the Project.

6.3.     STUDY AREA AND RESOURCE UNIT DELINEATION
6.3.1.   For the purposes of this assessment reach of the Vakhsh River located downstream of the Rogun
         Dam and upstream of the Nurek Reservoir, incorporating a 15 km river section is relevant, refer to
         Figure 6-3.
6.3.2.   For the purposes of this assessment, this extends up to a precautionary maximum distance of 750
         m either side of the Project, within which a level impact from displaced biodiversity and humans can
         reasonably be expected to occur.

Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment   PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                   Page 15
         HYDRAULIC HABITATS AND GEOMORPHOLOGY
6.3.3.   The shape and slope of river channels, influenced by geomorphological processes affect flow
         velocity and water depth. Channels with gentle slopes and wide floodplains can store more water
         helping to maintain minimum flows.
6.3.4.   Geomorphological processes control sediment transport and deposition, which can alter river
         channels and affect flow patterns.
6.3.5.   The size, shape and topography of drainage basins influence how water is collected and distributed
         and can impact on minimum flows if basins do not have efficient drainage networks.
6.3.6.   At the dam site, the river channel is narrow and the valley profile has a relatively tight “V” shape with
         steep slopes. The favourable morphology of the retention structure is due to the river cutting across
         the general structural trend, as well because of the bedding.
6.3.7.   Refer to the Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water and the Volume 1, Chapter 15: Biodiversity of this
         ESIA, for a more detailed description of the river geomorphology and hydraulic habitats. This section
         summarizes information as relevant to E-Flows only.
6.3.8.   Habitat diversity for freshwater biota within the Vakhsh River was limited and most river reaches
         were relatively homogeneous. In general, the river could be described under three main habitat
         types; including confined channels (gorges) with steep banks, open gorges with alluvial cobbles and
         boulders, and braided channels.

6.4.     SITE GEOLOGY
6.4.1.   At the dam site, the river channel is narrow and the valley profile has a relatively tight “V” shape with
         steep slopes. The favourable morphology of the retention structure is due to the river cutting across
         the general structural trend, as well because of the bedding.

6.5.     HYDROLOGY
         BASE FLOW AND CATCHMENT INFLOWS
6.5.1.   The Vakhsh River flow assessment has been conducted as part of the assessment set out in
         Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water of this ESIA. Data used in Coyne et Bellier et al. (2014) and the
         World Bank (2023) can be considered a good approximation to the flow conditions downstream of
         Rogun before the construction of the dam. The data sources available are:
          Rogun dam inflows for the period from 1932 to 2008 (Coyne et Bellier et al., 2014); and
          Nurek HPP inflows for the period from 2009 to 2019 (World Bank, 2023).
         BASE FLOW AND CATCHMENT INFLOWS
6.5.2.   The Vakhsh river flow assessment has been conducted as part of the Volume 1, Chapter 13:
         Water of this ESIA. At the time of writing no direct flow measurement downstream of Rogun is
         available to WSP. However, the data used in Coyne et Bellier et al. (2014) and the World Bank
         (2023) can be considered a good approximation to the flow conditions downstream of Rogun before
         the construction of the dam. The data sources available are:
            Rogun dam inflows for the period from 1932 to 2008 (Coyne et Bellier et al., 2014); and
            Nurek HPP inflows for the period from 2009 to 2019 (World Bank, 2023).
6.5.3.   A comparison of these datasets has been conducted in the Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water and it
         was found that the data from 2008 to 2019 present a similar box plot comparing to the dataset from
         1932 to 2007, resulting in a combined boxplot where the statistics are in the same range of values of
         the Old dataset used for the previous studies. From this qualitative comparison it can be concluded
         that there is not a substantial difference in flow data, which can be used to describe the flow
         conditions of the Vakhsh river before the construction of Rogun dam.
         PREVIOUS BASELINE
6.5.4.   This assessment has been presented in the Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water of this ESIA.
6.5.5.   Before the construction of the latest Rogun Dam design , the average annual flow ranged from 450
         m³/s to 867 m³/s, with an average value of 645 m³/s. The average flows during the spring-summer
         period (i.e. high flow season) are more than four times higher than the average flows in the autumn-
         spring period (i.e. low flow season) with an average flow of 1,054 m³/s and 234 m³/s respectively.
         The maximum average flow registered in the April-September period is 1,438 m³/s, and the
         minimum is 678 m³/s. During the October-March period the maximum average flow is 326 m³/s and
         the minimum 182 m³/s.
6.5.6.   A summary of flow statistics over the ‘full’ dataset are provided for Annual average flows, April-
         September average flows and October-March average flows within Table 6-1.

         Table 6-1 - Vakhsh Average Flow Statistics at Rogun Dam over the ‘Full’ Dataset (Annual,
         April-September and October-March Periods)

          Average Flow             Annual Period (m3/s)    April-September         October-March

                                   Prior to latest Rogun

6.5.7.   Additional hydrometric data has been provided by the National Agency for Hydrometeorology of the
         Republic of Tajikistan. Average flow statistics from two stations closest to Rogun Dam are presented
         in Table 6-2 and Table 6-3. These stations are:
          Darband hydrometric station: located on the Vakhsh River just downstream of the confluence
           between the Surkhob and Obihingou rivers; and
 Garm hydrometric station: located on the Surkhob River before the confluence with the Obihingou
  River.

         CHANGE TO BASE AND INFLOWS
6.5.8.   The construction of a dam for hydropower production purposes has three main effects on the
         surface water regime (Poyry 2014):
          Change in the downstream flow regime: the operation of hydropower turbines can shift
           considerably the discharges downstream of the dam between different seasons. Water is
           generally stored in the reservoir during the wet season, to be then released during the drier
           season. Poyry 2014 analyse how the increment in the number of cascading reservoirs in a river
           basin have made more evident the tendency to decrease the wet season flows and increment the
           dry season ones. Hydropeaking is another effect of turbine operations on the flow regime. To
           match the electricity demand, the hydroelectric facilities tend to discharge water at variable rates
           throughout the day, causing short-term changes in the downstream water flows and levels.
          Change in the habitat: due to the impoundment, the habitat changes from riverine to lacustrine,
           which might represent a threat for local aquatic ecosystems, where some species might not be
           able to adapt to the new environment, while others will thrive. Another consequence of the
           modification of the habitat can be a change in the water quality. The main drivers affecting the
           reservoir water quality are the pre-impoundment conditions of the area (i.e. the presence of
           biomass that will be submerged during the filling of the reservoir might deteriorate the water
           quality of the deeper layers), the use of the reservoir as disposal for municipal, agricultural and
           industrial wastewater. These aspects become even more relevant when the reservoir is used as
           a water supply for local communities or is the habitat of endangered species.
          Change of local water balance: gains from direct precipitations, and losses from evaporation
           and infiltration.
         MINIMUM ENVIRONMENTAL FLOW (E-FLOW)
6.5.9.   The availability of long term measured or modelled daily or monthly hydrological data to represent
         climatic variability, especially where high variability within year and between years are experienced,
         is required for the determination of the E-Flow to provide high confidence results. These flows form
         the baseline or reference flows to build the hydrological foundation for the determination.
6.5.10. The reference flows can refer to natural or minimally impacted flows at certain points in the rivers
        (important tributaries, Environmental Flow Requirement sites, gauging weirs, or large development
        sites) in a catchment or at the outlet of an entire basin. If a long enough observed flow record is
        available from a gauging station, the record period could be separated for both baseline (before
        developments) and for present day development conditions. For example, if the observed flow
        record is from 1920 to 2015 and the only development was the construction of a dam and
        associated infrastructure for irrigation in 1960, the period 1920 to 1960 could be used as baseline
        and the latter period as present day flows.
6.5.11. The main graphs and statistics required for these flow records are hydrographs (monthly or daily),
        mean, median (high variability), minimum, maximum, flood peaks, etc. to provide information to the
        ecologists at the various selected sites where biological, vegetation and hydraulics data is available.
6.5.12. Monthly observed flows for the Vakhsh River are available from the gauge at Darband for the period
        1932 to 2019 and was used as the reference flows for this assessment. The gauge is situated
        upstream of Rogun Dam, but after the confluence of the Surkhob and Obikhingou Rivers, thus
        providing good reference flows for the Vakhsh River. From the statistics calculated from the data, it
        was observed that the variability between years is very low (median and mean values per month
         almost the same), with a reference Mean Annual Runoff of 20 309 Mm3. A baseflow separation was
         undertaken using the method as developed by Smaktin in 2001. The baseflows give an indication of
         the flows in the river without the influence of floods and provides a guide for the determination of the
         low flow requirements of the E-Flows (Figure 6-4).

         Figure 6-4 - Monthly hydrograph of flows at Darband (REF-Reference mean, BF-Baseflows)




6.6.     BIODIVERSITY
         AQUATIC SPECIES
6.6.1.   Ecological indicators used for environmental flow determination typically incorporate fish, benthic
         invertebrates and instream vegetation. There is also a component that incorporates river-dependant
         herpetofauna, birds and mammals. Further to this, habitat connectivity and heterogeneity is
         considered for different species life history traits (i.e. spawning, nursery grounds, feeding).
6.6.2.   In the case of the Vakhsh River between Rogun and Nurek, these indicators need to be approached
         in a pragmatic manner that takes into consideration the section of river that is being considered.
6.6.3.   Refer to Volume 1, Chapter 15:Biodiversity of this ESIA for further detail on aquatic ecology within
         the relevant river reach.
         TERRESTRIAL ECOLOGY
6.6.4.   Refer to Volume 1, Chapter 15: Biodiversity of this ESIA for further detail. The below summarises
         the key information relevant to the E-Flow assessment.
         Flora
6.6.5.   No floral species have been noted to occur within the 15 km river reach relevant to the E-Flow
         assessment. Therefore, there is no impact on flora due to E-Flows.
6.6.6.   No invasive plant species were noted during studies to inform the 2014 ESIA, or from field surveys
         in 2023 and therefore no relevance to E-Flows in the 15 km river reach.
         Fauna
         Mammals
6.6.7.   No mammals were recorded to be present within the 15 km river reach relevant to the E-Flow
         assessment that would be impacted by a change in E-Flows due to the project.
         Amphibians
6.6.8.   Tajikistan has 14 native amphibian species, two of which are endemic to the region. The desktop
         study recorded identified only two amphibian species within the Vakhsh River valley: green toad
         Bufo viridis; and lake frog Rana ridibunda. Both species were also recorded during the 2023 fields
         surveys and are common and widespread within the region, in areas where standing water is
         present. Although the species has not specifically been recorded in the 15 km river reach of interest
         to E-Flows, it can be likely that there is some presence of this species in this reach.
         Reptiles
6.6.9.   A diverse reptile fauna is present across Tajikistan, with 46 species confirmed as being present, 22
         of which are endemic to the region. None of the reptile species identified is expected to be impacted
         due to a change in E-Flows.
         Birds
6.6.10. Most species recorded are common and widespread, although 16 species are listed on the TRB
        and/or are of increased conservation interest/status which has been recorded from reservoirs
        downstream of Rogun, close to Nurek. This is beyond the river reach associated with E-Flows and
        therefore change in E-Flows in the study area will not impact these species to a significant extent.
         HUMAN LIVELIHOODS
6.6.11. This section as taken from the Volume 1, Chapter 13: Water of this ESIA is relevant to the E-Flow
        assessment.
6.6.12. The Project site is located approximately 100 km east of the Tajik capital of Dushanbe on the
        Vakhsh River in the Rasht region. The Rasht region is divided into seven districts (rayons) namely,
        Faizobod, Rogun, Nurobod, Rasht, Tavildara, Tojikobod and Jirgital.
6.6.13. In the Vakhsh river catchment, agriculture and housing are concentrated in the valley bottoms where
        the terrain is less steep, making up only around 5 to 7% of the land use outside of the mountainous
        regions. Most of the land use is low productivity grazing pasture, comprising 75% to 35% of the land
        depending on altitude. Forest and scrub comprise 3% of the land, as much of the original forest that
        covered the Pamir mountain foothills has been cleared for grazing, Pöyry, (2014).
6.6.14. The Vakhsh river provides water for several irrigation schemes, which are all located downstream of
        Nurek dam. The main irrigation infrastructures are the Danghara tunnel with a capacity of 100 m³/s,
        the Yavan tunnel with a capacity of 75 m³/s and the Vakhsh main canal with a capacity of 210 m³/s,
        in addition to several smaller irrigation systems (Poyry 2014). In the upper Vakhsh river, upstream of
        Nurek, the demand for irrigation is limited due to the lack of suitable land (Poyry 2014).
6.6.15. The abstraction demand for irrigation follows the vegetation period and the is subject to the crop
        type and elevation. A first peak in water consumption is observed in March, to artificially inundate
        the fields before the starting the cultures, while a second and higher peak is observed between June
        and August, when most of the demand is concentrated (Poyry 2014).
6.6.16. Terrestrial habitats relevant to human livelihood includes cultivated lands containing crops for
        humans and livestock, pasture habitats and urban areas where the settlements are located. The
        2014 ESIA concluded that fish diversity and abundance within the Project AoI was low, and that fish
        were not economically important for the local population.
