1        RESERVOIR SLOPE STABILITY
1.1.1.   This annex describes the current understanding of landslide hazard of the reservoir slopes that may
         be affected by the Project activities, the potential impacts of reservoir induced landslides and
         potential mitigation strategies.
1.1.2.   A comprehensive description of baseline conditions regarding geomorphology and soils and geology
         is presented in Volume 2, Annex 7.1: Baseline Conditions Report and a summary is provided in
         Volume 1, Chapter 7 of this ESIA. An assessment of the potential Project impacts on reservoir
         slope stability is presented in Section 22.5. Where necessary, mitigation measures for the avoidance
         of impact, impact minimization and/or long-term management are included in Section 22.6.
1.1.3.   Condition of the slopes around the reservoir has been described at a preliminary level in ESIA
         (2014) and TEAS (2014). No additional field or remote assessment has been reviewed in
         preparation of this document. Knowledge of reservoir landslide assessment, behaviour,
         consequences and management has been reviewed and incorporated from various literature
         sources (including ICOLD Bulletin 124 and Reimer (1991)), which describes a large number of case
         studies of reservoirs affected by slope instability. Relevant data from studies that have included the
         Rogun reservoir area and other similar large reservoirs have also informed this assessment.
1.1.4.   To a limited extent, A comprehensive assessment of the potential impacts of reservoir slope
         instability is currently underway for the Project area for the full raise to 1290 m ASL, which will
         inform the reservoir filling schedule. potential impacts related to reservoir slope instability have been
         assessed previously (including a specific assessment for raising the water in the reservoir to 1100 m
         ASL) and are summarised in this document.

1.2      BASELINE DATA SUMMARY
         POYRY (2014) ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT FOR ROGUN
         HYDRO POWER PLANT
1.2.1.   The 2014 ESIA report discussed the findings of the review of previous Project reports relating to
         slope stability within the Project area of influence and noted:
          The soils associated with Quaternary deposits are most prone to erosion and the exposure of the
           reservoir to landslides, mud and debris flows is mostly from its left bank with a few million cubic
           meters estimated as the maximum volume for such events; and
          An assessment of the landslide risk in the reservoir area has been carried out by the Institute of
           Geology, Earthquake Engineering and Seismology of the Academy of Sciences of the Republic of
           Tajikistan and landslide prone areas, where landslide processes have been observed and are
           expected, are divided into areas:
            • on the shoreline of the future reservoir, which therefore can be affected by the oscillation of the
              reservoir water level
            • out of the reservoir area (in brown in Figure 2-7), which therefore are not influenced by it, but
              could block tributaries of the Vakhsh river.
          Areas of highest risk for landslides, which might be influenced by the drawdown of the reservoir,
           are located between Chorsada and Nurobod, and near the confluence of Surkhob and Obihingou
           rivers, both on the right bank of the river. While landslides in these areas are not expected to


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                    May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page 1
           present the risk of impulse waves overtopping or damaging the dam and the hydraulic facilities,
           they present a risk for the settlements located nearby.
         COYNE ET BELLIER (2014) TECHNO-ECONOMIC ASSESSMENT STUDY FOR ROGUN
         HYDROELECTRIC CONSTRUCTION PROJECT
1.2.2.   The report titled Phase II: Project Definition Options Volume 3: Engineering and Design Chapter 3:
         Design of Alternatives specifically noted that:
          “With regard to geodynamics phenomena, rockfalls, collapses and landslides are intensively
           developed on and around the dam site. Rockfalls are common, especially when raining, due to
           the steepness of slopes and differential erosion between the interlayered siltstones and
           sandstones.”;
          The right bank of the Vakhsh is characterised by a peculiar morphological feature; the so-called
           ‘disturbed zone’ and “… the presence of large potentially unstable masses in the front part of this
           structure, at the foot of which large amount of debris produced by the scouring of the slope
           accumulate”; and
          Slope instability is common in the diverse tributaries of the Vakhsh River, especially the Obi-Shur
           River, which joins with the Vakhsh River on left bank immediately downstream of the dam site
           that is a source of mudflows that, in May 1993, temporarily dammed the Vakhsh River resulting in
           the flooding of the lower intake tunnels and the transformer house.
         STUCKY (2018A) SLOPE STABILITY ASSESSMENT FOR LAKE FILLING TO THE
         INTERMEDIATE 1100 M ABOVE SEA LEVEL (ASL) STAGE
1.2.3.   This report described a detailed analysis of slope stability of a number of ‘risk-areas’ around the
         reservoir that may be affected by lake filling to the intermediate 1100m ASL stage and provided a
         description of known reservoir slope stability but does not assess all potential reservoir landslide
         risks associated with raising the reservoir to final elevation.
         STUCKY (2018B) DAM BREACH ASSESSMENT OF THE INTERMEDIATE 1100 M ASL
         RESERVOIR STAGE
1.2.4.   This report presented the results of a dam breach assessment of the dam in the intermediate
         1100 m ASL reservoir stage. The assessment concludes that a breach of this dam configuration
         would cause significant damage downstream but be contained with the Nurek Dam reservoir,
         without overtopping.
         SEISTER (2024) SEISMIC HAZARD UPDATE STUDY AT ROGUN DAM
1.2.5.   A comprehensive seismic hazard assessment is presented in this report including the results of a
         probabilistic seismic hazard assessment that describes known seismic sources and distributed
         seismicity models for the region, estimates of ground shaking for a range of likelihood, probabilistic
         fault displacement hazard assessment for several faults located close to the dam site, an
         assessment of reservoir triggered seismicity during and following reservoir filling and as assessment
         of topographic amplification around the reservoir area.
         OTHER LITERATURE
1.2.6.   Research publications such as Reimer (1992), Havenith et al (2018), Jones et al (2021) and
         Metzger et al (2021) provided relevant information on reservoir landslide case studies, local slope
         stability characteristics and tectonic setting. Tang et al (2019) describes some of the reservoir


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |

         landslides activated by filling of the Three Gorges Reservoir in China. Strom (2013) describes some
         potential impacts of large landslides within the Rogun HPP catchment.

1.3      STANDARDS AND GUIDELINES
1.3.1.   Management of reservoir slope instability risk will need to be compliant with all applicable Tajik
         legislation. Primarily this is anticipated to relate to the management of dam safety. Many countries
         do not have applicable legislation or guidelines for managing reservoir slope instability and rely on
         guidance developed by other organisations as described below.
1.3.2.   Guidance on investigating and managing reservoir slope instability risk is provided by ICOLD
         Bulletin 124, which is frequently referenced in this document.
1.3.3.   ICOLD Bulletin 124 describes current good international practice for investigating, monitoring and
         mitigating risk of reservoir landslides. The general approach incorporates thorough geological
         investigations to identify potential slope instability that could affect the reservoir, followed by
         assessment of the associated risk to life safety, the environment and infrastructure. Driven by the
         results of these assessments, risk mitigation strategies are developed, which usually include
         deformation monitoring and associated emergency management plans if unacceptable slope
         behaviour is identified, and potentially engineering mitigation measures such as drainage of
         groundwater, buttressing and slope modification.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                   May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 3
2        BASELINE DATA

2.1      RESERVOIR GEOLOGY
2.1.1.   The geology/geomorphology of the Rogun HPP is characterised by mountainous terrain, the incised
         Vakhsh River and a number of tectonically active faults. The tectonic setting is the western end of
         the India/Asian collision zone, characterised by oblique compression of up to about 20 mm/year.
         The oldest rocks exposed in the catchment are the Paleozoic Tien Shan granitic basement (pink
         areas in Figure 2-1), which outcrop from the Obidjushon River on the right bank (north side) of the
         reservoir area, from upstream of the dam site and on left bank (south side) of the reservoir from the
         Khodja-Alisho River, about 6 km upstream of the dam site. The Tien Shan rocks are complexly
         juxtaposed with younger rocks by a series of thrust and reverse faults. The overlying rocks comprise
         the Cretaceous/Tertiary aged Tajik Depression sedimentary sequence (green units in Figure 2-1),
         which have been folded and uplifted by activity of the Tajik basin Fold-Thrust Belt. A more detailed
         geological map of the dam site and lower reservoir area is presented as Figure 2-2 and Figure 2-3,
         showing the distribution of Quaternary deposits and recognised faults as well as the distribution of
         underlying rock types. The complex thrust fault system of the Tajik basin Fold-Thrust Belt is
         illustrated conceptually in Figure 2-4. Figure 2-5 depicts a series of geological cross sections
         through the reservoir.
2.1.2.   A number of faults that have been recognised in the vicinity of the Rogun HPP are associated with
         this tectonic setting, including the Vakhsh Thrust fault which is one of the highest strain structures in
         the region (Figure 2-1). The Ionaksh Fault passes through the dam site and the Gulizindan Fault
         around the left abutment of the dam.
2.1.3.   The Tajik Depression sedimentary sequence comprises Jurassic to Cretaceous age argillite,
         sandstone, mudstone, limestone and includes evaporites such as gypsum.
2.1.4.   Separating the Palaeozoic basement and Tajik Depression sequence is a series of younger Tertiary
         age limestone and terrigenous sediments that accumulated in a foreland basin and are now
         tectonically incorporated between the older formations.
2.1.5.   Thick quaternary loess deposits are common, particularly overlying the basement rocks and
         extensive alluvial fans characterise the left bank of the reservoir.
2.1.6.   The high rate of crustal tectonic deformation in the area leads to a high rate of earthquake activity,
         including six magnitude M7 and 18 M6.5 earthquakes in the past 115 years (Metzger et al 2021).
         Estimates of ground shaking for a range of likelihood are presented by Seister (2024) indicating a
         high seismic hazard in the area.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                     May 2025

(Seister 2024). The pink areas are underlain by Paleozoic Tien Shan granitic basement and
the remaining areas are underlain by Cretaceous/Tertiary aged Tajik Depression sedimentary
sequence.

2.2      RECOGNISED LANDSLIDES IN THE RESERVOIR AREA
2.2.1.   Technical studies for the Project have identified landslide processes within the reservoir area (for
         example ESIA 2014, TEAS 2014, Stucky 2018a). Figure 2-6 from ESIA 2014 shows recognised
         landslides around the reservoir that could be directly affected by reservoir inundation (in red
         hatching) and landslides that are outside the reservoir footprint but that could affect tributary rivers
         (yellow hatching). Reservoir landslide studies indicate potential effects could include slope
         movement resulting from changed groundwater systems in response to the reservoir rising or
         impulse waves created by impact of a large, fast-moving landslide into the reservoir displacing water
         in front of the moving mass. In addition, a number of independent research studies have presented
         relevant slope instability information to characterise aspects of landslide hazard in the reservoir and
         catchment area (for example Jones et al 2021). Landslides that are large enough to block the
         reservoir or any tributary to the reservoir represent a potential ‘landslide dam’ breach hazard.
         Breaching of a landslide dam within the reservoir area, such as by overtopping, can result in large
         outflows that can represent a hazard to communities around the reservoir and the dam and
         associated structures.

         Figure 2-6 - Map of landslide prone areas around the Rogun reservoir (ESIA 2014)




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                   May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 9
2.3      SPECIFIC SLOPE INSTABILITY FEATURES
2.3.1.   A site specific analysis of areas around the reservoir that could be affected by lake filling to 1100 m
         ASL has been prepared by consultants for the Project (Stucky 2018a). The assessment specifically
         addresses the slope instability associated with a number of ‘risk-areas’ that could be affected by
         raising the lake to the intermediate 1100 m ASL elevation. The risk-areas include slopes that are not
         currently recognised as landslides but are potentially susceptible to slope movement in response to
         rising groundwater levels or toe erosion as a result of the reservoir. Risks associated with lake filling
         include potential for landslide dams and damaging impulse waves that could affect the dam or other
         infrastructure around the impoundment. The assessment includes geological models for each slope
         area, stability analysis and recommendations for deformation monitoring. The report does not
         include any assessment of slopes that may be affected by future impoundment raises from 1100 m
         ASL to the proposed final 1290 m ASL level.

         Figure 2-7 - Engineering geological plan from Stucky (2018a) showing ‘risk areas’ potentially
         adversely affected by impounding the reservoir to 1100 m ASL




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                   PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                     May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                   Page 10
2.3.2.   It characterizes the slope instability hazards affected by lake filling to 1100 m ASL as (locations
         shown in Figure 2-7):
          L1-B, L1-D, L1-E, R1-F, and R1-G: alluvial terraces on the left and right bank of the Vakhsh
           River, directly facing the river and affected by active fluvial erosion; potential collapse of the
           terrace edges related to the impounding phase and to oscillations of the 1100 supply level during
           hydropower generation.
          L2-A: composite area including both rock slopes cut by the river in the basement and alluvial
           terraces on the left and right bank; the riverbanks are prone to erosion and partial collapse during
           the impounding phase, before full submersion of the area.
          L1-F: collapse of the artificial embankments upon which some service roads are located; potential
           triggering of slope-scale instability.
          R1-B: potential reactivation of a large rockslide resting on the slope above the elevation of
           1100 m (marginally affected in this phase).
          R3-B: potential collapse of an active landslide located by the watershed.
2.3.3.   The potential consequences of the above hazards include local instability, impulse waves mainly
         affecting the construction site, local landslide dam and impulse waves potentially overtopping the
         dam. Potential triggers include the changed groundwater regime during lakefilling, earthquake
         shaking and toe erosion. While the assessment predates the site-specific seismic hazard
         assessment prepared by Seister (2024), the peak ground accelerations assumed are similar. While
         this assessment only focusses on the identified areas that may be affected by raising the reservoir
         to 1100 m ASL (and not subsequent reservoir raising), similar effects are anticipated to occur during
         later impoundment filling to the full reservoir and operation. The main populations at risk from
         impulse waves and flooding from these features are Obigarm, Pasi Muhraho and Rogun City (refer
         to Figure 2-9).
2.3.4.   A landslide feature referred to as the ‘atypical zone’ (ATPZ) occupies an area of about 5km2 on the
         right bank of the Vakhsh River, downstream of the dam site, with a maximum disturbed depth of up
         to about 150m. This is an important feature because it has the potential to interrupt the power
         generation, to flood the powerhouse and damage its equipment and installations, to damage the
         outlets of the diversion tunnels and/or to affect the spillway discharge capacity.
2.3.5.   In 1949 the M7.4 Khait earthquake occurred in the Rogun catchment generating large rockfalls, rock
         avalanches and many large flow slides within the loess soils burying several villages and killing an
         estimated 7200 people (Evans et al 2009). The impact of the earthquake indicates that widespread
         landslides would be expected during a future large landslide within the catchment, that could
         generate landslide dams or significant impact waves in the reservoir. The effects of the Khait
         earthquake also indicate the importance of considering landslide hazard when designing relocated
         infrastructure and residential areas.
2.3.6.   Strom and Zhirkevich (2013) describe a debris flow from the Obi-Shur Creek tributary that blocked
         the Vakhsh River just downstream from the dam causing inundation of the underground powerhouse
         and breached the coffer dam, resulting in significant project delays. Mitigation in the form of a
         retention dam on Obi-Shur Creek was subsequently constructed to retain material from a future
         debris flow in the catchment. A subsequent debris flow occurred during 2024 and resulted in some
         flooding of the construction site.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                    May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 11
2.3.7.   Potential for landslide dams is described by Strom and Zhirkevich (2013) with a high level
         assessment of potential inflows to the reservoir resulting from breach of a landslide dam. This
         assessment concludes that the inflow volume for a foreseeable landslide dam failure could be
         accommodated by the proposed dam given the large size of the reservoir and large freeboard.
2.3.8.   Jones et al (2021) presents the results of a study of slope instability of the 10,000 km² catchment of
         the Rogun HPP. This study maps active landslides using analysis of satellite borne InSAR imagery
         and infer 222 active features in the study area (Figure 2-8 and Figure 2-9). Most of the features are
         inferred to be rock slope deformation, rockslides, or soil creep with mean velocity of about 0.3 to 0.5
         m/year and volume of up to 100 million m³. There are limitations to the data including a lack of field
         verification for the landslides, relatively low resolution of the data (smaller landslides may not be
         identified), lack of data while there is snow cover and maximum measurable velocity (of about 0.85
         m/year) which may exclude identification of some faster moving landslides. Therefore, the study
         does not represent a comprehensive assessment of reservoir landslides. However, the study
         confirms that large creeping landslides are present in the catchment, presumably in geology similar
         to the reservoir slopes. Jones et al (2021) infer that rapid failure of such features could lead to
         landslide dams. While the size of the reservoir is large compared to the identified landslides, and
         therefore it is unlikely that a landslide dam could block the reservoir following lakefilling, it is possible
         that a landslide dam could form during lakefilling while the reservoir is narrower. It is also possible
         that failure of a landslide dam in a tributary of the reservoir could lead to inundation of areas around
         the impounded reservoir as a result of a sudden, large inflow into the reservoir.

         Figure 2-8 - Map of the Rogun HPP reservoir and catchment showing landslides inferred from
         InSAR data (after Jones et al 2021). The detailed areas are presented in Figure 9 showing
         interpreted landslide type for each feature




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                        May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                      Page 12
         Figure 2-9 - Interpreted landslide type for features identified from InSAR data by Jones et al
         (2021)




2.3.9.   An analysis of expected changes in climate over the century (Volume 1, Chapter 10: Climate
         Resilience) indicates that summer precipitation is expected to decrease, but winter precipitation will
         increase significantly. The total annual precipitation is expected to increase as a result.
         Temperatures are expected to generally increase and as a result, more precipitation is expected to
         occur as rain and less as snow. Glaciers in the upper catchment of the Project are expected to
         continue melting with increased melt water flooding in the next few decades. The effect of the above
         changes in climate are expecting to include an increase in rate of landslide processes due to rainfall
         triggering, particularly more fluid landslide types such as debris flows and mud flows. Increased
         landslide activity in the glaciated upper catchment is expected due to retreat of glacial ice support of
         adjacent mountain slopes.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                    May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 13
2.4      SUMMARY OF KNOWN RESERVOIR LANDSLIDE TYPES
2.4.1.   The key recognised landslide types and mechanisms include:
          Scree deposits and ‘ancient landslides’ around the dam site and intake tunnels;
          Scarps in alluvial terraces interpreted to be large scale landslide features activated by dissolution
           of salt deposits at the toe of the slopes. These landslides could by up to hundreds of thousands
           of cubic metres in volume;
          Rockslides within the catchment of up to 100 Mm³;
          A very large structurally controlled landslide estimated to be 30Mm³ in volume (ATPZ) on the right
           bank downstream of the dam;
          Rockfalls of relatively small volume are common on steep slope but of minor consequence.
          Evaporite dissolution and karst processes associated with salt and gypsum deposits can cause
           large scale deformation; and
          Large scale topographic adjustment or landslide deformation is implied by ‘seismic scarps’.
          Debris flows or mud flows are common in the area (reported as annual occurrences) and can be
           of very large volume (up to several thousands or millions m³).
2.4.2.   Rock slopes surrounding the reservoir that are in situ or are currently not recognised as landslides
         may also be susceptible to large scale collapse as a result of strong earthquake shaking. If such
         slopes collapse into the reservoir, potentially damaging impact waves could occur.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                  May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 14
3        AREA OF INFLUENCE
3.1.1.   The potential Area of Influence (AoI) of reservoir landslide impacts includes the reservoir perimeter
         with the area most likely to be affected being close to the reservoir rim and including a buffer of
         about 1 km from the reservoir edge. In addition, the area downstream from the dam is also
         potentially affected by reservoir landslides because a landslide impact wave could lead to
         overtopping of the dam.

3.2      ASSESSMENT METHODOLOGY
3.2.1.   The potential impacts of landslides into and around the reservoir that may arise as a result of the
         Project during the construction and operation phase are considered using the approach outlined in
         this section. This approach follows the general impact assessment described in Volume 1, Chapter
         5: ESIA Methodology of this ESIA.
3.2.2.   Reservoir slope stability risk caused by impoundments has historically had significant impacts on
         communities, the environment and infrastructure and the proposed Rogun HPP will create a large
         reservoir that will introduce new landslide risks. The reservoir will affect slope stability risk in a
         number of ways:
          Some slopes will be modified by earthworks associated with the Project;
          Slopes will have increased groundwater levels due to the reservoir reducing stability of existing
           landslides or slopes that have not previously experienced significant instability;
          Impact of landslides into the reservoir may generate impulse waves that could inundate
           surrounding land or overtop the dam. Overtopping of the dam could lead to significant damage
           and potentially result in a dam failure, leading to uncontrolled release of the reservoir;
          Landslide dams in tributary rivers may fail by overtopping and create a large inflow into the
           reservoir that could inundate surrounding land that is not currently at risk from inundation and
           overtop the dam; and
          Changed land use around the reservoir area could increase the landslide risk by increasing the
           vulnerability of communities and infrastructure at threat from landslide movement or landslide
           impact waves.
3.2.3.   Impacts caused by construction of assets outside the reservoir area are not considered to be a
         potential cause of reservoir instability because they may not be directly affected by the impounded
         reservoir.
         IMPACT PREDICTION
3.2.4.   This assessment describes the potential impacts of landslides into the reservoir on the physical,
         biological, cultural or human environment. The impact assessment also considers the existing and
         potential controls or mitigating elements that could be adopted.
3.2.5.   Specific landslide impacts cited by ICOLD Bulletin 124 from historical case studies that could affect
         the reservoir and environs include:
            Large scale landslide movement of pre-existing features or first-time failures;
            Superficial slides or activation of frontal lobes of pre-existing landslides;
            Wave erosion around reservoir;
            Landslide dams and subsequent dam breach floods;



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                    May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 15
          Impact (or impulse) waves;
          Additional potential impacts (but less likely to affect the reservoir) include:
             •   Sliding of sediment deltas within the reservoir;
             •   Slides downstream of the dam caused by scour, seepage or scour;
             •   Internal erosion due to fluctuating water levels;
             •   Subsidence and collapse of loss soils;
             •   Collapse of karst terrain (related to gypsum or evaporite soils);
             •   Flotation of peat or pumiceous soils;
             •   Accelerated breakup and retreat of glaciers; and
             •   Dissolution of evaporite rocks.
3.2.6.   The potential effects of the above landslide impacts could include:
          Upstream flooding caused by a landslide dam;
          Damage to downstream communities and to the dam caused by breaching of a landslide dam in
           the reservoir or catchment;
          Direct landslide impact or impulse wave damage to the dam or ancillary structures;
          Overtopping of the dam by a large impulse wave or inflow from a landslide dam breach affecting
           infrastructure and communities downstream of the Project area. This effect could be severe given
           the possibility of breach of Rogun Dam and the presence of other hydropower assets, large
           population and critical food production areas in the downstream areas;
          Impulse wave damage and flooding of shoreline communities and infrastructure;
          Damage by air blasts caused by rapid landslides in narrow valleys; and
          Blockage by debris of low level outlets.
3.2.7.   Additional impacts to the Project may occur, including:
            Operational constraints on the reservoir;
            Project delays or even abandonment;
            Cost of mitigation works to offset the effect of the reservoir on landslide risk;
            Loss of reservoir storage;
            Land use loss or restrictions along the reservoir shoreline;
            Loss of recreational value; and
            Reduction in water quality.
3.2.8.   A dam breach assessment for the reservoir raise to 1100 m ASL prepared by Stucky (2018b)
         indicates that overtopping of the dam at the intermediate crest stage will cause significant damage
         downstream of the dam but will not overtop the Nurek Dam. A dam breach assessment of the full
         reservoir to 1290 m ASL is a critical input to assessing the potential consequences of reservoir
         landslides and is currently being undertaken by the design team.
         IMPACT MAGNITUDE
3.2.9.   The qualitatively categorization of the magnitude of the potential impact is described in Table 3-1

         RECEPTOR SENSITIVITY
3.2.10. A receptor is the element of the receiving environment that is impacted, i.e. component of the
        physical, ecological, or human environment. The receptors in the context of landslides are identified
        as:
          life safety of populations that could be impacted,
          infrastructure around the reservoir perimeter ranging from occupied land to essential
           infrastructure and
          valued ecological or archaeological features around the reservoir perimeter that could be
           inundated or damaged by landslide movement.
3.2.11. To identify the sensitivity of potential receptors, a scale of relative sensitivity presented in Table 3-2.

         Table 3-2 – Receptor Sensitivity Categories

          Receptor Sensitivity      Rationale

                                    Critical infrastructure located with 1 km of the reservoir at full height and
                                    less than 100 m elevation above FSL.
                                    Archaeological, historical and paleontological sites considered to be of
                                    international or national importance with the greatest potential for further,
                                    significant associated discoveries to be made. Cultural sites which have

                                  been frequented by the local community for longstanding cultural
                                  purposes.

          Medium                  Communities with households between 100 and 10 that are within 1 km
                                  of the reservoir at full height but more than 100 m elevation above FSL.
                                  Critical infrastructure located within 1 km of the reservoir at full height,
                                  but more than 100 m above FSL.
                                  Archaeological, historical and paleontological sites considered to be of
                                  regional importance. Cultural sites which may be no longer in use but are
                                  known to the community and associated with settlement history/oral
                                  history.

                                  moveable.

        and moderate impacts are deemed to be significant, while minor and negligible impacts are not
        considered significant.

3.3      IMPACT ASSESSMENT
3.3.1.   This section describes the assessment of the potential impacts of landslides on the reservoir, the
         dam and communities surrounding the Project for both the construction phase and the operational
         phase.
         CONSTRUCTION
         Bulk Earthworks
3.3.2.   Construction activities such as bulk earthworks preparing a dam site or shaping dam foundations
         can potentially directly initiate slope instability. The magnitude of the impact of a slope failure due to
         bulk earthworks would be small for communities given that the sensitivity of communities is low as
         there are no communities, no critical infrastructure or heritage resources that are of local importance
         within 1 km of the construction activities. The significance of the effect is negligible.
         OPERATION
3.3.3.   Reservoir filling is expected to have significant impacts on stability of the slopes around the
         reservoir. The period of lake-filling, including operational reservoir fluctuations during that period and
         the following years or decades, and any additional adverse loading conditions, such as earthquakes
         or floods, are expected to have the associated highest likelihood of adverse reservoir landslide
         impacts. The potential impacts described above are therefore most likely to occur during those
         times.
3.3.4.   An analysis of the potential impacts of slope instability is required to assess the risk posed by the
         Project. This analysis will need to consider the communities, environment and infrastructure that will
         occupy the area around the reservoir that could be impacted by direct landslide displacement,
         flooding by an impact wave, inundated by a landslide dam or affected by flooding downstream of the


         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                       May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                     Page 19
         dam in the case of overtopping. Section 3.4 describes measures that will need to be implemented
         to mitigate slope instability impacts.
3.3.5.   A summary of the slope stability impacts for the operational phase is provided in the following
         sections.
         Landslide induced impact waves
3.3.6.   The presence of a reservoir introduces the potential for impact waves generated a landslide entering
         the reservoir impacting on communities, infrastructure and the environment adjacent to the
         reservoir. It is also possible that a landslide induced impact wave could damage or overtop the dam
         and cause damage downstream. The magnitude of the impact of landslide induced impact waves
         could be large, resulting in multiple fatalities, particularly high sensitivity communities and critical
         infrastructure around the reservoir edge (within 1 km of the reservoir and less than 100 m elevation
         above FSL). The significance of the potential for landslide induced impact waves is therefore major.
         Mitigation for the potential impact of landslide induced waves is to evaluate the potential for
         landslides around the reservoir perimeter and moving communities and critical infrastructure outside
         the potential impact zone if necessary. If communities and critical infrastructure is moved outside the
         area potentially affected by impact waves, the significance could be minor or negligible.
         Large scale landslide movement around the reservoir perimeter
3.3.7.   Interaction of the reservoir inundation and fluctuation during operation could increase activity of
         existing landslides or trigger new landslides. The magnitude of the impact of large scale landslide
         movement could be medium, for high sensitivity communities and critical infrastructure around the
         reservoir edge (within 1 km of the reservoir and less than 100 m elevation above FSL, in areas that
         are prone to landslide development). The significance of the potential for large scale landslide
         movement around the reservoir perimeter is therefore major. Mitigation of the potential impact of
         large scale landslide movement around the reservoir perimeter is to evaluate the likely performance
         of the areas around the reservoir. In areas where large scale landslide behaviour is judged to be
         likely to occur, communities or critical infrastructure could be relocated, or engineering works
         undertaken to reduce the likelihood of large scale movement. The significance could therefore be
         reduced to minor or negligible.
         Landslide dam breach in reservoir tributary
3.3.8.   Flood outflows from a breached landslide dam in a tributary of the reservoir could be very large and
         raise reservoir levels significantly. However, the large size of the reservoir and the fluctuating
         operational reservoir level will tend to mitigate the potential impact. The magnitude of impact is
         expected to be small, even for high sensitivity communities around the reservoir edge. The
         significance of landslide dams in reservoir tributaries is therefore potentially moderate. Mitigation for
         the impact of landslide dams is monitoring to ensure that any landslide dam is identified before there
         is any likelihood of a breach. Given sufficient warning and an emergency management plan, the risk
         to human life is expected to be low and consequently the significance of landslide dam breach is
         potentially minor. The potential for damage to communities living within the tributary valleys as a
         result of breach of a landslide dam, is not considered here because the risk exists without the
         proposed reservoir.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                    May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 20
         Reservoir shore erosion
3.3.9.   Erosion and minor landslide movement is anticipated to occur around the reservoir perimeter
         following filling. From a reservoir landslide perspective the magnitude of impact is expected to be
         small, even for high sensitivity communities around the reservoir edge. The significance of
         shoreline erosion is therefore potentially moderate. Mitigation of the impact of shoreline erosion
         requires evaluation of the potential erodibility of the ground around the reservoir perimeter. Where
         erosion effects are predicted to be significant, relocation of some dwellings, buildings or other
         infrastructure may be required, or engineering works constructed to mitigate the erosion risk. Given
         these considerations, the residual effect of shoreline erosion is anticipated to be minor.

3.4      MITIGATION OF RESERVOIR SLOPE STABILITY IMPACTS
3.4.1.   Before raising the dam height beyond the level for which slope stability assessment was completed
         in 2018, an effective programme of landslide reconnaissance, investigation and monitoring is an
         important tool in understanding the risk associated with reservoir filling and operation and the effect
         of the reservoir on the surrounding environment. Critical elements of managing reservoir landslide
         risk include comprehensive assessment of the slopes that will surround the final reservoir, including
         the one currently underway for filling up between 1100 m ASL and FSL of 1290 m ASL, and
         catchment as well as an assessment of the potential consequences of landslides entering the
         reservoir. The assessment of reservoir slopes should be undertaken by engineering geologists and
         geotechnical engineers with extensive experience of investigating landslides and developing risk
         mitigation. The assessment of reservoir landslides must be overseen by a suitable independent
         review panel experienced in reservoir landslide management to ensure that current international
         best practice is met. Assessments of reservoir slopes are required to be completed ahead of raising
         the reservoir to ensure that any mitigation measures, including engineered measures and
         emergency management plans, can be undertaken before the risk is realised. These studies will
         guide design development in accordance with the recommendations of the Dam Safety Panel of
         Experts (DSPoE) and international standards for reservoirs for such large dams.
3.4.2.   Seismic stability of slopes, including recognised landslides, around the reservoir should be analysed
         taking into account the results of the recent seismic hazard assessment (Seister 2024), including
         assessment ground motions, reservoir triggered seismicity rates and topographic amplification.
         Consideration may need to be given of the variability of ground shaking hazard around the reservoir,
         given its large size, which is not reported in the current seismic hazard assessment.
3.4.3.   Assessment of the potential effects of reservoir landslides on the Project must take into account the
         expected changes in climate. This is important because predicted increase in winter rainfall and
         glacial melting may have a significant impact on the likelihood and consequences of landslides
         affecting the reservoir.
3.4.4.   Hazard avoidance in siting: Avoid placing temporary infrastructure (e.g. construction camps,
         laydown areas) in known hazard-prone areas such as historic landslide tracks. This approach is
         consistent with the World Bank Environmental and Social Standard 4 (ESS4), which requires that
         infrastructure be designed and located to minimise community exposure to natural hazards.
3.4.5.   Emergency preparedness planning: Develop and disseminate site-specific emergency response
         plans for landslides. These must be aligned with the broader Emergency Preparedness and
         Response Framework required under ESS4 and reviewed by the DSPoE.



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                 PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                   May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 21
3.4.6.    Training and capacity building: Deliver hazard awareness training for all construction contractors
          and site personnel, including safe working practices, response procedures, and interpretation of
          early warnings. This supports both occupational safety and community safety risk reduction.
3.4.7.    Early warning and monitoring systems for planning construction activities: Although not formally
          mandated at the pre-construction stage, the establishment of monitoring networks for rainfall, river
          stage, slope movement, and seismic activity is strongly recommended to support hazard-informed
          design and construction planning. These systems should be so designed that they can be ultimately
          integrated into an early warning framework and inform hazard response protocols approved by the
          DSPoE, in line with good international practice and World Bank’s policies on Safety of Dams.
3.4.8.    During Construction: Slope stabilisation and excavation control: Implement targeted slope protection
          measures such as bolting, drainage, mesh, and shotcrete at high-risk excavation zones, tunnel
          portals, and dam abutments. Excavation sequencing must be guided by slope risk zonation and
          validated through continuous geotechnical supervision.
3.4.9.    Real-time monitoring systems: Maintain and expand monitoring networks slope deformation, and
          seismic activity across active construction zones. Threshold-based warning systems should trigger
          predefined response protocols and site evacuation if needed as per plans developed and adopted
          above.
3.4.10. First filling hazard assessment: For impoundment beyond 1100m ASL, complete the detailed risk
        assessment of slope stability and reservoir rim behaviour under changing hydraulic conditions,
        which has commenced in (2025). Conduct numerical modelling where needed to assess potential
        for reactivation of landslides or new instability zones.
3.4.11. Controlled first filling plan: Develop the Filling Plan and confirm its review by DSPoE review followed
        by their No Objection. The plan should include staged impoundment targets, continuous monitoring
        of slope response, clear decision points for hold levels, and protocols for controlled releases in case
        of instability or unforeseen inflow events.
3.4.12. During Operation phase: Protocols to be put in place to monitor landslide-prone slopes along the
        reservoir perimeter, particularly areas with known instability, fractured geology, or signs of previous
        movement. Long-term slope stability modelling may be required in zones identified in detailed
        assessment of slope stability – Stucky 2018a and the assessment currently underway for slopes at
        1100 m ASL and up to 1290 m ASL.
3.4.13. This should be in conjunction with the permanent network of seismic and ground deformation
        monitoring stations, given the site geology including the presence of Ionaksh and other significant
        faults. Reservoir-triggered seismicity and long-term slope movement around the reservoir rim should
        be tracked and evaluated periodically, particularly after large flood events or seasonal drawdown.
3.4.14. Use the channels available as part of the agreements related to Amu Darya water management to
        maintain open communication with downstream riparian states during abnormal or emergency
        operations including more advanced joint early warning mechanisms that strengthen real-time
        communication with downstream operators and authorities to support pre-emptive actions to reduce
        losses along the cascade.


3.4.15.



          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number                                                                  May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 22
        SUMMARY OF MITIGATION MEASURES
3.4.16. Effective management of reservoir slope stability risk requires:
         Sound knowledge of the reservoir geology, existing slope instability affecting the reservoir area
          and potential mechanisms of first-time failures that may occur when the changed conditions of
          the reservoir are implemented. Investigations of reservoir slopes must be undertaken by a
          suitably experienced team, well in advance of reservoir raising to ensure that landslide risks can
          be managed;
         Assessment of the potential impacts of the foreseen landslide types and expected behaviour on
          the area potentially affected;
         If the likelihood of unacceptable impacts occurring is too high, then a strategy for mitigating the
          effects will need to be implemented. This could include significant impacts on the Project as
          described above. Engineering measures to mitigate the effects of reservoir slope stability risk
          have been implemented on various dam projects around the world, including monitoring,
          drainage works, bulk earthworks and buttressing, and moving communities or critical
          infrastructure away from areas potentially affected by landslides or impact waves;
         An effective monitoring programme, likely including deformation and piezometric monitoring,
          when combined with a detailed knowledge of landslide mechanisms and behaviour, should be
          implemented and maintained to demonstrate that engineering assumptions about landslide
          behaviour remain valid and that landslide risk is being managed effectively;
         A controlled lakefilling programme, accompanied by the above monitoring programme, will be
          required to manage the potential effects of lakefilling. The proposed lakefilling will take about 16
          years to reach full storage level. This relatively long lakefilling programme will be helpful in
          understanding the progressive effects of the reservoir on surrounding slopes; and
         An effective emergency management plan must be developed ahead of raising the reservoir. The
          emergency management planning must take into account the results of reservoir landslide
          investigations and assessments of the potential consequences of landslides. The emergency
          management plan must clearly define roles and responsibilities of decision makers and key
          workers, define required response to potential emergency triggers including earthquakes,
          flooding, landslide dams and dam breach, include dissemination of appropriate information to the
          potentially affected communities, and preparedness training for parties responsible for
          emergency response.
         Oversight of reservoir landslide assessment and monitoring by an independent review panel of
          experts to ensure that international best practice is being considered and implemented where
          needed. Specific issues that will be reviewed by the panel of experts include:
           • Confirmation that the assessment of reservoir landslides has been thorough and robust.
           • Confirmation that the potential impacts of reservoir landslides have been adequately
             assessed, including the potential life safety risks to communities surrounding and downstream
             of the reservoir.
           • Confirmation that the reservoir landslide monitoring programme is appropriate for the types of
             landslides that have been identified and that frequency of data collection and reporting is
             appropriate.
           • Regular review of the behaviour of reservoir landslides is acceptable and the risk is
             appropriately managed.




        Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
        Project No.: project number                                                                  May 2025
        Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 23
3.5      RESIDUAL EFFECTS
3.5.1.   It is anticipated that a long-term landslide management system will be developed and implemented
         prior to lakefilling and continued for the operational life of the reservoir. The elements of the
         management plan are described in Section 9. This approach to managing reservoir landslide risk is
         in line with international practice as described in ICOLD Bulletin 124. As described in Section 3.3
         the residual impact of reservoir landslides should be minor.
3.5.2.   An effective reservoir landslide management system is expected to maintain long term risk to
         acceptable levels.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment              PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number                                                                May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                              Page 24
3.6      SUMMARY
3.6.1.   Reservoir landslides have the potential to severely impact communities and infrastructure around
         the reservoir. Impacts include inundation by impact waves, damage due to large scale ground
         deformation, landslide dam breach flooding and reservoir edge erosion. The potential significance of
         these impacts could be moderate or major. However, with a thorough evaluation of the slopes
         surrounding the reservoir, appropriate planning, potential engineering mitigation works and
         development of emergency management plans, the impact significance of reservoir landslide could
         be reduced to minor.

Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment   PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                          May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun”, DFZ
1        INTRODUCTION
1.1.1.   The Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) for the Rogun Hydropower Plant (HPP)
         being constructed on the Vakhsh River in Tajikistan is being updated. The Rogun Dam is located in
         the upper reaches of the Vakhsh River at the head of a cascade of HPPs. The Nurek Reservoir is
         the next reservoir and HPP downstream of Rogun Reservoir. Nurek Dam is located about 70 km
         downstream of the Rogun Dam with the incremental catchment area between Nurek and Rogun
         Reservoirs contributing an insignificant streamflow volume. Water is abstracted for irrigation and
         domestic use from the Vakhsh River.
1.1.2.   The construction of Rogun Reservoir has started with two turbines available for electricity
         generation. The dam will be raised to its final crown elevation of 1 300 meters above sea level
         (masl) by 2033 (WSP, 2024). The hydropower capacity will also be increased to 3 600 MW by 2032
         with the installation of a further 4 turbines and the upgrade of the existing two turbines.
1.1.3.   The impact of the change in flow rates and flow pattern on the ecology and the downstream users
         are of concern related to filling and operation of the Rogun HPP. The flow changes between Rogun
         and Nurek Reservoirs as well as below Nurek Reservoir are assessed in this report using a
         simulation model of the Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs based on the operating rule described in the
         Techno-Economic Assessment (TEAS) of the Rogun Hydroelectric Project (OSHPC Barki Tojik,
         2013).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment             PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                    May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun”, DFZ                                                 Page | 1
2        REGIONAL SETTING
2.1.1.   The Vakhsh River catchment is part of the Amu Darya River Basin. The location of the Vakhsh River
         catchment in the Amu Darya River Basin is shown in Figure 2-1. The Amu Darya River water
         resource is shared between Afghanistan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, Tajikistan, Kirgizstan and the
         Aral Sea. The allocation of the water resource between the basin countries, other than Afghanistan,
         is governed by the Nukus Declaration and Protocol 566. The Amu Darya Basin Water Organisation
         implements the declaration and protocol.
2.1.2.   Rogun HPP is located approximately 70 km upstream of the Nurek Dam on the Vakhsh River. The
         Rogun Dam site is located 34 km downstream of the confluence of the Surkhob and Obihingou
         Rivers, which are the two main tributaries of the catchment and join to form the Vakhsh River
         (Figure 2-2). At the Rogun Dam site, the Vakhsh River drains a catchment area of approximately
         30 390 km² and at this location the river flows through a narrow, 400 to 500 m deep V-shaped gorge
         with gradients of the valley sides up to 50° (Pöyry Energy Ltd, 2014).
2.1.3.   The Surkhob River flows from the north-eastern part of the catchment, bordered to the north by the
         Pamir mountains. The Obihingou flows from the south-eastern part of the catchment and drains the
         mountains of the central Pamir, particularly the Somoni Peak Range at 7 495 mamsl.
2.1.4.   Approximately 30% of the Vakhsh River catchment lies above 4 000 mamsl, within the snow and
         glacier cover zone. Among the many glaciers which feed the Vakhsh tributaries, the Fedchenko
         Glacier is reported to extend 77 km, and is the world longest glacier outside the Polar Regions, with
         a maximum thickness of 1,000 metres. Within the catchment, the total glacier area is estimated to
         be between 3 882 km² and 5 000 km², which represent between 13% and 16% of the Vakhsh River
         catchment at Rogun Dam (Coyne et Bellier, 2014b).




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                       May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun”, DFZ                                                    Page | 2
Figure 2-1 – Rogun Dam locality map




Figure 2-2 – Rogun Dam location map, Vakhsh River and tributaries
3        VAKHSH CASCADE OF HPPS
3.1.1.   A schematic of the cascade of HPP and locations of the irrigation abstractions is shown in Figure 3-
         1. The Rogun (once construction completed) and Nurek Reservoirs are the largest reservoirs in the
         cascade and have sufficient capacity to provide flow regulation. The Shurob Reservoir is planned to
         be constructed between Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs. The Shurob Reservoir is being planned and
         is not considered in the simulations. The capacity of the remaining reservoirs in the cascade are
         small and are treated as run of river HPPs. There are abstractions for irrigation and some domestic
         use from the reservoirs and river reaches between the reservoirs. Water is abstracted from the river
         into the Main Vakhsh canal to supply water for irrigation and two HPP before returning water to the
         river. The return flows include irrigation flushing water and the return from the Main Vakhsh canal.
         The full cascade was simulated in the TEAS (OSHPC Barki Tojik, 2013). In the current assessment,
         the full cascade is not modelled. The Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs are modelled to assess the
         change in flows in the river reach between Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs and the flows downstream
         from Nurek Reservoir resulting from Rogun HPP.

         Figure 3-1 - Schematic of cascade of HPP (taken from (OSHPC Barki Tojik, 2013))
3.1.2.   The construction of Nurek HPP began in 1961 and the first turbine was operating in 1972. By 1979,
         9 turbines had been installed giving a total of 2 700 MW. The hydropower capacity was increased to
         3 000 MW in 1988 (OSHC Barqi Tojik, 2020). The capacity of the dam has reduced due to
         sedimentation. The rate of sedimentation of Nurek Reservoir will be reduced by Rogun Reservoir.
3.1.3.   The construction of Rogun Dam started in the 1970s and has been through a number of stop/start
         cycles with the project implementation resuming in 2008. The coffer dam was destroyed in 1993 by
         a flood. Maintenance work was undertaken on the dam in about May 2008 to prevent additional
         damage to the dam structures already built. Water is currently impounded to an elevation of
         1 075 masl and conditions are being discussed to enable impoundment to an elevation of
         1 100 masl planned for mid-October 2026 (Gruner, 2024). The impounding of water to the reservoir
         Full Storage Level (FSL) of 1 290 masl is planned to be able to start by November 2033.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
4        MODELLING APPROACH
4.1.1.   A simulation model was built of the Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs using the GoldSim simulation
         software (www.Goldsim.com). The model used the monthly average flow and precipitation data for
         the period 1932 to 2022 as input. Flow and precipitation data were time shifted by a year to allow the
         simulation of the system for different sequences of flow and precipitation. Time shifting allows for the
         assessment of the impact of wet and dry periods on filling times and flow changes due to Rogun
         Reservoir.
4.1.2.   The rules developed in the TEAS were applied in the model to calculate the discharge from the
         reservoirs. The results of the TEAS showed that the optimal rule to operate the Nurek and Rogun
         Reservoirs was to use Rogun Reservoir for regulation and operate Nurek Reservoir similar to a run
         of river HPP. The application of this rule resulted in the discharges from the Nurek and Rogun
         reservoirs being similar. The present model was set up to operate the reservoirs using this rule.
4.1.3.   The approach followed in the TEAS was to calibrate an operating rule based on the measured
         releases from Nurek Dam over the period 1991 to 2008. The construction of Rogun Reservoir was
         halted during this period and the coffer dam was destroyed in 1993 by a flood. Maintenance work
         was undertaken on the dam in about May 2008 to prevent additional damage to the dam structures
         already built. The construction of the dam was resumed in 2016. The flows at a monthly time step
         were unlikely to have been affected by the construction of Rogun Dam during the period 1991 to
         2008. An operating rule curve based on the average monthly measured water elevations in the
         reservoir was adjusted in the TEAS simulation model to achieve the best calibration against the
         measured outflows from the reservoir and energy production. The operating rule curve of monthly
         water elevations is presented in Figure 4-1. The Operating Rule Curve represents the desired water
         level in the reservoir. Water is stored or discharged through the turbines and spillways to reach the
         water level imposed by the rule. The rule shows that water is stored in the reservoir over the high
         flow summer period for release during the winter period to generate energy during the winter period.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
         Figure 4-1 - Operating rule curve used to calculate the discharges from Nurek Reservoir
         (taken from (OSHPC Barki Tojik, 2013))

4.1.4.   The model was applied to Nurek without Rogun Reservoir. The discharges from the reservoir were
         simulated for the calibration period for the TEAS (1991 to 2008). The monthly averages and the flow
         duration curves obtained from the simulation results were compared to TEAS results to confirm the
         correct operation of the model.
4.1.5.   The Rogun Reservoir was added to the model with the discharges from Rogun as the inflows to
         Nurek. The operation rule was applied to Nurek Reservoir to calculate the outflows required from the
         reservoir as per the operating rule curve. The calculated outflows were then applied to Rogun
         Reservoir to ensure that the same releases required from Nurek Reservoir were made from Rogun.
         In this way, the Nurek Reservoir will operate similar to a run of river HPP. Additional water will be
         released from Nurek Reservoir when the releases from Rogun do not meet the releases required
         from Nurek when applying the operating rule curve. The model was applied to the Rogun Reservoir
         filling and operations. Statistics of the discharges from the reservoirs were compared to the natural
         flows and discharges from Nurek Reservoir if Rogun Reservoir had not been built.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
5        MODEL INPUTS

5.1      HYDROLOGY
         FLOWS
5.1.1.   Due to the limited flow timeseries available at the Rogun Dam location, a monthly flow record was
         compiled (Coyne et Bellier, 2014a) by combining data from different hydrometric stations, as follows:
          From 1932 to 1972: flow rates recorded at Tutkaul gauging station (see Figure 2-2).
          From 1973 to 1988: flow rates at Tutkaul are reconstituted based on observations made at
           Komsomolabad. Correlations between the two stations are based on two periods of common
           record (1949-1957 and 1963-1972).
          From 1988 to 2008: flow rates are calculated based on Nurek HPP inflows.
          From 2008 to 2022: flow rates were provided by the World Bank. WSP understands that the flow
           rates for this additional (2008 to 2022) data have been calculated as per Nurek HPP data.
5.1.2.   The inflows to the Nurek Reservoir calculated using the Nurek HPP data are considered to be valid
         for the Rogun site given the incremental catchment area between the dams is small (less than 3% of
         the catchment) (Coyne et Bellier, 2014b). The flows calculated using the Nurek Reservoir HPP flows
         are considered representative of the flow conditions at Rogun Reservoir.
5.1.3.   In summary, the Rogun Reservoir flow data used for this assessment has been sourced from:
          (Coyne et Bellier, 2014b): Appendix 2, Table 1, for the period 1932-2007; and
          World Bank provided data, for the period 2008-2022.
5.1.4.   The average monthly streamflow record, annual totals and a box and whisker plot of the average
         monthly flows are plotted in Figure 5-1 and Figure 5-2 and Figure 5-3 respectively. The plot of the
         accumulative total of the runoff volumes is shown in Figure 5-4.
5.1.5.   The plots highlight the following:
          The streamflow is seasonal with the low flows occurring during the winter period. The lowest
           flows occur in February before the snow melt starts in spring.
          The flows during the winter period do not vary significantly and range from 0.3 to 0.9 km3/month.
          The annual streamflow total is variable ranging from 13 km3/yr to 26 km3/yr with an average of
           20 km3/yr (634 m3/s).
          The highest streamflow occurs in July.
          The streamflow during the summer months is more variable than the winter streamflow.
          The plot of the accumulative streamflow total shows a consistent slope and does not highlight any
           anomalies.

         PRECIPITATION
5.1.6.   Most of the precipitation in the project area falls during the winter and spring months, while
         precipitation is low in the summer months. Winter precipitation falls mainly as snow (Coyne et
         Bellier, 2014a). A monthly precipitation record was sourced from the Garm meteorological station
         located in the area of the Rogun HPP dam at an elevation of 1 316 meters above mean sea level
         (mamsl). The station had a record with data available from 1931 to 2010. The monthly average
         precipitation is plotted in Figure 5-5. The precipitation depths were taken to be water depths.

         Figure 5-5 –Average monthly precipitation depths




         EVAPORATION

         average monthly depths are presented in Table 5-1. The average monthly depths are used to
         estimate the volumes evaporated from the water surface in the reservoirs.

 6       RESERVOIR CHARACTERISTICS

 6.1     ROGUN RESERVOIR
6.1.1.   The characteristics of the final Rogun HPP are summarised in Table 6-1. As of late 2024, the dam
         has been constructed to an elevation of 1 100 masl with two turbines (U5 and U6) installed (WSP,


         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
         2024). The available power of the turbines is anticipated to be 408 MW by 2027 with the
         replacement of the runners. The dam will be raised to a final full storage level (FSL) of 1 290 masl
         by November 2033 with a minimum operating level (MOL) of 1 185 masl (Tractebel Engineering
         S.A./ELC Electroconsult S.P.A., 2024). The MOL is the level in the reservoir below which the water
         level should not be lowered to prevent sediment and debris from entering the turbines. A further 2
         turbines (U3 and U4) will be added by January 2029 when the dam reaches 1 175 masl with the
         final two turbines added by July 2031 when the dam reaches 1 230 masl in about 2032.

 6.2     NUREK RESERVOIR
6.2.1.   Bathymetric surveys of the dam basin have been undertaken but the accuracy of the surveys have
         been questioned (OSHPC Barki Tojik, 2013). The two approaches used to estimate the stage
         storage curve for the reservoir basin were:
          A formula was developed for the TEAS using the measured water levels and inflow and outflow
           volumes from Nurek (OSHPC Barki Tojik, 2013).
          The available radar and satellite survey data was also used to estimate the storage
           characteristics of the reservoir basin. The water storage volumes below the water surface were
           not able to be measured as the radar did not penetrate through the water surface and
           radar/satellite data was not available for the period before the dam started filling.

         used in the simulations. The stage-storage-area curves are plotted in Figure 6-2.

 7       RESULTS

 7.1     MODEL CALIBRATION PERIOD
7.1.1.   The simulation model used in the TEAS was calibrated against the measured outflows from Nurek
         Reservoir for the period 1991 to 2008. The construction of Rogun Reservoir was halted during this
         period and the coffer dam was destroyed in 1993 by a flood. Maintenance work was undertaken on
         the dam in about May 2008 to prevent additional damage to the dam structures already built. The
         construction of the dam was resumed in 2016. The flows at a monthly time step were unlikely to
         have been affected by the construction of Rogun Dam during the period 1991 to 2008. The
         simulation model built for this assessment was run for the same period and operating rules and the
         simulated discharges compared to the results from the TEAS. The results from the TEAS are shown
         in Figure 7-1 and from the simulation model developed for this assessment in Figure 7-2. The
         average flows and the flow duration curve comparing the simulated and historic outflows from Nurek
         Reservoir are shown in Figure 7-3 and Figure 7-4 respectively.
7.1.2.   The results from the model developed for this water resource assessment have similar patterns,
         monthly average flows and flow duration curve to the historic Nurek Reservoir outflows. The model
         is considered suitable for use in assessing the impact on the flows resulting from the filling and
         operation of Rogun Reservoir.
