Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
 8       ROGUN RESERVOIR FILLING
8.1.1.   The guiding principle for the impoundment of Rogun reservoir is that the Government of Tajikistan
         will impound the flows that are otherwise not-used from its annual water share allocation, as per the
         water allocations to countries regionally agreed each year at ICWC meetings. Historically, the
         unused water share allocation of Tajikistan has been in average higher than 1.2 km3/year. Tajikistan
         will respect at all times the regional water sharing agreements during the impoundment of Rogun
         reservoir, and will not go beyond its allocated water share. It is expected that in ICWC negotiations
         each year, TJ will likely get a greater allocation during above-average years (water abundant years)
         and the unused water share will exceed the historical average; and conversely, during drought
         years, the unused allocation may be smaller than the historical average. For this reason, the
         impoundment of Rogun reservoir will depend mainly on the volume of inflows from the Vakhsh River
         during impoundment years. Given the need to respect the regional water sharing agreements at all
         times, an exact, fixed impoundment plan is difficult to produce.
8.1.2.   Based on the construction progress, an illustrative schedule of reservoir impounding elevations and
         the volume of water that could be stored in the reservoir at each elevation are listed in Table 8-1
         (Tractebel Engineering S.A./ELC Electroconsult S.P.A., 2024). The dates are based on the
         optimised dates for the overall implementation programme for dam construction. The dates and
         elevations listed in Table 8-1 are the dates at which water can be impounded to that elevation in the
         reservoir if the construction of the dam embankment and associated infrastructure (flood
         management structures: diversion tunnels and spillways at different elevations, etc) has progressed
         sufficiently to allow for the impoundment of water to that elevation. Table 8-1 is tentative based on
         early planning and it is liable to be revised towards later completion of impoundment. Table 8-1 -
         Planned tentative impoundment elevations, water volumes that can be stored at the elevations

          Date            Impounding Elevation (masl)      Storage Volume (Km3)

          1 Jan 2025      1 075                            0.56

          15 Oct 2026     1 100                            0.86

          15 Aug 2028     1 175                            2.68

          15 June 2032    1 230                            6.16

          15 Nov 2033     1 290                            13.30

8.1.3.   The volume of water proposed to be used to fill Rogun Reservoir is the difference between
         Tajikistan’s annual allocation from the Vakhsh River made as per the protocols and that years
         expected water use by Tajikistan. The allocation (non-vegetation and vegetation), actual water used
         from the Vakhsh River as given in the annual reports on the SIC ICWC web site (http://sic.icwc-
         aral.uz/reports_e.htm) are presented in Table 8-2 and plotted in Figure 8-1. The differential
         between the allocation and actual use is included as well as the annual total streamflow volume
         (January to December) at Rogun Dam site..
8.1.4.   The information shown in Table 8-2 and Figure 8-1 reflects the following:
          The lower allocation and actual use in 2012 are due to the data for the non-vegetation water
           allocation and use was not available on the web site.


         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
 The allocation is fairly constant over the period 2010 to 2023. The allocation has been constant in
  time because Tajikistan has not been using its full allocation, in wet or dry years. Thus, in the
  historical period there is no relationship between allocation and VaKhsh river streamflows.
 The use varies over the period as indicated by the varying differential between allocation and
  actual use. The average differential is 1.33 km3/yr which is higher than the 1.211 km3/yr used in
  the filling assessment undertaken in the TEAS.
 On one hand, the data indicates that the differential between the allocation and actual use, i.e.
  the unused share available for filling the reservoir, shows a declining trend over the time period
  analysed. The differential is particularly low in 2022 and 2023. Tajikistan will have to manage the
  water use to realise the volume required to fill Rogun Reservoir.
 On the other hand, it is well known that a significant part of the flows diverted into canals in
  Tajikistan return back to the river, and the actual use of the water share is overestimated. Two
  simple examples are as follows: (i) in winter, the irrigation canals downstream of the cascade are
  used for the several HPPs existing along the canals but there is no consumptive use and the
  totality of the water diverted returns to the river; (ii) the conveyance and irrigation efficiency in
  Tajikistan is low, with numerous return flows to the river. The Ministry of Energy and Water
  Resources of Tajikistan is making steps to install hydroposts to measure these unaccounted
  return flows to the river, which are significant.
 As Tajikistan has not used its full allocation, the ICWC-agreed allocation has been constant in
  time, without any increases during wet years. This is subject to change in future yearly ICWC
  meeting negotiations given the need to impound Rogun..

8.1.5.   As a sensitivity analysis to assess the impacts of different filling rates, the filling times were
         estimated for several average differentials, or unused-allocations: a low differential – 0.59 km3/year
         (5 percentile), the filling rate of 1.211 km3/year (close to the average) used in the TEAS and a high
         differential 2.09 km3/year (95 percentile). The start water elevation in the reservoir was taken as the
         1075 mamsl or 0.56 km3 of water that is in storage in the reservoir in January 2025. The balance of
         the water not used to fill the dam is released downstream to Nurek Reservoir through the turbines
         and spillways. Nurek Reservoir was operated using the operation rule curve as used in the TEAS
         and given in Figure 4-1.
8.1.6.   The simulation model was used to assess:
          The filling times for Rogun Reservoir for the three fill rates.
          The impact of the filling of Rogun Reservoir on the flows in the river reach between Rogun and
           Nurek reservoirs.
          The impact of the filling of Rogun Reservoir on the releases from Nurek Reservoir.
8.1.7.   The simulated trajectories of the water volumes stored in the Rogun Reservoir during filling for the
         three fill rates are plotted in Figure 8-2. The estimated fill dates are by December 2048 (23 years),
         July 2038 (13 years) and December 2035 (10 years) for the 0.59 km3/yr, 1.211 km3/yr and 2.09
         km3/yr respectively.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan
         Figure 8-2 - Plot of simulated volumes in storage during filling of Rogun for the three
         constant filling rates and allowable impoundment as per construction progress.

8.1.8.   The water volume available for filling the reservoir is dependent on the differential between the
         allocation and water use so the actual time to fill will fall between the 23 and 10 years simulated for
         the low and high fill scenarios. The annual allocation will be calculated based on the protocols and
         the fill rate will be actively managed by Tajikistan based on the expected use and the allocation.
8.1.9.   The model was used to simulate the impact of the different filling rates on the flows discharged from
         Rogun Reservoir to Nurek. The model was run by time shifting the inflows to Rogun by a year and
         simulating the flows downstream of Rogun for the three fill rates. The time shifting of the inflows
         allows for the simulation of the discharges for different inflow sequences to Rogun Reservoir. The
         inflow volumes exceeding the fill volume were assumed to be released downstream either through
         the turbines, spillways or flow bypass infrastructure. The minimum downstream flow rate simulated
         for each month are compared to the minimum inflow rate and the Environmental Water
         Requirements (EWR) in Figure 8-3. The simulations showed that for the three filing rates, the EWR
         are always met for the reach of the Vakhsh River between Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs.




         ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

         Project Management Group for Energy Facilities Construction under the President of the Republic of Tajikistan

          three fill rates compared to the inflow and EWR flows

8.1.10.   The model was applied using the operating rule curve shown in Figure 4-1 to simulate the flows
          released from Nurek Reservoir during the filling period for comparison to the flows that would have
          been released if Rogun Reservoir was not in place. The assessment was done for the inflow
          sequence with the lowest total volume for the filling duration for the three fill rates. This will give a
          conservative view of the impact of the filling of Rogun Reservoir on the flows able to be released
          from Nurek Reservoir. The dates of the selected flow sequences, total runoff volume, average
          annual total and the minimum annual total over the filling period are listed in Error! Reference source
          not found..
8.1.11.   The percentiles (exceedance percentage) of the releases from Nurek Reservoir with and without
          Rogun were calculated from the simulation results for the three filing rates. The decrease in the
          flows due to the filling of Rogun were also calculated and are presented in Error! Reference source
          not found. together with the percentiles.
8.1.12.   The results show the following:
           The volumes that will be released from Nurek are reduced during the filling of Rogun.
           The higher the filling rate the larger the reduction in flows from Nurek Reservoir.
           The percent reduction is the largest for the low flows (95th percentile) during the winter period.

8.1.13.   This sensitivity analysis simulations show the order of magnitude of flow reductions below Nurek
          during the filling period, assuming the impoundment rate is uniform throughout the year. The
          reduction depends on the filling rate but, if applied uniformly, could significantly affect the 95th
          percentile low flows for high fill rates. While the discharges from Nurek will be reduced, Tajikistan
          have agreed not to exceed their allocation in filling the reservoir so on a basin scale, the filling of the
          reservoir will not affect the flows expected from the Vakhsh River to the downstream river system
          based on the international agreements. Realistically, most of the impounded volumes are likely to
          take place during the summer peak flows and when electricity demand is lowest.

 9       JOINT OPERATION OF ROGUN AND NUREK
9.1.1.   The model was applied to assess the change of flows between Rogun Reservoir and Nurek
         Reservoir and downstream of Nurek Reservoir after the Rogun Reservoir filling period. The Rogun
         and Nurek reservoirs are planned to be operated together (OSHPC Barki Tojik, 2013) with Rogun
         Reservoir providing the regulation and Nurek HPP being operated similar to a run of river HPP. The
         start volumes in the dams were set at the minimum operating level and the system was simulated
         from 2025 to 2115. Nurek Reservoir without Rogun Reservoir was also modelled as a base case to
         simulate the flows downstream of Nurek Reservoir against which to compare the flows downstream
         of Nurek Reservoir with Rogun Reservoir in place. In the simulations, no allowance was made for
         maintenance of turbines. It was assumed that the turbines are available all the time.
         ASSESSMENT OF FLOW CHANGES BETWEEN ROGUN AND NUREK RESERVOIRS
9.1.2.   The simulated flows downstream of Rogun Reservoir are compared to the inflows to assess the
         changes in flow resulting from Rogun Reservoir HPP. The average monthly inflow and simulated
         release volumes over the simulation period are plotted in Figure 9-1.These changes are not
         expected to be significantly different under climate change conditions for the next 50 to 100 years,
         as the main impacts of climate change are expected to be an earlier snowmelt and a shift of the
         hydrograph towards earlier in the year.
9.1.3.   The plot (Figure 9-1) shows that the discharges from Rogun Reservoir during the low flow winter
         months are higher than the natural flows into Rogun Reservoir based on the HPP operating rule.
         The flows released from Rogun Reservoir during the summer months are reduced as water is stored
         in the reservoir to maintain the releases during winter required to generate electricity. The overall
         inflow volume to the reservoir is released but the release pattern is different with flow from the high
         summer flows stored for release during the winter. The average volume shifted annually from
         summer to winter was simulated to be 4.2 km3. This is the current release pattern for Nurek
         Reservoir with up to 4.5 km3 shifted from summer to winter. Tajikistan will continue to implement the
         current operating rules and will not increase the water volume shifted from summer to winter.

         Figure 9-1 – Plot of average monthly inflows and outflows from Rogun Reservoir over the
         simulation period 2025 to 2115

         ASSESSMENT OF FLOW CHANGES DOWNSTREAM OF NUREK RESERVOIR
9.1.4.   The guiding principle of the operation of the combined Rogun and Nurek reservoirs is that the flow
         regime below Nurek is not expected to change from the historical period, given that Tajikistan has
         committed to not shift more than 4.2 km3/year from summer to winter. This is the amount historically
         shifted by Nurek operations (emptying itself in winter, and being refilled in summer, by the amount of
         4.2 km3/year). This commitment from Tajikistan is reflected in the minutes of riparian consultations as
         well as in the financing agreement as per the obligation to comply at all times with the water sharing
         agreements (which include the national water allocations and the seasonally agreed deliveries) and
         avoiding significant harm downstream. Thus, after impoundment, the presence and operation of
         Rogun is likely to be invisible to downstream countries, given that the flow regime downstream of
         Nurek will not change from the historical period.
9.1.5.   The flows released from Nurek Reservoir without Rogun and with Rogun Reservoir in place were
         simulated. A plot of the median of the monthly flows for the two cases are compared in Figure 9-2.
         The plot shows that the inflow to Nurek from Rogun is released without any changes in the flow pattern
         indicating that Nurek is being used as a run of river HPP. The median of the simulated releases from
         Nurek with and without Rogun Reservoir in place are the same. This is due to the Nurek operating
         rule curve continuing to be applied to Nurek with releases being made from Rogun to keep Nurek full
         so that Nurek is operated as a run of river HPP. The simulations show that based on the operating
         rules, the impact of Rogun on the median flows downstream of Nurek is insignificant.

         Figure 9-2 – Median of simulated releases from Nurek Reservoir with and without Rogun
         Reservoir in place

 10       CONCLUSIONS
10.1.1.   The following conclusions can be made as a result of this study:
           A simulation model was built of the Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs. The model was applied to
            simulate the Nurek Reservoir releases based on the operating rule curve given in the TEAS,
            which is designed to ensure Tajikistan meets its obligations under international agreements. The
            model results compared favourably with those of the TEAS. The model calibration was therefore
            considered adequate to use in assessing flow changes due to the filling and operation of Rogun
            Reservoir.
           The filling of Rogun will not be at a constant rate each year, as it will depend on the river flows
            and the agreed water allocation for each particular year. As a sensitivity analysis, model
            simulations were performed to assess the filling time of Rogun Reservoir for filling rates of 0.59
            km3/year (low), 1.211 km3/year (TEAS) and 2.09 km3/year (high). The estimated time of filling for
            the three fill rates is 2047 (23 years), 2037 (13 years) and 2034 (10 years) for the low, TEAS and
            high filling rates respectively. The flows discharged from Rogun Reservoir will be reduced during
            the filling period but the EWR are still met for all the filling rates simulated.
           The releases from Nurek Reservoir will be reduced during the filling period. The reduction
            depends on the filling rate. The higher the filling rate the larger the reduction but the shorter the
            time period. Tajikistan will use the difference between their allocation determined under the
            international water sharing agreements and the estimated use of the allocation as the fill volume.
            While the discharges from Nurek will be reduced, Tajikistan have agreed not to exceed their
            allocation in filling the reservoir so on a basin scale, the filling of the reservoir will not affect the
            flows expected from the Vakhsh River to the downstream river system based on the international
            agreements.
           During operations, the flow pattern in the river reach between Rogun and Nurek Reservoirs is
            changed due to the HPP operating rule. The high summer flows are reduced, and water is stored
            in Rogun for release during winter. The winter flows will be higher udue to the energy generation
            requirements. This flow pattern currently downstream of Nurek will continue to be the case with
            Rogun Reservoir in place.
           The simulations showed that the proposed operation of Nurek and Rogun reservoirs will not
            impact flows downstream from Nurek. The current discharge pattern will be maintained with the
            shift of a maximum of 4.5 km3 of water annually from summer to the winter months.




          ROGUN HYDROPOWER PROJECT – UPDATED ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ
1        INTRODUCTION
1.1.1.   This Annex provides an assessment of the nature and extent of Critical Habitat relevant to the
         Project, together with an assessment of the Project against the requirements of World Bank’s
         Environmental and Social Standard 6 (ESS6). It has the following aims:
            • To identify biodiversity that would qualify as Critical Habitat (as defined under ESS6);
            • Assess the implications of Critical Habitat on the Project; and
            • Outline the mitigation measures required for the Project to progress in line with ESS6.
1.1.2.   The report also identifies and defines areas of Natural Habitat, working through a similar process to
         ensure ongoing Project compliance with World Bank ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and
         Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources.

1.2      WORLD BANK ESS6
1.2.1.   ESS6 has four specific objectives:
            • to protect and conserve biodiversity and habitats;
            • to apply the mitigation hierarchy (as set out in ESS1) and the precautionary approach in the
              design and implementation of projects that could have an impact on biodiversity;
            • to promote the sustainable management of living natural resources; and
            • to support livelihoods of local communities, including Indigenous Peoples, and inclusive
              economic development, through the adoption of practices that integrate conservation needs
              and development priorities.
1.2.2.   ESS6 is applied to a project based upon the perceived risks to biodiversity, ecosystem services, and
         natural resources, which are screened as part of the risks and impacts identification process as set
         out in ESS1.
1.2.3.   Under ESS6, habitats are classified as either Natural or Modified Habitats, based upon the level of
         human influence that has occurred.
1.2.4.   Modified habitats are areas that may contain a large proportion of plant and/or animal species of
         nonnative origin, and/or where human activity has substantially modified an area’s primary
         ecological functions and species composition.
1.2.5.   Natural habitats are areas composed of viable assemblages of plant and/or animal species of
         largely native origin, and/or where human activity has not essentially modified an area’s primary eco
1.2.6.   Critical Habitats being a subset of either of these habitat types that reflects the most significant
         biodiversity values.

1.3      THE PROJECT
1.3.1.   A full Project description is provided with Volume 1, Chapter 3 of the ESIA. However, to provide
         context for the reader, a summary is included as follows.
1.3.2.   The Project comprises a partially completed rock-filled dam across the Vakhsh River together with
         the filling of the reservoir up until the maximum inundation level (i.e. to 1,290 m – hereafter referred
         to as the ‘flooding zone’), along with other Project components such as new roads and stockpiling



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC | WSP
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                           May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 1 of 56
                                                                                                        Official Use Only
         areas. Consequently, this Critical Habitat Assessment (CHA) has been conducted within the context
         of a heavily modified landscape.
1.3.3.   The timeline for completion of the Project and flooding of the reservoir to maximum inundation level
         (1290m) is currently 2038.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment           PUBLIC | WSP
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                      May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                            Page 2 of 56
                                                                                                   Official Use Only
2        METHODOLOGY

2.1      THE CRITICAL HABITAT ASSESSMENT PROCESS
2.1.1.   The CHA process assesses the importance of a defined area to one or more biodiversity receptors
         that meet the requirements of ESS6 in terms of Critical Habitat.
2.1.2.   Determining the presence of Critical Habitat/Natural Habitat is a process that is explicitly separate
         from any consideration of a project’s impacts, mitigation strategy, etc. The only influence of a project
         on the CHA process is for identification of the location and extent of the area to be assessed. This is
         determined by the Project’s Area of Influence (AoI).
2.1.3.   The AoI is the area over which impacts from the Project could legitimately be expected to occur.
         This is described fully within Volume 1, Chapter 15: Biodiversity of this ESIA.
2.1.4.   To assist with the determination of Critical Habitat and Natural Habitat, the guidance described
         within updated ESS6 Guidance Note 6 has been followed (World Bank, 2018). This has been
         supplemented through explicit reference to International Finance Corporation (IFC) Performance
         Standard 6 with regards criteria thresholds for defining Critical Habitat (IFC, 2019).
2.1.5.   ESS6 defines Critical Habitat/Natural Habitat as:
            • Critical Habitats fall into one (or more) of the following categories:
               a. Habitat of significant importance to Critically Endangered or Endangered species, as listed
                  in the IUCN Red List of threatened species or equivalent national approaches;
               b.   Habitat of significant importance to endemic or restricted-range species;
               c.    Habitat supporting globally or nationally significant concentrations of migratory or
                    congregatory species;
               d.   Highly threatened or unique ecosystems; and
               e. Ecological functions or characteristics that are needed to maintain the viability of the
                  biodiversity values described above in (a) to (d).
2.1.6.   This CHA will also consider the presence of Natural Habitat, which is defined as areas composed of
         viable assemblages of plant and/or animal species of largely native origin, and/or where human
         activity has not essentially modified an area’s primary ecological functions and species composition.
2.1.7.   Any habitat that triggers one (or more) of the above will result in Critical Habitat/Natural Habitat
         being classified (as such, features being considered against the above criteria are referred to as
         potential Critical Habitat or Natural Habitat ‘triggers’).
2.1.8.   ESS6 makes explicit reference to International Finance Corporation’s Performance Standard 6
         (PS6) to assist in implementing ESS6; as such, the systematic process to undertaking CHA,
         comprising four steps, is used here to frame this report.
         STEP 1 – DEFINE THE STUDY AREA
2.1.9.   It is necessary for an appropriately defined area to be assessed for the presence of Critical Habitat
         and Natural Habitat. IFC PS6 and other similar guidance refers to an ‘ecological appropriate area of
         analysis’ (EAAA), which is the wider distribution of a feature or receptor (often at a landscape level)



         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                PUBLIC | WSP
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                           May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                 Page 3 of 56
                                                                                                        Official Use Only
          within which there is connectivity between the ecological process that supports the feature or
          receptor. The EAAA is used throughout this report as the basis for the application of each of the
          criteria thresholds to determine Critical Habitat.
2.1.10.   Defining an EAAA is an important step in the CHA process as it ensures assessment of an
          ecologically relevant feature or area, rather than an area influenced only by the project footprint. It
          also has inherent appreciation of ecological function across an area, and therefore avoids the risk of
          considering the specific areas in which a Critical Habitat trigger may be present discontinuously or
          seasonally.
2.1.11.   EAAAs should be identified separately for each individual biodiversity receptor being assessed
          during the CHA process.
          STEP 2 – STAKEHOLDER CONSULTATION AND INITIAL DESKTOP REVIEW OF
          AVAILABLE DATA
2.1.12.   Step 2 is a screening process designed to identify features that may trigger Critical Habitat/Natural
          Habitat. This is done through consultation with relevant stakeholders and a review of data and
          published literature. At this stage, use of the Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool (IBAT) is
          recommended.
          STEP 3 – VERIFICATION OF BIODIVERSITY PRESENT
2.1.13.   This includes baseline fieldwork to confirm and engagement with relevant stakeholders/specialists to
          finalize the list of biodiversity features within the AoI.
          STEP 4 – CRITICAL HABITAT DETERMINATION
2.1.14.   This comprises quantitative and qualitative screening of confirmed biodiversity elements against
          critical habitat criteria defined in 2.2.

2.2       CRITICAL HABITAT CRITERIA
2.2.1.    Following PS6 guidance, the assessment of each biodiversity receptor against the Critical Habitat
          criteria makes use of both qualitative and quantitative thresholds and these are detailed in IFC
          (2019). In the interests of clarity, the alpha-numeric codes reflect PS6 criteria from this point forward.

Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                     Page 6 of 56
2.3      NATURAL HABITAT IDENTIFICATION
2.3.1.   Classification of Natural Habitats relies upon a more qualitative approach, with areas defined as
         Natural Habitats being ‘…composed of viable assemblages of plant and/or animal species of largely
         native origin, and/or where human activity has not essentially modified an area’s primary ecological
         functions and species composition.’
2.3.2.   Given the more qualitative approach to defining Natural Habitat, the process can include
         consultation with relevant expert stakeholders. In the absence of quantitative thresholds, definition of
         Natural Habitat includes all necessary evidence/justification and considers the habitat value in
         isolation, together with the presence of viable species assemblages dependent on the functioning of
         that habitat when classifying an area as Natural Habitat.
2.3.3.   Natural Habitats will be discussed alongside the CHA findings throughout this report.

2.4      DATA SOURCES
2.4.1.   This CHA is based upon a range of data collected specifically to inform this study, as well as existing
         information collated from a wide range of published literature as detailed in Table 2-2. Much of this
         has been presented within this Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA), which should
         be read alongside this report.

         EXPERT STAKEHOLDER ENGAGEMENT
2.4.2.   A vital part of the CHA process is engagement with, and ongoing involvement of, relevant external
         experts. There are limited active biodiversity organisations operating in Tajikistan (e.g., there is no
         national Birdlife International partner organisation); however, the survey team involved in data
         collection comprises esteemed academics and those with abundant experience of Tajik biodiversity.

2.5      LIMITATIONS
2.5.1.   There are areas of the AoI that are difficult to survey because access is challenging and or the
         health and safety of field researchers could be compromised. Consequently, for some terrestrial
         species groups, most notably bats, the level of survey effort has been lower than would be expected
         to fully align with Good International Industry Practice (GIIP). To appropriately address this
         limitation, a precautionary approach has been taken to the assessment in places and this is
         referenced accordingly.
2.5.2.   With regards the aquatic biodiversity baseline, there were significant constraints to completing
         ‘traditional’ survey effort within the Project AoI as described above. To address this, eDNA analysis
         was incorporated into the study design.




         Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                      PUBLIC |
         Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                            May 2025
         Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 8 of 56
2.6       COMPLIANCE WITH ESS6
2.6.1.    The principles of ESS6 seek to avoid impacts to biodiversity, and in particular impacts to biodiversity
          of high value. It is recognised that in some circumstances, development may be appropriate within
          such areas, although only when supported by robust justification, sufficient mitigation, and reasoned
          assessment.
2.6.2.    Upon determination of Critical Habitat and Natural Habitat, it is necessary to review the Project
          compliance with ESS6. For projects potentially adversely affecting such features, ESS6 has several
          explicit conditions, all of which need to be met to demonstrate project compliance. These conditions
          are listed within Table 2-3.
2.6.3.    Due consideration has also been given to other relevant Lender standards, in particular European
          Investment Bank (EIB) Standard 4 – Biodiversity and Ecosystems. Critical Habitats are similarly
          assessed as per ESS6, although the inclusion of IUCN VU species is at a slightly lower level, with
          ‘significant concentrations’ of such species being required to trigger Critical Habitat, rather than just
          those for which losses may result in the change of IUCN Red List classification (as explicitly the
          case for ESS6). Standard 4 classifies ‘high-value biodiversity’, which can be assumed to align with
          Natural Habitats as per ESS6. On this basis, no material distinction between applicable Lender
          standards is made within this CHA unless otherwise stated.

          Table 2-3 - ESS6 Conditions for Working Within Critical Habitat / Natural Habitat

           exist for development of the project in habitats of     feasible alternatives.

3.1      CANDIDATE LIST OF BIODIVERSITY RECEPTORS
3.1.1.   This CHA focusses on biodiversity receptors identified during the baseline data collection process as

         considered to have the potential to trigger Critical Habitat or Natural Habitat. Receptors were
         screened against the various Critical Habitat criteria, in consideration of the following factors as per
         Table 3-1.

         for their inclusion or exclusion. An assessment of Critical Habitat against each of the ‘screened in’
         candidate receptors is provided in Section 4.

                                                                                                           undertaken in September/October
                                                                                                           2023 has corroborated this conclusion,
                                                                                                           with a plant species assemblage that,
                                                                                                           although not of significant diversity, is
                                                                                                           characteristic of the region’s natural
                                                                                                           floodplain habitats.

 Juniperus sp.     DS/FS                -                                                                  Screened out: The woodland habitat         -       Yes
 woodland                                                                                                  within the AoI aligns with mesophilic
                                                                                                           broad-leaved woodland in which J.
                                                                                                           seravschanica is a common tree
                                                                                                           species. These woodlands tend to be
                                                                                                           at altitudes of above 1500m and so the
                                                                                                           examples mapped within the AoI are at
                                                                                                           the lower limit of this.
                                                                                                           These woodlands exhibit signs of
                                                                                                           human influence in places, where
                                                                                                           grazing animals have reduced the
                                                                                                           sward and will likely have affected tree
                                                                                                           regeneration and resulted in
                                                                                                           preferential spread of those species
                                                                                                           unpalatable to livestock; however, the
                                                                                                           overall composition appears to be
                                                                                                           broadly natural and will still be
                                                                                                           functioning as a naïve woodland
                                                                                                           habitat. While not of sufficient
                                                                                                           biodiversity value to trigger Critical
                                                                                                           Habitat (i.e., the EAAA for this habitat
                                                                                                           within the AoI is not considered to
                                                                                                           support any sufficiently rare/sensitive
                                                                                                           flora or fauna, nor is this habitat
                                                                                                           included on the IUCN Red List of
                                                                                                           threatened habitats), it is still
                                                                                                           considered to be Natural Habitat as
                                                                                                           per ESS6.

Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                                            Page 32 of 56
3.2      SCREENING SUMMARY
3.2.1.   The above screening exercise identified 15 biodiversity values that will require close monitoringand
         continued discussion with the academics and stakeholders:
         •   Egyptian vulture
         •   Saker falcon
         •   Allium stipitatum
         •   Juno Nicolai
         •   Crocus korolkowii
         •   Fritillaria eduardii
         •   Tulipa praestans
         •   Vitex agnus-castus
         •   Small Amu-Darya Shovelnose Sturgeon
         •   Amu Darya Shovelnose Sturgeon
         •   Aral Barbel
         •   Bulatmai Barbel
         •   Aral Bulltrout
         •   Protected areas (Nurek State Complex Area and Sarykhosorsky Nature Refuge)
3.2.2.   The following biodiversity values have been confirmed as meeting the requirements for Natural
         Habitat classification (see Figure 15A1):
         •   Floodplain habitats
         •   Mesophilic broad-leaved woodland with J. seravschanica
         DETERMINING THE EAAA
3.2.3.   Determining the EAAA varies depending on the biodiversity receptor. In the case of protected areas
         and those species associated with these areas, the EAAA extends to the protected area boundary.
         For other species/habitats, the extent of the EAAA has been influenced by factors such as known
         range or distribution, where available, prevailing habitat types and land uses and with the application
         of expert opinion where considered appropriate. In some instances, a necessarily broad and
         conservative EAAA has been estimated.
3.2.4.   For terrestrial ecology, the EAAA varies by receptor group and so is presented where relevant
         throughout the assessment section and illustrated on figures within Appendix A.
3.2.5.   From an aquatic biodiversity perspective, two distinct EAAAs have been defined based on
         connectivity within the Vakhsh River system. The first EAAA (downstream) comprising the Nurek
         Reservoir and the reach of river between the Nurek Reservoir and Rogun Dam, while the second
         EAAA (upstream) compromises the Vakhsh River upstream of the Rogun Dam to Damburacha,
         which is approximately 100 km upstream from full supply level at an elevation of 500 m above FSL.
         These are illustrated on figures within Appendix A.
3.2.6.   The EAAAs are also described within the Volume 1, Chapter 15: Biodiversity of this ESIA, the
         EAAA for each biodiversity receptor is summarized in the following assessment tables (Table 3-3 to
         Table 3-12). The EAAA figures referenced in the tables are included in Appendix A.

Table 3-4 - Amu Darya Shovelnose Sturgeon (Pseudoscaphirhynchus kaufmanni)

 Factor                  Description

 Species                 IUCN Critically Endangered (A2c)
 Conservation
                         Tajikistan Red Book Critically Endangered
 Status

 Species EAAA            This species is endemic to the Amu Darya River drainage where historically
                         it occurred along all of the river from the upper reaches (Pyandzh River) to
                         the delta. the species is still present are the tributaries to the Amu Darya
                         e.g. the lower section of Vakhsh River, the lower section of Surkhan Darya
                         River and the middle and lower reaches of the Amu Darya itself, from the
                         confluence of the Vaksh River to Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan (Mugue &
                         Karimov, 2022b). The Project AoI falls outside of the mapped geographic
                         extant (resident) as well as the ‘possibly extinct’ range for the species
                         (Mugue & Karimov, 2022b).
                         The EAAA for this species includes the mapped distribution as per
                         Nedoluzhko, et al (2020) and the IUCN mapped geographic extant
                         (resident) as well as the ‘possibly extinct’ range for the species.

 Population              Unknown. There are no records of Pseudoscaphirhynchus hermanni
 Estimate in EAAA        occurring or historically having occurred within either the ‘downstream’ or
                         ‘upstream’ the EAAAs (Project AoI or upper reaches of the Vakhsh River).

 Global Population       Decreasing. Since the mid-1960s, a continuous decline of the species
 and Trend               started and it is now relatively rare. But it continues to reproduce in the Amu
                         Darya River and in the lower section of Vakhsh River, however, the rate of
                         reproduction of the population is uncertain. The catches and by-catches of
                         this species between 2009–2017 from the lower and middle sections of the
                         river and the lower section of Vakhsh River (Tadjikistan), indicate that this
                         species is still extant in almost all of its distribution (Mugue & Karimov,
                         2022b and Berg, 1948).

 Assessment              This species is being considered under criterion 1a, given their IUCN CR
                         status.
                         This species is not known to be present within either of the EAAAs
                         (Project AoI or upper Vakhsh River), nor does its ‘possibly extinct’ range
                         overlap either of the EAAAs.
                         The species is endemic to the Amu Darya (Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan)
                         and occurs from the Pyandzh (near Faizabad-kala) to the mouth, inclusive
                         of the lower section of Vakhsh River (Reshetnikov and Shakirova, 1993 and
                         Mugue and Karimov, 2022 ). With the construction of the Nurek Reservoir
                         and other downstream dams, migration into the Project AoI would not be
                         possible.
                         The species occurs in the turbid waters of rivers with fast flowing turbulent
                         current, with sandy or rock-gravel substrates at depths of up to two meters
                         in highly turbulent, turbid water at flow velocities of 0.4 to 1.9m/s (Salnikov
                         et al. 2004, Kuhajda et al. 2007).
                         Not detected in eDNA samples collected in October 2023.

Table 3-5 - Aral Bulltrout (Salmo trutta spp aralensis)

 Factor                  Description

 Species                 IUCN Critically Endangered (A2c)
 Conservation
                         Tajikistan Red Book Endangered
 Status

 Species EAAA            The EAAA for this species includes both the ‘downstream’ and ‘upstream’
                         EAAAs, which incorporates the Vakhsh River drainage upstream of the
                         Nurek Reservoir and Rogun Dam. See Appendix A: Figure 15A2.

 Population              Unknown. The most recent IUCN assessment (2000) that identifies this
 Estimate in EAAA        species as subspecies does not describe range or population (Birstein,
                         2000).
                         The TRB (2017) states that this species inhabits the Aral Sea and the Amu
                         Darya River Basin and is reportedly found very rarely in the Kofarnihon and
                         Vakhsh rivers and Nurek Reservoir. From 1974 until 1994 the Aral Bulltrout
                         was mostly caught in the Nurek Reservoir. A rare, but relatively stable
                         population within the Nurek Reservoir is mentioned in the TRB (2017),
                         indicating that similar to the Aral Sea, the species persists in lectic habitats.
                         Artaev et.al (2025), does not recognise Salmo trutta spp aralensis as a
                         species currently present within Tajikistan, stating that “Salmo aralensis”
                         (synonym) was extinct as a consequence of the collapse of the Aral Sea
                         Basin ecosystem.
                         Schöffmann (2001), notes that the anadromous trout of Lake Aral Salmo
                         trutta aralensis was notified as extinct since the 1970s.
                         Mitrofanov and Mamilov (2015), note that the Aral Trout (Salmo trutta
                         aralensis), is an endemic subspecies of the widespread Brown Trout, Salmo
                         trutta, and that it most likely no longer occurs within the Amu Darya.
                         Dr. Nadir Shamilevich Mamilov from the Al-Farabi Kazakh National
                         University indicated that they do not have any relevant information regarding
                         Salmo trutta ssp. aralensis (the Aral Sea and Amu Darya River stock) during
                         the last 40 years (Personal communication 2024).
                         One specimen was reportedly caught in 2004 by the Kazakh Fishery
                         Research Institute in the Syr Darya delta, and a second oral report from
                         lower reaches of the Syr Darya River has been reported through the Kazakh
                         Centre of Fisheries and Aquaculture. These are the most current anecdotal
                         records available.

 Global Population       Unspecified, widely regarded as extinct.
 and Trend25

 Assessment              This subspecies is being considered under criterion 1a and 1c, given their
                         IUCN CR status and given they are listed nationally or regionally as EN
                         (Red Book of Tajikistan).




Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                       PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                             May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                  Page 38 of 56
 Factor                  Description

                         Due to limited information on the species' estimated extent of occurrence
                         (EOO) or population, we rely on available literature and subject matter
                         experts. Originally endemic to the Aral Sea and its affluent river mouths, the
                         Amu Darya and Syr Darya Rivers, this species is now widely considered
                         extinct. Historical records from the Nurek Reservoir (1974 to 1994) likely
                         represented a non-viable population that could not migrate downstream to
                         the lower Amu Darya and Aral Sea, leading to its eventual disappearance.
                         Based on the weighted evidence, Salmo trutta aralensis is considered
                         extirpated from the Amu Darya drainage as a consequence of the
                         disappearance of the Aral Sea and habitat alterations (overfishing,
                         damming and channel management, extraction of water for irrigation,
                         and water pollution). Therefore, no Critical Habitat for Salmo trutta
                         aralensis has been identified within the Project EAAA.

Table 3-6 - Aral Barbel (Luciobarbus brachycephalus)

 Factor                  Description

 Species                 IUCN Endangered (A2cd)
 Conservation
                         Tajikistan Red Book Vulnerable
 Status

 Species EAAA            The EAAA for this species includes both the ‘downstream’ and ‘upstream’
                         EAAAs, which incorporates the Vakhsh River drainage upstream of the
                         Nurek Reservoir and Rogun Dam. See Appendix A: Figure 15A2.

 Population              Unknown. Present within the Aral basin (extirpated in the sea - due to
 Estimate in EAAA        salinity, only survives in the reservoirs of its tributaries), Chu drainage and
                         southern and western Caspian Sea. Known to migrate up larger tributaries
                         of western and southern coasts: Terek, Samur, Kura, lower Aras. Rarely in
                         lower Volga (up to Volgograd) and Ural (Freyhof & Kottelat, 2008a). The
                         IUCN mapped geographic extant (resident) as well as the ‘possibly extinct’
                         range for the species does not overlap with the EAAA (Ford 2024a).

 Global Population       Decreasing as a large part of his species' range (estimated 20%), the Aral
 and Trend               Sea, has disappeared due to intensive water abstraction leading to
                         increased salinity levels. Currently, the species only occurs in a few
                         reservoirs in the Amu Darya and Syr Darya (tributaries to the Aral), and
                         Karakum Canal (where it is invasive) where its status is unknown (Freyhof &
                         Kottelat, 2008a). In the western Caspian Sea, the landlocked population (in
                         the Kura river) is thought be stable, and the
                         anadromous population has declined, due to the lack of spawning sites and
                         poaching, but it is still frequent in the southern Caspian tributaries in
                         Azerbaijan and Iran (Freyhof & Kottelat, 2008a).
                         Overall, there is a continuing to decline due to expanding hydropower
                         development and strong ecological impacts on Caspian and Aral Sea
                         basins. It is suspected that the total population has declined by at least 30%
                         in the past 30 years (Freyhof & Kottelat, 2008a).

 Assessment              This species is being considered under criterion 1b, given their IUCN EN
                         status.
                         The primary habitat for L. brachycephalus is at sea, at depths of up to 25 m
                         and in rivers, in deep stretches with gravel or stone bottom. Based on the
                         habitat preferences and know distribution downstream, it is unlikely that
                         either of the EAAAs would support a population large enough that their loss
                         would result in the Red List status changing to Endangered (based on
                         IUCN, 2019).
                         Not detected in eDNA samples collected in October 2023.
                         The EAAAs are therefore not considered of sufficient value for it to
                         qualify as Critical Habitat under Criterion 1b for Aral Barbel.

                         Atrek (Iran). Was always very rare in Volga. Aral basin, in Amu Darya, Syr

 Assessment              This species is being considered under criterion 1b, given their IUCN VU
                         status.

                         (Freyhof & Kottelat, 2008b).
                         Based on the habitat preferences and know distribution downstream, along
                         with the fact that L. capito is semi- anadromous but rarely landlocked in
                         reservoirs, it is unlikely that either of the EAAAs would support a population
                         large enough that their loss would result in the Red List status changing to
                         Endangered (based on IUCN, 2019 and Fricke, 2024).
                         Not detected in eDNA samples collected in October 2023.

 Population and          greater numbers present as passage migrants.

 Assessment              This species is being considered under criterion 1c, given their Tajikistan

                         The species mainly occupies territories with valley and cliff features but will
                         forage across a wide range of habitats, including those close to human

                         rather than those occupying territories (the species is generally solitary/in
                         pairs during the breeding season, so the group of birds recorded in autumn
                         2023 would certainly reinforce this suggestion). The Project AoI does not
                         contain habitat/features that appear of any increased importance to the
                         species.
                         Based on the assumption regarding recorded birds being passage
                         individuals, the EAAA is not considered to be of any explicit importance that
                         would trigger classification as Critical Habitat.
                         The EAAA is therefore not considered of sufficient value for it to
                         qualify as Critical Habitat under Criterion 1c for Egyptian vulture.

                         to be of any significant importance that would trigger classification as Critical

                         during 2023 surveys, with habitats generally sub-optimal (the species grows
                         beneath xerophilous woodland and shrubs at a height of >1200m. Assumed
                         small population.

                         TRB, it was classified as Least Concern (LC) in Nowak & Nobis (2020). Only

                         during 2023 surveys, and the habitat is suboptimal across the AoI, with the
                         species preference being woodland and xerophilous shrubs on stony slopes.

                         TRB, it was classified as Least Concern (LC) in Nowak & Nobis (2020), and
                         has a mapped distribution from across Tajikistan. Only sparsely recorded from



Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                  PUBLIC |
Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                        May 2025
Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                             Page 44 of 56
 Factor                  Description
                         the literature review with a single small population recorded during 2023
                         surveys. Given its relatively large size and conspicuous nature is considered
                         likely to be absent across much of the AoI, with those present comprising
                         widely scattered individual plants/small groups of plants.

 Global Population       Allium stipitatum: This species is endemic to Central Asia, with records from
 and Trend (TRB,         Tajikistan, Turkestan, Kyrgystah, Uzbekistan and Afghanistan. It is considered
 2017)                   to be rare and declining, although thought to be LC in Tajikistan.

                         Juno nicolai: This species is known from Tajikistan, Uzbekistan and
                         Afghanistan. It is considered to be rare and declining, largely due to is
                         collection by people for its flowers. Listed as LC in Tajikistan.

                         Crocus korolkowii: This species is endemic to Central Asia, with records from
                         Tajikistan, Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan. It is considered to be rare and
                         declining, largely due to its continued collection for its flowers. Listed as LC in
                         Tajikistan.

                         Fritillaria eduardii: This species is endemic to Central Asia, with records from
                         Tajikistan and northern Afghanistan. It is considered to be rare and declining,
                         largely due to its continued collection for its flowers and its bulb as a food
                         source. Listed as LC in Tajikistan.

                         Тulipa praestans: Endemic to Tajikistan, this species has declined massively
                         due to collection for its flowers and bulbs and also widespread habitat loss to
                         agriculture. Listed as LC in Tajikistan.

                         Vitex agnus-castus: The species has a relatively widespread distribution,
                         including Central Asia and west into the Mediterranean region. It is readily used
                         for firewood as well as its pharmaceutical properties and has declined
                         significantly as a result of this and general habitat loss. Listed as LC in
                         Tajikistan.

 Assessment              These plants have been screened in for consideration under criterion 1c
                         ‘…areas containing important concentrations of a nationally or regionally listed
                         EN or CR species.’ However, there is no indication that the EAAAs comprise
                         any notably important areas for these species with only limited field survey
                         records in 2023 (with no records described from 2014, besides from the
                         literature review), and recent distribution mapping does not infer any increased
                         importance/value of the Vakhsh River valley to any of these plants. There are
                         no reliable population estimates for any of the above species, and most have
                         been included on a precautionary basis. The most recent population trend
                         review for these species (from Nowak & Nobis 2020) has all species classified
                         as LC. As per the text above, the habitats present within the AoI are generally
                         sub-optimal, with the prevailing legacy of modification across the AoI likely to
                         have significantly reduced the populations and habitat suitability for these
                         species. In addition, all of these species are traditionally collected, for various
                         uses, and so this will have contributed to the decline.
                         The EAAA is therefore not considered of sufficient value for it to qualify
                         as Critical Habitat under Criterion 1a for nationally threatened plants.

3.2.10.   Two biodiversity values potentially triggering Critical Habitat under Criterion 4 have been identified
          for the Project.

          Table 3-11 - Sarykhosorsky Nature Refuge

           Factor          Description

           Designation     Nature Refuge (IUCN management category IV)

           Receptor        Nature Refuge boundary See Appendix A: Figure 15A5
           EAAA

           Summary         There is limited information on the management objectives of this Nature Refuge;
           description     however, it is known to support mountain forest ecosystems including fruit and nut
                           trees, which align with habitats elsewhere within the Vakhsh River valley. The site
                           is subject to management activities to restore forest ecosystems (including fruit and
                           nut forests) and agroforestry. It also includes sites of recreational and cultural
                           significance.

           Assessment      The Sarykhosorsky Nature Refuge is not supported by any specific management
                           plan, and so details regarding the baseline conditions at the site are limited.
                           However, it is known that forest habitats, including fruit and nut trees are present
                           and exploited to some extent by local communities. Additionally, forestry
                           management takes place there and FFI (and previously UNDP) are involved on
                           capacity building with regards ongoing forest protection, agroforestry and
                           conservation (FFI, 2016). Additionally, the reserve provides recreational and
                           cultural resources and so does not have a purely conservation focus. The IUCN
                           management category for the site is IV, and so not at a level that would
                           automatically qualify this site as Critical Habitat. The reserve extends across
                           elevations of 1200-1600m.
                           Given the likely lack of ecosystems of sufficient conservation value, and history of
                           human influence in the reserve, together with the absence of any management
                           plan for the site, the Sarykhosorsky Nature Refuge is not classified as Critical
                           Habitat under Criteria 4 for highly threatened or unique ecosystems.




          Rogun Hydropower Project – Updated Environmental and Social Impact Assessment                     PUBLIC |
          Project No.: project number | Our Ref No.: Project REF                                           May 2025
          Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                Page 46 of 56
          Table 3-12 - Nurek State Complex Area

              Factor        Description

              Designation   Nature Refuge (IUCN management category IV)

              Receptor      Nature Refuge boundary See Appendix A: Figure 15A6
              EAAA

              Summary       The Nurek State Complex Area was created to protect local juniper and other
              description   forest types, and therefore provide suitable habitat for species of wildlife found
                            within this area.

              Assessment    The Nurek State Complex Area is not supported by any specific management plan,
                            and so details regarding the baseline conditions at the site are limited. However, it
                            is known that the main goal of the reserve’s designation was to preserve mountain
                            ecosystems, juniper forests and other forest types, and to offset effects caused by
                            the Nurek Reservoir on these ecosystems, and further study their evolution. The
                            IUCN management category for the site is IV, and so not at a level that would
                            automatically qualify this site as Critical Habitat. From in-person discussions with
                            stakeholders, it appears that a component of the Management Area is to provide a
                            game hunting resource, with gamebirds released periodically into the site, which
                            suggest some limitation to the conservation objectives of the site. Given the similar
                            habitats within the Vakhsh River valley (such as juniper woodland), which do not
                            qualify as Critical Habitat, it is reasonable to assume that this protected area does
                            not support highly threatened or unique ecosystems. Additionally, for qualification
                            under criterion 4, it would be expected that a management plan would be in place
                            (especially for a site that has been designated since 1984)
                            The Nurek State Complex Area is therefore not considered of sufficient value
                            for it to qualify as Critical Habitat under Criteria 4 for highly threatened or
                            unique ecosystems.

          CRITERION 5 – KEY ECOLOGICAL FUNCTIONS
3.2.11.   No biodiversity values triggering Critical Habitat under Criterion 5 have been identified for the
          Project.

3.3       SUMMARY
          CRITICAL HABITAT
3.3.1.    In summary, based on our current understanding of the Project AoI and information gathered
          through available literature and in field investigations, together with application of the precautionary
          principle, there are no biodiversity features that are considered to trigger Critical Habitat for the
          associated EAAA.
          NATURAL HABITAT
3.3.2.    The following biodiversity values are considered to trigger Natural Habitat for the associated EAAAs:
          •     Floodplain habitats
          •     Mesophilic broad-leaved woodland with J. seravschanica

4        ESS6 COMPLIANCE REVIEW

4.1      INTRODUCTION
4.1.1.   Upon completion of the CHA it is necessary to review Project compliance against the requirements
         of ESS6. This has been done with the assessment outcome derived from the Project ESIA and
         should be read in conjunction with that report. The assessment requirements of ESS6 are explicitly
         detailed within Section B1.2 and discussed individually below in terms of Project compliance.
4.1.2.   As per the requirements of ESS6, this process applies to all areas of Critical Habitat in which Project
         activities where potential adverse effects impacts exist. In terms of Natural Habitat, this compliance
         review only applies where ‘…significant, adverse and irreversible…’ impacts have been identified.
4.1.3.   Table 4-1 summarises this position for each Natural Habitat Feature that was derived from the
         assessment within Volume 1, Chapter 15: Biodiversity of this ESIA.

         Table 4-1 – Natural Habitat Compliance Screening

          Natural Habitat                             Assessment Conclusion4

          Floodplain                                  Approximately 77 ha lost; Major habitat loss effect (significant)

          Juniper woodland                            Approximately 185 ha lost; Major habitat loss effect (significant)

4.1.4.   The compliance review is detailed within Table 4-2 and any mitigation required to achieve
         compliance is described. Note that these compliance criteria apply where adverse impacts (to
         Critical Habitats/Natural Habitats) are identified.

Project Management Group, JSC “Rogun HPP”, DFZ                                                                                      Page 51 of 56
5        MITIGATION PROPOSALS
5.1.1.   Several bespoke and generic mitigation measures are described within this ESIA to minimise
         impacts to biodiversity receptors so that the significance of residual impact is negligible or minor.
         With regards Natural Habitat, the requirement for mitigation extends to ensuring no net loss.
5.1.2.   Details regarding these measures is presented within the Volume 3, A4: BMP and Volume 3, A5:
         NNLF for the Project and summarised in Table 5-1.
         Table 5-1 – Mitigation Summary

          Receptor and Effect          Mitigation

          Floodplain habitat           A no net loss management plan was developed to mitigate the impct
                                       on this natural habitat.

          Juniper woodland             A no net loss management plan was developed to mitigate the impct
          habitat                      on this natural habitat.

5.1.3.   In addition to the above, it is expected that complementary mitigation proposals that will be delivered
         through separate technical specialists will also contribute to the overall mitigation of effects to the
         above receptors (note that any such benefits will be supplementary to the dedicated mitigation
         required for these effects). In particular, this will comprise tree/vegetation planting around the Project
         and flooding zone for the purposes of landscaping and soil stabilisation. Further information in this
         regard can be found in Volume 1, Chapter 12: Soils & Geology and Volume 1, Chapter 14:
         Landscape & Visual of this ESIA.

5.2      CONCLUSIONS
5.2.1.   This CHA has concluded that there are no areas of Critical Habitat within the Project AoI.
5.2.2.   Two Natural Habitat features (floodplain and juniper woodland habitat) have been identified, both of
         which will be subject to significant adverse effects in the absence of mitigation.
5.2.3.   In order for the Project to be compliant with the requirements of ESS6 and other similar Lender
         standard), a series of mitigation measures have been recommended that will be delivered through a
         Project Biodiversity Management Plan and, in the case of Natural Habitat, an No Net Loss
         Framework, the latter with specific aims to achieve a No Net Loss for floodplain and juniper
         woodland habitats, thereby achieving a key aspect of ESS6 compliance.
