 discussions (for    Nurobod district                                              Khakimii miyona and Khakimii bolo. These will be relocated.
 the 2014 ESIA)
                                         Kofara            To be resettled     •   There is a cemetery in each of these villages. As the villages are not within
                                                                                   the inundation zone, it is unlikely that the cemeteries will be relocated.
                                         Khasandara        To be resettled
                                                                               •   There are no known holy places in these villages.
                                         Shahtuti poyon    To be resettled

 2011 focus group                        Khakimii bolo     Will be resettled   •   There are two cemeteries on the territory of the villages of Khakimii poyon,
 discussions (for                                                                  Khakimii miyona and Khakimii bolo. These will be relocated. There is also
 the 2014 ESIA),                                                                   a modern mosque in Khakimii bolo.
 2023 ethnographic                                                             •   There is a holy place where the local population worships. People from
 survey (for the                                                                   Avlod Eshonov and Sayidzod also visit this holy place. Locals did not know
 present ESIA)                                                                     whether this would be relocated.
                                                                               •   The 17th to 18th century tomb of Oftobhuga, who, according to legend,
                                                                                   was a herbal healer and religious scholar who came from the southern
                                                                                   regions and founded the village of Khakimii bolo (Khakim meant ‘healing’).
                                                                               •   There is another 17th to 18th century tomb in Khakimii bolo.
                                                                               •   The 19th century tomb of theologian, Dumullo Olim, which is visited by
                                                                                   pilgrims from all over Tajikistan, is located in this village.
                                                                               •   Locals explained that sericulture (the cultivation of silkworms to produce
                                                                                   silk) was an important occupation in the village during the Soviet era,
                                                                                   mostly for women. The large number of mulberry trees planted in the
                                                                                   village, whose leaves served as food for the worms, is testament to this.
                                                                               •   A woman described amulets traditionally used in the village, including a
                                                                                   bear’s tooth used to protect against evil spirits. Various items, such as
                                                                                   beads and seeds, were often hung in the cradle of a young child to ward
                                                                                   off spirits.


                                                                                                                                           Page 1
                                     •   Traditionally, women in the village would embroider a wall panel, dress
                                         and scarves before her wedding. Women continue to make clothes,
                                         carpets and decorations, such as ribbons for wedding bedspreads and
                                         cradles. Traditional hair decorations were also discussed, some of which
                                         involved bean and button decorations.
                                     •   Other traditional crafts undertaken in the village included the production of
                                         hearths for cooking and pottery production, beekeeping and the breeding
                                         of Turkish partridges, which are reared for fighting.
                                     •   Local informants also discussed how medicinal herbs were grown and
                                         used for treatment. Plantain, for example, is used in the village to treat
                                         stomach pain.

Khakimii poyon   Will be resettled   •   There are two cemeteries on the territory of the villages of Khakimii poyon,
                                         Khakimii miyona and Khakimii bolo. These will be relocated. There is also
                                         a modern mosque in Khakimii poyon.
                                     •   There is an ancient cemetery in this village and a small burial place of 18th
                                         and 19th century date.
                                     •   There are holy places Hucha Abdurazzok and Hucha Mir. Locals did not
                                         know whether these would be relocated.
                                     •   One local explained that, in the past, newlyweds would dress in a national
                                         costume when being escorted to the groom’s house. European veils are
                                         more commonly worn at present, however. Another informant described
                                         how the tradition of shaving the groom’s head and presenting him with a
                                         turban on the wedding day (know as sartaroshon) was no longer observed
                                         in the village. A local woman described a former wedding tradition called
                                         muysarbofon where a carpet was laid in the middle of a courtyard, around
                                         which the bride and her friends sat.
                                     •   Women in the village are increasingly going to university after school, with
                                         the director of the local school claiming that 80% of female graduates of
                                         the school have gone on to study at universities in recent years.
                                     •   Traditional crafts undertaken by women in the village include weaving,
                                         embroidering and braiding. Women traditionally embroidered their own
                                         dowry, including dresses, pillows, towels and bedspreads.
                                     •   An informant described as an ‘artisan’ explained how there are two types
                                         of embroider in the region; traditional and modern. Old motifs and patterns
                                         are used in the former, while modern patterns tend to depict objects, such
                                         as flowers, more realistically. According to this informant, young people in
                                         the village are no longer interested in embroidery as everything they need
                                           for a dowry can now be purchased at the market. The informant believes
                                           that the traditional folk craft is disappearing.
                                       •   Whilst discussing traditional practices, one informant stated that ashes are
                                           still used in gardens as fertiliser. They also claimed that ashes were placed
                                           in dirty dishes in order to clean them.
                                       •   A history teacher who was interviewed in the village explained that he
                                           made instruments and plays in the local folk style, while a physics and
                                           mathematics teacher plays the accordion at school events. The history
                                           teacher plays the dutar, setor, doira and flute, often at weddings. To make
                                           the musical instruments, he stores wood (usually apricot) underground in
                                           his garden for six months in order to reduce its acidity and make it less
                                           likely to crack. Cow skin is usually used on the surface of the instruments.
                                           According to this informant, the doira (or tambourine) is predominantly
                                           played by women, particularly at social gatherings.
                                       •   One local told the legend about the Karategin Valley within which Khakimii
                                           poyon is located. The valley was supposedly frequently captured by
                                           Kazakhs, with locals inhabitants often robbed and killed. A fighter from the
                                           other side of the valley named Kara helped to fight off the Kazakhs, and
                                           the head Kazakh addressed Kara by saying “Kara – tegin”, meaning “Stop,
                                           Kara”. In an offer of peace, the Kazakhs gave the valley to Kara.

Khumrogh (formerly   To be resettled   •   There is a modern mosque and cemetery.
Kumok or Qumoq)
                                       •   There is a 16th to 17th century tomb of the great theologian Mir Sayyid
                                           Aliya Hamadoni.
                                       •   There is another 16th to 17th century tomb.
                                       •   An informant explained how many wedding traditions, including
                                           sartaroshon, muysarbofon, domod talbon, kelintalbon, are no longer
                                           performed today.
                                       •   The informant described the process of making bread, such as chahlak
                                           and tanur, and crackers in hearths.
                                       •   The rite of gavhorabandon, which is performed after a woman has her first
                                           child, was discussed. The woman’s parents buy a cradle (or gakhvora) for
                                           her as a gift. The cradle contains flour products, sweets and other gifts. At
                                           the ceremony, which is attended by close relatives, a woman close to the
                                           daughter will take the baby in her arms and asks, “shall I put it in the
                                           cradle?”, to which everyone replies “no”. This is repeated until, after the
                                           fourth time of asking, the woman puts the child in the cradle. The cradle is


                                                                                                   Page 3
                                        then covered with gifts, a prayer is read and people wish the child a long
                                        life. A lullaby is sung to the baby to end the ceremony.
                                    •   Local women in the village said they wove carpets, mostly for themselves.
                                        One informant particularly enjoyed weaving sherozi carpets because the
                                        technique is more complex and the result more beautiful.

Obiboriki bolo    Resettlement in   •   There is a modern mosque and cemetery in this village. As the village is
                  progress              not within the inundation zone, it is unlikely that the cemetery will be
                                        relocated.
                                    •   A spring was identified in this village.
                                    •   An informant learned the craft of beekeeping from his father, and now has
                                        20 beehives of his own. He claimed that the honey produced was
                                        particularly tasty because the village is at altitude and the air is cleaner.
                                    •   Another informant in the village learned how to carve wood from his father.
                                        He makes dishes, spoons and cups. His father gave him his blessing to
                                        produce such items so he could feed his family. He is also a blacksmith
                                        and produces tools for his fellow villagers. Another blacksmith in the village
                                        also learned his craft from his father. He reads a prayer daily and
                                        dedicates it to the patron saint of blacksmiths, Hazrati Dovudu.

Obiboriki poyon   Resettlement in   •   There is a cemetery in this village. As the village is not within the
                  progress              inundation zone, it is unlikely that the cemetery will be relocated. There is
                                        also a modern mosque.
                                    •   A spring was recorded in this village.
                                    •   The village chairman told the survey team that there are no artisans left in
                                        the village, and all products they need are purchased from elsewhere in
                                        the district or in the city.
                                    •   The chairman described a wedding tradition called Ravganrezon, where
                                        the bride is ushered to the sound of music into a courtyard in the village
                                        towards a tablecloth on which stands a large metal bowl containing sweets
                                        and a sieve. There is also a bowl of butter and flour. A young girl brings
                                        water for the bride to wash her hands with. The bride then holds the sieve
                                        and flour is poured, through which the bride must sift. Shen then dips three
                                        fingers into a bowl of oil. The sweets are then thrown to guests and the
                                        groom’s mother places a ring on her daughter-in-law’s finger.
2011 focus group   Nurobod district,   Mehrobod                Resettlement in   •   In 1936 the urban village of Shahrak was renamed Komsomolobod. It was
discussions (for   Darband city,                               progress              renamed Darband in 1995 and Mehrobod in 2021.
the 2014 ESIA),    Jamoat Mehrobod
                   (formerly                                                     •   Carpet weaving is popular amongst girls in the village. The carpets they
2015                                                                                 produce feature Iranian style ornamental motifs which the girls have
                   Komsomolobod)
Ethnographic                                                                         copied. These include Sasanian motifs, patterns of Safavid carpets and
Study of Nurobod                                                                     miniatures of the 11th centuries.
and Rogun
                                                                                 •   Pottery production (including jugs, pots and dishes) and women’s
                                                                                     needlework are other developed crafts in the village. Traditional painted
                                                                                     cradles (gahvor), which are crafted locally, can be found in the shops of
                                                                                     the village.
                                                                                 •   There is a large cemetery in the centre of Mehrobod. People from this
                                                                                     area, as well as the lower village, village Airport, are buried here.
                                                                                 •   There is a holy place (Hattie Bibi) and Sycamore tree (Chanor), which is
                                                                                     thought to be over 1,000 years old, in the centre of the village.
                                                                                 •   The grave of a Bibi Otun, an influential female religious figure, is located
                                                                                     within the village. According to locals, people visit this grave and rub dirt
                                                                                     from it on wounded parts of their body to heal them.

                                                                                 •   One villager stated that this sacred place can heal diseases. They claimed,
                                                                                     for example, that it can heal children ‘who have not developed speech for
                                                                                     a long time’ as well as illness resulted from ‘fright’.

Study of Nurobod                                     •   There is a monument (makbara) made by the local population. This
and Rogun                                                mausoleum has an ancient history and was restored in the 20th century.
2023 ethnographic                                    •   According to legend, Eshoni Emomi, who was a saint, founded this mazar
survey (for the                                          440 years ago. He was supposedly buried here, and two drops of his blood
present ESIA)                                            fell into the garden behind the mazard building during the burial, where red
                                                         pears currently grow. As a result, nobody collects these pears. Locals did
                                                         not know whether the monument would be relocated.
                                                     •   There is a stone in the courtyard at the entrance to the mazar on which,
                                                         according to legend, the horse of the prophet Ali left his mark.
                                                     •   The mazar attracted many pilgrims, especially infertile women. The sand
                                                         around the mazar also has healing properties according to locals.
                                                     •   One villager associated all of the good luck in their life with the ‘spiritual
                                                         support and care’ received from these holy places. They and their family
                                                         have always visited these places for pilgrimage.
                                                     •   The shrine of Aligalabon, who was a scholar of Islam, is located here, and
                                                         it is believed that his descendants still live in the village. There are three
                                                         tombs located in the village: two of them are of unknown scholars of Islam
                                                         who were supposedly martyred and the third is the tomb of Eshan Asrari.
                                                         Eshan Asrari’s tomb is visited more often than the other two.
                                                     •   There are two teahouses. A scene depicting an eagle descending from the
                                                         mountains to carry away a ram is depicted on the walls of one of them.
                                                     •   According to legend, all criminals and delinquents were forced to plant
                                                         1,000 mulberry trees as punishment, explaining the large number of trees
                                                         in the village. According to another legend, Ali Galabon was a person who
                                                         planted the trees in order to atone for his guilt. Residents made syrup and
                                                         jam from the mulberries.
                                                     •   There was a group in the village who specialise making stretchers for the
                                                         dead from willow branches tied together with twine. Single-chamber
                                                         graves were prepared. The ancient ritual of eating food at the coffin was
                                                         observed here approximately 20 years ago.
                                                     •   Dairy products were made by women in the village using the Kirghizia
                                                         device. This practice was noted in many of the other villages surveyed.
                                                     •   Women embroidered mostly dresses, frieze strips for walls and men’s
                                                         waist scarves.

2015               Rogun District   Mirogh         Completely   •   This was the highest mountain village visited during the survey.
Ethnographic                                       resettled
Study of Nurobod                                                •   According to legend, it was established over a century ago during the reign
and Rogun                                                           of the Emir of Bukhara.
                                                                •   There is little land in and around the village, so the population never grew
                                                                    wheat. Instead they raised livestock, mainly cows and goats and
                                                                    sometimes sheep.
                                                                •   There is a mosque, although this was not in use at the time of the survey
                                                                    because the resettlement was underway.
                                                                •   At the time of the survey, women in the village were preparing to make thin
                                                                    cakes (chapoti) for the upcoming holiday of Idi Kurbon. According to
                                                                    tradition, the central dastarkhan (eating space) should be decorated with
                                                                    thin cakes, small loaves (kulcha) and pieces of fried dough (orzuk), festive
                                                                    memorial pancakes (chalpak) and cookies. Small livestock were
                                                                    slaughtered for the holiday and either fried meat or soup was cooked.
                                                                    Children were given chewing gum and candy.
                                                                •   There was a tradition in the village for the spring holiday Navruz to cook
                                                                    flour products using spring herbs. These were then stuffed with mountain
                                                                    plants. Gruel was prepared from the intestines of an animal and several
                                                                    types of legumes were made. During the survey, the villagers were seen
                                                                    congratulating each other on the new year and the beginning of arable
                                                                    work.
                                                                •   Cousin and cross-cousin marriages were common in the village. Such
                                                                    marriages were relatively inexpensive and allowed the women to keep
                                                                    their property.
                                                                •   Wedding celebrations were fairly modest. The groom would arrive at the
                                                                    bride’s house where music would be played. The bride would bring gifts to
                                                                    her husband’s house, including white halva, a bugget of fried pieces of
                                                                    orzuk dough, a bucket of eggs and pieces of large wedding bread.
                                                                •   Local women were traditionally engaged in needlework. One woman
                                                                    embroidered pillows, curtains, wedding veils etc.

                                    Talhakchasma   Completely   •   The village is known for its mazar Hazrati Khujai Gor, whom the local
                                                   resettled        population revere as the grandfather of all ishans. According to legend, a
                                                                             saint named Eshoni Nematullohuja came here from the town of Pushtai
                                                                             Khryng in Sari Kosh.
                                                                         •   Most of the inhabitants of the village were resettled in the Jilikul region in
                                                                             the mid-20th century by the government. However, many of them later
                                                                             returned.
                                                                         •   Residents were engaged in forestry, cattle breeding and agriculture.
                                                                         •   A carpenter made musical instruments (e.g. dutars), cradles, spoons etc.
                                                                             and engaged in woodcarving. The people of the village were considered to
                                                                             be very musical, with many families owning old instruments.
                                                                         •   Hikmat Rizo, a famous folk hafiz / singer, came from this village.
                                                                         •   Blacksmiths in the village made scythes, axes, horseshoes etc.
                                                                         •   Although the traditional crafts of embroidery and pottery had mostly died
                                                                             out in the village, the population has preserved individual relics, such as a
                                                                             wall frieze made for a wedding and early 20th century ceramic products.

survey (for the
present ESIA)                                                            •   The owner of a mill was interviewed. The mill, which is believed to be over
                                                                             100 years old, was inherited from the man’s father. They make different
                                                                             types of flour, and in the autumn they make a special dish from boiled
                                                                             wheat and peas called talkon.

                                       Bedikho         To be resettled   •   There are several burial sites in the village of Bedikho. One is the
                                                                             mausoleum of a young scientist who, during the establishment of Soviet
                                                                             power, supposedly tried to escape the rulers and took refuge in the village.
                                                                             However, he was caught by the authorities and killed. A further four burial
                                                                             sites were recorded in the village.

2015                Jamoat Sicharog,   Sicharog        Completely        •   The Jamoat Sicharog includes several high-mountain villages.
Ethnographic        Rogun District                     resettled
Study of Nurobod                                                         •   According to legend, very few people lived in the village of Sicharog
and Rogun                                                                    initially, and travellers were afraid to approach it because it was
                                                                             surrounded by impenetrable thickets and wild animals. The Emir of
                                                                             Bukhara began to exile prisoners here, mostly middle-aged and elderly
                                                                             men. The village was called Degi sieh for a long time, meaning black
                                                                             cauldron.
                                                                         •   Villagers across the valley supposedly watched 30 charog lights being lit
                                                                             under a large walnut tree in Sicharog on Friday evenings, spread out at the

                                                                                                                                      Page 11
    entrance to the village. They therefore named this settlement Sicharog,
    which means thirty lights.
•   At the time of the survey, most of the villagers were related to Bobo Avgon
    (b. 1937). Village men mostly married their relatives. Cross-cousin
    marriage was particularly common. This meant that the inheritance would
    not go into the hands of a stranger, and that the wedding would not require
    large expenses.
•   As the 20th century progressed, parents increasingly remained to live with
    one of their married sons, usually he who was most able to provide for
    them. Any remaining brothers often formed their own households
    separately.
•   Women of the village mainly undertook household chores. Very few of
    them were engaged in traditional crafts, partially because ready-made
    goods can be purchased in the markets more easily.
•   However, one woman who was interviewed was still making hearths and
    plastering walls. Her house, like many others in the region, had a welcome
    post/pillar, where visitors are greeted. It was unpainted and stood in the
    centre of the room.
•   According to one local, women played an important role observing ritual
    actions during ceremonies (weddings, funerals, births of children) because
    they preserve and pass on traditions to the next generation through the
    female line.
•   According to tradition, circumcision usually takes place at the age of 5-7
    years. Food is not cooked in the house following the death of a family
    member, but is instead brought by neighbours. Shurpo is cooked at the
    wake.
•   Navruz is the most popular holiday in the jamoat. Festive treats are
    prepared, including sumalak, and events such as folk wrestling are held.
•   Bobo Avgon was previously a carpenter and built his own house. He also
    used to make toys for his children and grandchildren and cradles
    decorated in styles traditional in the mountainous regions, with the use of
    geometric ornamental motifs in black and red.
•   Crafts such as woodworking and blacksmithing developed in the Jamoat in
    the late 20th century.
•   The locals mostly observed the rituals and customs of the inhabitants of
    Karategin, despite the Sarichosor origins of most of its inhabitants.
•   Locals used sleds to export manure, straw, grain etc.
                         •   There is one mosque of early-to-mid-20th century date in the village, called
                             panchvakta (meaning ‘five times prayer’). It had apparently been restored
                             recently. This is a good example of local wooden architecture.
                         •   The village has a second, new cemetery in a high-mountain area. The old
                             cemetery will be flooded.
                         •   The traditional use of hearths for cooking and boiling water in the ground in
                             houses continued in this village, as it does across the whole region.

Sech        Completely   •   The mazar Khuja Said is situated within the village, although the villagers
            resettled        interviewed did not know its history.
                         •   The villagers were engaged in agriculture and animal husbandry, and
                             mainly kept goats.
                         •   The most common crafts were woodworking, metalworking and felting.
                         •   Wedding celebrations were usually held in the autumn after the harvest.
                             To determine the bride’s dowry, up to 40 people would be sent to the
                             groom’s house. The mothers of the bride and groom traditionally cut the
                             wedding dress together (tukkuzporakuni). Wedding guests were invited by
                             a special courier who presented them with a handkerchief. Two people
                             would sing as the wedding procession approached the bride’s house. The
                             groom would then approach the house and would be presented with a
                             robe or a piece of fabric. The exist of the bride from the house was
                             accompanied by music, drums and dancing. They would then proceed to
                             the groom’s house. At most weddings there would have been a clergyman
                             and two witnesses, usually uncles and brothers. The groom goes under
                             the chodar (curtain) three times and exits from the other side. The
                             newlyweds would then stand next to each other and step on each other’s
                             feet. It is believed that the one who manages to step on the partner’s feet
                             first will be the head of the family. These traditions are similar to those in
                             other areas of Karategin.

Tagi Agba   Completely   •   According to legend, the village was founded 120-140 years ago.
            resettled        According to one informant, the inhabitants of the village trace their origins
                             back to the Shah of Iraq, Amir Oli Hassan, whose son supposedly married
                             a local girl.
                         •   In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, there were large patriarchal
                             families in the village, whose members lived in one large house.




                                                                                     Page 13
                          •   The main occupation in the village was farming, mainly grain growing.
                              There were also goof pastures around the village where cattle grazed.
                          •   The male population hunted mountain goats, bears and quails. Bear fat
                              was used to help with diseases associated with inflammation of the lungs,
                              gallbladder and in cases of female infertility.
                          •   Pottery, felting and embroidery were the most common crafts. Women
                              made threads from sheep whool, from which they knitted socks and
                              embroidered skullcaps etc.

Tagi Kamar   Completely   •   Some locals claimed that the village’s name originates from its position at
             resettled        a saddle / ledge (Kamar) in the mountains.
                          •   Part of the village’s population was resettled in the Pakhtakori Surkh
                              collective farm in the Jilikul district of the Vakhsh valley, where they lived
                              until 1967. They later began to return and build houses in the village.
                          •   The principal occupation in the village was agriculture, mostly growing
                              wheat, flax, barley, onions and carrots.
                          •   Men in the village hunted, mainly for quails.
                          •   As they do elsewhere in the region, women used earthen hearths to make
                              bread.
                          •   Blacksmithing, felting and woodworking were the most common crafts in
                              the village. One blacksmith made sickles, axes, hatchets, knives and
                              horseshoes. The blacksmith’s wife wore traditional Tajik silver and coral
                              jewellery, which is becoming increasingly rare.
                          •   A skilled female embroiderer in the village made men’s waist scarves,
                              inserts for pillow cases and various decorative items.
                          •   A male carpenter in the village made musical instruments, which he also
                              enjoyed playing.
                          •   Marriages were mostly between cousins. Most wedding rituals were held in
                              the family circle with the participation of a small number of guests. Food
                              played an important role in wedding ceremonies. Cake is broken and the
                              white cloth is cut as part of one ritual where relatives and neighbours are
                              informed of the engagement. Sweet water is given to everyone before the
                              start of the wedding meal, to ensure that they have a “good outcome”. In
                              the husband’s house he showers his bride with sweets and coins,
                              representing prosperity and abundance. Participants in the festival must
                              pick up the sweets and money in order to achieve this. It was said that a
    man tying a waist scarf indicated the onset of the time of marital intimacy
    in the family.
•   According to the villagers, it was common for large families to live in one
    spacious room in the late 20th century. Members of the family would
    gather and warm themselves during the winter.
•   There were beliefs associated with the central pillar of greeting (sutuni
    salomga). One informant’s grandfather had told him that “in the absence of
    this pillar in the house, the owners would die an unworthy death”. Almost
    all of the houses visited in the Jamoat Sicharog during the survey had
    such a pole. They provide strength to the structure of the building and
    protect it from destruction during earthquakes and other natural disasters.
    They also served as an indicator of the marital obligations of members of a
    large undivided family.

               Traditional Construction Styles and Techniques – Houses are            Wicker hedges / fences are widely used in the villages. Thin sticks and branches of
               usually made of clay, with bricks only used in the construction of     various wild shrubs are interwoven with vines to build these fences.

               cement and crushed stone and ceilings are covered with logs and

                Crafts – Making and playing traditional musical instruments, like          A history teacher plays the dutar, setor, doira and flute, often at weddings. He makes
                dutar, setor, doira and flute. This comprises the storage of wood          instruments and plays in the local folk style, while a physics and mathematics teacher
                (usually apricot) underground for six months to reduce its acidity and     plays the accordion at school events.
                make it less likely to crack. Cow skin is usually used on the surface of
                the instruments. According to this informant, the doira (or tambourine)
                is predominantly played by women, particularly at social gatherings.




                     A Local displaying his Collection of Handmade Musical
                                Instruments (Ashurmadova, 2023)


       1.1.1.   Practice (Beliefs) – The valley was supposedly frequently captured
                by Kazakhs, with locals inhabitants often robbed and killed. The
                legend about the Karategin Valley, within which Khakimii poyon is
                located, is that a fighter from the other side of the valley named Kara
                helped to fight off the Kazakhs, and the head Kazakh addressed Kara
                by saying “Kara – tegin”, meaning “Stop, Kara”. In an offer of peace,
                the Kazakhs gave the valley to Kara.

Khumrogh
       1.1.2.   Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – Wedding traditions called              No longer performed
(formerly       sartaroshon, muysarbofon, domod talbon, kelintalbon

                                                                                                                                                              7
Village Name   Theme                                                                      Present status

Kumok or
               Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – The rite of gavhorabandon,             Performed after a woman has her first child.
Qumoq)
               involves a woman’s parents buy a cradle (or gakhvora) which
               contains flour products, sweets and other gifts. At the ceremony,
               attended by close relatives, a woman close to the daughter will take
               the baby in her arms and asks, “shall I put it in the cradle?”, to which
               everyone replies “no”. This is repeated until, after the fourth time of
               asking, the woman puts the child in the cradle. The cradle is then
               covered with gifts, a prayer is read and people wish the child a long
               life. A lullaby is sung to the baby to end the ceremony.


      1.1.3.   Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – The reading of an azon (call to
               prayer) in the ear of a new-born baby. Circumcision of boys typically
               takes places when they are 5-7 years old.

      1.1.4.   Foods – The process of making bread, such as chahlak and tanur,
               and crackers in hearths. Nonichavi, which are barley flour flatbreads,
               have traditionally been widespread in the villages.




                                                                                                                    Flatbreads (Ashurmadova, 2023)




                                                                                                                                                     8
         Village Name    Theme                                                                     Present status


                1.1.5.   Foods – During the Muslim festivals of Ramadan and Idi Kurbon,
                         cattle are slaughtered and either fried meat or soup is cooked.
                         Children are also given sweets for the holidays.

                1.1.6.   Foods – Traditional dishes include umoch, which is a flour soup with
                         small dumplings; atoll, which is flour porridge; atoli ordbieren, which
                         is made from milk, flour, meat, onions and dried apricots and atolai
                         bo shir, which is made from flour and milk with salt.

                1.1.7.   Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – Following the death of a family       Shurpo (soup) is served at funerals.
                         member, food is not cooked inside the family house, but is instead
                         brought by neighbours.

                1.1.8.   Crafts – Local women wove carpets, mostly for themselves. One
                         informant particularly enjoyed weaving sherozi carpets because the
                         technique is more complex and the result more beautiful.

1.1.9.   Obiboriki

                        beekeeping from his father. He claimed that the honey produced was
                        particularly tasty because the village is at altitude and the air is
                        cleaner.

                1.1.11. Practice (Occupation) – An informant in the village learned how to         He makes dishes, spoons and cups.
                        carve wood from his father. His father gave him his blessing to
                        produce such items so he could feed his family.

                         from his father. He reads a prayer daily and dedicates it to the patron
                         saint of blacksmiths, Hazrati Dovudu.

               1.1.12. Crafts                                                                      The village chairman informed that there are no artisans left in the village, and all
            poyon                                                                                  products they need are purchased from elsewhere in the district or in the city.

                                                                                                                                                                         9
Village Name    Theme                                                                 Present status


      1.1.13. Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – Ravganrezon, a wedding
              tradition where the bride is ushered to the sound of music into a
              courtyard in the village towards a tablecloth on which stands a large
              metal bowl containing sweets and a sieve. There is also a bowl of
              butter and flour. A young girl brings water for the bride to wash her
              hands with. The bride then holds the sieve and flour is poured,
              through which the bride must sift. She then dips three fingers into a
              bowl of oil. The sweets are then thrown to guests and the groom’s
              mother places a ring on her daughter-in-law’s finger.

 Bulbuldara

  Lughuri      Foods and Practice – The owner of the historical mill in Lughuri           The mill, which is believed to be over 100 years old, was inherited from the man’s
   poyon       poyon (RHPP-AR-12) informed that they make different types of              father. It has been used by at least three generations of the same family.
               flour, and in the autumn, they make a special dish from boiled wheat
               and peas called talkon. Villagers interviewed in Khakimii bolo said
               that they travel to the mill in Lughuri poyon to grind their grain since
               the mill in their village had been destroyed.

                                                                                                                                                            11
Village Name   Theme                                                                       Present status


               Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – traditionally, the bride and            Today, couples do not marry without meeting, and both parties must give their consent
               groom often had little say in who they married and would only meet          to the marriage.
               each other on the day of the wedding.
               Another local described a wedding tradition called sumpertoi, where
               the groom sits in the middle of a square with a lock around his neck
               whilst a senior member of the family shaved the groom’s head
               cleanly with a blade. Relatives and friends of the groom would put
               money in his hand, and the collected money was either given to the
               barber or to the groom’s family.
               If a man becomes a widower and remarries, it is said that he should         This ritual is still performed today.
               pour a bucket of cold water on his former wife’s grave in order to cool
               her soul and allow her to sleep peacefully.
               Another ritual performed during dry spells involved villagers preparing
               food in large pots and sprinkling each with water. It was believed that
               this would make it rain.


 Aligalaboni   Traditional Construction Styles and Techniques – In new houses,             This is still practiced today.
     bolo      a straight log, called "dstak", is placed on top of the house with a horn
               on it, for good luck.

                                                                                                                                                           12
Village Name   Theme                                                                       Present status


               Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – They have some wedding
               ceremonies that are still practiced for the prosperity of the couple.
               After three days of the wedding, the bride kneads dough and bakes
               bread in the oven.


               Practice (Beliefs) – It is considered a great honour to for an artist to
               learn his craft from someone, and to teach your skills further.
               It is believed that making pies and chappals and distributing them to
               neighbours or children will make the spirits of the elderly happy.

      1.1.15. If there is ash on the roads, it must be removed or not walked over,
               as it is believed that it has negative properties. If a person says that
               he stepped on ash, they perform traditional treatments.

                                                                                           Embroidery and needlework was widely known and practiced by the community.
  Bedikho      Crafts


               Medicine – Medicinal herbs like Spand, kiyom, zelol, parpi, thach,
               lemongrass, mint, chamomile, subinak, bugma and bitter gourd are
               still used.


               Practice (Beliefs) – According to legends, the first inhabitants of the
               village came from Sari Khosor of Baljuvan district. At first, the village
               was called Dilkhoh, however, during the Soviet era, the word Dilkhoh
               was misunderstood and named by the Bedis.


  Roghuni      Foods – Chahlaki potatoes – soaked in brine and put in the chahlaki         This is still prepared today.
   poyon       (type of sugar syrup that has a different taste).

               cultivation is being carried out in greenhouses in the village.




                                                                                                                                                       13
Village Name   Theme                                                                     Present status


  Chanor       Crafts                                                                    Several artisans including a master plate cutter, woodcutter, cattle breeder, labor
                                                                                         veteran and a language and literature teacher are well known in the village.


               Practice (Beliefs) – The name Chano represents the four sycamore
               trees planted here for the first time. The villagers help celebrations,
               especially Nowruz and for rain, in the shade of those trees.
               There were great chantors in the village, where people gathered to
               pray.
               It is believed that no food must be cooked in the house of the
               deceased for three days to avoid misfortune.




                                                                                                                                                            14
Village Name   Theme                                                                       Present status


               Practice (Rituals and Ceremonies) – Several practices are
               undertaken during the wedding ceremony:
               A chapoti (bread), said to be a symbol of healing, is distributed.
               Elakdaroron - on the wedding day the groom accompanies the bride
               from the house to the table where, a basket and a sieve were placed.
               They lifted the sifter three times in turn and put it on the ground, and
               candy is eaten mainly with the intention of opening one's own or
               one's children's fortune.
               Chador - The mother of the bride wears a chador made of fabric.
               Khusur Salam - The bride and groom and the groom's relatives, go to
               the bride's house to exchange gifts.
               Dstarkhuonandoz (table setter) - a woman sets the table for the party,
               and the mother, aunt or sister of the bride gives her a cloth or scarf
               for this.
               Dstovgir (hand washing) - a woman before bringing food, washes the
               hands of the bridegroom's bride's mother, aunt or sister.
               Kandov - Before the food is brought, a teapot full of sugar is brought
               to the mrovahs (bridesmaids), which symbolizes that the bride and
               groom will have a sweet life.
               Kashk - The first meal - crushed wheat, beef belly, red, white beans
               and other grains, white rice, boiled overnight in a pot with oil, brought
               to the bride's guests.
               In the past, they brought up to 7 types of national dishes to mrovas:
               "Shirrugan", "Shirbat", "Shirbirinj", "Oshi barik", "Gushtbiryan",
               "Shurbo", "Oshipalav" and others.


               Practice (Beliefs) – The territory of the village was mainly a road
               towards Kulob, between the mountains. The name Rogun, which is
               derived from the word "rogh".
